```mermaid
    participant AS as Authorization Server

    rect rgba(52, 152, 219, 0.14)
        Note over User,AS: Initial Authentication

        User->>GW: API call with access token AT-001<br/>(sub: user-456)
        GW->>GW: Validate AT-001 (signature, exp, scope)
    Note right of AS: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(46, 204, 113, 0.14)
        Note over User,AS: Token Exchange: Gateway Delegation

        GW->>AS: POST /token<br/>grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange<br/>subject_token=AT-001<br/>subject_token_type=...access_token<br/>actor_token=GW-credential<br/>actor_token_type=...access_token<br/>resource=https://service-a.example.com<br/>scope=api:read
        AS->>AS: Validate subject_token (AT-001)<br/>Validate actor_token (GW identity)<br/>Check delegation policy<br/>Issue delegated token AT-002
        AS->>GW: 200 OK<br/>access_token: AT-002<br/>(sub: user-456, act.sub: api-gateway)
    Note right of AS: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(241, 196, 15, 0.14)
        Note over User,AS: Service-to-Service Call with Delegated Token

        GW->>SVC: GET /api/data<br/>Authorization: Bearer AT-002
        SVC->>SVC: Validate AT-002<br/>Verify delegation chain (act.sub)<br/>Check scope
        SVC->>SVC: Process request on behalf of user-456
        SVC->>GW: 200 OK<br/>{"data": "..."}
        GW->>User: 200 OK<br/>{"data": "..."}
    Note right of AS: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end
```

<details><summary><strong>1. User attempts API call to the Gateway</strong></summary>

The client application attaches the user's primary access token (AT-001, representing the `user-456` principal) as a Bearer token and hits a protected endpoint on the edge API Gateway.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>2. API Gateway validates the initial token locally</strong></summary>

The Gateway intercepts the request, locally parsing and cryptographically verifying the JWT signature, checking the `exp` claim, and ensuring the `scope` is sufficient for the target route.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>3. API Gateway initiates Token Exchange request</strong></summary>

Realizing it needs to call a downstream system (Service A), the Gateway sends a POST to the Authorization Server's `/token` endpoint using the `urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange` flow. It includes the user's AT-001 as the `subject_token` and its own credential as the `actor_token`.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>4. Authorization Server evaluates delegation policies</strong></summary>

The AS validates both the user's `subject_token` and the Gateway's `actor_token`. Crucially, it processes its internal delegation matrices to authorize whether this specific Gateway is legally allowed to downscope or mint delegated tokens on behalf of `user-456` targeting Service A.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>5. Authorization Server returns delegated Token to Gateway</strong></summary>

The AS mints a brand-new, customized token (AT-002) and returns it in a 200 OK wrapper. This token natively embeds the delegation chain inside the `act` claim, explicitly recording the Gateway as the active proxy.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>6. API Gateway calls downstream Service A</strong></summary>

The Gateway attaches the newly minted, delegated AT-002 Bearer token and executes the backend service-to-service GET request across the private network mesh.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>7. Service A validates the delegated token</strong></summary>

Service A independently pulls the public JWKS keys from the AS, validates the token's cryptographic signatures, confirms its own identifier is in the `aud` claim, and unpacks the `act.sub` structure to verify the provenance of the delegation chain.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>8. Service A executes on behalf of User</strong></summary>

With authorization validated across both the upstream user and the hopping gateway, the backend executes the actual logic requested by the principal (`user-456`).

</details>
<details><summary><strong>9. Service A returns internal result to Gateway</strong></summary>

The backend microservice returns its processed JSON payload locally to the waiting proxy.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>10. API Gateway returns final payload to User</strong></summary>

The Gateway unwraps the backend response and relays the final HTTP 200 OK block, complete with data, back out across the edge to the user's calling application.

</details>

###### 3.6.3.2 Token Exchange Real-World Use Cases

| Use Case | Subject Token | Actor Token | Resulting Token | Semantics |
|:---------|:-------------|:------------|:----------------|:----------|
| **Microservice mesh** | User's access token | Service's client credential | Delegated token with `act` chain | Delegation — full audit trail |
| **API Gateway on-behalf-of** | User's access token | Gateway credential | Token for downstream service | Delegation |
| **Legacy backend integration** | User's access token | Gateway credential | Impersonation token (no `act`) | Impersonation — backward compatibility |
| **Cross-domain federation** | SAML assertion | Service credential | OAuth access token | Protocol bridging |
| **Customer support impersonation** | Admin's access token | Admin credential | Impersonation token (sub = customer) | Impersonation — admin consent required |
| **Cloud storage scoped access** | Service account token | None | Narrowly-scoped token for specific bucket | Downscoping — least privilege |
| **AI agent delegation** | User's access token | Agent credential | Delegated token with agent in `act` chain | Delegation — agent acting for user |

**Refresh tokens from token exchange.** RFC 8693 §4.1 permits the authorization server to include a `refresh_token` in the token exchange response alongside the issued access token. This is policy-dependent — the AS evaluates whether to issue a refresh token based on the client's grant type, the subject token type, and the delegation semantics. Issuing a refresh token is critical for long-running delegated tasks: without one, the agent or gateway must re-perform the full token exchange (including re-authentication or re-authorization) when the access token expires, which may be impractical for tasks spanning hours. When issued, the refresh token is subject to the same rotation and expiry policies as standard refresh tokens (§27.4) — including absolute expiry, idle timeout, and rotation with token family-based fraud detection. Support varies across implementations: Microsoft Entra ID and Okta support refresh token issuance in token exchange responses; some open-source authorization servers do not.

##### 3.6.4 Transaction Tokens (draft-ietf-oauth-transaction-tokens)

RFC 8693 addresses single-hop token delegation — a resource server exchanges an incoming access token for a backend token via a single round-trip to the authorization server. However, modern microservice architectures execute transactions that span **multiple synchronous hops**: an API gateway calls a risk-evaluation service, which calls an approval service, which calls a settlement engine. Each hop needs to know *who* initiated the transaction, *what* the original request parameters were, and *whether* those parameters have been tampered with — without returning to the authorization server at every hop.

The OAuth Transaction Token specification (draft-ietf-oauth-transaction-tokens, v08, IETF OAuth Working Group Last Call) solves this by introducing a short-lived, cryptographically signed JWT that carries immutable authorization context through an entire call chain. The token is issued once by a Transaction Token Service (TTS) — itself an RFC 8693 profile — and then propagated **unmodified** through every downstream workload. Each workload validates the token's signature, audience, and expiry, then extracts the transaction context for its own authorization decision. The trade-off is deliberate: Transaction Tokens sacrifice the dynamic re-evaluation that per-hop AS round-trips would enable, in exchange for millisecond-latency propagation and cryptographic integrity guarantees.

###### JWT Structure and Wire Format

A Transaction Token is a JWS-signed JWT (RFC 7515, RFC 7519) with the media type `application/txntoken+jwt`, identified by the `typ` header parameter `txntoken+jwt`. The specification defines the following claims:

- **`txn`** — Unique transaction identifier (string or UUID), correlated with RFC 8417 Security Event Tokens for cross-system traceability.
- **`sub`** — Principal identity (user or workload) unique within the trust domain.
- **`aud`** — Trust domain identifier; defines the validity boundary of the token.
- **`iat`**, **`exp`** — Issued-at and expiration timestamps. Tokens are intentionally short-lived (typically 30–120 seconds).
- **`scope`** — Transaction purpose or intent, narrowly scoped (e.g. `trade.stocks`).
- **`req_wl`** — Requesting workload identifier(s); traces which workloads initiated the chain.
- **`rctx`** — Requester context (JSON object): contains the original requester's IP address (`req_ip`) and authentication method (`authn`).
- **`tctx`** — Transaction context (JSON object): immutable request parameters and computed authorization details. This is the core innovation — signed, tamper-evident, and propagated unchanged.
- **`cnf`** — Confirmation claim (RFC 7800, optional): proof-of-possession binding, linking the token to a client-held key pair.

<details>
<summary>JWT wire format — Transaction Token payload</summary>

```json
{
  "iat": 1686536226,
  "aud": "finance.trust-domain.example",
  "exp": 1686536586,
  "txn": "97053963-771d-49cc-a4e3-20aad399c312",
  "sub": "d084sdrt234fsaw34tr23t",
  "req_wl": "apigateway.finance.trust-domain.example",
  "rctx": {
    "req_ip": "203.0.113.45",
    "authn": "urn:ietf:rfc:6749"
  },
  "scope": "trade.stocks",
  "tctx": {
    "action": "BUY",
    "ticker": "MSFT",
    "quantity": "100",
    "customer_type": {
      "geo": "US",
      "level": "VIP"
    }
  },
  "cnf": {
    "jkt": "0ZcOCORZNYy-DWpqq30jZyJGHTN0d2HglBV3uiguA4I"
  }
}
```
</details>

The token propagates via the unstructured HTTP header `Txn-Token` (RFC 9110), distinct from the `Authorization` header which may carry a separate workload-to-workload credential:

```http
POST /api/v1/trade/execute HTTP/1.1
Host: settlement.finance.trust-domain.example
Txn-Token: eyJ0eXAiOiJ0eG50b2tlbiter3d0IiwiYWxnIjoiUlMyNTYifQ...
Authorization: Bearer workload-cred-service-a
```

###### Protocol Flow

The issuance flow integrates with RFC 8693 Token Exchange as a profile. An external client presents an OAuth access token to an API Gateway at the trust domain boundary. The Gateway sends a token exchange request to the TTS, specifying `requested_token_type` as `urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:txn_token` along with `request_context` and `request_details` parameters that the TTS maps into the `rctx` and `tctx` claims respectively. The TTS validates the subject token, evaluates scope policy (it may maintain or reduce scope but must never expand it), and returns the signed Transaction Token. The Gateway then initiates the internal call chain, attaching the Txn-Token to every downstream HTTP request.

```mermaid
---
config:
  themeVariables:
    noteBkgColor: "transparent"
    noteBorderColor: "transparent"
    noteTextColor: "#e2e8f0"
    actorTextColor: "#e2e8f0"
    actorBorder: "#64748b"
    actorBkg: "#1e293b"
    signalColor: "#e2e8f0"
    signalTextColor: "#e2e8f0"
    labelTextColor: "#e2e8f0"
    labelBoxBkgColor: "#1e293b"
    labelBoxBorderColor: "#64748b"
    loopTextColor: "#e2e8f0"
  sequence:
    actorMargin: 250
---
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Client
    participant GW as API Gateway
    participant TTS as Transaction Token<br/>Service (TTS)
    participant WL1 as Workload 1<br/>Risk Service
    participant WL2 as Workload 2<br/>Approval Service
    participant WL3 as Workload 3<br/>Settlement

    rect rgba(52, 152, 219, 0.14)
        Note over Client,TTS: Token Issuance (RFC 8693 profile)
        Client->>GW: POST /trade (Authorization: Bearer <access_token>)
        GW->>TTS: POST /token (grant_type=token-exchange,<br/>requested_token_type=txn_token,<br/>subject_token=<access_token>,<br/>request_context, request_details)
        TTS-->>GW: 200 OK { access_token: <signed JWT>,<br/>issued_token_type: txn_token,<br/>expires_in: 60 }
        Note right of WL3: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(46, 204, 113, 0.14)
        Note over GW,WL3: Call Chain Propagation (Txn-Token forwarded unmodified)
        GW->>WL1: POST /risk/evaluate (Txn-Token: <JWT>)
        Note right of WL1: Validate: signature, aud, exp, tctx
        WL1->>WL2: POST /approval/check (Txn-Token: <JWT>)
        Note right of WL2: Validate: signature, aud, exp, tctx
        WL2->>WL3: POST /settlement/execute (Txn-Token: <JWT>)
        Note right of WL3: Validate: signature, aud, exp, tctx<br/>Execute with tctx params (action, ticker, qty)
        WL3-->>WL2: 200 OK { settlement_id }
        WL2-->>WL1: 200 OK { approved: true }
        WL1-->>GW: 200 OK { risk_score: 0.12 }
        Note right of WL3: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    GW-->>Client: 200 OK { trade_confirmed }
```

<details><summary><strong>1. Client submits trade request to API Gateway</strong></summary>

The client application initiates the transaction by sending a `/trade` request. It authenticates to the domain boundary API Gateway using its external OAuth access token.

```http
POST /trade HTTP/1.1
Authorization: Bearer <external_access_token>
```

</details>
<details><summary><strong>2. API Gateway requests Token Exchange from TTS</strong></summary>

The API Gateway presents the original bearer token to the Transaction Token Service (TTS), explicitly requesting a token exchange under RFC 8693. It supplies contextual parameters for the downstream operations.

```http
POST /token HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange
&requested_token_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:txn_token
&subject_token=<external_access_token>
&request_context=...
&request_details=...
```

</details>
<details><summary><strong>3. TTS issues signed Transaction Token to API Gateway</strong></summary>

The TTS validates the initial token, applies security policy, and returns a short-lived (e.g., 60-second) signed JWT (the Transaction Token) representing the internal propagation identity.

```json
{
  "access_token": "eyJhbG...<signed_jwt>...Qz",
  "issued_token_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:txn_token",
  "token_type": "Bearer",
  "expires_in": 60
}
```

</details>
<details><summary><strong>4. API Gateway invokes Risk Service with Txn-Token</strong></summary>

The API Gateway drops the external access token and forwards the internal `Txn-Token` downstream to the Risk Service (Workload 1).

```http
POST /risk/evaluate HTTP/1.1
Txn-Token: eyJhbG...<signed_jwt>...Qz
```

</details>
<details><summary><strong>5. Risk Service delegates to Approval Service with Txn-Token</strong></summary>

After verifying the token payload (including signature, expiry, and `tctx`), the Risk Service propagates the **exact same** unmodified token to the Approval Service (Workload 2).

</details>
<details><summary><strong>6. Approval Service instructs Settlement Service with Txn-Token</strong></summary>

The Approval Service performs its own independent verification of the token, then forwards it unchanged to the Settlement Service (Workload 3).

</details>
<details><summary><strong>7. Settlement Service executes payload and returns confirmation</strong></summary>

The Settlement Service executes its specific routines based on the parameters found in the claims inside the `tctx` context property (such as the requested action, target ticker, and quantity). Having verified the token validity and identity, it returns a 200 OK signal back up the chain.

```json
{
  "settlement_id": "SETTLE-987654321"
}
```

</details>
<details><summary><strong>8. Approval Service returns approval status to Risk Service</strong></summary>

The Approval Service observes the successful settlement and returns its own confirmation context up the chain.

```json
{
  "approved": true
}
```

</details>
<details><summary><strong>9. Risk Service returns risk score to API Gateway</strong></summary>

The Risk Service completes its local operations and responds to the gateway with the final risk assessment metric.

```json
{
  "risk_score": 0.12
}
```

</details>
<details><summary><strong>10. API Gateway confirms trade to Client</strong></summary>

The API Gateway abstracts all internal hops and translates the chain of internal responses into a single external confirmation response to the original caller.

</details>

<br/>

Each workload in the chain performs the same validation steps: verify the JWS signature against the TTS's public key, confirm that the `aud` claim matches the local trust domain, check that `exp` has not passed, and extract authorization context from `tctx`, `scope`, and `rctx`. Crucially, the token is **forwarded as-received** — workloads must not modify, re-sign, or selectively strip claims.

###### Call Chain vs. RFC 8693 Token Exchange

Transaction Tokens and vanilla RFC 8693 Token Exchange address different topologies. RFC 8693 is designed for **single-hop token translation**: a resource server exchanges an incoming token for a different token type (OAuth → SAML, or broad-scope → narrow-scope) in one AS round-trip. The resulting token is consumed immediately by the downstream service — there is no expectation of further propagation.

Transaction Tokens extend this model to **multi-hop call chains** within a single trust domain. Rather than requiring each hop to perform its own token exchange (which would introduce latency and a dependency on AS availability at every hop), the TTS issues a single token that is cryptographically bound to immutable transaction context. The trade-off is that the authorization decision is "baked in" at issuance time — downstream workloads cannot request expanded scope or re-evaluate the principal's permissions dynamically. The two mechanisms are complementary: RFC 8693 handles cross-domain or cross-protocol federation at the trust boundary, while Transaction Tokens handle efficient, integrity-preserving context propagation inside the trust domain.

###### Agent Extension (draft-oauth-transaction-tokens-for-agents-04)

A companion draft, draft-oauth-transaction-tokens-for-agents-04, extends the Transaction Token format for AI agent and autonomous workload scenarios. It introduces three additional claims:

- **`actor`** — Identifies the AI agent performing the action (agent ID, version, deployment).
- **`principal`** — Identifies the human who initiated the agent's task. Omitted for fully autonomous agents with no human trigger.
- **`agentic_ctx`** — Agent operational constraints: `agent_type`, `intent`, `allowed_actions`, and `environment_constraints` (e.g. region, environment tier).

<details>
<summary>Agent-extended Transaction Token payload</summary>

```json
{
  "iat": 1686536226,
  "aud": "platform.trust-domain.example",
  "exp": 1686536586,
  "txn": "agent-task-20250328-550e8400",
  "sub": "system",
  "principal": "user:alice@example.com",
  "actor": {
    "agent_id": "agent-1234",
    "version": "v2.1.0",
    "deployment": "prod-us-east-1"
  },
  "agentic_ctx": {
    "agent_type": "tool-orchestrator",
    "intent": "enumerate and validate production search services",
    "allowed_actions": ["read"],
    "environment_constraints": {
      "environment": "prod",
      "region": "us"
    }
  },
  "scope": "inventory.read",
  "req_wl": "agent-controller.platform.trust-domain.example",
  "tctx": {
    "resource_type": "search_service",
    "query_filter": "status=active"
  }
}
```
</details>

This enables downstream services to enforce agent-specific policies — a workload receiving a token with `allowed_actions: ["read"]` can deny write operations regardless of the agent's transport-level credentials.

###### Use Cases

**Microservice authorization** — In a multi-tier architecture (gateway → risk → approval → settlement), each service independently verifies transaction legitimacy using the cryptographically signed `tctx` without trusting upstream services or maintaining shared session state. The Tokenetes project (CNCF Sandbox, contributed by SGNL.ai) provides a Kubernetes-native reference implementation with a sidecar agent for per-workload validation and SPIFFE/SPIRE integration for workload identity bootstrapping.

**Zero-trust architectures** — Transaction Tokens operationalize the "never trust, always verify" principle at the application layer. Every hop validates the token independently — network proximity, prior requests, and upstream service identity are irrelevant. Combined with mTLS (which provides channel confidentiality and workload authentication), Transaction Tokens add request-level authorization context, yielding defense-in-depth.

**Financial transaction integrity** — Trading platforms use the immutable `tctx` to guarantee that order parameters (symbol, quantity, price, side) cannot be modified between the risk check, compliance approval, and settlement execution. The unique `txn` ID links logs across all services into a single audit trail — a requirement under financial regulations such as MiFID II and SEC Rule 17a-4.

**AI agent traceability** — The agent extension's `actor`, `principal`, and `agentic_ctx` claims enable downstream services to enforce action constraints, detect behavioral anomalies (e.g. an agent attempting writes when only reads are allowed), and maintain human accountability chains even when the agent acts autonomously across multiple internal services.

###### Relationship to DR-0003

Transaction Tokens complement several mechanisms covered elsewhere in this document. Compared to DPoP (§29.3), which sender-constrains tokens at the **external** client–AS/RS boundary, Transaction Tokens operate at the **internal** workload-to-workload layer — the two are orthogonal and can be deployed together (DPoP for client-to-gateway, Txn-Tokens for gateway-to-workloads). Compared to CAEP (§31), which propagates **backward** security signals (session revocation, credential compromise) from the AS to relying parties, Transaction Tokens propagate **forward** authorization context from the gateway to downstream services — again, complementary. Architecturally, Transaction Tokens extend the token exchange patterns in §3.6 from single-hop delegation to multi-hop call-chain authorization within a trust domain.

#### 3.7 OAuth 2.1 Consolidation and FAPI 2.0 Security Profile

##### 3.7.1 FAPI 2.0 Security Profile

The Financial-grade API (FAPI) 2.0 Security Profile, published by the OpenID Foundation, constrains OIDC and OAuth 2.0 for environments where the consequences of security failure are severe — financial services (open banking), healthcare (patient data), government identity, and other high-value APIs.

FAPI 2.0 mandates the following security mechanisms:

**Pushed Authorization Requests (PAR, RFC 9126)** — instead of sending the authorization request parameters as query parameters in the front-channel redirect (where they can be intercepted, logged, or tampered with), the client first POSTs the authorization request to a dedicated endpoint at the authorization server:

```
POST /par HTTP/1.1
Host: auth.example.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
(client authentication via private_key_jwt or tls_client_auth)

response_type=code
&client_id=fapi-client-123
&redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Fapp.example.com%2Fcallback
&scope=openid+accounts
&code_challenge=E9Melhoa2OwvFrEMTJguCH...
&code_challenge_method=S256
```

The authorization server validates the request, authenticates the client, and returns a `request_uri`:

```json
{
  "request_uri": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:request_uri:bwc4JK-ESC0w8acc191e-Y1LTC2",
  "expires_in": 60
}
```

The client then redirects the user to the authorization endpoint with only the `request_uri`:

```
GET /authorize?client_id=fapi-client-123&request_uri=urn:ietf:params:oauth:request_uri:bwc4JK-ESC0w8acc191e-Y1LTC2
```

PAR provides three security benefits: (1) the full authorization request parameters are transmitted back-channel with client authentication, preventing parameter tampering; (2) the authorization request is validated before the user is redirected, enabling early error detection; (3) the `request_uri` is opaque and short-lived, reducing information leakage in the URL.

**JWT-Secured Authorization Requests (JAR, RFC 9101)** — the authorization request parameters are packaged as a signed (and optionally encrypted) JWT `request` object. In FAPI 2.0, JAR is not required in the baseline Security Profile because PAR provides equivalent integrity protection — JAR is available in the optional **Message Signing** profile for scenarios requiring cryptographic non-repudiation of the authorization request.

**JWT-Secured Authorization Responses (JARM)** — the authorization response (containing the authorization code) is returned as a signed JWT rather than plain query parameters. Like JAR, JARM is not required in the FAPI 2.0 baseline — with PAR mandatory and `response_type=code`, the authorization response contains only the code and state, which are already protected by PKCE and state validation. JARM is available in the Message Signing profile.

**Sender-constrained access tokens** — FAPI 2.0 mandates that all access tokens be sender-constrained using either DPoP (RFC 9449) or mTLS certificate binding (RFC 8705). This ensures that stolen access tokens are unusable by attackers who do not possess the corresponding proof key. Sender-constrained tokens are covered in detail in §29.

**Asymmetric client authentication** — only `private_key_jwt` and `tls_client_auth` (including `self_signed_tls_client_auth`) are permitted. Shared-secret methods (`client_secret_basic`, `client_secret_post`, `client_secret_jwt`) are prohibited because they require the authorization server to store the client secret, creating a high-value secret compromise target.

**Response type** — only `response_type=code` is permitted. All tokens are delivered via the back-channel token endpoint, never in the front-channel authorization response.

**FAPI 2.0 Hardened Authorization Flow (PAR + PKCE + DPoP):**

```mermaid
---
config:
  sequence:
    messageAlign: left
    noteAlign: left
    actorFontWeight: bold
    messageFontWeight: normal
    actorMargin: 250
  themeVariables:
    noteBkgColor: "transparent"
    noteBorderColor: "transparent"
---
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber

    participant U as User Agent<br/>(Browser)
    participant C as Client Application<br/>(Relying Party)
    participant AS as Authorization Server
    participant RS as Resource Server

    rect rgba(148, 163, 184, 0.14)
        Note over U,RS: Phase 1 — Pushed Authorization Request (PAR)

        C->>AS: POST /par<br/>client_id + redirect_uri + scope=openid profile<br/>response_type=code<br/>code_challenge + code_challenge_method=S256<br/>state + nonce<br/>(client auth: private_key_jwt or mTLS)
        AS->>AS: Validate PAR request:<br/>1. Authenticate client<br/>2. Validate redirect_uri (exact match)<br/>3. Validate scopes<br/>4. Store code_challenge<br/>5. Generate request_uri
        AS->>C: 201 Created<br/>request_uri=urn:ietf:params:oauth:request_uri:...<br/>expires_in=60
    Note right of RS: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(52, 152, 219, 0.14)
        Note over U,RS: Phase 2 — User Authentication (Front-Channel)

        C->>U: 302 Redirect to /authorize<br/>client_id + request_uri + response_type=code
        U->>AS: GET /authorize?client_id=...&request_uri=...
        AS->>AS: Retrieve stored PAR request by request_uri<br/>Verify client_id matches<br/>Present login UI
        U->>AS: Submit credentials (password, MFA, passkey)
        AS->>AS: Authenticate user<br/>Generate authorization code<br/>Bind to code_challenge + redirect_uri
        AS->>U: 302 Redirect to redirect_uri<br/>code + state
        U->>C: GET /callback?code=...&state=...
        C->>C: Verify state matches<br/>(CSRF protection)
    Note right of RS: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(46, 204, 113, 0.14)
        Note over U,RS: Phase 3 — Token Exchange (Back-Channel with DPoP)

        C->>AS: POST /token<br/>grant_type=authorization_code<br/>code + redirect_uri + client_id<br/>code_verifier<br/>DPoP: DPoP header with jwk + htm + htu<br/>(client auth: private_key_jwt or mTLS)
        AS->>AS: Validate:<br/>1. Code valid, unused, not expired<br/>2. PKCE: SHA256(code_verifier) == stored challenge<br/>3. redirect_uri + client_id match<br/>4. Client authentication valid<br/>5. DPoP proof valid (jkt matches)
        AS->>AS: Issue DPoP-bound access token:<br/>cnf.jkt = SHA256(DPoP public key)
        AS->>C: 200 OK<br/>access_token + token_type=DPoP<br/>refresh_token + id_token + scope
        C->>C: Validate ID Token:<br/>sig, iss, aud, exp, nonce
    Note right of RS: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(241, 196, 15, 0.14)
        Note over U,RS: Phase 4 — Resource Access with Sender-Constrained Token

        C->>RS: GET /api/userinfo<br/>Authorization: DPoP {access_token}<br/>DPoP: proof JWT (jwk + htm + htu + ath)
        RS->>RS: Validate access token:<br/>1. Signature + exp + iss + aud + scope<br/>2. cnf.jkt matches DPoP proof key<br/>3. htm matches HTTP method<br/>4. htu matches URL<br/>5. ath matches access token hash
        RS->>C: 200 OK<br/>{sub, name, email, ...}
    Note right of RS: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end
```

<details><summary><strong>1. Client Application pushes authorization request to PAR endpoint</strong></summary>

The client POSTs the full authorization request parameters securely over the back-channel to the new PAR endpoint (`/par`), performing authentication (e.g., `private_key_jwt`). This hides the request parameters from the browser.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>2. Authorization Server validates PAR request internally</strong></summary>

The AS validates the client credentials, verifies `redirect_uri` exact string matching, evaluates scopes, and securely commits the `code_challenge` directly to its internal session state.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>3. Authorization Server returns request_uri to Client</strong></summary>

Upon successful validation, the AS generates a short-lived (60 seconds), unguessable `request_uri` and returns it to the client in a `201 Created` HTTP response.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>4. Client Application redirects User Agent</strong></summary>

The client now initiates the front-channel flow by issuing an HTTP 302. Stripped of sensitive parameters, the redirect URL contains only the bare essentials (`client_id`, `response_type=code`, and the newly issued `request_uri`).

</details>
<details><summary><strong>5. User Agent requests authorization endpoint</strong></summary>

The browser executes the redirect and hits the AS authorization server endpoint with the opaque `request_uri`.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>6. Authorization Server maps request_uri and presents UI</strong></summary>

The AS leverages the `request_uri` to pluck the original full PAR payload from its internal cache, verifies the `client_id` binding, and renders the authentication interface for the user.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>7. User Agent submits credentials</strong></summary>

The user enters their credentials (password, passkey, MFA) into the authorization server's native form boundary.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>8. Authorization Server authenticates and generates code</strong></summary>

The AS authenticates the user, processes consent, and mints a single-use authorization code, permanently binding it to the session's PKCE `code_challenge` and `redirect_uri`.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>9. Authorization Server redirects User Agent</strong></summary>

The AS issues an HTTP 302 sending the browser back to the registered callback URL, appending the generated code and the original CSRF `state`.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>10. User Agent delivers code to Client Application</strong></summary>

The browser executes the redirect and drops the authorization code at the client application's `/callback` route.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>11. Client Application verifies state locally</strong></summary>

The client extracts the `state` parameter and validates it against its local session cache to prevent CSRF callback manipulation.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>12. Client Application submits back-channel DPoP Token Request</strong></summary>

The client contacts the `/token` endpoint to exchange the code. It must include the original `code_verifier`, perform client authentication, and—for DPoP—inject an asymmetric HTTP signature (`DPoP` header) confirming it holds the private key half of a newly generated ephemeral keypair.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>13. Authorization Server validates Request, PKCE and DPoP Proof</strong></summary>

The AS validates all standard code exchange mechanics (expiry, single-use, URI match, client auth). It also performs the crucial PKCE check (`SHA256(verifier) == challenge`) and rigorously validates the inbound DPoP header signature.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>14. Authorization Server issues DPoP-bound access token</strong></summary>

The AS mints an access token. Unlike a generic Bearer token, the AS binds this token to the client's DPoP public key by injecting the `cnf.jkt` claim (combining the SHA256 thumbprint of the client's key directly into the token's JSON structure).

</details>
<details><summary><strong>15. Authorization Server returns Token bundle to Client</strong></summary>

The AS responds with HTTP 200, delivering the sender-constrained access token alongside an ID token and refresh token.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>16. Client Application validates ID Token statically</strong></summary>

The client processes the OIDC ID Token locally, confirming the issuer's signature, audience bounds, expiration times, and replay nonce.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>17. Client Application queries Resource Server with DPoP proof</strong></summary>

To call an API, the client attaches the access token via `Authorization: DPoP <token>` and MUST synthesize a fresh DPoP proof JWT for this specific HTTP method and URL path, placing it in the `DPoP` HTTP header.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>18. Resource Server validates access token and DPoP binding</strong></summary>

The RS validates the access token's signatures and claims. It then intercepts the DPoP proof JWT header, validates its signature, string-matches the request URL (`htu`), standardizes the HTTP method (`htm`), and confirms that the public key hash inside the DPoP proof equals the `cnf.jkt` claim hardcoded inside the access token.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>19. Resource Server returns Protected Resource</strong></summary>

Satisfied that the caller legitimately possesses both the access token and the private key of the original requester, the RS processes the operation and returns an HTTP 200.

</details>

<br/>

**FAPI 2.0 vs OIDC Core Security Comparison:**

| Security Requirement | OIDC Core / OAuth 2.0 | FAPI 2.0 Security Profile | Security Benefit |
|:---------------------|:----------------------|:---------------------------|:-----------------|
| **PKCE** | Optional (recommended for public clients) | ✅ Mandatory (`S256` only) | Prevents authorization code interception |
| **Implicit grant** | Allowed | ❌ Prohibited | Eliminates token-in-URL exposure |
| **ROPC grant** | Allowed | ❌ Prohibited | Eliminates credential exposure |
| **PAR** | Optional | ✅ Mandatory | Prevents parameter tampering; back-channel validation |
| **JAR** | Optional | Optional (baseline) / Mandatory (Message Signing) | Cryptographic request integrity |
| **JARM** | Optional | Optional (baseline) / Mandatory (Message Signing) | Cryptographic response integrity |
| **Sender-constrained tokens** | Optional | ✅ Mandatory (DPoP or mTLS) | Token theft mitigation |
| **Client authentication** | Any method (including shared secrets) | ✅ `private_key_jwt` or `tls_client_auth` only | Eliminates shared secret compromise |
| **Redirect URI matching** | Implementation-dependent | ✅ Exact string matching | Prevents open redirect |
| **response_type** | Any (`code`, `token`, `id_token`, hybrids) | ✅ `code` only | All tokens via back-channel only |
| **Refresh token rotation** | Optional | ✅ Recommended | Detects token theft |
| **Token endpoint signing algorithm** | Any (including `none`) | ✅ PS256, ES256, ES384, ES512, RS256 (no `none`, no HMAC) | Prevents algorithm confusion |
| **ID Token encryption** | Optional | Optional (recommended for sensitive data) | Protects identity claims in transit |
| **Scopes** | Client-defined | ✅ Restrictive scope definitions per use case | Limits excessive permissions |

**PAR Security Properties Comparison:**

| Property | Standard OAuth 2.0 | OAuth 2.0 + PAR (RFC 9126) |
|:---------|:-------------------|:---------------------------|
| Authorization parameters exposed in URL | ✅ Yes (full scope, redirect_uri, state, nonce) | ❌ No (only client_id + request_uri) |
| Client authentication at authorization time | ❌ No (client auth only at token endpoint) | ✅ Yes (client auth at PAR endpoint) |
| Parameter tampering possible | ✅ Yes (front-channel URL parameters) | ❌ No (parameters validated server-side) |
| Early error detection | ❌ No (errors displayed to user) | ✅ Yes (errors returned to client directly) |
| Authorization code injection (RFC 9700 §2.1.1) | ⚠️ Possible | ❌ Prevented by PAR binding |

#### 3.8 Protocol Comparison Matrix: SAML vs. OIDC vs. OAuth 2.0

The following matrix compares the three dominant federation and authorization protocols across architectural, security, and operational dimensions. SAML 2.0 is covered in detail in §2; this comparison synthesises the key differences between all three.

| Dimension | SAML 2.0 | OpenID Connect 1.0 | OAuth 2.0 |
|:----------|:---------|:-------------------|:----------|
| **Primary purpose** | Authentication (SSO) + Attribute exchange | Authentication (identity layer) | Authorisation (delegated resource access) |
| **Year / maturity** | 2005 (OASIS Standard) | 2014 (OpenID Foundation) | 2012 (IETF RFC 6749) |
| **Token format** | XML Assertion (signed, optionally encrypted) | JWT (ID Token) + opaque/JWT access token | No standardised format (opaque or JWT) |
| **Transport** | XML over HTTP-POST, HTTP-Redirect, SOAP, Artifact | JSON/JWT over HTTPS | JSON over HTTPS |
| **Serialisation** | XML (verbose, schema-validated) | JSON (compact, easy to parse) | JSON |
| **Signature model** | Enveloped XML DSig (canonicalisation required) | JWS (RFC 7515) — base64url + HMAC/RSA/EC | JWS for JWT access tokens; N/A for opaque |
| **Encryption** | XML Encryption (EncryptedAssertion) | JWE (RFC 7516) for ID Token/UserInfo | JWE for JWT access tokens (rare) |
| **Metadata discovery** | XML EntityDescriptor (out-of-band exchange) | `/.well-known/openid-configuration` (HTTP) | RFC 8414 (Authorization Server Metadata) |
| **Dynamic registration** | ❌ Not supported | ✅ OIDC Dynamic Client Registration | ✅ RFC 7591 (OAuth Dynamic Registration) |
| **Client auth methods** | N/A (SP authenticates via TLS + metadata trust) | 7 methods (§3.4) | Same as OIDC (shared IETF RFCs) |
| **Logout** | SLO Profile (front/back-channel, SOAP) | Front-Channel, Back-Channel, RP-Initiated Logout | N/A (no native logout) |
| **Multi-factor support** | `RequestedAuthnContext` + `AuthnContextClassRef` | `acr_values` + `acr`/`amr` claims | N/A (authorisation only) |
| **Mobile/native app support** | ❌ Poor (XML/browser-dependent) | ✅ Excellent (JSON/JWT, PKCE, native SDKs) | ✅ Excellent |
| **API protection** | ❌ Not designed for APIs | ✅ Via access tokens (JWT or opaque) | ✅ Primary purpose |
| **Attack surface** | XML Signature Wrapping, XXE, DNS-based hijacking | Token leakage, CSRF, code interception (mitigated by PKCE) | Same as OIDC (shared grant types) |
| **Dominant adoption** | Enterprise SSO, government, academic federations | Consumer apps, modern enterprise, financial APIs | API authorisation (complement to OIDC for identity) |
| **Security hardening** | Metadata-driven trust, assertion encryption | FAPI 2.0 (PAR, DPoP/mTLS, private_key_jwt) | Same as OIDC (shared FAPI profile) |

**When to use each:**

- **SAML 2.0** — when integrating with enterprise IdPs (ADFS, legacy Shibboleth), government federations, academic federations (InCommon, eduGAIN), or healthcare systems that mandate SAML. Migration to OIDC is recommended for new deployments
- **OpenID Connect** — the default choice for all new authentication integrations. Use OIDC for consumer-facing apps, employee SSO, mobile apps, and API-first architectures. Pair with FAPI 2.0 for financial and high-security environments
- **OAuth 2.0 (without OIDC)** — when only authorisation is needed and user identity is not required at the client (e.g., M2M with Client Credentials Grant, API-to-API delegation with Token Exchange). Adding the `openid` scope to any OAuth 2.0 flow activates the OIDC identity layer

#### 3.8.1 Dual-Protocol Federation Bridge (SAML↔OIDC)

Organisations running SAML-based SSO with legacy IdPs often need to expose OIDC endpoints to modern applications — mobile apps, SPAs, and API-first microservices that cannot consume SAML directly. The most common migration approach is a **protocol bridge** — a reverse proxy or identity broker that accepts SAML assertions from legacy IdPs and issues OIDC tokens to modern SPs (or vice versa), enabling incremental migration without requiring all parties to change simultaneously.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     Protocol Bridge (Broker)                     │
│                                                                 │
│   SAML Endpoint              │              OIDC Endpoint       │
│   /saml2/acs                 │              /oidc/authorize      │
│   /saml2/slo                 │              /oidc/token          │
│                              │              /oidc/userinfo       │
│   Receives SAML Assertions   │              Issues JWT tokens    │
│   Validates signatures       │              Generates ID tokens  │
│   Extracts attributes        │              Maps claims          │
└──────────┬───────────────────┴──────────────────┬────────────────┘
           │                                       │
     SAML Response                          OIDC ID/Access Tokens
           │                                       │
    ┌──────┴──────┐                          ┌─────┴─────┐
    │  Legacy IdP  │                          │  Modern SP │
    │  (SAML)      │                          │  (OIDC)    │
    └─────────────┘                          └───────────┘
```

**Token mapping rules** — the bridge translates SAML assertion elements into OIDC claims:

| SAML Element | OIDC Claim | Notes |
|:-------------|:-----------|:------|
| `NameID` (persistent) | `sub` | Use the persistent NameID as the OIDC `sub` claim for identity continuity |
| `AuthnStatement/AuthnContextClassRef` | `acr` | Map SAML authn context class to ACR values; use `acr_values` in OIDC to request equivalent strength |
| `AttributeStatement/eduPersonPrincipalName` | `email` | Direct mapping for email-type attributes |
| `AttributeStatement/eduPersonAffiliation` | `groups` or `roles` | May require transformation — SAML multi-valued attributes map to JSON arrays |
| `Conditions/NotOnOrAfter` | `exp` | Map assertion expiry to JWT expiry |
| `SessionIndex` | `sid` (session ID claim) | OIDC supports session management via back-channel logout or front-channel logout specs |

#### 3.8.2 SAML-to-OIDC Migration Decision Matrix

The following matrix helps organisations decide whether to stay on SAML, migrate to OIDC, or bridge both protocols. Each factor is evaluated against the three strategies:

| Factor | Stay on SAML | Migrate to OIDC | Bridge Both |
|:-------|:-------------|:----------------|:------------|
| **New SP integration** | ✅ If IdP is SAML-only | ✅ If SP supports OIDC | ✅ Bridge transparently |
| **Mobile app authentication** | ❌ SAML is browser-only | ✅ OIDC native support | ✅ Bridge with custom code |
| **SPA (React/Angular)** | ⚠️ Fragile (form POST workaround) | ✅ PKCE + Authorization Code | ✅ Bridge simplifies SPA auth |
| **API-first architecture** | ❌ No native API auth | ✅ Access tokens for APIs | ✅ Bridge issues API tokens |
| **Academic federation (InCommon, eduGAIN)** | ✅ Required | ❌ Not supported | ⚠️ Some bridges exist |
| **Government federation (FranceConnect, eHerkenning)** | ✅ Required | ❌ Not supported | ❌ Must use SAML |
| **Compliance (FIPS 201, HIPAA, PSD2)** | ✅ Long track record | ✅ Growing acceptance | ⚠️ May introduce risk |
| **Mature SAML IdP (Okta, Entra ID, PingIdentity)** | ✅ Full support | ✅ Both protocols native | ✅ No bridge needed — IdP handles both |
| **Budget and timeline** | ✅ No migration cost | ❌ Significant migration effort | ⚠️ Medium effort; ongoing maintenance |

**Key insight:** Modern commercial IdPs (Okta, Microsoft Entra ID, PingIdentity, Forgerock) natively support **both SAML and OIDC**. If the IdP is one of these platforms, migration of individual SPs from SAML to OIDC is a configuration change at the IdP, not a protocol bridge. The bridge approach is necessary only when the IdP is SAML-only (e.g., Shibboleth IdP, legacy custom IdP).

#### 3.9 OAuth Vulnerabilities and Attack Vectors

OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect have been the subject of extensive security research. This section catalogues the most significant protocol-specific vulnerabilities. For the broader authentication attack taxonomy, see §16.

##### 3.9.1 Token Leakage Vectors

Token leakage is the most common class of OAuth vulnerabilities — it occurs when access tokens, authorization codes, or other sensitive credentials are exposed through channels not intended for token transport.

| Leakage Vector | Risk Level | Affected Token Type | Mitigation |
|:---------------|:----------:|:-------------------:|:-----------|
| URL fragment (implicit grant) | High | Access token | Don't use implicit grant |
| URL query parameter | Medium | Authorization code | Short code lifetime; use PAR |
| Referer header | Medium–High | Code, token, state | `Referrer-Policy: no-referrer`; use PAR |
| localStorage/sessionStorage | High | Access token, refresh token | BFF pattern; HttpOnly cookies |
| Browser history | Medium | Authorization code | Short code lifetime |
| Proxy/server logs | Medium | Authorization code | Use PAR; short code lifetime |
| XSS | Critical | Any browser-stored token | BFF pattern; CSP; HttpOnly cookies |
| Browser extension | High | Any browser-stored token | BFF pattern; minimal extension permissions |

The **Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) pattern** is the most comprehensive mitigation — tokens are stored in HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookies on the backend. The browser never sees the access token; it holds only a session cookie that the BFF server uses to make API calls on behalf of the user (see §25).

##### 3.9.2 CSRF and redirect_uri Attacks

**Open redirect via redirect_uri** — If the authorization server accepts a `redirect_uri` that differs from the client's registered callback URLs, an attacker can redirect the authorization code to a malicious endpoint. OAuth 2.1 mandates **exact string matching** of the `redirect_uri` against registered values (RFC 9700 §4.1.1). RFC 9700 further recommends that authorization servers reject authorization requests with `redirect_uri` parameters entirely when only one redirect URI is registered for the client.

**Authorization code injection (RFC 9700 §2.1.1)** — A more subtle CSRF variant: the attacker initiates their own OAuth flow with a different client at the same authorization server, obtains an authorization code for their own session, and then tricks the victim's browser into completing the callback with the attacker's code. If the victim's client does not validate the `state` parameter or if the attacker can predict it, the victim's session is bound to the attacker's authorization code. PAR prevents this attack by binding the authorization code to a specific PAR request that includes client authentication. PKCE also provides protection — the attacker cannot exchange the code without the verifier.

##### 3.9.3 PKCE Bypass Techniques

**PKCE downgrade attack** — If the authorization server accepts both `S256` and `plain` code challenge methods, an attacker who intercepts the authorization request can remove the `code_challenge_method` parameter or change it from `S256` to `plain`. If the authorization server falls back to `plain` when no method is specified, the attacker can use the intercepted `code_challenge` value as the `code_verifier`. **Mitigation:** Authorization servers MUST reject `code_challenge_method=plain` in FAPI 2.0 environments. OAuth 2.1 deprecates `plain` and recommends `S256` as the only acceptable method.

**PKCE code reuse** — If the authorization server does not properly enforce single-use of authorization codes, an attacker who intercepts the code can use it without the PKCE verifier. **Mitigation:** RFC 9700 §4.1.2 states that if the authorization code is used more than once, the authorization server MUST revoke all tokens issued based on that code and reject all subsequent uses.

##### 3.9.4 Refresh Token Rotation Bypass

**Race condition bypass** — If the legitimate client and an attacker both use the same refresh token concurrently, the authorization server may process both requests before either invalidation takes effect. **Mitigation:** Use atomic operations (database transactions with row-level locking) to ensure that refresh token validation and rotation are atomic.

**Network retry false positive** — A legitimate client may retry a token refresh request due to a network timeout after the server has already processed it. The retry uses the same (now-invalidated) refresh token, triggering reuse detection and family revocation — a false positive that terminates the user's legitimate session. **Mitigations:** Use idempotent token refresh at the client — only retry if the server response was definitely not received (e.g., TCP connection reset, not HTTP 500). Implement a short grace period (5–10 seconds) before triggering family revocation, during which reuse is logged but not acted upon. Use a retry counter — allow 1–2 retries within the grace period before revoking (Lucid, 2023).

##### 3.9.5 Authorization Server Confusion Attacks

**IdP mix-up** — When a client is registered with multiple authorization servers, an attacker can trick the client into sending an authorization request to the wrong authorization server, causing the client to accept tokens from an untrusted issuer. **Mitigation:** Include the `iss` (issuer) parameter in the authorization request — the authorization server MUST include `iss` in the authorization response, and the client MUST verify it matches the expected issuer (RFC 9207). Validate the `iss` claim in the ID Token against the expected issuer URL from the discovery document.

**Universal Cross-App Attacks** (Luo et al., USENIX Security 2025) — demonstrated that authorization server confusion can extend across applications on the same device. An attacker's app on the same device can intercept OAuth callbacks intended for a legitimate app when the OS does not enforce scheme exclusivity for custom URI schemes (e.g., `myapp://callback`). **Mitigation:** Use **App Links** (Android) and **Universal Links** (iOS) instead of custom URI schemes for mobile OAuth callbacks. These mechanisms require the legitimate app to verify ownership of the callback domain through a digital asset link file.

##### 3.9.6 RFC 9700 Key Recommendations

RFC 9700 ("OAuth 2.0 Security Best Current Practice", BCP 240, January 2025) consolidates security guidance from a decade of OAuth deployment experience:

| Category | Recommendation | RFC 9700 Section |
|:---------|:--------------|:-----------------|
| **Grant type** | Use Authorization Code + PKCE for all interactive flows | §2.1.1 |
| **Grant type** | Use Client Credentials for M2M | §2.1.2 |
| **Implicit grant** | Do not use; use Authorization Code + PKCE | §2.1.1 |
| **ROPC grant** | Do not use | §2.1.3 |
| **PKCE** | Use `S256` method exclusively; reject `plain` | §2.1.1 |
| **Redirect URI** | Use exact string matching | §4.1.1 |
| **State parameter** | Use for CSRF protection in all front-channel flows | §4.3.1 |
| **Code injection** | Validate issuer in authorization response | §4.1.3 |
| **Code reuse** | Revoke all tokens if authorization code is reused | §4.1.2 |
| **Refresh tokens** | Use rotation with reuse detection | §2.2.1 |
| **Sender-constrained tokens** | Use DPoP or mTLS for all token types | §4.11 |
| **Bearer tokens in URL** | Never send bearer tokens in query parameters | §4.2.2 |
| **Token leakage** | Prevent token exposure via Referer, browser history, logs | §4.2 |
| **Client authentication** | Use `private_key_jwt` or `tls_client_auth` for confidential clients | §4.10 |
| **Browser apps** | Use BFF pattern; do not store tokens in browser | §5 |
| **Native apps** | Use system browser; do not use embedded webviews | §6 |
| **Single-page apps** | Use Authorization Code + PKCE; consider BFF | §7 |
| **Token introspection** | Use HTTPS; authenticate the caller; return minimal info for inactive tokens | §4.7.2 |
| **Token revocation** | Always return 200 OK; revoke associated access tokens when revoking refresh tokens | §4.7.1 |
| **Authorization server metadata** | Publish and validate AS metadata (RFC 8414) | §4.1.4 |
| **Protected resource metadata** | Publish PRM (RFC 9728) for resource servers | §4.1.4 |

#### 3.10 Vendor Implementation Comparison

The following table compares the OAuth/OIDC/FAPI capabilities of the five most widely deployed identity and access management (IAM) platforms, based on publicly available documentation as of March 2026:

| Feature | Okta | Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) | Auth0 (Okta) | Keycloak (OSS) | AWS Cognito |
|:--------|:----:|:----------------------------:|:------------:|:--------------:|:-----------:|
| **OAuth 2.0** | ✅ Full | ✅ Full | ✅ Full | ✅ Full | ✅ Full |
| **OpenID Connect** | ✅ Full | ✅ Full | ✅ Full | ✅ Full | ✅ Full |
| **PKCE (S256)** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **PKCE mandatory** | ✅ Configurable | ✅ For public clients | ✅ Configurable | ✅ Configurable | ⚠️ Optional |
| **Implicit grant** | ⚠️ Deprecated | ❌ Removed | ⚠️ Deprecated | ⚠️ Available (not recommended) | ⚠️ Available |
| **ROPC grant** | ⚠️ Available | ⚠️ Available | ⚠️ Available | ✅ Available | ⚠️ Available |
| **Device Code Grant** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Client Credentials** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Token Exchange (RFC 8693)** | ✅ | ✅ (On-Behalf-Of) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **PAR (RFC 9126)** | ✅ | ✅ Preview | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **DPoP (RFC 9449)** | ✅ | ⚠️ Preview | ✅ | ✅ (since 26.x) | ❌ |
| **mTLS client auth (RFC 8705)** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **private_key_jwt** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ Limited |
| **tls_client_auth** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **FAPI 2.0 Security Profile** | ✅ | ⚠️ Partial | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **FAPI 2.0 Message Signing** | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **Refresh Token Rotation** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Refresh Token Reuse Detection** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ Basic |
| **JWT Access Tokens (RFC 9068)** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ Proprietary format |
| **Opaque Access Tokens** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Token Introspection (RFC 7662)** | ✅ | ✅ (MS Graph) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Token Revocation (RFC 7009)** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ Limited |
| **Dynamic Client Registration (RFC 7591)** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **OIDC Discovery** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Back-Channel Logout** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **CIBA (Backchannel Auth)** | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |

**Vendor notes:**

**Okta** has the most comprehensive FAPI 2.0 support, with full compliance for both the Security Profile and Message Signing profile. Okta's implementation serves as the reference for Open Banking deployments in the UK, Australia, and Brazil. DPoP support is production-ready with SDK support for major platforms.

**Microsoft Entra ID** is the enterprise default for organizations in the Microsoft ecosystem. FAPI 2.0 support is partial — PAR and DPoP are in preview as of early 2026. The proprietary "On-Behalf-Of" (OBO) flow predates RFC 8693 but provides equivalent delegation semantics. Conditional Access provides step-up authentication capabilities beyond standard OIDC `acr_values`.

**Auth0** (an Okta company) offers strong FAPI 2.0 support and a developer-friendly management API. Auth0's extensibility model (Custom APIs, Actions, Rules) enables custom token exchange logic and claim mapping beyond standard specifications. PAR and DPoP are fully supported.

**Keycloak** is the leading open-source IAM platform with significant FAPI 2.0 compliance progress in recent releases (26.x+). DPoP, PAR, JAR, and JARM are all supported. Keycloak's open-source nature enables deployment flexibility (on-premises, cloud, hybrid) attractive for regulated industries. However, Protected Resource Metadata (RFC 9728) is not yet supported.

**AWS Cognito** provides the most basic OAuth/OIDC feature set. It lacks PAR, DPoP, mTLS, JAR, JARM, FAPI 2.0, Token Exchange, and Dynamic Client Registration. Cognito's access tokens use a proprietary JWT format that does not conform to RFC 9068. Cognito is best suited for simple web and mobile app authentication where FAPI-grade security is not required.

---

### 4. WS-Federation

WS-Federation is an identity federation specification originally developed by IBM, Microsoft, BEA Systems, and VeriSign, later standardised as an OASIS standard (WS-Federation 1.2, 2009). It defines how security tokens — typically SAML assertions — are transported between security realms to enable cross-domain SSO. While SAML 2.0 (§2) defines a self-contained protocol with its own binding and profile layer, WS-Federation takes a different architectural approach: it wraps the token issuance semantics of **WS-Trust** inside browser-compatible HTTP redirects and form POSTs, delegating the actual token format and issuance logic to the underlying WS-* stack.

#### 4.1 Protocol Foundations and the WS-* Stack

WS-Federation does not operate in isolation — it sits atop a layered stack of WS-* specifications, each handling a distinct aspect of security token management:

**WS-Security (OASIS, 2004)** — the foundational layer. WS-Security defines how security tokens, XML digital signatures, and XML encryption are attached to SOAP messages. It provides message-level security (confidentiality and integrity) independent of the transport layer — a SOAP message secured with WS-Security remains protected even if relayed through intermediaries. WS-Security supports multiple token types: X.509 certificates, Kerberos tickets, SAML assertions, and username/password tokens.

**WS-Trust (OASIS, 2007)** — the token issuance protocol. WS-Trust defines the **Security Token Service (STS)** model and the SOAP-based message exchange for requesting, issuing, renewing, validating, and cancelling security tokens:

- **RST (RequestSecurityToken)** — a SOAP message sent by a client (or by a relying party on behalf of a client) to an STS, requesting a security token. The RST specifies the desired token type (`wst:TokenType`), the target realm (`wsp:AppliesTo`), and optionally a proof key for holder-of-key confirmation
- **RSTR (RequestSecurityTokenResponse)** — the STS's response, containing the issued security token (e.g., a SAML assertion), the token's lifetime, and any proof-of-possession key material

In WS-Trust's **active** (SOAP) profile, the RST/RSTR exchange happens directly between the client application and the STS over a SOAP channel. The client must be a SOAP-capable application — this is the "active requestor" model used by WCF services and SOAP-based web services.

**WS-SecurityPolicy (OASIS, 2007)** — declares the security requirements of a service endpoint in a machine-readable WS-Policy assertion. A service endpoint's WSDL includes policy assertions specifying which token types are required, which transport security is needed (TLS vs. message-level), and which cryptographic algorithms are acceptable.

**WS-Federation (OASIS, 2009)** — the federation layer. WS-Federation extends WS-Trust to browser-based environments where the user agent (web browser) cannot construct SOAP messages. Instead of SOAP, WS-Federation encodes the RST/RSTR semantics into URL query parameters (for redirects) and HTML form fields (for POST-based token delivery). This is the **passive requestor** profile — the browser acts as a passive intermediary, carrying token requests and responses between the RP and the STS via HTTP redirects and form POSTs.

The key architectural distinction from SAML 2.0: SAML defines its own protocol layer — `AuthnRequest`, `Response`, bindings (Redirect, POST, Artifact), and profiles (Web SSO, SLO, ECP) — as a self-contained specification. WS-Federation, by contrast, is a thin browser-transport wrapper around WS-Trust's existing token issuance semantics. The token format (SAML 1.1, SAML 2.0, or JWT) is orthogonal to the federation protocol — WS-Federation can carry any token type supported by the STS.

**Protocol actions** — WS-Federation defines a small set of actions (the `wa` parameter) that control the federation flow:

| Action | `wa` Parameter | Purpose | Direction |
|:-------|:--------------|:---------|:----------|
| **Sign In** | `wsignin1.0` | Request a security token from the STS | RP → STS → RP |
| **Sign Out** | `wsignout1.0` | Terminate the SSO session at the STS | RP → STS |
| **Sign Out Cleanup** | `wsignoutcleanup1.0` | Notify an RP to delete its local session | STS → RP (via iframe) |

**Sign In** — the RP redirects the user to the STS with `wa=wsignin1.0` plus realm, reply, and optional context parameters. After authenticating the user, the STS returns a signed security token via auto-submit POST form. **Sign Out** — the RP redirects to the STS with `wa=wsignout1.0`; the STS terminates the SSO session and renders cleanup iframes for every known RP. **Sign Out Cleanup** — the STS loads each RP's cleanup endpoint in a hidden iframe; each RP deletes its `FedAuth` cookie and returns a small image indicating success or failure.

WS-Federation was originally developed by IBM, Microsoft, BEA Systems, and VeriSign as part of the broader WS-* web services architecture, standardised as WS-Federation 1.2 by OASIS in 2009. Its primary adopter was the Microsoft ecosystem — ADFS implements WS-Federation as its native browser SSO protocol, and the .NET Framework provided first-class support via Windows Identity Foundation (WIF) and later OWIN middleware. WS-Federation predates SAML 2.0's widespread adoption and was the dominant browser SSO protocol in Microsoft-centric enterprises throughout the 2008–2018 period. Key adopters include SharePoint (on-premises and Online), Exchange hybrid deployments, Dynamics CRM, and custom .NET applications using WIF or OWIN WS-Federation middleware. As of 2026, WS-Federation is considered a **legacy protocol** — Microsoft has deprecated WS-Federation in Entra ID for new applications and recommends OIDC for all new development, though it remains in active use in enterprises with legacy ADFS deployments.

#### 4.2 Passive Requestor Profile (Browser SSO)

The Passive Requestor Profile is the core of WS-Federation's browser-based SSO. It defines how an unauthenticated user is redirected from a Relying Party (RP) to a Security Token Service (STS), authenticated, and returned with a security token — all using standard HTTP mechanisms.

##### 4.2.1 Request Parameters

WS-Federation sign-in requests use URL query parameters to encode the token request. The RP redirects the user's browser to the STS with the following parameters:

| Parameter | Required | Description |
|:----------|:---------|:-----------|
| `wa` | ✅ | **Action** — `wsignin1.0` for sign-in, `wsignout1.0` for sign-out |
| `wtrealm` | ✅ | **Realm** — URI identifying the relying party (equivalent to OIDC `client_id` or SAML `Issuer`) |
| `wreply` | ❌ | **Reply URL** — the URL to which the STS returns the token response (equivalent to OIDC `redirect_uri`). If omitted, the STS uses the pre-configured endpoint for the realm |
| `wctx` | ❌ | **Context** — opaque value created by the RP, echoed back by the STS. Used to maintain state across the redirect (equivalent to OIDC `state`). Prevents CSRF when validated |
| `wfresh` | ❌ | **Freshness** — maximum acceptable age (in minutes) of the user's authentication. `0` forces re-authentication regardless of existing STS session (equivalent to OIDC `prompt=login` or SAML `ForceAuthn="true"`) |
| `whr` | ❌ | **Home Realm** — identifier of the upstream identity provider the STS should use. Bypasses Home Realm Discovery (HRD) — equivalent to OIDC `idp` hint or SAML `IDPEntry` scoping |
| `wauth` | ❌ | **Authentication type** — URI specifying the requested authentication method (equivalent to OIDC `acr_values` or SAML `RequestedAuthnContext`) |
| `wreq` | ❌ | **Token request** — a full WS-Trust RST serialised as a query parameter. Rarely used in practice — most RPs rely on the STS to construct the RST based on the realm's Relying Party Trust configuration |

**Example sign-in redirect:**

```
GET /adfs/ls/?wa=wsignin1.0
    &wtrealm=https%3A%2F%2Fapp.contoso.com%2F
    &wreply=https%3A%2F%2Fapp.contoso.com%2Fauth%2Fwsfed
    &wctx=rm%3D0%26id%3Dpassive%26ru%3D%252F
    &whr=https%3A%2F%2Fpartner-idp.example.com%2F
HTTP/1.1
Host: sts.contoso.com
```

##### 4.2.2 Passive Requestor Flow

```mermaid
---
config:
  sequence:
    messageAlign: left
    noteAlign: left
    actorMargin: 250
  themeVariables:
    noteBkgColor: "transparent"
    noteBorderColor: "transparent"
---
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant User as User (Browser)
    participant RP as Relying Party
    participant STS as Security Token Service<br/>(ADFS / Entra ID)
    participant AD as Identity Store<br/>(Active Directory)

    rect rgba(148, 163, 184, 0.14)
    Note over User, AD: Phase 1 — Unauthenticated Access

    User->>RP: GET /protected-resource
    RP->>User: HTTP 302 Redirect →<br/>STS/adfs/ls/?wa=wsignin1.0<br/>&wtrealm=https://app.contoso.com/<br/>&wreply=https://app.contoso.com/auth/wsfed<br/>&wctx=rm=0&id=passive&ru=%2F
    Note right of AD: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(52, 152, 219, 0.14)
    Note over User, AD: Phase 2 — Authentication at STS

    User->>STS: GET /adfs/ls/?wa=wsignin1.0&wtrealm=...&wreply=...&wctx=...
    STS->>User: Login page (username/password, MFA, certificate, etc.)
    User->>STS: POST credentials
    STS->>AD: Validate credentials (Kerberos / LDAP bind)
    AD->>STS: Authentication result + user attributes (UPN, groups, email)
    Note right of AD: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(46, 204, 113, 0.14)
    Note over User, AD: Phase 3 — Token Issuance and Delivery

    STS->>STS: Construct RSTR:<br/>— Apply Issuance Transform Rules<br/>— Build SAML assertion with claims<br/>— Sign assertion with token-signing certificate
    STS->>User: HTTP 200 with auto-submit HTML form:<br/><form method="POST" action="wreply"><br/>  <input name="wa" value="wsignin1.0"/><br/>  <input name="wresult" value="<RSTR>…</RSTR>"/><br/>  <input name="wctx" value="rm=0&id=passive&ru=%2F"/><br/></form>
    User->>RP: POST /auth/wsfed<br/>wa=wsignin1.0<br/>&wresult=<RSTR with signed SAML assertion><br/>&wctx=rm=0&id=passive&ru=%2F
    Note right of AD: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(241, 196, 15, 0.14)
    Note over User, AD: Phase 4 — Token Validation and Session Creation

    RP->>RP: Validate token:<br/>— Verify XML signature against STS certificate<br/>— Check assertion lifetime (NotBefore / NotOnOrAfter)<br/>— Validate audience (wtrealm matches)<br/>— Extract claims from assertion
    RP->>RP: Create local session<br/>(set FedAuth cookie)
    RP->>User: HTTP 302 → /protected-resource<br/>(with FedAuth session cookie)
    Note right of AD: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end
```

<details><summary><strong>1. User requests protected resource</strong></summary>

The user navigates to a protected page on the Relying Party application. The RP's authentication middleware (e.g., WS-Federation OWIN middleware in .NET, or Windows Identity Foundation in legacy .NET Framework applications) detects that the request has no valid session cookie (`FedAuth`) and initiates the WS-Federation sign-in flow.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>2. Relying Party redirects to STS</strong></summary>

The RP constructs a WS-Federation sign-in URL using the configured STS endpoint and the RP's realm identifier. The redirect includes `wa=wsignin1.0` (action), `wtrealm` (the RP's identifier registered at the STS), `wreply` (the URL where the STS should POST the token), and `wctx` (opaque state to maintain context across the redirect). The browser follows the HTTP 302 redirect to the STS.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>3. Browser arrives at STS</strong></summary>

The user's browser sends the WS-Federation sign-in request to the STS (e.g., ADFS). The STS parses the query parameters, identifies the target RP from the `wtrealm` value, and looks up the associated **Relying Party Trust** configuration to determine which claims rules apply, which token type to issue, and which authentication requirements are configured.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>4. STS presents authentication challenge</strong></summary>

The STS renders its authentication page. Depending on configuration, this may be a username/password form, a certificate selection dialog, Windows Integrated Authentication (Kerberos/NTLM), or a multi-factor authentication challenge. If the user already has a valid SSO session at the STS (from a previous authentication), and `wfresh` does not require re-authentication, this step is skipped.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>5. User submits credentials</strong></summary>

The user provides their authentication credentials to the STS. The specific credential type depends on the STS's authentication policy — forms-based authentication sends credentials via POST, Windows Integrated Authentication uses Kerberos tickets transparently, and certificate-based authentication presents an X.509 client certificate during TLS handshake.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>6. STS validates credentials against identity store</strong></summary>

The STS validates the user's credentials against its configured identity store — typically Active Directory via Kerberos authentication or LDAP bind. For federated scenarios with upstream Claims Provider Trusts, the STS may redirect to an upstream IdP instead of validating locally.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>7. Identity store returns authentication result</strong></summary>

Active Directory returns the authentication result along with the user's attributes — User Principal Name (UPN), group memberships, email address, display name, and other directory attributes. These attributes are the raw material from which the STS constructs claims for the issued token.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>8. STS constructs the security token</strong></summary>

The STS processes the user's attributes through its claims pipeline. First, **Acceptance Transform Rules** on the Claims Provider Trust normalise the incoming attributes into internal claim types. Then, **Issuance Transform Rules** on the Relying Party Trust map internal claims to the outgoing claim types expected by the RP. Finally, the STS constructs a security token (typically a SAML 1.1 assertion) containing the resulting claims, sets the assertion's lifetime (`NotBefore` / `NotOnOrAfter`), and signs it with the STS's token-signing certificate.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>9. STS returns token via auto-submit POST form</strong></summary>

The STS cannot redirect the token back to the RP as a URL query parameter — security tokens are too large and would exceed URL length limits. Instead, the STS returns an HTML page containing a hidden form with the token in the `wresult` field (an XML-encoded `RequestSecurityTokenResponse`), the original `wctx` value, and `wa=wsignin1.0`. JavaScript on the page automatically submits the form to the RP's `wreply` URL. This is functionally equivalent to the SAML HTTP-POST binding.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>10. Browser POSTs token to Relying Party</strong></summary>

The browser submits the auto-POST form to the RP's WS-Federation authentication endpoint. The POST body contains three fields: `wa` (action — `wsignin1.0`), `wresult` (the full RSTR containing the signed security token), and `wctx` (the original context value for state restoration).

</details>

<details><summary><strong>11. Relying Party validates the token</strong></summary>

The RP's authentication middleware extracts the security token from the `wresult` parameter and performs validation: (1) verifies the XML digital signature using the STS's public token-signing certificate (obtained from federation metadata or pre-configured); (2) checks that the assertion's `NotBefore` and `NotOnOrAfter` timestamps are valid; (3) confirms the assertion's audience restriction matches the RP's realm; (4) extracts the claims from the assertion.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>12. Relying Party creates local session</strong></summary>

After successful token validation, the RP creates a local application session. In .NET applications using WIF or OWIN middleware, this results in a `FedAuth` cookie — a chunked, encrypted, and signed cookie containing the serialised claims principal. The `FedAuth` cookie is the RP's session token; subsequent requests are authenticated by validating this cookie without contacting the STS.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>13. User accesses protected resource</strong></summary>

The RP redirects the user to the originally requested resource (using the URL preserved in `wctx`). The browser now includes the `FedAuth` session cookie, and the RP's middleware authenticates the request from the cookie without repeating the WS-Federation flow.

</details>

##### 4.2.3 WS-Trust RST/RSTR Structures

The walkthrough above describes the Passive Requestor Profile at the HTTP level — URL redirects and form POSTs. Underneath, the STS constructs and processes WS-Trust messages internally. Understanding these structures is essential for understanding what happens inside the STS and for the **Active Requestor Profile** (SOAP-based clients).

**RST — RequestSecurityToken** — the token request message. In the passive profile, the STS constructs the RST internally from the URL query parameters (`wa`, `wtrealm`, `wreply`). In the active profile, the client sends a full SOAP RST directly. Key elements:

| Element | Purpose |
|:--------|:--------|
| `t:RequestType` | The operation — `Issue` (new token), `Renew` (extend existing), `Cancel` (revoke), `Validate` (check validity) |
| `t:TokenType` | The requested token type — typically `urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.0:assertion`, `urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:assertion`, or `urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:jwt` |
| `wsp:AppliesTo` | The target realm (RP identifier) — the STS uses this to look up the Relying Party Trust and determine which claims rules to apply |
| `t:KeyType` | How the token is bound to the requestor — `Bearer` (anyone can present it), `SymmetricKey` (STS generates a shared secret), `PublicKey` (client's public key) |
| `t:Lifetime` | Requested token validity window (`Created` / `Expires`) — the STS may override based on policy |
| `t:Claims` | Optional list of claim types the client requests — the STS may include additional claims based on policy |

**RSTR — RequestSecurityTokenResponse** — the STS's response containing the issued token. In the passive profile, this RSTR is embedded as an XML string in the `wresult` form field. The RSTR contains the signed security token (SAML assertion), the token's lifetime, and any proof-of-possession key material.

**Key types** — WS-Trust supports three key types that determine how the issued token is bound to the requestor:

| Key Type | URI | Description | Use Case |
|:---------|:---|:-----------|:---------|
| **Bearer** | `.../NoProofKey` | No proof-of-possession — anyone who holds the token can present it | Browser-based SSO (Passive Requestor Profile) |
| **Symmetric Key** | `.../AuthenticatedKey` | STS generates a symmetric key, encrypts part for client and part for RP | WCF message security with mutual authentication |
| **Public Key** | `.../AsymmetricKey` | STS encrypts the proof key using the client's public key | High-security WCF scenarios with client certificates |

In the Passive Requestor Profile, only Bearer is used — the browser has no mechanism for cryptographic proof-of-possession. The Active Requestor Profile supports all three types.

**Lifetime, AppliesTo, and Audience Restriction** — three mechanisms control token scope and validity:

| Mechanism | Where Specified | Enforced By | Purpose |
|:----------|:---------------|:------------|:--------|
| `t:Lifetime` | RST (requested) / RSTR (issued) | RP at token validation | Time-bounds the token's validity |
| `wsp:AppliesTo` | RST | STS (trust selection) | Identifies the target RP for claims rules |
| `AudienceRestriction` | Issued assertion | RP at token validation | Prevents token misuse across RPs |

The `NotBefore` and `NotOnOrAfter` timestamps in the assertion's `<Conditions>` element define the token's validity window. ADFS typically issues tokens with a 1-hour lifetime (configurable per Relying Party Trust). The `<AudienceRestrictionCondition>` contains URIs identifying intended consumers — the RP must verify its own realm appears in the audience list to prevent cross-RP token replay.

#### 4.3 Token Formats

WS-Federation is token-format agnostic — the STS can issue any token type supported by the underlying WS-Trust implementation. In practice, three token formats are used:

**SAML 1.1 tokens (default)** — ADFS issues SAML 1.1 assertion tokens by default for WS-Federation relying parties. SAML 1.1 assertions use the `urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.0:assertion` namespace and carry claims as `<Attribute>` elements within an `<AttributeStatement>`. The subject is identified by a `<NameIdentifier>` (not `<NameID>` as in SAML 2.0). This is a historical choice — WS-Federation predates SAML 2.0, and ADFS maintained SAML 1.1 as the default token type for backward compatibility with legacy WIF-based applications.

**SAML 2.0 tokens** — supported by ADFS and other STS implementations. SAML 2.0 assertions use the `urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:assertion` namespace and provide richer semantics — `<NameID>` with format attributes, `<AudienceRestriction>`, and `<AuthnContext>`. To configure SAML 2.0 tokens in ADFS, the Relying Party Trust's `TokenType` property is set to `urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:assertion`. Note: this is WS-Federation carrying a SAML 2.0 *token* — it is not the SAML 2.0 *protocol* (which has its own distinct binding and profile layer described in §2).

**JWT tokens** — modern STS implementations (Entra ID, IdentityServer, Duende IdentityServer) support issuing JWTs in WS-Federation responses.
