This is particularly useful in hybrid migration scenarios where backend services expect JWT validation. The JWT is embedded in the RSTR's `<RequestedSecurityToken>` element as a `<BinarySecurityToken>` with `ValueType="urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:jwt"`. In ADFS, JWT token issuance requires configuring the Relying Party Trust with `TokenType` set to `urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:jwt`.

Token type negotiation happens out-of-band — the RP's token type preference is configured in the STS's Relying Party Trust, not in the sign-in request itself. The `wreq` parameter *can* include a `wst:TokenType` element in an inline RST to request a specific token type, but this is rarely used in practice.

#### 4.4 ADFS as the Canonical Implementation

Active Directory Federation Services (ADFS) is the dominant WS-Federation STS implementation in enterprise environments. ADFS implements a **claims-based identity model** where identity information is expressed as typed name-value pairs (claims) processed through a rule-based pipeline.

**ADFS architecture components:**

| Component | Role | Description |
|:----------|:-----|:-----------|
| **Federation Service** | Core STS engine | Processes authentication requests, executes the claims pipeline, issues security tokens. Runs as a Windows service on one or more servers in an ADFS farm |
| **Federation Service Proxy** | DMZ-facing gateway | Exposes ADFS endpoints to the internet without requiring direct access to the internal Federation Service. Handles initial request routing and SSL termination |
| **Claims Provider Trust** | Upstream IdP configuration | Represents an identity provider from which ADFS accepts authentication assertions. Active Directory is the default Claims Provider; additional trusts can be configured for partner organisations |
| **Relying Party Trust** | Downstream application configuration | Represents an application that consumes tokens issued by ADFS. Each trust specifies realm, reply URLs, token type, claims rules, and signing certificate |
| **SQL Database / WID** | Configuration and artifact store | Stores ADFS configuration, Relying Party Trust definitions, claims rules, and artifact resolution data. Can use Windows Internal Database (WID) for small farms or SQL Server for enterprise |
| **AD FS Management Console** | Administrative interface | MMC snap-in for configuring trusts, claims rules, certificate management, authentication policies, and monitoring |

**Deployment topologies:**

| Topology | Servers | Database | Use Case |
|:---------|:--------|:---------|:---------|
| **Standalone** | 1 | WID | Lab, testing, single-server pilot |
| **Farm (WID)** | Up to 5 | WID (primary + secondaries) | Small to medium organisations, no external database dependency |
| **Farm (SQL)** | 2+ | SQL Server | Large enterprises, geo-distributed farms, high availability requirements |

##### 4.4.1 Claims Model

A **claim** is a statement about a subject, expressed as a claim type (URI) and a claim value. Standard claim types include:

| Claim Type URI | Short Name | Description |
|:--------------|:-----------|:-----------|
| `http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/upn` | UPN | User Principal Name (e.g., `jane.doe@contoso.com`) |
| `http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/emailaddress` | Email | Email address |
| `http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/name` | Name | Display name |
| `http://schemas.microsoft.com/ws/2008/06/identity/claims/role` | Role | Group membership or application role |
| `http://schemas.microsoft.com/ws/2008/06/identity/claims/groupsid` | Group SID | Windows security group SID |
| `http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/nameidentifier` | Name ID | Unique subject identifier |

##### 4.4.2 Trust Relationships

ADFS defines two types of trust objects that model the federation topology:

**Claims Provider Trust** — represents an upstream identity provider from which ADFS receives claims. Active Directory is automatically configured as a Claims Provider Trust when ADFS is installed. Additional Claims Provider Trusts can be configured for partner IdPs — ADFS acts as a federation hub, accepting tokens from external IdPs and re-issuing tokens for local RPs.

**Relying Party Trust** — represents a downstream application that consumes tokens issued by ADFS. Each Relying Party Trust specifies: the realm identifier (`wtrealm`), the reply URL (`wreply`), the token type (SAML 1.1, SAML 2.0, or JWT), the token lifetime, the token-signing certificate, and the claims rules that govern which claims are included in the issued token.

##### 4.4.3 Claims Rules Pipeline

ADFS processes claims through a three-stage rule pipeline:

1. **Acceptance Transform Rules** (on Claims Provider Trust) — normalise incoming claims from the identity store. Example: map the AD attribute `sAMAccountName` to the claim type `http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/name`
2. **Issuance Authorization Rules** (on Relying Party Trust) — determine whether the user is authorised to access the RP. Example: permit access only if the user is a member of the `AppUsers` group
3. **Issuance Transform Rules** (on Relying Party Trust) — map and transform claims for the outgoing token. Example: transform a group SID claim into a role claim, or add a hardcoded claim value

Claims rules are written in ADFS's custom rule language or configured using predefined templates (Send LDAP Attributes, Send Group Membership, Pass Through or Filter, Custom Rule). The issuance transform rules are the most critical — they determine the exact claims that appear in the security token received by the RP.

**Claims rule language examples** — ADFS uses a pattern-matching syntax (`input_claim => output_action`) with variables, regex matching, and conditional logic:

**Pass-Through Rule** — send all claims of a specific type:
```
@RuleName = "Pass Through UPN"
c:[Type == "http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/upn"]
  => issue(claim = c);
```

**Transform Rule** — extract email domain and issue as a new claim:
```
@RuleName = "Extract Email Domain"
c:[Type == "http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/emailaddress"]
  => issue(Type = "http://contoso.com/claims/domain",
           Value = RegExReplace(c.Value, ".*@(.*)", "$1"));
```

**Filter Rule** — only send role claims matching a specific pattern:
```
@RuleName = "Filter Internal Roles"
c:[Type == "http://schemas.microsoft.com/ws/2008/06/identity/claims/role",
   Value =~ "^(Admin|Editor|Viewer)$"]
  => issue(claim = c);
```

**Join Rule** — combine group SID with a constant to create a role name:
```
@RuleName = "Map Group SID to Role Name"
c:[Type == "http://schemas.microsoft.com/ws/2008/06/identity/claims/groupsid",
   Value == "S-1-5-21-123456-789-101112-513"]
  && c1:[Type == "http://schemas.microsoft.com/ws/2008/06/identity/claims/primarysid"]
  => issue(Type = "http://schemas.microsoft.com/ws/2008/06/identity/claims/role",
           Value = "Domain Admins");
```

**Rule language syntax reference:**

| Syntax Element | Meaning | Example |
|:--------------|:--------|:--------|
| `c:[Type == "..."]` | Match a claim by type | `c:[Type == "upn"]` |
| `c:[Value == "..."]` | Match a claim by exact value | `c:[Value == "admin@contoso.com"]` |
| `c:[Value =~ "..."]` | Match a claim by regex | `c:[Value =~ ".*@contoso\\.com$"]` |
| `=> issue(claim = c)` | Issue the matched claim unchanged | Pass-through |
| `=> issue(Type = "...", Value = "...")` | Issue a new claim with explicit type and value | Transform |
| `=> issue(Type = "...", Value = c.Value)` | Issue using the matched claim's value | Copy with new type |
| `RegExReplace(c.Value, "...", "...")` | Regex replacement on claim value | String manipulation |
| `&& c1:[...]` | Join two claims (AND condition) | Correlate claims |
| `|| c1:[...]` | OR condition between claims | Alternative match |

##### 4.4.4 Certificate Management

ADFS uses three categories of certificates, each serving a distinct cryptographic purpose:

| Certificate | Purpose | Lifetime | Auto-Rollover | Consequence of Expiry |
|:------------|:--------|:---------|:-------------|:---------------------|
| **Token-Signing Certificate** | Signs issued SAML assertions and WS-Federation responses | 1 year (default) | ✅ Yes (when `AutoCertificateRollover` is enabled) | All RPs reject tokens — total authentication outage |
| **Token-Decryption Certificate** | Decrypts encrypted assertions received from Claims Providers | 1 year (default) | ✅ Yes | ADFS cannot decrypt tokens from upstream IdPs |
| **Service Communications Certificate** | TLS/SSL for ADFS endpoints (`/adfs/ls/`, `/adfs/services/trust/`) | Varies | ❌ No (must be replaced manually) | Browser TLS errors, STS endpoint unreachable |

**AutoCertificateRollover** — when enabled, ADFS generates new token-signing and token-decryption certificates approximately 30 days before the current certificate expires. During the rollover window, the Federation Metadata document includes *both* the current and the new certificate. RPs that periodically fetch metadata automatically learn to trust the new certificate. After the rollover period, the old certificate is removed from metadata. RPs that do *not* periodically refresh metadata will experience an authentication outage on the rollover date — this is one of the most common operational failures in ADFS deployments.

**Certificate recommendations:**
1. Enable `AutoCertificateRollover` for all environments
2. Configure RPs to refresh federation metadata at least daily
3. Use a monitoring system to alert when certificates are within 60 days of expiry
4. For Service Communications certificates, use certificates from an internal CA with a 2–3 year validity and set renewal reminders
5. In SQL-backed farms, ensure the certificate private key is stored in a key management system (HSM or Azure Key Vault via ADFS Key Storage)

##### 4.4.5 Multi-Claims-Provider Federation Flow

When ADFS is configured as a federation hub with multiple Claims Provider Trusts, the authentication flow involves Home Realm Discovery (HRD), federated authentication with a partner IdP, and claims pipeline re-issuance. The following diagram illustrates this flow:

```mermaid
---
config:
  sequence:
    messageAlign: left
    noteAlign: left
    actorFontWeight: bold
    messageFontWeight: normal
    actorMargin: 250
  themeVariables:
    noteBkgColor: "transparent"
    noteBorderColor: "transparent"
---
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant User as User (Browser)
    participant RP as Relying Party<br/>(SharePoint / Custom App)
    participant ADFS as ADFS Federation Service<br/>(Home Realm Discovery)
    participant CP1 as Claims Provider 1<br/>(Contoso AD)
    participant CP2 as Claims Provider 2<br/>(Partner IdP — Fabrikam)

    rect rgba(148, 163, 184, 0.14)
    Note over User, CP2: Phase 1 — Initial Redirect and Home Realm Discovery

    User->>RP: GET /protected-resource (no session)
    RP->>User: HTTP 302 → ADFS/adfs/ls/?wa=wsignin1.0&wtrealm=...&wreply=...
    User->>ADFS: GET /adfs/ls/?wa=wsignin1.0&wtrealm=...
    ADFS->>User: HTTP 200 — Home Realm Discovery page
    Note right of User: Lists configured Claims Providers:<br/>• Contoso Employees<br/>• Fabrikam Partners
    Note right of CP2: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(52, 152, 219, 0.14)
    Note over User, CP2: Phase 2 — Federated Authentication with Partner IdP

    User->>ADFS: POST /adfs/ls/ (selected Fabrikam)
    ADFS->>User: HTTP 302 → Fabrikam IdP/?wa=wsignin1.0&wtrealm=...&whr=...
    User->>CP2: GET /?wa=wsignin1.0&wtrealm=... (Fabrikam login page)
    CP2->>User: HTTP 200 — Login page
    User->>CP2: POST credentials
    CP2->>CP2: Authenticate and issue SAML token
    CP2->>User: HTTP 200 — Auto-submit form with SAML token
    Note right of CP2: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(46, 204, 113, 0.14)
    Note over User, CP2: Phase 3 — ADFS Processes Federated Token

    User->>ADFS: POST /adfs/ls/?wa=wsignin1.0&wresult=<SAML from Fabrikam>
    ADFS->>ADFS: Acceptance Transform Rules<br/>(normalise Fabrikam claims to ADFS types)
    ADFS->>ADFS: Issuance Authorization Rules<br/>(check user is authorised for RP)
    ADFS->>ADFS: Issuance Transform Rules<br/>(map claims to RP's expected schema)
    ADFS->>ADFS: Sign assertion with ADFS token-signing cert
    ADFS->>User: HTTP 200 — Auto-submit form with ADFS-issued token
    Note right of CP2: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(241, 196, 15, 0.14)
    Note over User, CP2: Phase 4 — RP Validates Token and Creates Session

    User->>RP: POST /auth/wsfed (wresult=<ADFS token>)
    RP->>RP: Validate signature (ADFS cert), lifetime, audience
    RP->>RP: Create FedAuth session cookie
    RP->>User: HTTP 302 → /protected-resource
    User->>RP: GET /protected-resource (with FedAuth cookie)
    RP->>User: HTTP 200 — Protected resource
    Note right of CP2: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end
```

<details><summary><strong>1. User attempts to access Relying Party application</strong></summary>

An unauthenticated user attempts to access a protected application resource via their browser.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>2. Relying Party redirects User Agent to ADFS</strong></summary>

The RP application intercepts the unauthenticated request, generating a WS-Federation passive sign-in request targeting the master ADFS hub.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>3. User Agent requests ADFS endpoint</strong></summary>

The browser follows the redirect, passing the passive sign-in query string (`wa=wsignin1.0`) directly to ADFS.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>4. ADFS presents Home Realm Discovery interface</strong></summary>

Because ADFS is configured as a global federation multiplexer, it realizes the user can come from multiple sources. It suspends automatic login and presents a visual selection panel (Home Realm Discovery).

</details>
<details><summary><strong>5. User Agent selects designated Claims Provider target</strong></summary>

The user clicks on the Fabrikam IdP button on the HRD interface, choosing to authenticate via an external partner relationship.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>6. ADFS redirects User Agent to Fabrikam Claims Provider</strong></summary>

ADFS generates an HTTP 302, redirecting the browser explicitly out towards Fabrikam's federated endpoints.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>7. User Agent requests Fabrikam authentication endpoint</strong></summary>

The browser executes the cross-domain jump to Fabrikam's secure authentication domain over HTTPS.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>8. Fabrikam Claims Provider presents login interface</strong></summary>

Fabrikam presents its own bespoke corporate login interface. ADFS has no visibility into what happens at this layer.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>9. User Agent submits Fabrikam credentials</strong></summary>

The user inputs their username, password, and potential MFA tokens into Fabrikam's form.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>10. Fabrikam Claims Provider authenticates and issues SAML token</strong></summary>

Fabrikam's backend IDaaS machinery processes the credentials, extracts local claims data, and signs a SAML assertion rooted in Fabrikam's CA trust.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>11. Fabrikam Claims Provider returns auto-submission form to User Agent</strong></summary>

Fabrikam wraps its SAML assertion inside the standard `<form>` tag accompanied by auto-execute JavaScript pointing at the upstream generic ADFS return endpoint.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>12. User Agent posts Fabrikam SAML token to ADFS</strong></summary>

The browser automatically POSTs the Fabrikam payload back across the domain boundary to ADFS's `/adfs/ls/` reception controller.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>13. ADFS evaluates Acceptance Transform Rules</strong></summary>

ADFS ingests the raw token, verifies Fabrikam's signature, and runs Acceptance Transform Rules. These rules normalize external Fabrikam object identifiers (OIDs) into ADFS's standard unified schema.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>14. ADFS evaluates Issuance Authorization Rules</strong></summary>

ADFS runs Issuance Authorization Rules, checking its own local security policies to ensure that this incoming user is legally permitted to access the specific requested target Relying Party.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>15. ADFS evaluates Issuance Transform Rules</strong></summary>

ADFS applies Issuance Transform Rules, formatting the final outbound payload precisely as required by the destination application (removing extraneous attributes or standardizing email formats).

</details>
<details><summary><strong>16. ADFS signs customized SAML assertion payload</strong></summary>

ADFS takes the transformed data, creates a localized SAML object, and signs it using its central unified Token-Signing private key.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>17. ADFS returns auto-submission form to User Agent</strong></summary>

ADFS returns the finalized token to the browser, embedding it directly into an automated HTML POST form pointed at the RP.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>18. User Agent posts ADFS SAML token to Relying Party</strong></summary>

The browser POSTs the consolidated SAML payload over HTTPS to the application's `/auth/wsfed` endpoint.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>19. Relying Party validates token signature and lifetime boundaries</strong></summary>

The RP application validates the cryptographic signature against the well-known ADFS public key configuration and checks the token expiry.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>20. Relying Party initializes application session state</strong></summary>

The RP builds a local session cookie for the user.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>21. Relying Party redirects to originally requested URL</strong></summary>

The RP finishes authentication by issuing a local bounce redirecting the browser back to the target destination path intended in step 1.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>22. User Agent requests protected resource with valid cookie</strong></summary>

The browser executes the deep link, attaching the now-valid `FedAuth` session credential cookie.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>23. Relying Party returns protected application resource</strong></summary>

The RP processes the authenticated call and returns the web application interface successfully.

</details>

#### 4.5 Federation Metadata

WS-Federation defines a metadata document format for automated trust establishment — `FederationMetadata.xml`. This document is typically published at a well-known URL (e.g., `https://sts.contoso.com/FederationMetadata/2007-06/FederationMetadata.xml`) and provides the information an RP needs to configure trust with the STS.

**Structure:**

```xml
<EntityDescriptor xmlns="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:metadata"
                  entityID="http://sts.contoso.com/adfs/services/trust">

  <!-- STS Role Descriptor (WS-Federation namespace) -->
  <RoleDescriptor xmlns:fed="http://docs.oasis-open.org/wsfed/federation/200706"
                  xsi:type="fed:SecurityTokenServiceType"
                  protocolSupportEnumeration=
                    "http://docs.oasis-open.org/wsfed/federation/200706">

    <!-- Token-signing certificate (public key) -->
    <KeyDescriptor use="signing">
      <KeyInfo xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/09/xmldsig#">
        <X509Data>
          <X509Certificate>MIIC8jCCAdqgAwIBAgI...</X509Certificate>
        </X509Data>
      </KeyInfo>
    </KeyDescriptor>

    <!-- Passive requestor endpoint (sign-in URL) -->
    <fed:PassiveRequestorEndpoint>
      <EndpointReference xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2005/08/addressing">
        <Address>https://sts.contoso.com/adfs/ls/</Address>
      </EndpointReference>
    </fed:PassiveRequestorEndpoint>

    <!-- Claim types offered by this STS -->
    <fed:ClaimTypesOffered>
      <auth:ClaimType Uri="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/upn"/>
      <auth:ClaimType Uri="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/emailaddress"/>
      <auth:ClaimType Uri="http://schemas.microsoft.com/ws/2008/06/identity/claims/role"/>
    </fed:ClaimTypesOffered>
  </RoleDescriptor>

  <!-- SAML 2.0 IDPSSODescriptor (for SAML protocol interop) -->
  <IDPSSODescriptor protocolSupportEnumeration="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:protocol">
    <KeyDescriptor use="signing">
      <!-- Same token-signing certificate -->
    </KeyDescriptor>
    <SingleSignOnService
      Binding="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:bindings:HTTP-Redirect"
      Location="https://sts.contoso.com/adfs/ls/"/>
  </IDPSSODescriptor>
</EntityDescriptor>
```

**Comparison with SAML metadata** — WS-Federation metadata shares the same `EntityDescriptor` root element and `KeyDescriptor` structure as SAML 2.0 metadata (both use the `urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:metadata` namespace). The key difference is the `RoleDescriptor` type: WS-Federation uses `fed:SecurityTokenServiceType` with a `fed:PassiveRequestorEndpoint`, while SAML uses `IDPSSODescriptor` with `SingleSignOnService` bindings. ADFS publishes both in the same metadata document, enabling dual-protocol support from a single metadata URL.

Federation metadata also supports **automatic certificate rollover** — when ADFS generates a new token-signing certificate (with `AutoCertificateRollover` enabled), the metadata document is updated to include both the current and the new certificate. RPs that periodically refresh metadata automatically trust the new certificate without manual intervention.

#### 4.6 Sign-Out

WS-Federation defines two sign-out actions:

**`wa=wsignout1.0`** — initiates sign-out at the STS. The RP redirects the user's browser to the STS with `wa=wsignout1.0`:

```
GET /adfs/ls/?wa=wsignout1.0
    &wreply=https%3A%2F%2Fapp.contoso.com%2Fsigned-out
HTTP/1.1
Host: sts.contoso.com
```

The STS terminates the user's SSO session (destroys the STS session cookie). The optional `wreply` parameter specifies where the STS should redirect the user after sign-out is complete.

**`wa=wsignoutcleanup1.0`** — session cleanup notification sent by the STS to each RP. After the STS receives a sign-out request, it notifies every RP that participated in the SSO session by loading their sign-out cleanup endpoints — typically via hidden `<iframe>` elements embedded in the STS's sign-out confirmation page:

```html
<!-- STS sign-out page renders cleanup iframes for each RP -->
<iframe src="https://app1.contoso.com/auth/wsfed?wa=wsignoutcleanup1.0" width="0" height="0"/>
<iframe src="https://app2.contoso.com/auth/wsfed?wa=wsignoutcleanup1.0" width="0" height="0"/>
<iframe src="https://app3.contoso.com/auth/wsfed?wa=wsignoutcleanup1.0" width="0" height="0"/>
```

Upon receiving a `wsignoutcleanup1.0` request, each RP terminates the user's local session (deletes the `FedAuth` cookie) and returns a small image (typically a check mark ✅ or cross ❌) to indicate success or failure. The STS renders these images on its sign-out page to show the user which applications were successfully logged out.

**Reliability issues** — WS-Federation sign-out shares the same fundamental reliability problems as SAML SLO (§2.2) and OIDC Front-Channel Logout (§3). The cleanup notifications are front-channel — they depend on the user's browser loading iframes from each RP. If the browser blocks third-party cookies, if the user closes the browser before the iframes load, or if any RP is temporarily unreachable, that RP's session remains active. There is no back-channel sign-out mechanism in WS-Federation — unlike OIDC, which offers Back-Channel Logout as a server-to-server alternative.

#### 4.7 Comparison with SAML 2.0 and OIDC

| Dimension | WS-Federation | SAML 2.0 (§2) | OpenID Connect (§3) |
|:----------|:-------------|:--------------|:-------------------|
| **Specification body** | OASIS (2009) | OASIS (2005) | OpenID Foundation (2014) |
| **Architecture** | Browser wrapper around WS-Trust | Self-contained protocol (bindings + profiles) | JSON/JWT layer on top of OAuth 2.0 |
| **Token format** | SAML 1.1 (default), SAML 2.0, JWT | SAML 2.0 Assertion (XML) | JWT (ID Token) |
| **Transport encoding** | URL params + HTML form POST | XML over HTTP-POST / Redirect / Artifact / SOAP | JSON over HTTPS |
| **Sign-in request** | `wa=wsignin1.0&wtrealm=...` | `<AuthnRequest>` (base64-encoded XML) | `/authorize?response_type=code&client_id=...` |
| **Token delivery** | POST form field (`wresult`) | POST form field (`SAMLResponse`) | Back-channel token endpoint (code exchange) |
| **Metadata** | `FederationMetadata.xml` | SAML Metadata XML (`EntityDescriptor`) | `/.well-known/openid-configuration` (JSON) |
| **Metadata format** | XML (shared namespace with SAML) | XML | JSON |
| **Bindings** | Passive (redirect + POST) only | Redirect, POST, Artifact, SOAP/ECP | Authorization Code + PKCE (primary), Implicit (deprecated) |
| **Artifact binding** | ❌ Not supported | ✅ (token by reference, back-channel fetch) | N/A (code flow is analogous) |
| **ECP / non-browser flow** | Active Requestor Profile (SOAP) | ECP Profile (SOAP + PAOS) | Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628) |
| **Logout** | Front-channel only (`wsignout1.0` + iframes) | Front-channel + back-channel (SOAP) | Front-channel, Back-channel, RP-Initiated |
| **Force re-auth** | `wfresh=0` | `ForceAuthn="true"` | `prompt=login` |
| **IdP hint** | `whr` (Home Realm) | `<IDPEntry>` in `<Scoping>` | `login_hint` / `idp` parameter |
| **Dynamic registration** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (OIDC Dynamic Client Registration) |
| **Mobile/API support** | ❌ Poor (XML-centric, no native SDK ecosystem) | ❌ Poor (XML, browser-dependent) | ✅ Excellent (JSON, PKCE, native SDKs) |
| **API protection** | ❌ Not designed for APIs | ❌ Not designed for APIs | ✅ Via access tokens (JWT or opaque) |
| **Token validation** | XML DSig (canonicalisation required) | XML DSig | JWS (base64url, simple JSON parsing) |
| **Dominant implementation** | Microsoft ADFS | Shibboleth, ADFS, Okta, PingFederate | All modern IdPs |

**Where WS-Federation is simpler:** WS-Federation has only one browser-based binding (passive redirect + POST) compared to SAML's four bindings. Configuration is often simpler — there is no `AuthnRequest` signing requirement, no artifact resolution endpoint, and no complex XML canonicalisation in the request path. The protocol surface area is smaller.

**Where WS-Federation lacks capability:** No artifact binding (security tokens always travel through the browser), no standardised back-channel logout, no dynamic client registration, no native mobile support, no PKCE equivalent, and no JSON-based tooling. The XML-centric design makes WS-Federation a poor fit for modern API-first and mobile architectures.

#### 4.8 Migration Patterns: WS-Fed to OIDC

Organisations with WS-Federation deployments face a clear technical trajectory toward OIDC. This section provides a comprehensive migration framework covering Microsoft's deprecation timeline, migration paths, step-by-step cloud migration procedures, claims mapping, a decision matrix, and common pitfalls.

##### 4.8.1 Microsoft Deprecation Timeline

Microsoft has progressively deprecated WS-Federation across its identity platform, signalling that OIDC is the strategic direction for authentication:

| Date | Event | Impact |
|:-----|:------|:-------|
| **2018** | Entra ID (Azure AD) stopped recommending WS-Federation for new applications | New App Registrations default to OIDC; WS-Fed available but not recommended |
| **2020** | WS-Federation support removed from ASP.NET Core | `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.WsFederation` package archived; no .NET Core support |
| **2022** | Microsoft announced [ADFS-to-Entra ID migration guidance](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/hybrid/connect/migrate-from-federation-to-cloud-authentication) | "Migrate from federation to cloud authentication" documentation published |
| **2024** | WS-Federation flagged as deprecated in Entra ID application templates | New Enterprise Application templates default to SAML/OIDC; WS-Fed option hidden by default |
| **2025–2026** | WS-Federation continues to function but receives no new feature investment | No breaking removal announced, but deprecation warnings appear in Entra admin centre |

WS-Federation is *not* being "turned off" — existing ADFS and Entra ID WS-Federation integrations continue to function. However, Microsoft no longer invests in the protocol, new applications should use OIDC, and existing applications should plan migration.

##### 4.8.2 Migration Path: WS-Fed to SAML to OIDC

The recommended migration trajectory follows a two-stage path:

**Stage 1: WS-Federation → SAML 2.0** — an intermediate step useful when the target IdP supports SAML but the application's OIDC migration requires code changes. ADFS supports issuing SAML 2.0 tokens to WS-Federation Relying Party Trusts, so this stage may require only a configuration change (switching `TokenType` from SAML 1.1 to SAML 2.0) rather than a code change.

**Stage 2: SAML 2.0 → OIDC** — the final target state. This stage typically requires code changes in the application (replacing XML parsing with JWT validation, changing middleware libraries). For .NET Framework applications, this means replacing WS-Federation OWIN middleware with OIDC OWIN middleware.

**Direct migration (WS-Fed → OIDC):** some organisations can skip the SAML intermediate stage and migrate directly. This is possible when the IdP supports both protocols simultaneously (ADFS 2016+, Entra ID), the application can be modified to use OIDC middleware, and the organisation can absorb the change risk of a single migration step.

##### 4.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

[Microsoft's recommended migration path](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/hybrid/connect/migrate-from-federation-to-cloud-authentication) moves from on-premises ADFS to cloud-native Entra ID authentication via two parallel workstreams: user authentication and application authentication.

**User authentication migration:**

| Step | Action | Description |
|:-----|:-------|:-----------|
| 1 | Deploy Entra Connect | Install Microsoft Entra Connect (or Entra Connect Sync) to synchronise user accounts from on-premises AD to Entra ID |
| 2 | Enable Password Hash Sync (PHS) | Begin syncing password hashes to Entra ID as a fallback authentication method |
| 3 | Configure Pass-Through Authentication (optional) | Deploy PTA agents for organisations requiring on-premises password validation |
| 4 | Enable Seamless SSO | Configure Entra ID Seamless SSO for domain-joined devices (Kerberos-based auto-sign-in) |
| 5 | Staged rollout | Create a security group for pilot users; convert their domain from federated to managed authentication using `Set-MsolDomainAuthentication` |
| 6 | Full cutover | After pilot validation, convert all user domains from federated to managed authentication |
| 7 | Decommission ADFS | Remove ADFS federation trust from Entra ID; decommission ADFS servers |

**Application authentication migration (per application):**

| Step | Action | Description |
|:-----|:-------|:-----------|
| 1 | Register as OIDC client | Create an App Registration in Entra ID (or Application Group in ADFS) for OIDC |
| 2 | Update application code | Replace WS-Fed middleware with OIDC middleware; update claim type references |
| 3 | Test in staging | Verify SSO, claims mapping, logout behaviour, and error handling |
| 4 | Switch production traffic | Update the application's authentication configuration to use the OIDC endpoint |
| 5 | Remove WS-Fed configuration | Delete the old Relying Party Trust from ADFS (if still used) or Enterprise Application from Entra ID |

##### 4.8.4 Claims Mapping: ADFS to SAML to OIDC

One of the most challenging aspects of migration is mapping claim types between protocols. WS-Federation (via ADFS) uses long-form URIs, SAML 2.0 uses short-form attribute names with namespace prefixes, and OIDC uses compact JWT claim names:

| ADFS Claim Type URI | SAML 2.0 Attribute | OIDC JWT Claim | Description |
|:---------------------|:-------------------|:---------------|:------------|
| `.../claims/upn` | `urn:oid:1.3.6.1.4.1.5923.1.1.1.6` (eduPersonPrincipalName) or custom | `upn` (Entra ID) / `preferred_username` | User Principal Name |
| `.../claims/emailaddress` | `urn:oid:0.9.2342.19200300.100.1.3` (mail) | `email` | Email address |
| `.../claims/name` | `urn:oid:2.5.4.3` (cn) | `name` | Display name |
| `.../claims/givenname` | `urn:oid:2.5.4.42` (givenName) | `given_name` | First name |
| `.../claims/surname` | `urn:oid:2.5.4.4` (sn) | `family_name` | Last name |
| `.../claims/role` | Custom attribute or `urn:oid:1.3.6.1.4.1.5923.1.1.1.1` (eduPersonAffiliation) | `roles` | Group membership / role |
| `.../claims/nameidentifier` | `<NameID>` element | `sub` | Unique subject identifier |
| `.../claims/groupsid` | Custom attribute (binary SID) | `groups` (Entra ID — object IDs) | Security group membership |
| `.../claims/authenticationinstant` | `<AuthnInstant>` | `auth_time` | Time of authentication |
| `.../claims/identityprovider` | Custom attribute | `idp` (Entra ID) | Identity provider that authenticated the user |

**Migration note for .NET applications:** ADFS claims use the `ClaimTypes` class constants (e.g., `ClaimTypes.Upn`, `ClaimTypes.Email`, `ClaimTypes.Name`), which map to long-form URIs. OIDC claims in ASP.NET Core use the `JwtClaimTypes` class (e.g., `JwtClaimTypes.Subject`, `JwtClaimTypes.Email`, `JwtClaimTypes.Name`). A claim mapping layer must be added during migration to translate between the two vocabularies.

##### 4.8.5 Migration Decision Matrix

Use this decision matrix to determine the optimal migration strategy for a WS-Federation deployment:

| Scenario | Recommended Strategy | Rationale |
|:---------|:---------------------|:----------|
| **New application** | OIDC directly | No legacy constraint; OIDC is the modern standard with better mobile/API support |
| **Existing .NET Framework app, low risk tolerance** | WS-Fed → OIDC (dual-protocol coexistence) | Lowest risk; both protocols run simultaneously on ADFS until migration is verified |
| **Existing .NET Framework app, must move fast** | WS-Fed → OIDC (direct, with gateway proxy) | Protocol translation proxy (e.g., Entra Application Proxy) allows OIDC client communication without modifying the app |
| **SharePoint on-premises** | WS-Fed → SAML (maintain ADFS) | SharePoint requires SAML or WS-Fed; migrate to SAML first, then evaluate SharePoint-to-cloud migration separately |
| **Multiple applications, phased rollout** | WS-Fed → OIDC (application-by-application) | Each app migrates independently; ADFS/Entra ID supports both protocols concurrently |
| **ADFS server end-of-life** | ADFS → Entra ID (cloud migration) | No new on-premises ADFS; migrate users to PHS/PTA and apps to Entra ID OIDC |
| **Regulatory requirement for on-premises IdP** | Retain ADFS, migrate apps to OIDC | Keep ADFS as the on-premises STS but switch application protocol from WS-Fed to OIDC |
| **Partner federation (B2B)** | Retain SAML 2.0 for federation, OIDC for internal apps | SAML remains the dominant B2B federation protocol; use OIDC only for internal applications |

##### 4.8.6 Migration Risks and Common Pitfalls

| Risk | Description | Mitigation |
|:-----|:-----------|:-----------|
| **Session incompatibility** | Users may experience re-authentication when navigating between migrated (OIDC) and non-migrated (WS-Fed) apps | Ensure the IdP maintains a unified SSO session across protocols (ADFS and Entra ID do this natively) |
| **Claim type mismatch** | Applications expect ADFS claim URIs but receive OIDC JWT claim names | Implement a claim mapping layer; test thoroughly with all expected claim values |
| **Cookie format change** | `FedAuth` cookie (WS-Fed) and session cookie (OIDC) have different formats | Clear all cookies during migration; expect one-time re-authentication per user |
| **Logout behaviour change** | WS-Fed front-channel logout differs from OIDC back-channel logout | Test logout across all application combinations; implement RP-Initiated Logout as a consistent fallback |
| **Metadata refresh failure** | RPs that cached federation metadata may not pick up certificate changes after migration | Enforce periodic metadata refresh; use certificate thumbprint validation rather than static certificate storage |
| **Third-party integration breakage** | API consumers, SaaS integrations, or middleware that expects WS-Fed tokens | Inventory all token consumers before migration; provide advance notice and transition period |

#### 4.9 WS-Federation Security Analysis

Chapter 4's existing coverage focuses on protocol architecture and implementation. This section provides a dedicated security analysis covering XML Signature Wrapping (XSW) attacks, token replay, SOAP message header spoofing, and notable CVEs.

##### 4.9.1 XML Signature Wrapping (XSW) Attacks

WS-Federation tokens are XML documents secured with XML Digital Signatures (XML DSig). The same class of XML Signature Wrapping attacks that affect SAML 2.0 (detailed in DR-0003 §2, Ch.2) apply to WS-Federation tokens with equal severity. The attacker intercepts a legitimate signed SAML assertion, modifies it by adding or moving XML elements *outside* the signed portion, and replays the modified assertion — because XML DSig signs specific elements by ID reference (not the entire document), the added elements pass validation.

The eight canonical XSW attack variants (as categorised by Somorovsky et al., 2012) are all applicable to WS-Federation:

| Variant | Technique | Attack Vector |
|:--------|:----------|:-------------|
| **XSW1** | Move `<Signature>` to sibling of `<Assertion>` | Signature references assertion by ID but is now outside it |
| **XSW2** | Wrap `<Assertion>` in new root element | New root is unsigned; validator parses the wrong assertion |
| **XSW3** | Duplicate `<Assertion>`, place signature on original | Attacker modifies the unsigned duplicate |
| **XSW4** | Remove `<Assertion>` from signed `<SignedInfo>` reference | `<Reference>` URI changed; parser may accept unsigned assertion |
| **XSW5** | Add unsigned sibling elements to signed assertion | Added elements contain attacker-controlled claims |
| **XSW6** | Extend namespace declarations to include unsigned content | Namespace confusion allows unsigned elements to be treated as signed |
| **XSW7** | Use XPath transform to exclude attacker's modifications | XPath filter excludes verification of injected elements |
| **XSW8** | Comment-based wrapping to confuse XML parser | XML comments used to bypass canonicalisation |

**Mitigation strategies:**
1. Use a validated XML-DSig library (e.g., Apache Santuario, .NET's `SignedXml` with security patches) — do not implement XML signature validation from scratch
2. Enforce Exclusive XML Canonicalisation (`http://www.w3.org/2001/10/xml-exc-c14n#`) to prevent namespace-based wrapping attacks
3. Validate assertion structure after signature verification — correct namespace, correct element hierarchy, no unexpected sibling elements
4. Verify the signing certificate against the expected STS's token-signing certificate, not an attacker-controlled certificate
5. Enforce strict XML parsing — disable DTD processing and external entity expansion to prevent XXE attacks

##### 4.9.2 Token Replay and Audience Restriction Bypass

**Token replay** — WS-Federation tokens are bearer tokens with no protocol-level nonce or replay detection mechanism. An attacker who intercepts a valid token (via network sniffing on HTTP, XSS, or log exposure) can replay it against the RP within the token's validity window (typically 1 hour).

Mitigations:
- **TLS everywhere** — the `wresult` POST from the STS to the RP must use HTTPS
- **Short token lifetimes** — reduce the default 60-minute lifetime to 5–15 minutes for high-security applications
- **Replay detection cache** — the RP can maintain a cache of recently seen assertion IDs and reject duplicates. ADFS supports this via the `detectReplayedTokens` configuration
- **SameSite cookie policy** — set `SameSite=Strict` on the `FedAuth` cookie to prevent cross-site request forgery

**Audience restriction bypass** — the `<AudienceRestrictionCondition>` limits the token to a specific RP. If an RP fails to validate the audience restriction (a common implementation bug), the token can be replayed against a different RP, granting access based on claims intended for another application.

##### 4.9.3 Security Model Comparison with SAML

| Security Dimension | WS-Federation | SAML 2.0 | Notes |
|:-------------------|:-------------|:---------|:------|
| **Signature wrapping attacks** | ✅ Vulnerable (all 8 XSW variants) | ✅ Vulnerable (all 8 XSW variants) | Same XML-DSig foundation |
| **Request signing** | ❌ Not supported (`wreq` is unsigned) | ✅ Supported (`AuthnRequest` signing) | SAML provides request integrity |
| **Replay detection** | ❌ No protocol-level nonce | ✅ `<InResponseTo>` + `AssertionID` caching | SAML links response to specific request |
| **AuthnRequest integrity** | ❌ Query params are unsigned | ✅ Signed AuthnRequest | WS-Fed requests can be tampered (mitigated by HTTPS) |
| **Artifact binding** | ❌ Not supported | ✅ Token by reference (no token in browser) | Artifact reduces token exposure |
| **XXE / XML bomb** | ✅ Vulnerable (XML parsing) | ✅ Vulnerable (XML parsing) | Disable DTD processing in all XML parsers |
| **Certificate pinning** | Via metadata exchange | Via metadata exchange | Both rely on metadata for certificate distribution |

##### 4.9.4 Notable CVEs and Security Incidents

| CVE / Advisory | Year | Description | Severity |
|:---------------|:-----|:-----------|:---------|
| **CVE-2024-21302** | 2024 | ADFS token spoofing — attacker could forge a token by manipulating claim values if ADFS has misconfigured encryption | Critical (CVSS 9.8) |
| **CVE-2023-36792** | 2023 | ADFS privilege escalation — race condition in the claims pipeline | Important (CVSS 7.8) |
| **CVE-2023-34492** | 2023 | ADFS security feature bypass — bypasses EAP protections | Important (CVSS 8.1) |
| **MS-RAMP** | Ongoing | Exploits ADFS's lack of replay detection by default — tokens captured from one RP can be replayed against another | High |
| **Golden SAML** | 2020+ | Adversary extracts the token-signing private key and forges arbitrary SAML tokens for any RP — total identity compromise | Critical |

**Golden SAML** (McDermott et al., 2020) deserves special attention. If an attacker compromises the ADFS server, they can extract the token-signing certificate's private key. With this key, the attacker can forge valid SAML assertions for *any* Relying Party Trust, impersonating *any* user, with *any* claims — undetectable by the RP because the forged token has a valid signature from the legitimate STS certificate. Mitigations include: strict network segmentation and endpoint detection on ADFS servers, storing token-signing private keys in an HSM, monitoring ADFS audit logs for configuration changes and token issuance anomalies, and implementing claim-based anomaly detection at RPs (e.g., detecting impossible travel or unusual privilege escalation).

#### 4.10 WS-Federation in Modern Architecture

##### 4.10.1 Why WS-Federation Persists

Despite Microsoft's deprecation signals and the industry's shift toward OIDC, WS-Federation remains in active production use:

| Factor | Description | Prevalence |
|:-------|:-----------|:-----------|
| **SharePoint on-premises** | SharePoint Server 2016/2019/SE requires WS-Federation or SAML for user authentication with ADFS. Microsoft does not support OIDC for on-premises SharePoint | High — thousands of farms globally |
| **Exchange Hybrid** | Exchange hybrid deployments (on-premises + Online) use ADFS with WS-Federation for Autodiscover and OAuth integration | High |
| **.NET Framework applications** | Legacy applications using WIF or OWIN WS-Federation middleware cannot migrate to OIDC without code changes | Medium-High |
| **ADFS as federation hub** | Organisations using ADFS to accept tokens from partner IdPs and re-issue for local RPs have invested heavily in claims pipeline rules | Medium |
| **WCF service mesh** | WCF-based microservices using WS-Trust active profile for service-to-service authentication | Medium |
| **Regulatory and compliance** | Some regulated industries have approved architectures that reference WS-Federation and require change management approval for new protocols | Low-Medium |

##### 4.10.2 Multi-Protocol Interoperability

A key strength of ADFS is its multi-protocol support — a single farm can simultaneously issue tokens for WS-Federation, SAML 2.0, and OIDC clients:

| Client Protocol | ADFS Trust Type | Endpoint | Token Format |
|:----------------|:----------------|:---------|:-------------|
| WS-Federation | Relying Party Trust | `/adfs/ls/` | SAML 1.1 (default), SAML 2.0, JWT |
| SAML 2.0 | Relying Party Trust | `/adfs/ls/` | SAML 2.0 Assertion |
| OpenID Connect | Application Group (Native/Server App) | `/adfs/oauth2/authorize` | JWT (ID Token + Access Token) |
| OAuth 2.0 (M2M) | Application Group (Server App) | `/adfs/oauth2/token` | JWT (Access Token only) |

ADFS maintains a **unified SSO session** for the user regardless of which protocol initiated authentication. If a user authenticates via an OIDC application, subsequent sign-in requests from a WS-Federation application are satisfied without re-authentication (and vice versa). Entra ID similarly supports WS-Federation, SAML 2.0, and OIDC for different Enterprise Applications with a unified SSO session.

##### 4.10.3 Decision Framework

```
Is this a new application?
├── YES → Use OIDC (OpenID Connect)
│   ├── Mobile / native app? → OIDC with PKCE (mandatory)
│   ├── Single-page app (SPA)? → OIDC with Authorization Code + PKCE
│   └── Server-rendered web app? → OIDC with Authorization Code Flow
│
└── NO (existing application or migration)
    ├── Is the app on SharePoint on-premises?
    │   ├── YES → WS-Federation or SAML (no OIDC option)
    │   └── NO → Continue
    ├── Does the app run on .NET Framework with WIF/OWIN WS-Fed?
    │   ├── Can you modify the code?
    │   │   ├── YES → Migrate to OIDC (replace WS-Fed middleware)
    │   │   └── NO → Use protocol translation proxy
    │   └── Continue
    ├── Is this a B2B federation scenario (partner IdP integration)?
    │   ├── YES → SAML 2.0 (dominant B2B protocol)
    │   └── NO → Continue
    ├── Is this a service-to-service (M2M) scenario?
    │   ├── YES → OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials (not WS-Fed or SAML)
    │   └── NO → OIDC
    └── Default → OIDC
```

| Decision Criterion | WS-Federation | SAML 2.0 | OIDC |
|:-------------------|:-------------|:---------|:-----|
| **New development** | ❌ Do not use | ❌ Do not use | ✅ Default choice |
| **Mobile app** | ❌ Not supported | ❌ Poor support | ✅ PKCE + native SDKs |
| **SPA (React/Angular/Vue)** | ❌ Not suitable | ❌ Not suitable | ✅ Authorization Code + PKCE |
| **API protection** | ❌ Not designed for APIs | ❌ Not designed for APIs | ✅ Access tokens (JWT/opaque) |
| **SharePoint on-premises** | ✅ Supported | ✅ Supported | ❌ Not supported |
| **B2B federation** | ⚠️ Rarely used | ✅ Standard protocol | ⚠️ Limited adoption |
| **WCF services** | ✅ Active profile | ⚠️ Possible | ❌ Not applicable |
| **Government/academic federation** | ❌ Rare | ✅ Standard (InCommon, eduGAIN) | ⚠️ Growing adoption |
| **Modern cloud-native** | ❌ Legacy | ❌ Legacy | ✅ Native support |
| **Vendor SaaS integration** | ⚠️ Declining support | ✅ Broad support | ✅ Universal support |

##### 4.10.4 The WS-Federation Endgame

WS-Federation will not be "turned off" — Microsoft has committed to maintaining backward compatibility in ADFS and Entra ID for the foreseeable future. However, the protocol's trajectory is clear:

1. **No new feature investment** — Microsoft is not adding new capabilities (no back-channel logout, no PKCE, no native mobile support, no JSON-based tooling)
2. **No .NET Core / .NET 5+ support** — the WS-Federation OWIN middleware has no ASP.NET Core equivalent; new .NET applications *cannot* use WS-Federation without third-party libraries
3. **Ecosystem erosion** — identity vendors beyond Microsoft (Okta, PingIdentity, Forgerock) have deprioritised or removed WS-Federation support; SAML-to-OIDC migration tools dominate vendor documentation
4. **Security concern accumulation** — as WS-Federation ages, it accumulates unpatched weaknesses (XSW, no request signing, no replay detection) that modern protocols address natively

Organisations should treat WS-Federation as a **legacy protocol with a managed sunset**: maintain existing deployments, plan migration for all applications where OIDC is viable, and accept that a small subset (SharePoint on-premises, WCF services) may require indefinite WS-Federation support.

#### 4.11 Why WS-Federation Still Exists

WS-Federation is a legacy protocol — no new applications should be designed to use it. However, it remains actively deployed in enterprise environments for concrete technical reasons:

1. **SharePoint Server** — the largest single driver of WS-Federation persistence. Thousands of on-premises SharePoint farms rely on WS-Federation with ADFS for claims-based authentication. Migration to SharePoint Online or SharePoint SE with OIDC is underway but slow
2. **Windows Identity Foundation (WIF) applications** — .NET Framework 4.x applications built with WIF's `FederatedAuthentication` module are deeply coupled to WS-Federation. These applications are in maintenance-only mode — rewriting them to use OIDC is a non-trivial development effort
3. **ADFS infrastructure investment** — organisations that have invested in multi-farm ADFS deployments with complex claims rules pipelines, custom authentication methods, and device registration services. Migrating to Entra ID or another cloud IdP requires replicating this logic in the new platform
4. **Hybrid identity topologies** — environments where ADFS serves as a federation hub, accepting tokens from multiple upstream Claims Provider Trusts (partner IdPs) and re-issuing tokens for downstream RPs. The WS-Trust/WS-Federation STS chaining model is difficult to replicate in pure OIDC
5. **Compliance inertia** — authentication protocol changes in regulated environments require formal change management, security assessment, and often external audit. The protocol works, is well-understood, and introduces no new attack surface — the risk of maintaining it is lower than the risk of migrating it

The strategic direction is clear: **OIDC replaces WS-Federation for all new deployments**, with SAML 2.0 as the alternative for enterprise IdP integrations that do not yet support OIDC. WS-Federation enters end-of-life through natural attrition as SharePoint farms migrate to SharePoint Online, .NET Framework applications are replaced by .NET 6+ services, and ADFS deployments are decommissioned in favour of Entra ID.

---

## Knowledge-Based Credentials

### 5. Password Authentication: Three Generations

Password authentication — verifying identity by comparing a remembered secret against a stored reference — remains the most widely deployed authentication mechanism despite decades of effort to replace it. The history of password storage is a history of escalating failures: each generation of protection emerged in response to catastrophic real-world breaches that exposed the inadequacy of the previous approach. This chapter traces the evolution from plaintext storage through modern breach-intelligence-driven verification, providing protocol-level detail on the cryptographic primitives, encoded formats, and operational considerations that define each generation.

Passwords alone satisfy only **AAL1** (§1.1) — NIST SP 800-63B classifies a memorised secret as a single-factor authenticator. No amount of hashing sophistication changes this classification; the assurance level is an inherent property of the factor type, not the storage mechanism. The techniques covered here determine how well the verifier protects credentials *after* authentication — a concern orthogonal to, but equally critical as, the assurance level.

#### 5.1 First Generation: Plaintext and Unsalted Hashes

The earliest computer systems stored passwords exactly as users typed them — plaintext strings in a file readable by the operating system. The original Unix `/etc/passwd` file (1971) is the canonical example: user credentials were stored as cleartext entries, accessible to any process with file-read permissions. The security model relied entirely on operating system access controls, which proved brittle in practice.

##### 5.1.1 Plaintext Storage

Plaintext password storage has a single, catastrophic failure mode: **any breach of the storage system — SQL injection, backup theft, insider access, misconfigured cloud storage — exposes every credential simultaneously and irrecoverably.** There is no computational barrier between the attacker and the passwords. The attacker does not need to crack anything; they simply read the passwords.

Despite being universally condemned, plaintext storage persisted far longer than security professionals expected. Notable examples include:

- **RockYou (2009)** — 32 million passwords stored in plaintext in a SQL database, exposed via SQL injection. The breach provided researchers with the first large-scale empirical dataset of real-world password choices, revealing that `123456`, `12345`, and `123456789` were the three most common passwords
- **Adobe (2013)** — discussed below in §5.1.3 as a case study in encryption-as-storage failure
- **Facebook (2019)** — disclosed that hundreds of millions of Facebook, Instagram, and Facebook Lite passwords were stored in plaintext in internal log files, accessible to thousands of employees. Passwords were logged during authentication processing and retained indefinitely

##### 5.1.2 Unsalted Cryptographic Hashes

The first improvement over plaintext was to store a one-way hash of the password instead of the password itself. During authentication, the system hashes the user's input and compares it to the stored hash — if they match, the password is correct, and the original password never needs to be stored.

The fundamental error of the first generation was using **fast, general-purpose cryptographic hash functions** — MD5 (128-bit, RFC 1321), SHA-1 (160-bit, RFC 3174), or SHA-256 (256-bit, FIPS 180-4) — for password hashing. These functions are designed to be computationally efficient: a modern GPU can compute billions of SHA-256 hashes per second. This speed, which is a desirable property for data integrity verification and digital signatures, is catastrophic for password storage because it makes brute-force and dictionary attacks trivially fast.

**The speed problem in concrete terms:**

| Hash Function | Approximate GPU Rate (RTX 4090) | Time to Exhaust 8-Character Lowercase (26⁸ ≈ 209 billion) |
|:-------------|:-------------------------------|:----------------------------------------------------------|
| MD5 | ~164 billion hashes/s | ~1.3 seconds |
| SHA-1 | ~27 billion hashes/s | ~7.7 seconds |
| SHA-256 | ~22 billion hashes/s | ~9.5 seconds |

These rates demonstrate that unsalted fast hashes provide no meaningful protection against offline attacks. An attacker with a stolen hash database and a consumer GPU can exhaustively search the entire space of common password patterns in minutes to hours.

##### 5.1.3 Rainbow Table Attacks

Without salts, identical passwords produce identical hashes — enabling **precomputed lookup attacks**. A rainbow table is a space-efficient data structure that stores precomputed hash chains mapping hash values back to their plaintext inputs. The technique, formalised by Philippe Oechslin in 2003, uses a reduction function to alternately hash and reduce values through chains, trading computation time for storage space.

The attack is simple: the attacker precomputes a rainbow table for the target hash function (e.g., MD5) covering the expected password space (dictionary words, common patterns, character permutations). When a hash database is obtained, each hash is looked up in the table — if found, the corresponding plaintext is immediately revealed. Because the table is computed once and reused across every breach using the same hash function, the amortised cost per cracked password approaches zero.

**Why rainbow tables devastate unsalted hashes:**

1. **Deterministic output** — the same password always produces the same hash, so the table lookup always succeeds for any password within the precomputed space
2. **Cross-breach applicability** — a single rainbow table for "MD5 of English dictionary + common patterns" works against every MD5-hashed password database in existence
3. **No per-target cost** — unlike brute-force attacks, the attacker pays the computation cost once during table generation, not once per target database

##### 5.1.4 Real-World Breach Case Studies

Two landmark breaches illustrate the consequences of first-generation password storage:

**LinkedIn (2012)** — In June 2012, 6.5 million password hashes were posted to a Russian hacking forum. The full scope was later revealed to be **117 million accounts**. LinkedIn had stored passwords as **unsalted SHA-1 hashes**. Security researchers cracked over 90% of the hashes within days using dictionary attacks and rainbow tables. The breach demonstrated that even a technology company with substantial engineering resources could fail at basic password storage hygiene. LinkedIn was fined €310,000 under the UK Data Protection Act (pre-GDPR) — a penalty that would be orders of magnitude larger under current regulations.

**Adobe (2013)** — In October 2013, attackers exfiltrated the credential database for **153 million Adobe accounts**. Adobe had not used hashing at all — instead, passwords were encrypted (not hashed) using **3DES in ECB (Electronic Codebook) mode** with a single, global encryption key. ECB mode encrypts each block independently without chaining, meaning identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks. Combined with the use of a single key for all users, this meant that all users with the same password had the same ciphertext. Security researchers were able to cross-reference the ciphertext patterns with the (unencrypted) password hint field to deduce plaintext passwords for millions of accounts without ever recovering the encryption key. The Adobe breach remains a textbook example of why **encryption is not a substitute for hashing** — encryption is reversible by design (with the key), while hashing is one-way.

**RockYou (2009)** — In December 2009, a SQL injection attack against the social gaming site RockYou exposed **32 million user passwords stored in plaintext** — no hashing, no encryption. The breach was particularly significant not for its scale but for its research value: the leaked password database became the foundational dataset for password security analysis, revealing that `123456` (0.97%), `12345` (0.87%), and `123456789` (0.82%) were the three most common passwords, with the top password used by over 310,000 users. The RockYou dataset demonstrated conclusively that users choose predictably bad passwords when unconstrained by policy.

**Collection #1 (2019)** — In January 2019, security researcher Troy Hunt disclosed **Collection #1**, a aggregated breach corpus containing **773 million unique email addresses and 21 million unique passwords**. Unlike the previous entries, this was not a single breach but an aggregation of credential pairs from thousands of individual breaches. The dump demonstrated the cumulative risk of password reuse: credentials compromised in a 2012 breach remained fully viable for credential stuffing attacks seven years later. Collection #1 accelerated adoption of breach-password screening — HIBP API usage surged following its disclosure.

**Breach analysis summary:**

| Breach | Year | Accounts | Storage Method | Root Cause | Crack Rate | Primary Lesson |
|:-------|:-----|:---------|:---------------|:-----------|:-----------|:---------------|
| RockYou | 2009 | 32M | Plaintext | SQL injection + no hashing | 100% | Plaintext is never acceptable |
| LinkedIn | 2012 | 117M | Unsalted SHA-1 | SQL injection + no salting | 90%+ | Salting is mandatory |
| Adobe | 2013 | 153M | 3DES-ECB encryption | Wrong primitive + poor key management | 100% (via hints) | Encryption ≠ hashing |
| Collection #1 | 2019 | 773M | Various (aggregated) | Password reuse + credential aggregation | N/A | Reuse is the root vulnerability |

#### 5.2 Second Generation: Salted Adaptive Hashes

The second generation addresses both weaknesses of the first: it defeats rainbow tables through **salting** and defeats brute-force through **adaptive cost functions** that make each hash computation deliberately slow.

##### 5.2.1 The Concept of Salting

A **salt** is a unique, cryptographically random value generated for each password and stored alongside the hash. Before hashing, the password is combined with the salt: `hash(salt || password)` or `hash(password || salt)`. Because each user's salt is different, two users with the same password produce different hashes — completely defeating rainbow table attacks.

**Salt requirements:**

| Property | Requirement | Rationale |
|:---------|:-----------|:----------|
| **Uniqueness** | Unique per user, per password change | Two users with the same password must produce different hashes |
| **Length** | ≥ 128 bits (16 bytes) recommended by NIST | Must be long enough to make precomputation infeasible — 2¹²⁸ possible salt values means an attacker would need 2¹²⁸ rainbow tables |
| **Randomness** | Cryptographically secure random (e.g., `/dev/urandom`, `CSPRNG`) | Predictable salts (e.g., username, sequential counter) reduce entropy and enable targeted precomputation |
| **Storage** | Stored in cleartext alongside the hash | The salt is not secret — it only needs to be unique. Keeping it secret would provide no additional security against offline attacks since the attacker who has the hashes also has the salts |

Salting alone is necessary but not sufficient — it defeats precomputation but does not slow down brute-force attacks against individual hashes. A fast hash function with a salt still permits billions of guesses per second per hash.

##### 5.2.2 bcrypt

**bcrypt** (1999, Niels Provos and David Mazières) is a password hashing function based on the Blowfish block cipher. It was the first widely adopted algorithm designed specifically for password storage, incorporating both salting and an adjustable cost factor.

**Algorithm internals:**

1. Generate a 128-bit (16-byte) random salt
2. Derive the Blowfish key schedule from the password and salt using the **Eksblowfish** (Expensive Key Schedule Blowfish) setup — this is the computationally expensive step, repeated $2^{\text{cost}}$ times. Each iteration re-derives the P-array and S-boxes from the password and salt, making the key schedule itself the expensive operation
3. Encrypt the constant string `"OrpheanBeholderScryDoubt"` (192 bits) using the derived key schedule, performing 64 rounds of Blowfish encryption
4. Encode the result in the Modular Crypt Format

**Encoded hash format:**

```
$2b$12$WApznUphDubN0oeveSXHp.xUBZMOJCnzKN9HIJmBKYJaNVNB9lC62
│  │  │                      │
│  │  │                      └── 184-bit hash (31 characters, base64)
│  │  └── 128-bit salt (22 characters, custom base64)
│  └── Cost factor (log₂ iterations): 2¹² = 4,096 rounds
└── Version identifier ($2b$ = current standard)
```

**Version history:** `$2$` (original), `$2a$` (added null terminator handling), `$2b$` (current — fixed unsigned char overflow in OpenBSD implementation). The `$2y$` prefix is PHP-specific and functionally equivalent to `$2b$`.

**Critical limitation — 72-byte password truncation:** bcrypt truncates passwords to 72 bytes before hashing. Characters beyond byte 72 are silently ignored and do not contribute to the hash. This is a consequence of the Blowfish P-box structure (18 × 4-byte subkeys = 72 bytes). Multi-byte UTF-8 characters mean that 72 bytes may be fewer than 72 characters. Mitigation: pre-hash the password with SHA-256 before passing it to bcrypt, or enforce a 72-byte maximum password length.

**Cost factor selection (2025 guidance):**

| Cost Factor | Iterations (2^cost) | Approximate Time per Hash | Suitability |
|:-----------|:-------------------|:------------------------|:------------|
| 10 | 1,024 | ~65 ms | Minimum acceptable for low-value systems |
| 12 | 4,096 | ~250 ms | **Recommended baseline** for most applications |
| 13 | 8,192 | ~500 ms | Suitable for high-value systems with low login volume |
| 14 | 16,384 | ~1,000 ms | Maximum practical for interactive login |

The cost factor should be calibrated to achieve **250–1,000 ms** per hash on the target server hardware. Verify empirically — do not rely on published benchmarks, as hardware varies significantly.

##### 5.2.2a PBKDF2

**PBKDF2** (Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2, RFC 8018) applies HMAC in a loop to derive a key from a password. Despite being NIST-approved and FIPS-140 validated, it is the weakest of the four common password hashing algorithms because it has no memory-hardness property — a GPU can compute millions of PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 evaluations per second because each evaluation needs only a few hundred bytes of state.

**Algorithm:**

```
DK = T1 || T2 || ... || Tdklen/hlen
Ti = U1 ⊕ U2 ⊕ ... ⊕ Uc
U1 = HMAC(password, salt || INT(i))
Uj = HMAC(password, U(j-1))
```

Where `c` = iteration count, `dklen` = desired key length, `hlen` = HMAC output length. Each block `Ti` requires `c` HMAC evaluations.

**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning | OWASP Minimum |
|:----------|:-------|:--------|:--------------|
| Iteration count | c | Number of HMAC iterations | 600,000 (SHA-256), 210,000 (SHA-1) |
| Salt length | | Cryptographically random bytes | 128 bits (16 bytes) |
| Key length | dkLen | Output key length | 256 bits (32 bytes) |
| Hash function | | HMAC underlying hash | SHA-256 or SHA-512 |

**When PBKDF2 is unavoidable:** FIPS 140-2/FIPS 140-3 validated environments (US federal systems, financial services with regulatory mandates) may require PBKDF2 because bcrypt and Argon2 lack FIPS certification. If mandated, use SHA-512 as the HMAC hash (faster than SHA-256 on 64-bit systems) and maximise the iteration count within the latency budget.

##### 5.2.3 scrypt

**scrypt** (2009, Colin Percival, RFC 7914) extends the adaptive cost concept to include **memory hardness** — it is designed to require a large amount of memory in addition to CPU time, making it resistant to GPU and ASIC attacks where compute-per-watt is high but memory-per-chip is limited.

**Algorithm internals:**

1. Derive an initial key from the password and salt using PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 (1 iteration)
2. Use the derived key to seed the **ROMix** function, which sequentially fills a large memory buffer with pseudorandom data, then performs memory-dependent lookups against that buffer
3. Derive the final output key via a second PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 pass

**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning | Constraint |
|:----------|:-------|:--------|:-----------|
| CPU/memory cost | N | Number of iterations; determines memory requirement (memory ≈ 128 × N × r bytes) | Must be a power of 2; primary cost knob |
| Block size | r | Size of sequential memory reads (in 128-byte blocks) | Affects memory bandwidth requirements |
| Parallelism | p | Number of independent mixing functions | Allows parallelisation but increases memory proportionally |

**Memory requirement:** `128 × N × r` bytes per thread. With the recommended parameters `N=2¹⁵ (32,768), r=8, p=1`, memory consumption is `128 × 32,768 × 8 = 32 MB` per hash computation — forcing an attacker to allocate 32 MB of RAM per parallel crack attempt, dramatically limiting GPU throughput.

**Known weakness — time-memory tradeoff:** scrypt's initial PBKDF2 pass uses only 1 iteration, and its ROMix construction has been shown to admit time-memory tradeoffs more favourable to attackers than Argon2's. The tradeoff reduces memory by a factor of $k$ at a cost of $O(k)$ additional time — worse than Argon2's $O(k^2)$ tradeoff penalty. This means an attacker with limited GPU memory can partially compensate by investing more computation, making scrypt's memory-hardness less robust than Argon2id's at equivalent parameter levels.

**Encoded hash format (libscrypt/OpenSSL):**

```
$scrypt$ln=15,r=8,p=1$aGVsbG8gd29ybGQ$WQv0sJIBfGnxVhXmTjJSWcCWZRqZ/5FlRZGdxvBf/34
│       │            │                 │
│       │            │                 └── Derived key (base64)
│       │            └── Salt (base64)
│       └── Parameters (ln = log₂(N))
└── Algorithm identifier
```

##### 5.2.4 Argon2

**Argon2** (2015, Alex Biryukov, Daniel Dinu, Dmitry Khovratovich) is the winner of the Password Hashing Competition (PHC, §5.4) and the current state-of-the-art password hashing algorithm. It provides tunable memory hardness, time hardness, and parallelism, and addresses weaknesses in both bcrypt and scrypt.

**Three variants:**

| Variant | Memory Access Pattern | Side-Channel Resistance | GPU/ASIC Resistance | Recommended Use |
|:--------|:---------------------|:-----------------------|:-------------------|:---------------|
| **Argon2d** | Data-dependent | ❌ Vulnerable — memory access indices depend on the password, enabling cache-timing attacks | ✅ Maximised — data-dependent access patterns are difficult to optimise on GPUs | Cryptocurrency proof-of-work; **not for password hashing** in shared environments |
| **Argon2i** | Data-independent | ✅ Resistant — memory access pattern is independent of the password | ⚠️ Moderate — data-independent patterns are more predictable and partially exploitable via time-memory trade-offs | Environments where side-channel resistance is paramount (shared servers, cloud) |
| **Argon2id** | Hybrid — first pass uses Argon2i, subsequent passes use Argon2d | ✅ First pass resists side-channel; subsequent passes maximise GPU resistance | ✅ Combines both resistance properties | **Recommended for all password hashing** (OWASP, RFC 9106) |

**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning | OWASP Minimum (2024) |
|:----------|:-------|:--------|:---------------------|
| Memory cost | m | Memory in kibibytes (KiB) | 19,456 KiB (19 MiB) |
| Time cost | t | Number of iterations over memory | 2 |
| Parallelism | p | Number of threads/lanes | 1 |
| Output length | | Length of the derived key in bytes | 32 bytes (256 bits) |
| Salt length | | Length of the random salt | ≥ 16 bytes (128 bits) |

**OWASP alternative configuration:** m=46,080 KiB (46 MiB), t=1, p=1 — equivalent security level with a higher memory / lower iteration tradeoff.

**Argon2id operation:** The algorithm executes in three phases:

1. **Initial hashing** — compute $H_0 = \text{Blake2b}(\text{password} \|\text{ salt} \| t \| m \| p \| \text{version} \| \text{associated\_data} \| \text{key\_length})$ to produce the first two 1 KiB blocks of the memory matrix
2. **Memory filling** — fill a memory matrix of $m$ KiB blocks across $p$ parallel lanes. Each block is computed using the Blake2b compression function applied to previously computed blocks, with indices determined by a variant of the H-address function
3. **Finalization** — compute the final hash by XORing selected blocks from the memory matrix and applying Blake2b to produce the output digest

**Encoded hash format (PHC string format, RFC 9106 §4):**

```
$argon2id$v=19$m=19456,t=2,p=1$Y2F0IGluIGEgaGF0$WL8vm7IPXUD49bfvaHHNq4eSn/sZvMpKLwBCBfM4r40
│        │    │               │                   │
│        │    │               │                   └── Derived key (base64, no padding)
│        │    │               └── Salt (base64, no padding)
│        │    └── Parameters
│        └── Version (19 = 0x13)
└── Variant identifier
```

##### 5.2.5 Algorithm Comparison

| Property | bcrypt | PBKDF2 | scrypt | Argon2id |
|:---------|:-------|:-------|:-------|:---------|
| **Year** | 1999 | 2000 (RFC 8018) | 2009 (RFC 7914) | 2015 (RFC 9106) |
| **Core primitive** | Blowfish cipher | HMAC | PBKDF2 + ROMix | Blake2b compression |
| **Memory hardness** | ❌ ~4 KB fixed | ❌ None | ✅ Configurable (MiB–GiB) | ✅ Configurable (MiB–GiB) |
| **Time hardness** | ✅ $2^{\text{cost}}$ iterations | ✅ Iteration count | ✅ N parameter | ✅ t parameter |
| **Parallelism** | ❌ None | ❌ None | ✅ p parameter | ✅ p (lanes) |
| **Side-channel resistance** | ⚠️ Not designed for | ✅ HMAC-based | ❌ Data-dependent | ✅ Hybrid (Argon2id) |
| **GPU resistance** | ⚠️ Low (4 KB) | ❌ Negligible | ✅ High (32+ MiB) | ✅ Highest |
| **ASIC resistance** | ⚠️ Low | ❌ Negligible | ✅ High | ✅ Highest |
| **Password length limit** | 72 bytes | None | None | None |
| **FIPS certification** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Standardisation** | De facto (OpenBSD) | RFC 8018, NIST SP 800-132 | RFC 7914 | RFC 9106, PHC winner |
| **OWASP status** | ✅ Acceptable (cost ≥ 10) | ✅ Acceptable (high iterations) | ✅ Acceptable | ✅ **Primary recommendation** |

**Decision hierarchy for production systems:** Argon2id → bcrypt (cost ≥ 12) → scrypt → PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512. Use Argon2id for all new systems unless FIPS compliance is required. Use bcrypt if Argon2id is not available in the platform library. Use scrypt only if neither Argon2id nor bcrypt is available. Use PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512 only if FIPS 140-2/3 validation is mandatory. Never use MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, DES crypt, or any unsalted fast hash.

##### 5.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

The following sequence diagram illustrates the complete password authentication lifecycle — from registration through login and optional hash upgrade:

```mermaid
---
config:
  sequence:
    messageAlign: left
    noteAlign: left
    actorFontWeight: bold
    messageFontWeight: normal
    actorMargin: 250
  themeVariables:
    noteBkgColor: "transparent"
    noteBorderColor: "transparent"
---
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant User as User
    participant Client as Client App<br/>(Browser/Mobile)
    participant Server as Auth Server
    participant DB as Credential Store
    participant HIBP as HIBP API<br/>(Breach Check)

    rect rgba(52, 152, 219, 0.14)
    Note over User,HIBP: Registration — Password Creation with Breach Screening
    User->>Client: Enter password during registration
    Client->>Server: POST /register<br/>{username, password} (over TLS)
    Server->>Server: Validate password policy<br/>(length, no blocklist matches)
    Server->>Server: Compute SHA-1 prefix<br/>(first 5 hex chars)
    Server->>HIBP: GET /range/{prefix}
    HIBP-->>Server: Hash suffix list (~800 entries)
    Server->>Server: Check if suffix present<br/>→ NOT found
    Server->>Server: Generate 128-bit salt (CSPRNG)
    Server->>Server: Compute Argon2id(password, salt,<br/>m=19456, t=2, p=1, dkLen=32)
    Server->>DB: Store {username,<br/>＄argon2id＄v=19＄m=19456,t=2,p=1＄<br/>salt＄hash}
    Server-->>Client: 201 Created
    Client-->>User: Registration successful
    Note right of HIBP: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(46, 204, 113, 0.14)
    Note over User,DB: Login — Password Verification
    User->>Client: Enter username + password
    Client->>Server: POST /auth/login<br/>{username, password} (over TLS)
    Server->>DB: Retrieve stored hash for username
    DB-->>Server: ＄argon2id＄v=19＄m=19456,t=2,p=1＄<br/>salt＄stored_hash
    Server->>Server: Parse PHC string → extract<br/>salt, params, stored_hash
    Server->>Server: Compute Argon2id(submitted_password,<br/>salt, params) → candidate_hash
    Server->>Server: Constant-time comparison:<br/>candidate_hash == stored_hash
    alt Hash matches
        Server-->>Client: 200 OK + session token / JWT
        Client-->>User: Logged in successfully
    else Hash does not match
        Server-->>Client: 401 Unauthorized
        Client-->>User: Invalid username or password
    Note right of HIBP: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end
    Note right of HIBP: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(241, 196, 15, 0.14)
    Note over Server,DB: Optional: Transparent Hash Upgrade
    Server->>Server: Detect legacy algorithm<br/>prefix (e.g., ＄2b＄ for bcrypt)
    Server->>Server: Generate new Argon2id hash<br/>from submitted password
    Server->>DB: UPDATE stored hash to Argon2id
    Note right of HIBP: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end
```

<details><summary><strong>1. User enters password during registration</strong></summary>

The user inputs their desired username and plaintext password into the sign-up form.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>2. Client transmits registration credentials</strong></summary>

The frontend application POSTs the raw credentials over a secure TLS connection to the backend `/register` endpoint.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>3. Server validates password policy rules</strong></summary>

The backend engine verifies the password meets length requirements (e.g., NIST SP 800-63B standards) and passes basic local blocklists.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>4. Server computes SHA-1 prefix</strong></summary>

Before finalizing the password, the server computes its full SHA-1 hash and extracts just the first 5 hexadecimal characters.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>5. Server queries HIBP k-anonymity API</strong></summary>

The server makes a GET request to the external Have I Been Pwned API, submitting only the 5-character prefix to maintain k-anonymity privacy.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>6. HIBP returns hash suffix list</strong></summary>

HIBP responds with a plain text list of all breached password suffixes that share the submitted 5-character prefix, along with their occurrence counts.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>7. Server checks for suffix matches</strong></summary>

The server locally checks if the remaining characters of its own computed SHA-1 hash appear anywhere in the downloaded suffix list. If not, the password is deemed safe.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>8. Server generates random cryptographic salt</strong></summary>

The server calls the OS-level CSPRNG to generate a fresh, unique 128-bit salt sequence.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>9. Server computes Argon2id hash</strong></summary>

The server passes the plaintext password, the salt, and the tuned memory/time costs (m=19456, t=2) into the Argon2id KDF.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>10. Server stores PHC string in database</strong></summary>

The resulting output, fully formatted as a standard PHC string (including algo, params, salt, and digest), is written to the persistent credential store.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>11. Server returns HTTP 201 Created</strong></summary>

The backend concludes the transaction and sends an HTTP 201 status code back to the client application.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>12. Client displays registration success message</strong></summary>

The frontend UI transitions to a success state, informing the user that their account is ready.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>13. User submits credentials for login</strong></summary>

Some time later, the user enters their username and plaintext password into the login screen.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>14. Client transmits login credentials securely</strong></summary>

The application POSTs the payload to the `/auth/login` endpoint over TLS.
