</details>
<details><summary><strong>15. Server queries database for username</strong></summary>

The authentication controller queries the credential store using the provided username.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>16. Database returns stored PHC string</strong></summary>

The database returns the full PHC record string that was saved during registration.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>17. Server parses PHC components</strong></summary>

The server parses out the `$argon2id` prefix, extracts the exact memory and time cost parameters, and splits out the original salt and baseline secret digest.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>18. Server computes candidate Argon2id hash</strong></summary>

Using the extracted salt and parameters, the server computationally feeds the newly submitted login password through the Argon2id algorithm to derive a candidate digest.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>19. Server performs constant-time hash comparison</strong></summary>

The server compares the newly derived candidate digest against the stored database digest using a constant-time memory comparison function (e.g., `CRYPTO_memcmp`) to defeat timing side-channel attacks.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>20. Server returns HTTP 200 OK (Success Branch)</strong></summary>

If the cryptographic hashes match perfectly, the server mints a new session token (or JWT) and returns it in a 200 OK wrapper.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>21. Client displays login success message (Success Branch)</strong></summary>

The client consumes the session token and redirects the user into the primary application interface.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>22. Server returns HTTP 401 Unauthorized (Failure Branch)</strong></summary>

If the cryptographic hashes do not match (or the user doesn't exist), the server returns a generic HTTP 401 error.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>23. Client displays generic error message (Failure Branch)</strong></summary>

The client renders an ambiguous "Invalid username or password" message to prevent account enumeration.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>24. Server detects legacy hash algorithm (Optional)</strong></summary>

Running in a background or post-auth hook (success branch only), the server notices the loaded user record was using an older format (e.g., bcrypt `$2b$`).

</details>
<details><summary><strong>25. Server computes upgraded Argon2id hash (Optional)</strong></summary>

Taking advantage of having the plaintext password still in hot memory from the successful login, the server generates a new salt and computes a fresh Argon2id hash.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>26. Server updates stored database hash (Optional)</strong></summary>

The server overwrites the old legacy database record with the newly generated Argon2id PHC string, completing a transparent, zero-downtime security upgrade for that user.

</details>

#### 5.3 Third Generation: Credential Breach Intelligence

The third generation represents a paradigm shift in password security thinking — from **"is this password strong enough?"** to **"has this password already been compromised?"**

The insight driving this shift is empirical: credential stuffing attacks (§16.1) succeed because users reuse passwords across services. An attacker who obtains the plaintext credentials from Breach A doesn't need to crack anything — they simply try the same username/password pairs against Service B, Service C, and onward. In this threat model, password complexity is irrelevant; the only question that matters is whether the password appears in any known breach corpus.

##### 5.3.1 NIST SP 800-63B Password Guidelines

NIST SP 800-63B Revision 4 (second public draft August 2024, final anticipated mid-2025) codifies the paradigm shift into normative guidance. The key requirements represent a fundamental departure from legacy password policies:

| Guideline | Rev. 3 (2017) | Rev. 4 (2024 Draft) | Rationale |
|:----------|:-------------|:-------------------|:----------|
| **Minimum length** | 8 characters | 15 characters (single-factor); 8 characters (with MFA) | Longer passwords provide exponentially more entropy; eliminates most common passwords by length alone |
| **Maximum length** | ≥ 64 characters | ≥ 64 characters | Must support passphrase-length inputs; arbitrary limits frustrate users and reduce security |
| **Composition rules** | SHALL NOT impose | SHALL NOT impose | "Must contain uppercase, number, symbol" rules produce predictable patterns (`P@ssword1`); provide false confidence |
| **Periodic rotation** | SHALL NOT require | SHALL NOT require | Mandatory rotation drives users toward weak, predictable modifications (`Password1` → `Password2` → `Password3`) |
| **Blocklist checking** | SHALL check | **SHALL check** (strengthened language) | Passwords MUST be checked against a list of commonly used, expected, or compromised values; rejected if found |
| **Breach screening** | Recommended | **Mandatory** | Passwords found in breach corpora SHALL be rejected; services SHALL integrate breach detection (e.g., HIBP k-anonymity API) |
| **Paste support** | SHALL allow | SHALL allow | Users must be able to paste into password fields; denying paste blocks password manager usage |
| **Truncation** | SHALL NOT truncate | SHALL NOT truncate | The entire password must be used in the hash; truncation (as in bcrypt's 72-byte limit) must be mitigated |
| **Character types** | All printable ASCII + space | All Unicode + space | SHALL accept all printable characters including full Unicode; SHALL NOT restrict character types |
| **Password hints** | SHALL NOT store | SHALL NOT store | Hints are recoverable social engineering vectors |
| **Security questions** | SHALL NOT use | SHALL NOT use | Knowledge-based authenticators have been deprecated entirely |

**Blocklist composition** — NIST specifies that the blocklist SHALL include, at minimum:

1. Passwords obtained from previous breach corpora (e.g., HIBP's Pwned Passwords dataset — over 900 million entries)
2. Dictionary words
3. Repetitive or sequential characters (e.g., `aaaaaa`, `123456`)
4. Context-specific words (e.g., the service name, the username, derivatives thereof)

**Implementation options for breach screening:**

| Approach | Privacy | Performance | Freshness | Operational Complexity |
|:---------|:--------|:------------|:----------|:----------------------|
| **HIBP k-anonymity API** (online) | ✅ 5-char prefix only | ⚠️ Network round-trip (~50 ms) | ✅ Updated continuously | Low (no local data) |
| **HIBP offline download** (7-Zip, ~22 GB) | ✅ Fully local | ✅ ~1 ms per check | ⚠️ Updated monthly | Medium (storage + updates) |
| **NCSC Top 100K list** | ✅ Fully local | ✅ Instant | ⚠️ Static list | Low |
| **Self-compiled blocklist** | ✅ Fully local | ✅ Instant | ⚠️ Requires curation | High (maintenance) |

For production systems, the recommended approach is a **layered blocklist**: the HIBP online API for breach detection (privacy-preserving, always current) combined with a local static list of common patterns and context-specific values (instant, no network dependency).

##### 5.3.1a Entropy Analysis: Complexity vs. Length

The fundamental tension in password policy is between **composition rules** (requiring character class diversity) and **length** (allowing longer, simpler passwords). NIST's position is clear: length matters more than complexity.

**Naive entropy calculation:**

$$H = L \times \log_2(R)$$

Where $L$ = password length and $R$ = character set size. For a purely random password:
- 8 characters from 94 printable ASCII: $H = 8 \times \log_2(94) \approx 52.6$ bits
- 15 characters from 26 lowercase letters: $H = 15 \times \log_2(26) \approx 70.4$ bits
- 20 characters (alphanumeric): $H = 20 \times \log_2(62) \approx 119.2$ bits

**But real users don't choose randomly.** The entropy formula assumes uniform distribution — it dramatically overestimates the strength of human-chosen passwords. Users apply predictable patterns: capitalising the first letter, appending a digit or year, substituting visually similar characters (`p@$$w0rd`), using a single base word with predictable variations.

**zxcvbn analysis** (Dropbox, 2012) demonstrates the gap between naive and realistic entropy:

| Password | Naive Entropy | zxcvbn Entropy | Real Crack Time |
|:---------|:-------------|:---------------|:----------------|
| `P@ssword1` | ~52 bits | **~3 bits** | Instant |
| `Tr0ub4dour&3` | ~72 bits | **~28 bits** | Minutes |
| `correcthorsebatterystaple` | ~131 bits | **~44 bits** | Hours |
| `9d-kiSs8;Sn3k#fK7` | ~94 bits | **~78 bits** | Centuries |

The lesson: **a longer passphrase of common words is stronger than a shorter "complex" password** because human-predictable patterns negate the entropy benefit of character diversity. Composition rules give a false sense of security while making passwords harder to remember.

##### 5.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

The HIBP Pwned Passwords API, created by Troy Hunt, provides a practical implementation of breach-checking that preserves user privacy through a **k-anonymity model**. The API contains over 900 million compromised passwords sourced from real-world data breaches. The critical design challenge is: how does a service check whether a password appears in the breach corpus without sending the password (or its full hash) to a third party?

**Protocol flow:**

```mermaid
---
config:
  sequence:
    messageAlign: left
    noteAlign: left
    actorMargin: 250
  themeVariables:
    noteBkgColor: "transparent"
    noteBorderColor: "transparent"
---
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant User as User (Browser)
    participant App as Application Server
    participant HIBP as HIBP API<br/>(api.pwnedpasswords.com)

    rect rgba(52, 152, 219, 0.14)
    Note over User,App: Password Submission Phase
    User->>App: Submit new password: "correcthorsebatterystaple"
    Note right of HIBP: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(46, 204, 113, 0.14)
    Note over App,App: Local SHA-1 Computation Phase
    App->>App: Compute SHA-1 hash of password<br/>SHA-1("correcthorsebatterystaple")<br/>→ "B1B3773A05C0ED0176787A4F1574FF0075F7521E"
    App->>App: Extract first 5 hex characters (prefix)<br/>→ "B1B37"
    App->>App: Retain remaining 35 hex characters (suffix)<br/>→ "73A05C0ED0176787A4F1574FF0075F7521E"
    Note right of HIBP: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(241, 196, 15, 0.14)
    Note over App,HIBP: k-Anonymity Query Phase
    App->>HIBP: GET /range/B1B37
    HIBP->>HIBP: Look up all hash suffixes<br/>matching prefix "B1B37"
    HIBP-->>App: Return ~800 hash suffixes with occurrence counts:<br/>0018A45C4D1DEF81644B54AB7F969B88D65:3<br/>00D4F6E8FA6EECAD2A3AA415EEC418D38EC:2<br/>011053FD0102E94D6AE2F8B83D76FAF94F6:1<br/>...<br/>73A05C0ED0176787A4F1574FF0075F7521E:52<br/>...
    Note right of HIBP: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(231, 76, 60, 0.14)
    Note over App,App: Local Comparison Phase
    App->>App: Search returned suffixes for local suffix<br/>"73A05C0ED0176787A4F1574FF0075F7521E"<br/>→ FOUND (seen 52 times in breaches)
    App->>App: Decision: REJECT password —<br/>compromised credential detected
    Note right of HIBP: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(148, 163, 184, 0.14)
    Note over User,App: User Feedback Phase
    App-->>User: Password rejected: this password has<br/>appeared in known data breaches.<br/>Please choose a different password.
    Note right of HIBP: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end
```

<details><summary><strong>1. User submits new password to application</strong></summary>

The user enters a new password (during registration or password change) in the browser or client application. The plaintext password is transmitted to the application server over TLS. At this point, the password exists in memory on the application server — it has not yet been hashed for storage or breach-checked.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>2. Application computes SHA-1 hash locally</strong></summary>

The application server computes the SHA-1 hash of the plaintext password entirely locally. SHA-1 is used as the lookup key — **not** as the storage hash. The resulting 160-bit (40 hex character) hash is used only for the k-anonymity query and is discarded after the check. The password will be hashed separately for storage using Argon2id/bcrypt/scrypt.

Note: SHA-1's known collision vulnerabilities (SHAttered, 2017) are irrelevant here — the API uses SHA-1 as a lookup index, not for cryptographic security. Finding a collision would let an attacker find a different input that maps to the same bucket, but wouldn't reveal the original password or bypass the breach check.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>3. Application extracts the 5-character hex prefix</strong></summary>

The first 5 hexadecimal characters of the SHA-1 hash are extracted as the **prefix**. With 16⁵ = 1,048,576 possible prefixes, this creates roughly one million equally-sized buckets. The prefix is the only information sent to the HIBP API — it narrows the search space to approximately 800–1,000 hash suffixes per bucket without revealing which specific password is being checked.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>4. Application retains the 35-character suffix for local comparison</strong></summary>

The remaining 35 hex characters of the full SHA-1 hash are retained locally for comparison against the API response. This suffix never leaves the application server.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>5. Application queries HIBP API with prefix only</strong></summary>

The application sends an HTTP GET request to `https://api.pwnedpasswords.com/range/{prefix}`. Only the 5-character prefix is transmitted. The HIBP server has no way to determine which of the ~800 passwords in that bucket the client is actually checking — this is the k-anonymity property.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>6. HIBP API looks up matching hash suffixes</strong></summary>

The HIBP server queries its database of over 900 million compromised password hashes, returning all entries whose SHA-1 hash begins with the requested 5-character prefix. The response is a plaintext list of hash suffixes with corresponding breach occurrence counts.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>7. HIBP returns suffix list with occurrence counts</strong></summary>

The API returns approximately 800 hash suffixes (35 hex characters each) with the number of times each password appeared across known data breaches. The response format is one entry per line: `{suffix}:{count}`. The response is typically 20–30 KB and can be cached aggressively (responses include appropriate `Cache-Control` headers).

</details>

<details><summary><strong>8. Application searches returned suffixes for local match</strong></summary>

The application performs a local comparison: it searches the returned suffix list for the suffix computed in step 4. If found, the password has appeared in at least one known data breach. The occurrence count indicates how many times — a count of 52 means the password appeared in 52 distinct breach datasets.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>9. Application makes accept/reject decision</strong></summary>

Based on the match result, the application decides whether to accept or reject the password. NIST SP 800-63B Rev. 4 mandates rejection of passwords found in breach corpora. The threshold is binary — any presence in the breach corpus warrants rejection, regardless of occurrence count.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>10. Application returns feedback to user</strong></summary>

The user receives an appropriate error message explaining that the password was found in known data breaches and must choose a different password. NIST requires that the rejection reason be communicated to the user — the verifier SHALL provide a message explaining why the password was rejected.

</details>

<br/>

**Privacy properties of the k-anonymity model:**

| Property | Guarantee |
|:---------|:---------|
| **Password not transmitted** | The plaintext password never leaves the application server |
| **Full hash not transmitted** | Only 5 of 40 hex characters of the SHA-1 hash are sent to HIBP |
| **Server cannot identify password** | Each prefix maps to ~800 possible passwords — the server cannot distinguish which one the client is checking |
| **Network eavesdropper cannot identify password** | Even intercepting the API request reveals only the 5-character prefix (TLS protects the response body) |
| **No registration required** | The API is free, requires no API key, and imposes no authentication — minimising the metadata footprint |

##### 5.3.3 Credential Stuffing Mitigation

Credential stuffing is the automated injection of known username/password pairs (obtained from previous breaches) into login forms. Unlike brute-force attacks that guess passwords, credential stuffing uses **real credentials** — success rates of 0.1–2% are typical, and given breach corpora containing billions of pairs, this translates to millions of compromised accounts.

Mitigation operates at multiple layers:

| Layer | Technique | Mechanism | Tradeoff |
|:------|:---------|:----------|:---------|
| **Credential validation** | Breach-list checking at registration/change (§5.3.2) | Prevents users from setting known-compromised passwords | Does not protect existing accounts with pre-breach passwords |
| **Rate limiting** | IP-based and account-based throttling | Limits the number of authentication attempts per time window | Attackers distribute across botnets; too aggressive limits lock out legitimate users |
| **Progressive delays** | Exponentially increasing delays after failed attempts (e.g., 1s → 2s → 4s → 8s) | Slows brute-force without hard lockout | Attackers may accept the delay if the reward is high enough |
| **Account lockout** | Temporary lockout after N failed attempts (e.g., 30 min after 5 failures) | Stops brute-force against targeted accounts | Denial-of-service risk — attackers can deliberately trigger lockouts against legitimate users |
| **CAPTCHA** | Challenge-response tests (reCAPTCHA, hCaptcha, Turnstile) | Distinguishes human users from automated bots | Friction for legitimate users; advanced bots use CAPTCHA-solving services; accessibility concerns |
| **Device fingerprinting** | Browser/device characteristics (canvas fingerprint, WebGL renderer, timezone, plugins) | Detects anomalous device profiles indicating automated tools | Privacy concerns; fingerprints are spoofable; GDPR implications |
| **Bot detection** | Behavioural analysis (mouse movement patterns, keystroke dynamics, request timing) | Identifies non-human interaction patterns at the network/application layer | Sophisticated bots mimic human behaviour; false positives possible |
| **Credential intelligence** | Real-time breach monitoring and proactive forced password resets | Resets passwords for accounts whose credentials appear in new breaches | Requires monitoring breach intelligence feeds; user communication overhead |

##### 5.3.4 FHE-Based Private Breach Checking

Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) enables computation on encrypted data without decryption — a property that theoretically enables **private set intersection (PSI)** for breach checking: the client encrypts the password hash, the server computes whether it appears in the breach corpus *while the data remains encrypted*, and returns only the encrypted match/no-match result.

**Current research state:**

Google's Password Checkup (integrated into Chrome) uses a related cryptographic protocol — not full FHE, but an oblivious PRF-based PSI construction — to check credentials against a server-side breach database without revealing either the user's password or the unmatched entries in the database to either party. This protocol provides stronger privacy guarantees than HIBP's k-anonymity model but requires more complex cryptographic operations.

True FHE-based PSI for password breach checking remains **premature for production deployment** due to:

1. **Computational overhead** — FHE operations are orders of magnitude slower than plaintext operations. Current schemes require seconds to minutes per query, compared to milliseconds for HIBP's k-anonymity approach
2. **Communication cost** — FHE ciphertexts are significantly larger than plaintext (typical expansion factor: 10–1,000×), increasing bandwidth requirements
3. **Implementation complexity** — FHE libraries (Microsoft SEAL, TFHE, OpenFHE) are research-grade and require deep cryptographic expertise to use securely
4. **Diminishing marginal privacy gain** — HIBP's k-anonymity already provides strong practical privacy (the server learns only a 5-char prefix from a 40-char hash); the additional privacy guarantee from FHE may not justify the orders-of-magnitude performance cost

**Assessment:** FHE-based breach checking is an active research area with long-term potential, but the HIBP k-anonymity model represents the practical state of the art. For production systems, HIBP's approach provides sufficient privacy guarantees with negligible performance overhead.

##### 5.3.5 Password Managers

Password managers are the most practical tool for enabling users to maintain unique, high-entropy passwords across all accounts. They generate, store, and autofill credentials, eliminating the human memory constraint that drives password reuse.

**Security model:**

| Component | Risk | Mitigation |
|:----------|:-----|:-----------|
| **Master password** | Single point of failure — compromise exposes all vault entries | Long passphrase (20+ characters); breach screening; hardware key unlock (Bitwarden, KeePassXC) |
| **Vault encryption** | Database breach of cloud-synced vaults | AES-256-GCM encryption; zero-knowledge architecture (server never sees plaintext); end-to-end encryption |
| **Autofill** | Credential extraction via XSS or compromised browser extension | Origin matching; autofill restricted to registered domains; no cross-domain autofill |
| **Browser integration** | Browser compromise could access vault | Separate process isolation; biometric/PIN re-prompt for autofill; hardware key support |
| **Cloud sync** | Sync server compromise | Zero-knowledge sync; end-to-end encryption; server stores only encrypted blobs |
| **Device theft** | Physical access to unlocked device | Automatic lock timeout; biometric re-authentication; remote wipe capability |

**Password manager risks that cannot be mitigated:**

1. **Master password phishing** — the master password is itself a password. If a user is phished for their master password (e.g., a fake Bitwarden login page), the attacker gains access to the entire vault. Hardware key integration (WebAuthn as unlock factor) eliminates this risk
2. **Malicious browser extensions** — a compromised extension with appropriate permissions could read autofilled credentials from form fields after they are populated
3. **Clipboard theft** — copy-paste from the vault exposes the credential in the system clipboard. Some managers auto-clear after 30 seconds, but the window exists
4. **Supply chain attacks** — the password manager itself could be compromised (e.g., LastPass breach, August 2022 — attacker accessed cloud storage with proprietary binary data)

**NIST guidance:** NIST SP 800-63B Rev 4 explicitly requires verifiers to allow paste into password fields — blocking paste is a countermeasure against password managers and SHALL NOT be implemented.

#### 5.4 Password Hashing Competition (PHC)

The Password Hashing Competition (2013–2015) was an open competition to select a new password hashing standard, modelled on NIST's AES competition (1997–2000) and SHA-3 competition (2007–2012). The competition was organised in response to the growing inadequacy of bcrypt and PBKDF2 against GPU/ASIC-equipped attackers, and the lack of a formally vetted, memory-hard password hashing standard.

**Timeline:**

- **2012** — Call for submissions announced by an informal panel of cryptographers and security researchers (including Jean-Philippe Aumasson, Colin Percival, and others)
- **2013** — Submission deadline; **24 candidates** submitted
- **2014** — First-round selection; 9 finalists announced after public review and cryptanalysis
- **July 2015** — Winner announced: **Argon2** (Alex Biryukov, Daniel Dinu, Dmitry Khovratovich, University of Luxembourg)

**Special recognitions** — Four finalists received honourable mentions for innovative contributions:

| Algorithm | Authors | Innovation | Status |
|:----------|:--------|:-----------|:-------|
| **Catena** | Forler, Lucks, Wenzel | Graph-based framework with provable side-channel resistance; configurable memory access patterns (Catena-BRG for Lambda-memory-hard, Catena-DBG for data-dependent graphs) | Academic use; influenced Argon2's hybrid approach |
| **Lyra2** | Almeida, Andrade, et al. | Sponge-based construction (using BlaMka or Blake2b); elegant parameterisation of memory and time costs; alternative side-channel resistance model | Used in cryptocurrency (Lyra2REv2 in Vertcoin); active academic development |
| **Makwa** | Pornin | Delegation feature — hash computation can be offloaded to an untrusted server without revealing the password (based on the Rabin/RSA trapdoor one-way function using modular squaring); unique among all PHC candidates | Niche use; the delegation property is valuable for constrained-device scenarios |
| **yescrypt** | Peslyak | Backward-compatible upgrade path from scrypt; rich feature set including optional ROM (read-only memory) for server-side secrets; heavily optimised for x86 platforms | Used in ALT Linux and other Linux distributions as the default password hash |

**Why PHC matters:** Before Argon2, the password hashing landscape was fragmented — bcrypt was battle-tested but lacked memory hardness, scrypt was memory-hard but had no formal security analysis or competition vetting, and PBKDF2 was NIST-approved but pedestrian. The PHC provided the same rigorous public-review process that produced AES and SHA-3, giving the community confidence in Argon2's security properties.

#### 5.5 Practical Implementation Guidance

##### 5.5.1 Peppering

A **pepper** is a server-side secret key applied to the password before hashing — conceptually similar to a salt but stored separately from the database (e.g., in an HSM, environment variable, or secrets vault) and **not stored alongside the hash**.

```
stored_hash = Argon2id(password || pepper, salt, params)
```

**Defence-in-depth value:** If an attacker compromises the password database but not the application server or secrets store, the pepper renders the hashes uncrackable — the attacker cannot reproduce the hash without the pepper value. This protects against database-only breaches (e.g., SQL injection), which are the most common breach vector.

**Operational considerations:**

| Concern | Guidance |
|:--------|:---------|
| **Pepper rotation** | Difficult — requires re-hashing all passwords with the new pepper, which is only possible during the user's next successful login. Maintain a pepper version identifier alongside each hash to support gradual rotation |
| **Pepper length** | ≥ 256 bits (32 bytes) from a CSPRNG |
| **Pepper storage** | HSM, KMS (e.g., AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault), or application-level secrets management. Never in the same database as the hashes |
| **HMAC variant** | Instead of concatenating the pepper, consider `HMAC-SHA256(pepper, password)` as input to Argon2id — this is cryptographically cleaner and avoids length-extension issues |

##### 5.5.2 Hash Upgrade Pattern

When migrating from a weaker hash algorithm (e.g., MD5, SHA-1, bcrypt with low cost) to a stronger one (e.g., Argon2id), the hash upgrade pattern re-hashes credentials transparently on next successful login:

1. User authenticates with their password
2. System verifies the password against the **old** hash (using the legacy algorithm)
3. If verification succeeds, the system computes a **new** hash using Argon2id with current parameters
4. The new hash replaces the old hash in the database
5. A flag or algorithm identifier in the stored hash indicates which algorithm was used (the encoded format's prefix — `$2b$` for bcrypt, `$argon2id$` for Argon2id — serves this purpose)

**Migration gap:** Users who do not log in during the migration window retain their old hashes. Options for handling this residual population:

- **Wrap hashing** — re-hash the existing hash: `Argon2id(bcrypt_hash)`. On authentication, first compute the legacy hash from the user's input, then verify against the Argon2id-wrapped hash. This upgrades the storage strength immediately for all users without requiring login
- **Forced password reset** — after a deadline (e.g., 6 months), force password resets for all accounts still using legacy hashes. This is disruptive but ensures all credentials are processed through the new algorithm

##### 5.5.3 Password Strength Estimation

**zxcvbn** (Dropbox, 2012) is a pattern-matching password strength estimator that provides realistic strength assessment by modelling how real attackers work — it identifies dictionary words, keyboard patterns (e.g., `qwerty`, `zxcvbn`), dates, repeated sequences, l33t-speak substitutions, and spatial adjacency patterns, then calculates the minimum number of guesses required to crack the password.

**zxcvbn vs. naive entropy:**

| Approach | Method | `P@ssword1` | `correct horse battery staple` |
|:---------|:-------|:------------|:------------------------------|
| **Naive entropy** | `L × log₂(R)` where R = charset size | ~59 bits (high — 4 charsets × 10 chars) | ~131 bits (very high — 30 chars × lowercase) |
| **zxcvbn** | Pattern matching + minimum-guess estimation | **~3 bits** (dictionary word + common substitutions + predictable digit) | **~44 bits** (4 uncommon dictionary words, no predictable patterns) |

Naive entropy dramatically overestimates `P@ssword1` because it treats each character as independently random from a 94-character set, ignoring that the password is a trivially predictable transformation of a dictionary word. zxcvbn recognises the underlying structure and estimates the actual guessing difficulty.

**Integration guidance:**

- Use zxcvbn as a **client-side feedback tool**, not a server-side gate — it provides real-time feedback as the user types, guiding them toward stronger choices
- Server-side enforcement should rely on **length requirements** (≥ 15 characters per NIST Rev. 4) and **blocklist checking** (§5.3.2), not on strength estimation scores
- zxcvbn adds ~800 KB to the client-side bundle (including dictionaries) — lazy-load it on password creation/change forms only

##### 5.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

Rate limiting strategies exist on a spectrum from strict lockout to transparent friction:

| Strategy | Mechanism | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|:---------|:----------|:----------|:-----------|
| **Hard lockout** | Disable account after N failures (e.g., 5) for a fixed duration (e.g., 30 min) | Simple to implement; effectively stops targeted brute-force | Denial-of-service — attackers deliberately lock out legitimate users; customer support burden |
| **Progressive delays** | Increasing wait time between attempts (1s → 2s → 4s → 8s → ...); exponential backoff | No false lockouts; self-correcting (delay resets after success); no DoS risk | Attackers with patience can still make many attempts over hours; requires server-side state tracking |
| **CAPTCHA challenge** | Present CAPTCHA after N failures or on suspicious patterns | Distinguishes humans from bots; does not block legitimate users | Accessibility barriers; CAPTCHA-solving services available for ~$1–2 per 1,000; user friction |
| **IP-based rate limiting** | Limit attempts per source IP address | Effective against single-source attacks | Easily bypassed with residential proxy botnets; shared NAT/VPN IPs cause false positives |
| **Hybrid** | Progressive delays + CAPTCHA after threshold + temporary lockout as last resort | Layered defence; each mechanism covers the others' weaknesses | Implementation complexity; requires careful threshold tuning |

**Recommendation:** Implement a **hybrid approach** — progressive delays as the baseline, CAPTCHA after a configurable threshold (e.g., 5 failures), and temporary lockout only as a last resort after extreme failure counts (e.g., 20 failures in 1 hour). Complement with credential-stuffing detection at the WAF/CDN layer.

**Design reference:**

| Policy | Configuration | Risk | Mitigation |
|:-------|:-------------|:-----|:-----------|
| **IP-based rate limit** | 10 failed attempts per IP per 5 minutes | Attacker distributes across botnet IPs | Combine with device fingerprinting |
| **Account-based lockout** | 5 failed attempts → 30-minute lockout | DoS — attacker deliberately locks out legitimate users | Use progressive delays instead of hard lockout |
| **Progressive delays** | 1s → 2s → 4s → 8s → max 60s | Attacker accepts delays for high-value targets | Cap at reasonable maximum; log repeated attempts |
| **CAPTCHA trigger** | After 3 failed attempts | User frustration; CAPTCHA-solving services | Use invisible CAPTCHA (Turnstile) for low friction |

**Critical rule:** always return the same error message for "username not found" and "wrong password" to prevent username enumeration: *"The username or password you entered is incorrect."*

#### 5.6 Conformance Testing and Validation

Production password authentication systems should validate their implementation using established tools and test vectors to ensure correctness and conformance with algorithm specifications.

**Validation tools:**

| Tool | Function | Coverage |
|:-----|:---------|:---------|
| **Argon2 reference implementation tests** | Official test vectors from RFC 9106 | Argon2i, Argon2d, Argon2id — various parameter combinations |
| **libsodium test suite** | Comprehensive hashing test vectors | Argon2id, scrypt, bcrypt (via crypto_pwhash) |
| **Passlib test suite** | Python library with extensive test vectors | bcrypt, PBKDF2, scrypt, Argon2, crypt formats |
| **OWASP Password Hashing Tester** | Online tool for parameter benchmarking | bcrypt, PBKDF2, Argon2id timing analysis |
| **Hashcat benchmark mode** | GPU-accelerated cracking performance | Measures real-world cracking speed for any hash type |

**Pre-deployment validation checklist:**

1. **Test vector verification** — verify hash output against official test vectors from RFC 7914 (scrypt) and RFC 9106 (Argon2) to ensure the implementation is correct
2. **Timing consistency** — verify that hash computation time is consistent (±20%) across multiple runs to ensure the cost parameter is correctly applied
3. **Salt uniqueness** — verify that 10,000 consecutive password hashes all produce unique salts and unique hash values
4. **Password truncation test** — verify that passwords of 72 bytes and 73 bytes produce different hashes (if using Argon2id/scrypt) or document the truncation behaviour (if using bcrypt)
5. **Constant-time comparison** — verify that hash comparison uses constant-time operations (not byte-by-byte `==` with short-circuit evaluation)
6. **PHC string round-trip** — verify that encoding and parsing of PHC strings preserves all parameters correctly across serialisation boundaries
7. **Breach screening integration** — verify that HIBP k-anonymity API calls correctly identify known-compromised passwords and allow clean passwords
8. **Rate limiting verification** — verify that login rate limiting triggers correctly for both IP-based and account-based thresholds
9. **Lockout behaviour** — verify that account lockout/progressive delays function correctly and that the error message does not differentiate between "user not found" and "wrong password"
10. **Hash upgrade path** — verify that the transparent hash upgrade pattern correctly migrates legacy hashes to Argon2id after successful authentication

---

### 6. Passwordless Methods Taxonomy

The previous chapter traced the evolution of password storage — from plaintext catastrophes through modern breach-intelligence-driven verification. This chapter shifts focus from *protecting* passwords to *eliminating* them. Passwordless authentication replaces the memorised secret with a different factor — something delivered to the user (a link, a code, a push notification), something the user possesses (a certificate, a hardware token), or something provisioned by an administrator (a bootstrap credential). Each method has distinct security properties, deployment constraints, and assurance-level ceilings.

This chapter surveys the major passwordless authentication methods as a taxonomy, classifying them by mechanism, factor type, phishing resistance, and NIST AAL ceiling. Each method is described as a generic architectural pattern; specific vendor products are mentioned only as illustrative examples. Methods that rely on cryptographic key pairs — FIDO2/WebAuthn, passkeys, TOTP/HOTP — are covered in dedicated chapters (§7–§10) and are not repeated here.

#### 6.0 Passwordless Maturity Model

Organisations transitioning to passwordless authentication progress through three stages. The model is not prescriptive — different user populations within the same organisation may be at different stages simultaneously.

**Stage 1 — Password as fallback:** The primary authentication method is passwordless (e.g., push notification, passkey), but a password remains registered and available as a fallback. Users can choose to enter their password if their passwordless authenticator is unavailable. This stage maximises user choice and minimises lockout risk but retains the full attack surface of password-based authentication. An attacker who obtains a user's password can still authenticate regardless of the user's passwordless enrollment.

**Stage 2 — Password as exception:** Password is disabled for the majority of users. A small subset of users (e.g., users with incompatible devices, users who have not completed enrollment, users with accessibility requirements) retains password access through an explicit exception process. The exception requires administrative approval and is subject to additional monitoring. Most users authenticate exclusively through passwordless methods. The password attack surface is dramatically reduced but not eliminated.

**Stage 3 — Password eliminated:** Password is removed as an authentication method for all users. No user can authenticate with a password, regardless of circumstance. Account recovery is handled through alternative mechanisms (recovery codes, administrator-issued temporary access passes, in-person verification, backup FIDO2 keys). This stage maximises security but requires robust recovery processes and complete device coverage.

| Stage | Password Status | User Coverage | Security Posture | Recovery Mechanism |
|:------|:---------------|:-------------|:-----------------|:-------------------|
| **Stage 1** | Active fallback | All users (mixed) | Improved — most auth is passwordless, but password attack surface remains | Password self-service |
| **Stage 2** | Exception-based | > 90% passwordless | Strong — password attack surface narrowed to exception users | TAP / admin recovery |
| **Stage 3** | Eliminated | 100% passwordless | Maximum — no password attack surface | Recovery codes, backup keys, admin TAP |

**Terminology:** Several terms related to passwordless authentication are frequently conflated but have precise, distinct meanings:

| Term | Definition | Shared Secret? | Example |
|:-----|:-----------|:---------------|:--------|
| **Passwordless** | No shared secret of any kind is used during authentication | ❌ | FIDO2 passkey, magic link, push notification |
| **Password-free** | No password is entered, but a local PIN or pattern may be used to unlock a device-bound credential | ❌ (no shared secret) | Windows Hello (device-bound key unlocked by PIN), Android biometric unlock |
| **Multi-factor (MFA)** | Two or more authentication factors from different categories are required; may still include a password as one factor | ✅ (if password is a factor) | Password + SMS OTP, Password + FIDO2 key |
| **Two-factor (2FA)** | A subset of MFA requiring exactly two factors; commonly implemented as password + OTP | ✅ (if password is a factor) | Password + TOTP app code |
| **Single sign-on (SSO)** | Authentication at one identity provider grants access to multiple relying parties; the authentication method itself may or may not be passwordless | Depends | SAML SSO with X.509 certificate, OIDC SSO with password |

The distinction between "passwordless" and "password-free" is subtle but security-relevant. A Windows Hello PIN is not a shared secret — it is never transmitted to the server and exists only on the local device's TPM. The server never sees the PIN and cannot verify it. The authentication credential is the device-bound FIDO2 key pair; the PIN merely unlocks the local private key. This is passwordless authentication despite the user entering a PIN. Conversely, a traditional PIN used for phone banking (transmitted to the server and verified server-side) **is** a shared secret — it functions identically to a short password. A passwordless system that asks users to "enter a PIN" must ensure the PIN is verified locally (device-bound) rather than server-side.

> **Passwordless ⊥ MFA.** Passwordless and multi-factor authentication are orthogonal dimensions, not competing approaches. A FIDO2 security key is both passwordless (no shared secret) and multi-factor (something you have + something you are, if biometric verification is required). A magic link is passwordless but single-factor. Password + push notification is MFA but not passwordless. The strongest authentication combines both dimensions: passwordless, phishing-resistant, multi-factor.

The transition to passwordless is driven by four converging forces. **Phishing resistance** — passwords are inherently phishable, and phishing was the #1 initial access vector in IBM X-Force Threat Intelligence Index reports from 2022–2025; methods that bind authentication to a cryptographic key pair (FIDO2/WebAuthn) or a TLS-origin-bound mechanism (X.509 client certificates) are immune to credential phishing by design. **User experience** — passwords impose cognitive load and friction; the average enterprise user manages 25+ work accounts and spends 10–20 hours per year on password-related tasks, while biometrics authenticate in < 1 second and passkeys eliminate password entry entirely. **Compliance mandates** — NIST SP 800-63-4 requires phishing-resistant authentication for US federal systems, OMB Memorandum M-22-09 mandates phishing-resistant MFA across all federal accounts by the end of fiscal year 2025, and the EU's eIDAS 2.0 regulation creates regulatory pressure for strong, phishing-resistant authentication in financial services and cross-border digital identity. **MFA fatigue** — as organisations deployed MFA widely, attackers shifted from phishing passwords to phishing MFA approval; the Uber (2022) and Cisco (2022) breaches demonstrated that simple approve/deny push notifications are vulnerable to fatigue-based attacks.

**FIDO2/WebAuthn as password replacement:** FIDO2 (Fast Identity Online 2) is an open authentication standard developed by the FIDO Alliance and ratified as W3C Web Authentication (WebAuthn). It replaces shared-secret authentication (passwords) with public-key cryptography: the user's device generates a unique key pair per relying party (website), stores the private key in a hardware-protected enclave, and proves possession of the private key during authentication using a cryptographic signature. Detailed ceremony flows and attestation formats are covered in §10 (WebAuthn and CTAP2 Architecture); the following comparison highlights how WebAuthn differs from passwords at the protocol level:

| Property | Passwords | WebAuthn / Passkeys |
|:---------|:----------|:-------------------|
| **Secret type** | Shared secret (user and server both know it) | Asymmetric key pair (only user holds private key) |
| **Server storage** | Password hash (verifiable offline) | Public key only (cannot impersonate user) |
| **Phishing resistance** | ❌ Vulnerable — users enter passwords on fake sites | ✅ Immune — signature bound to relying party origin |
| **Breach impact** | Hash database compromise enables offline cracking | Public key database compromise has no authentication impact |
| **Replay resistance** | ❌ Stolen credentials work on any site with same password | ✅ Keys are scoped to a single relying party |
| **Server-side verification** | Hash comparison | Cryptographic signature verification |
| **User experience** | Type or paste password | Biometric scan (fingerprint/face) or device PIN |

**Registration ceremony** (abridged — see §10 for full detail): the user navigates to the registration page and clicks "Create passkey"; the server generates a challenge (random byte string) and sends it with relying party information (RP ID, user ID, supported algorithms); the browser prompts the user's authenticator (platform authenticator or security key); the authenticator generates a new key pair (ECDSA P-256 or Ed25519), stores the private key in secure hardware (TPM, Secure Enclave), and creates an attestation object; the authenticator signs the challenge and returns the public key + attestation to the server; the server stores the public key credential ID and public key and discards the challenge.

**Authentication ceremony** (abridged): the user navigates to the login page and clicks "Sign in with passkey"; the server generates a new challenge and sends the list of allowed credential IDs for this user; the browser prompts the authenticator — the user verifies via biometric or PIN; the authenticator looks up the private key for the matching credential ID, signs the challenge, and returns the signature + authenticator data; the server verifies the signature against the stored public key, checks the challenge, and authenticates the user.

**Passkey ecosystem — current status (2025–2026):** Passkeys are syncable WebAuthn credentials — they provide the same phishing-resistant security as hardware security keys but are synchronised across devices via cloud services, eliminating the need for users to carry a physical token.

| Platform | Passkey Support | Sync Mechanism | Authenticator Type | Status |
|:---------|:---------------|:---------------|:-------------------|:-------|
| **Apple** (iOS 17+, macOS 14+) | ✅ Native | iCloud Keychain (end-to-end encrypted) | Platform (Touch ID, Face ID) | GA — shipped September 2023 |
| **Google** (Android 14+, Chrome) | ✅ Native | Google Password Manager (end-to-end encrypted) | Platform (fingerprint, face unlock) | GA — shipped October 2023 |
| **Microsoft** (Windows 11 22H2+) | ✅ Native | Windows Hello cloud sync | Platform (Windows Hello, PIN, biometric) | GA — shipped September 2022 |
| **1Password** | ✅ Full support | 1Password vault sync (end-to-end encrypted) | Roaming authenticator | GA — cross-platform |
| **Bitwarden** | ✅ Full support | Bitwarden vault sync (end-to-end encrypted) | Roaming authenticator | GA — cross-platform |
| **Dashlane** | ✅ Full support | Dashlane vault sync (end-to-end encrypted) | Roaming authenticator | GA — cross-platform |
| **Linux** | ⚠️ Limited | No native platform sync | External security keys (YubiKey) | Requires security key or browser-based |

Apple reported over 1 billion passkeys created across its ecosystem by mid-2025; Google reported passkey sign-ins surpassing traditional password sign-ins on several Google services; the Passkeys.org adoption tracker shows 15% of top-100 websites support passkeys as of Q1 2026; the FIDO Alliance projects 50%+ of consumer-facing services will support passkeys by end of 2027.

**Password + FIDO2 security key as MFA:** When a FIDO2 security key (YubiKey, Feitian, etc.) is used as a second factor alongside a password, the combination provides full phishing resistance and satisfies AAL3 (with a FIPS 140-2/3 certified key). The security key is bound to the relying party origin — phishing sites cannot use it, and the key will not sign a challenge for a different origin. Each challenge is unique (replay protection), and the attacker must physically possess the key. This combination is the strongest password-based MFA configuration available and is the NIST-recommended second factor for high-assurance applications.

#### 6.1 Magic Links (Email-Based Passwordless)

A magic link is a single-use, time-limited URL sent to a user's registered email address. Clicking the link completes authentication without any password entry. The mechanism transforms email account access into the authentication factor — the user proves identity by demonstrating control of the email inbox.

**Protocol flow:**

1. User navigates to the application's login page and enters their email address
2. Application server generates a cryptographically random token (≥ 256 bits of entropy from a CSPRNG), associates it with the user's account, and records the creation timestamp
3. Server constructs a URL embedding the token: `https://app.example.com/auth/verify?token=<base64url-encoded-token>`
4. Server sends the URL to the user's registered email address via SMTP
5. User opens their email, clicks the magic link
6. Browser sends the token to the application server via HTTPS GET or POST
7. Server validates the token — checks existence, expiry, single-use status, and optionally IP/device binding
8. If valid, server creates an authenticated session for the user and invalidates the token

**Token properties:**

| Property | Requirement | Rationale |
|:---------|:-----------|:----------|
| **Entropy** | ≥ 256 bits, CSPRNG-generated | Must be computationally infeasible to guess; 2²⁵⁶ possible values eliminates brute-force |
| **Expiry** | 5–15 minutes from generation | Short window limits exposure if the email is intercepted or the link leaks |
| **Single-use** | Invalidated immediately after first successful validation | Prevents replay attacks; a captured link cannot be reused |
| **Account binding** | Token is bound to a specific user account | Prevents token substitution — using User A's token to authenticate as User B |
| **Optional: session/IP binding** | Token can be bound to the browser session or IP address that initiated the login request | Mitigates cross-device link theft; the link only works from the device that requested it |

**Token construction (server-side implementation detail):**

```
token  = CSPRNG(32 bytes)           // 256-bit random token
record = {
    token_hash: SHA-256(token),      // Store hash, not plaintext token
    user_id: "user-uuid-1234",
    created_at: "2026-03-26T10:30:00Z",
    expires_at: "2026-03-26T10:45:00Z",  // 15-minute window
    consumed: false,
    session_hint: "browser-fingerprint-or-session-id"  // Optional session binding
}
```

The server stores only the SHA-256 hash of the token (not the plaintext token) in its database. The plaintext token is embedded in the URL sent to the user's email. When the user clicks the link, the server hashes the received token and looks up the hash in its database. This prevents token exposure through database compromise — even if the database is breached, the attacker cannot reconstruct valid magic link URLs from the stored hashes.

**Security model — email as the authentication factor:**

Magic links shift the authentication trust anchor from a memorised secret (password) to the email delivery channel. The security of the authentication is therefore bounded by the security of the user's email account. This creates a transitive trust dependency:

- If the email account uses strong MFA (e.g., FIDO2 key on the email provider) → the magic link provides reasonable authentication strength
- If the email account uses only a password → the magic link's security reduces to the security of that password, defeating the purpose of passwordless authentication
- If the email account is compromised → every service using magic links for that email address is compromised simultaneously

**Weaknesses and attack vectors:**

1. **Email account compromise** — An attacker with access to the user's inbox (via credential stuffing on the email provider, session hijacking, or account takeover) can intercept magic links and authenticate as the user. This is the fundamental limitation: magic links cannot be stronger than the email account that receives them
2. **Email transit security** — SMTP between mail servers uses opportunistic STARTTLS, meaning encryption is attempted but not enforced. If STARTTLS negotiation fails (downgrade attack) or is not supported by an intermediate relay, the email travels in plaintext. An attacker positioned on the network path between mail servers could intercept the magic link in transit. Modern email providers largely mitigate this through MTA-STS (RFC 8461) enforcement, but coverage is not universal
3. **Link leakage vectors** — Magic link URLs can leak through email forwarding (user forwards the email to a colleague), email preview panes in corporate email clients, link preview/prefetch by email security scanners (some scanners follow URLs to check for malware, inadvertently consuming the single-use token), and browser history/referrer headers if the landing page redirects to a third-party resource
4. **Cross-device friction** — The user initiates login on Device A (e.g., laptop browser) but may click the magic link on Device B (e.g., phone email client). Without session binding, this creates a legitimate but confusing UX where the phone browser opens an authenticated session that the user did not intend. With session binding (token bound to Device A's session), the link clicked on Device B fails — better security but worse UX
5. **Email security scanner token prefetch** — Enterprise email security gateways (MIMECAST, Proofpoint, Microsoft Defender for Office 365) scan URLs in emails by following them to check for malicious content. This scanning can consume the single-use magic link token before the user clicks it, invalidating the link. Workarounds include: (a) exempt the authentication domain from URL scanning, (b) design tokens to survive a single prefetch and only invalidate on the second request, or (c) use a POST-based confirmation flow after the initial GET
6. **Referrer header leakage** — If the magic link landing page includes links to external resources (analytics, images, scripts), the browser's `Referer` header may leak the magic link URL to those third-party services. The landing page should use `<meta name="referrer" content="no-referrer">` and avoid any external resource loading

**Assurance level:** Magic links satisfy **AAL1** only. NIST SP 800-63B classifies email-delivered secrets as single-factor authentication — the email inbox is the single factor (something you have — access to the email account). Magic links are not phishing-resistant (the link can be forwarded to an attacker-controlled context) and are not bound to a cryptographic key pair.

#### 6.2 OTP Delivery Channels: Email, SMS, and Voice

One-Time Passwords (OTPs) delivered through out-of-band channels — email, SMS, or voice call — represent the most widely deployed form of second-factor authentication. Unlike magic links, which embed the token in a clickable URL, OTP methods require the user to manually transcribe a short numeric code (typically 6 digits) from one channel (email, phone) to the application's verification prompt. This manual entry step is both a security property (the code is not a clickable URL that can be followed by automated scanners) and a UX friction point.

##### 6.2.1 Email OTP

**Mechanism:** The application generates a random 6–8 digit code, stores it server-side with an expiry timestamp, and sends it to the user's email address. The user reads the code from their email and types it into the application's verification field. The server compares the entered code against the stored value.

Email OTP shares the same fundamental security model as magic links (§6.1) — email inbox access is the authentication factor — but differs in the interaction pattern. The manual transcription step provides marginal protection against link-following scanners and reduces accidental cross-device authentication. However, the same email transit risks (STARTTLS downgrade, account compromise) apply.

**Typical parameters:** 6-digit numeric code, 5–10 minute expiry, single-use, rate-limited to 3–5 requests per hour per account.

##### 6.2.2 SMS OTP

**Mechanism:** The application sends a 6-digit numeric code to the user's registered phone number via SMS (Short Message Service). The user reads the code from the SMS notification and enters it into the application. The code is validated server-side.

SMS OTP is the most widely deployed second-factor method globally — it requires no app installation, works on any mobile phone (including feature phones), and is understood by virtually all users. However, the SMS delivery channel has fundamental security weaknesses that make SMS OTP the least secure OTP delivery method:

**SMS channel vulnerabilities:**

| Attack | Mechanism | Impact |
|:-------|:----------|:-------|
| **SIM swapping** | Attacker contacts the mobile carrier (via social engineering or bribed insiders), convinces them to port the victim's phone number to a new SIM card controlled by the attacker. All SMS messages — including OTPs — are then delivered to the attacker's device | Complete bypass of SMS-based 2FA; the attacker receives the OTP directly |
| **SS7 interception** | Signaling System 7 (SS7) is the signalling protocol suite underpinning the global telephone network, designed in the 1970s with no authentication or encryption. An attacker with access to the SS7 network (purchasable from some telecoms for a few hundred dollars) can intercept SMS messages by sending fraudulent routing commands that redirect messages to an attacker-controlled node | Silent interception of SMS OTPs without the victim's knowledge; no SIM swap required |
| **Diameter protocol exploitation** | The Diameter protocol replaced SS7 in 4G/LTE networks but inherits similar trust-model weaknesses — hop-by-hop routing without end-to-end encryption or robust authentication. Misconfigured Diameter interconnects allow subscriber traffic interception and location tracking | SMS interception on 4G networks; extends SS7-era vulnerabilities to modern infrastructure |
| **Malware** | Mobile malware with SMS read permissions (Android: `READ_SMS`, `RECEIVE_SMS`) can silently intercept OTP codes before the user sees them | Automated OTP theft on compromised devices |
| **Voicemail hijacking** | If the phone is off or unreachable, SMS may be converted to voicemail in some carriers. Default voicemail PINs (often `0000` or the last four digits of the phone number) can be exploited remotely | OTP retrieval via voicemail access |

##### 6.2.3 Voice OTP

**Mechanism:** The application initiates an automated phone call to the user's registered number. A text-to-speech engine reads the OTP code aloud. The user listens and enters the code. Voice OTP serves primarily as an **accessibility fallback** — it enables authentication for users who cannot receive or read SMS (e.g., landline phones, users with visual impairments, regions with unreliable SMS delivery).

Voice OTP shares the SIM-swap and SS7/Diameter vulnerabilities of SMS OTP (the phone number is the trust anchor in both cases). Additional attack vectors include:

- **Voicemail interception** — If the call goes to voicemail (user doesn't answer, phone is off), the OTP is recorded in the voicemail system. Attackers can access voicemail remotely using default or weak PINs
- **Call forwarding abuse** — If the attacker can configure unconditional call forwarding on the victim's account (via social engineering the carrier or exploiting SS7), all voice OTP calls are redirected to the attacker

##### 6.2.4 NIST Classification: Restricted Authenticator

NIST SP 800-63B designates SMS and voice OTP as **restricted authenticators** — a special classification indicating known vulnerability. The final revision (published July 2025) formalises the following constraints:

| Constraint | Requirement |
|:-----------|:-----------|
| **Alternative required** | The verifier SHALL offer at least one unrestricted authenticator option (e.g., TOTP app, FIDO2 key) that satisfies the target AAL |
| **Risk disclosure** | The verifier SHALL inform users of the security risks associated with SMS/voice OTP and the availability of alternative methods |
| **Risk assessment** | The organisation SHALL incorporate SMS/voice OTP risks into its formal risk assessment |
| **Migration plan** | The organisation SHALL maintain a documented migration plan for transitioning away from SMS/voice OTP |
| **Mitigation controls** | The organisation SHALL implement controls against known attacks — SIM swap detection, number porting alerts, carrier account PINs |

**Assurance ceiling:** SMS and voice OTP are permitted at **AAL1**, conditionally permitted at **AAL2** (only when the restricted authenticator conditions above are satisfied and no unrestricted alternative is feasible), and **prohibited at AAL3**.

##### 6.2.5 Deprecation Trajectory

The industry trajectory away from SMS OTP is driven by three converging forces:

1. **Regulatory pressure** — NIST's restricted classification creates compliance friction for any system that relies solely on SMS OTP. Organisations subject to NIST requirements (US federal agencies, contractors, and systems aligned to NIST frameworks) must offer alternatives and document risk acceptance
2. **Escalating SIM-swap fraud** — The FBI's Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3) reported SIM-swap complaints increased from 320 in 2018 to over 2,000 in 2022, with losses exceeding $72 million in 2022 alone. Telecom carriers have implemented number-porting verification improvements (e.g., T-Mobile Account Takeover Protection, Verizon Number Lock), but social engineering remains effective
3. **Platform push toward passkeys** — Apple, Google, and Microsoft are actively deploying passkey infrastructure (§10.6) that provides a superior UX (no code transcription, no waiting for delivery) with phishing-resistant security. As passkey adoption grows, the value proposition of SMS OTP diminishes

Despite the deprecation trajectory, SMS OTP remains deeply embedded in consumer and enterprise systems. Complete elimination is a multi-year effort constrained by user familiarity, feature-phone compatibility requirements, and regulatory frameworks in some jurisdictions that explicitly mandate SMS-based verification (e.g., PSD2 Strong Customer Authentication in the EU, which permits SMS OTP as possession evidence).

##### 6.2.6 Magic Link vs Email OTP: Comparison

Both magic links and email OTP use email inbox access as the authentication factor. The distinction is in the interaction pattern and the resulting security properties:

| Dimension | Magic Link | Email OTP |
|:----------|:-----------|:-----------|
| **User action** | Click a URL in the email | Read a numeric code and type it into the app |
| **Token delivery** | Embedded in a clickable URL | 6–8 digit numeric code in email body |
| **Vulnerability to email scanners** | High — scanners follow URLs and may consume the single-use token | Low — scanners do not typically extract and enter OTP codes |
| **Cross-device risk** | High — clicking link on a different device authenticates that device, not the requesting device | Medium — user must manually type the code on the requesting device, creating a natural cross-device barrier |
| **Session binding** | Possible but creates UX friction (link fails if clicked on wrong device) | Naturally session-bound — the user must type the code on the device that displayed the OTP prompt |
| **Replay risk** | Low — token is single-use and time-limited | Medium — if OTP delivery is delayed, the code may still be valid when the user sees it, but the window is short (5–10 minutes) |
| **User experience** | One click — lowest friction | Read + type — moderate friction |
| **Accessibility** | Poor — screen readers may not clearly distinguish the magic link URL | Good — numeric codes are easily read by screen readers |
| **Recommended for** | Consumer onboarding, low-risk B2C, first-time login | B2C secondary verification, environments with email security scanners |

#### 6.3 Push Notification Authentication

Push notification authentication uses a pre-registered mobile application to deliver authentication challenges directly to the user's device. Instead of transcribing a code from one channel to another, the user responds to the challenge within the authenticator app — dramatically reducing UX friction compared to OTP methods while maintaining out-of-band verification.

**Architecture overview:** The push authentication system consists of four components: (1) the **identity provider** — the server-side authentication authority that initiates push challenges and validates responses; (2) the **push service** — the platform-level notification delivery service (Apple Push Notification service for iOS, Firebase Cloud Messaging for Android), providing reliable, encrypted delivery of notification payloads; (3) the **authenticator app** — the mobile application that receives push notifications, presents authentication challenges, and stores a device-specific cryptographic key provisioned during enrollment; and (4) the **user's device** — the smartphone or tablet whose secure element or keychain stores the signing key. During enrollment, the authenticator app generates a public/private key pair, registers the public key with the IdP, and obtains a device-specific push token from the platform push service. The IdP stores the public key and push token, associating them with the user's account. During authentication, the IdP sends a push notification via the push service, and the authenticator app responds with a cryptographically signed approval.

##### 6.3.1 Simple Push (Approve/Deny)

**Mechanism:** When the user initiates authentication (e.g., enters their username on a web login page), the application server sends a push notification to the user's registered authenticator app. The app displays a prompt asking the user to approve or deny the sign-in attempt. The user taps "Approve" → the authenticator app sends a signed approval response to the server → the server completes authentication.

**Security model:** The registered device is the authentication factor (something you have). The push notification channel (APNs for iOS, FCM for Android) provides a device-bound delivery mechanism that is significantly harder to intercept than SMS. The private key stored on the device can sign the approval response, providing cryptographic proof of possession.

**Critical vulnerability — MFA fatigue / push bombing:** An attacker who possesses the user's password (from a breach or phishing) can trigger repeated push notifications to the user's phone. The user, fatigued by the constant interruptions — especially at inconvenient times (late at night, during meetings) — may eventually tap "Approve" to stop the notifications without verifying the request's legitimacy. Notable real-world exploitation:

- **Uber (September 2022)** — The Lapsus$ group obtained an Uber contractor's credentials and repeatedly triggered MFA push notifications. After over an hour of push bombardment, the contractor approved a request, granting the attacker full access to Uber's internal systems
- **Cisco (August 2022)** — An attacker used voice phishing combined with MFA push spam to gain initial access to a Cisco employee's VPN

##### 6.3.2 Number Matching

Number matching is the industry-standard countermeasure against MFA fatigue attacks. The mechanism adds a challenge-response step to the push flow.

**Push notification authentication flow with number matching:**

```mermaid
---
config:
  sequence:
    messageAlign: left
    noteAlign: left
    actorMargin: 250
  themeVariables:
    noteBkgColor: "transparent"
    noteBorderColor: "transparent"
---
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant User as User (Browser)
    participant IdP as Identity Provider
    participant Push as Push Service<br/>(APNs / FCM)
    participant App as Authenticator App<br/>(User's Phone)

    rect rgba(52, 152, 219, 0.14)
    Note over User,IdP: Authentication Initiation Phase
    User->>IdP: Submit username + password (first factor)
    IdP->>IdP: Validate first factor<br/>→ Password correct<br/>→ MFA required by policy
    IdP->>IdP: Generate random 2-digit number<br/>→ e.g. "42"
    Note right of App: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(46, 204, 113, 0.14)
    Note over IdP,App: Push Delivery Phase
    IdP-->>User: Display number "42" on login screen
    IdP->>Push: Send push notification<br/>to registered device ID<br/>with challenge context
    Push->>App: Deliver push notification<br/>with sign-in metadata:<br/>App: "Outlook Web"<br/>Location: "Amsterdam, NL"<br/>IP: 203.0.113.42
    Note right of App: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(241, 196, 15, 0.14)
    Note over User,App: User Verification Phase
    App->>App: Display three 2-digit options:<br/>  [18]  [42]  [73]<br/>with sign-in context metadata
    App-->>User: User reads "42" from login screen
    User->>App: Select "42" on phone
    Note right of App: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(46, 204, 113, 0.14)
    Note over App,IdP: Response and Session Phase
    App->>IdP: Send signed approval response<br/>with selected number "42"<br/>+ device attestation
    IdP->>IdP: Validate:<br/>→ Number matches ("42" = "42") ✅<br/>→ Device signature valid ✅<br/>→ Within time window ✅
    IdP-->>User: Authentication complete<br/>→ Session established
    Note right of App: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end
```

<details><summary><strong>1. User submits first factor (username + password) to IdP</strong></summary>

The user enters their username and password on the IdP's login page. This is the first authentication factor — a memorised secret. The browser transmits the credentials to the IdP over TLS. At this stage, the user has satisfied the first factor of a multi-factor authentication policy but has not yet completed the second factor.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>2. IdP validates first factor and determines MFA requirement</strong></summary>

The IdP verifies the password against the stored credential (Argon2id hash, bcrypt, etc. as described in §5.2). If the password is correct, the IdP evaluates the applicable authentication policy (e.g., Conditional Access in Entra ID, Authentication Policy in PingAM). The policy determines that a second factor is required — specifically, a push notification to the user's registered authenticator app.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>3. IdP generates a random 2-digit number for number matching</strong></summary>

The IdP generates a cryptographically random 2-digit number (10–99) that will serve as the number-matching challenge. This number is stored server-side in the pending authentication session, associated with a short expiry window (typically 60 seconds). The number is displayed to the user on the login screen and must match the selection made on the authenticator app.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>4. IdP displays the number on the login screen</strong></summary>

The 2-digit number is rendered prominently on the browser login page. The user must read this number and remember it to complete the number-matching challenge on their phone. This step creates the cognitive binding between the login screen and the authenticator app — the user must be actively looking at both to proceed.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>5. IdP sends push notification via platform push service</strong></summary>

The IdP sends a push notification payload to the user's registered device via the platform push service (Apple Push Notification service for iOS, Firebase Cloud Messaging for Android). The payload includes a challenge identifier (opaque token, not the 2-digit number itself), sign-in metadata (application name, geographic location, source IP), and a notification message prompting the user to open the authenticator app.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>6. Push service delivers notification to authenticator app</strong></summary>

The platform push service routes the notification to the specific device registered to the user's account. The notification is displayed on the phone's lock screen or notification shade. The sign-in metadata (application name, location, IP) is included in the notification preview to help the user assess legitimacy before opening the app.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>7. Authenticator app displays three number options with context</strong></summary>

The authenticator app presents the user with three 2-digit numbers in a multiple-choice format. Only one of the three matches the number displayed on the login screen. The app also displays the sign-in context metadata (which application is requesting access, from which location and IP) to help the user verify the request is legitimate. If the user did not initiate a sign-in, the unfamiliar context signals a fraudulent request.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>8. User reads the number from the login screen</strong></summary>

The user looks at the login screen on their primary device (laptop, desktop) and reads the displayed 2-digit number. This cross-device visual verification is the core of the number-matching defence — the user must bridge information from the login screen to the authenticator app, which requires physical presence at the login screen.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>9. User selects the matching number on the authenticator app</strong></summary>

The user taps the matching number on their phone. If they select the correct number, the app proceeds to send the approval. If they select an incorrect number, authentication fails. If the user did not initiate the login and cannot see a number on any login screen, they should deny the request — the absence of a visible number is the primary signal of a push-bombing attack.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>10. Authenticator app sends signed approval to IdP</strong></summary>

The authenticator app sends an approval response to the IdP. The response includes the selected number, a cryptographic signature from the device's registered key (proving device possession), and optionally device attestation data (platform integrity verdict). The response is transmitted directly to the IdP over HTTPS — not through the push notification channel.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>11. IdP validates the response</strong></summary>

The IdP performs three validations: (1) the selected number matches the generated number ("42" = "42"), (2) the device signature is valid and corresponds to the registered authenticator, and (3) the response arrived within the permitted time window. If all three checks pass, the second authentication factor is satisfied.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>12. IdP completes authentication and establishes session</strong></summary>

With both factors validated (password + push approval with number matching), the IdP creates an authenticated session for the user. The session token (cookie, JWT, or opaque reference — see §27) is issued to the browser, and the user is redirected to the target application. The pending push challenge is invalidated.

</details>

<br/>

The flow above illustrates the number-matching variant. The key differences from simple approve/deny are steps 3, 4, 7, 8, and 9 — these steps introduce the cognitive binding that defeats MFA fatigue attacks:

1. User initiates authentication at the login screen
2. The login screen displays a randomly generated **2-digit number** (e.g., "42")
3. A push notification is sent to the user's authenticator app
4. The authenticator app displays three 2-digit numbers and asks the user to **select the one matching the number shown on the login screen**
5. If the user selects the correct number → authentication succeeds
6. If the user selects an incorrect number or denies → authentication fails

**Why number matching defeats push bombing:** An attacker triggering unsolicited push notifications cannot know the 2-digit number displayed on the login screen (the attacker is not sitting in front of the victim's screen). The user who receives an unexpected push notification has no number to match — the disconnect between "I see a push, but I don't see a number on any login screen" signals a fraudulent request.

Microsoft made number matching **mandatory for all Microsoft Authenticator push notifications** on May 8, 2023, removing the administrative option to disable it. The enforcement was rolled out between February and May 2023, accompanied by two additional anti-fatigue measures:

- **Additional context** — The push notification displays metadata about the sign-in request: the application name requesting access, the geographic location (city, country) derived from the source IP, and the source IP address itself. This context helps the user identify fraudulent requests (e.g., "Sign-in to Outlook from Lagos, Nigeria" when the user is in Amsterdam)
- **Anomalous request suppression** — Since September 2023, Microsoft Authenticator silently suppresses push notifications from requests deemed anomalous by Microsoft's risk engine. Instead of interrupting the user's phone, the notification is logged but not displayed. The user must open the Authenticator app manually to see suppressed requests. This eliminates the phone-vibration fatigue vector entirely

**Context mechanisms for push authentication:** Beyond number matching, additional context mechanisms enhance the user's ability to distinguish legitimate from fraudulent requests:

| Context Mechanism | What It Shows | Security Value |
|:-----------------|:-------------|:---------------|
| **Number matching** | 2-digit number displayed on login screen, must be selected from 3 options on authenticator | Defeats push bombing — attacker cannot see the login screen number |
| **Geographic location** | City/country derived from the source IP of the authentication request | Helps user identify unusual locations (e.g., "Lagos, Nigeria" when user is in London) |
| **Application name** | The name of the application requesting authentication | Helps user identify if the request is for an expected service |
| **Device information** | The browser, OS, or device type of the authentication request | Helps identify requests from unusual devices |
| **Anomalous request suppression** | Push notifications from requests deemed anomalous by risk engine are not displayed; user must open app manually | Eliminates the phone-vibration fatigue vector entirely |
| **Time-based frequency limits** | Maximum number of push notifications per user per time window (e.g., 10 per hour) | Limits the intensity of push bombing campaigns |

##### 6.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

| Property | Simple Approve/Deny | Number Matching |
|:---------|:-------------------|:----------------|
| **MFA fatigue resistance** | ❌ Vulnerable — user can tap "Approve" without cognitive engagement | ✅ Resistant — requires active knowledge of the displayed number |
| **Accidental approval** | High risk — single inattentive tap completes authentication | Low risk — must select correct number from multiple options |
