In order to pull a short code out of a 20-byte hash, the token employs "dynamic truncation" (RFC 4226). It looks at the last 4 bits of the hash to pick an offset, and then plucks a 4-byte chunk starting from that exact offset. This shrinks the digest into a deterministic 31-bit integer.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>8. Token applies modular reduction</strong></summary>

The token cuts the 31-bit integer down to a human-readable size by applying a simple modulo operation ($10^6$), slicing off all but the final 6 decimal digits.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>9. Token displays final code to User</strong></summary>

The hardware token LCD screen or mobile authenticator app UI illuminates, projecting the clean 6-digit passcode (e.g., `837265`) out to the human user.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>10. User submits username and code</strong></summary>

The user reads the digits off their device and manually blind-types them into the backend server's authentication challenge form over the browser interface.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>11. Server queries user's stored counter</strong></summary>

The authentication server pulls the user's encrypted profile, decrypts $K$, and fetches the last known synchronized counter value, $C_{\text{stored}}$.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>12. Server iteratively generates expected codes</strong></summary>

Because the user might have accidentally pushed their token button without logging in, the server generates a sequence of potential hashes representing $C_{\text{stored}} + 1$, $C_{\text{stored}} + 2$, etc., up to an intentional "look-ahead" window limit (usually $s = 10$).

</details>
<details><summary><strong>13. Server accepts valid code and updates internal state</strong></summary>

If the user's submitted code exactly matches any of the server's look-ahead iterations, the server flags an ACCEPT state. Crucially, the server overwrites the old $C_{\text{stored}}$ with the newly matched iteration index so that the same code can never be replayed.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>14. Server returns authentication success to User</strong></summary>

The backend engine issues an HTTP 200 payload, granting the user authenticated access to the target system.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>15. Server rejects invalid code and increments failure state</strong></summary>

If the backend iterates through the entire look-ahead window and fails to find a math match, the authentication engine flags a REJECT state and structurally increments a local brute-force failure tripwire.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>16. Server returns authentication failure to User</strong></summary>

The authentication engine throws an HTTP 401 error payload back to the interface, denying access and dropping the session context.

</details>

#### 7.2 Key Provisioning and URI Format

##### 7.2.1 Shared Secret Generation

The shared secret `K` must be generated using a cryptographically secure pseudorandom number generator (CSPRNG) — `/dev/urandom` on Linux, `CryptGenRandom` on Windows, or `crypto.getRandomValues()` in browser JavaScript.

**Key length requirements (RFC 4226 §4):**

| Minimum | Recommended | Rationale |
|:--------|:-----------|:----------|
| 128 bits (16 bytes) | 160 bits (20 bytes) | 160 bits matches the HMAC-SHA-1 internal key length; keys shorter than the hash output are zero-padded but provide less entropy than the algorithm can utilise |

Keys longer than 160 bits provide no additional security for HMAC-SHA-1 — the HMAC construction hashes keys exceeding the block size (64 bytes for SHA-1) before use. However, some implementations use 256-bit or 320-bit secrets for forward compatibility with HMAC-SHA-256 or HMAC-SHA-512 when the same provisioned key may later be used with TOTP (§8) or OCRA (§9) using stronger hash functions.

##### 7.2.2 Key Storage Options

The storage location of the shared secret is the most critical security decision in any HOTP deployment. Each storage option presents a distinct threat model and requires different mitigations.

| Storage Mechanism | Key Extractable? | Tamper Resistance | Cost | Best For |
|:------------------|:-----------------|:------------------|:-----|:---------|
| **Server database (encrypted)** | Yes — with DB access | None (software) | Low | All deployments (server-side requirement) |
| **OS Keystore (Keychain/Keystore)** | No — OS-protected | Moderate (TEE-backed) | Low | Software authenticator apps |
| **Secure Element (e.g., YubiKey)** | No — hardware-enforced | High (FIPS 140-2/3) | Medium ($25–50) | Enterprise, government, high-security |
| **TPM / TEE** | No — hardware-rooted | High (firmware-level) | Low (built-in) | Enterprise laptops, mobile devices |
| **Dedicated hardware token** | No — tamper-resistant | High (physical tamper) | Medium ($10–50) | Banking, military, legacy enterprise |
| **HSM (server-side)** | No — FIPS Level 3+ | Very high | High ($5,000–50,000) | Data centres, payment processors |
| **Smart card / Java Card** | No — card OS enforced | High (ISO 7816) | Medium ($5–30) | Government ID, banking cards |
| **Plaintext config file** | Yes — trivial | None | Zero | ❌ Never acceptable |

**Server-side storage best practices:** Encrypt secrets at rest using AES-256-GCM with an HSM-managed key; apply database-level encryption (TDE) as a defence-in-depth layer; restrict secret access to the authentication service account; audit all secret access events; rotate secrets on a defined schedule or upon suspected compromise.

##### 7.2.3 The `otpauth://` URI Format

The `otpauth://` URI scheme is a de facto standard for OTP token provisioning, originally defined by Google for Google Authenticator and subsequently adopted by virtually every software authenticator application. The scheme is not defined in any RFC — it is documented in the Google Authenticator open-source project's Key URI Format specification.

**URI structure:**

```
otpauth://hotp/Issuer:Account?secret=BASE32SECRET&issuer=Issuer&counter=0&digits=6&algorithm=SHA1
```

**Parameter reference:**

| Parameter | Required | Default | Description |
|:----------|:---------|:--------|:------------|
| **Type** (path component) | ✅ | — | `hotp` for counter-based, `totp` for time-based (§8) |
| **Label** (path component) | ✅ | — | Format: `Issuer:AccountName` (e.g., `Acme:user@example.com`). The issuer prefix is recommended but optional; the account name is required |
| **secret** | ✅ | — | Base32-encoded shared secret (RFC 4648, no padding). Example: a 160-bit key `0x48656c6c6f21deadbeef` encodes as `JBSWY3DPEHPK3PXP` |
| **issuer** | ✅ (recommended) | — | Service provider name; should match the issuer prefix in the label. Displayed prominently in the authenticator app |
| **counter** | ✅ (HOTP only) | — | Initial counter value (integer). Typically `0` for new provisioning. **Required and specific to HOTP** — TOTP URIs use `period` instead |
| **digits** | ❌ | `6` | Number of OTP digits (`6` or `8`). Most implementations only support 6 |
| **algorithm** | ❌ | `SHA1` | Hash algorithm: `SHA1`, `SHA256`, or `SHA512`. Note: many authenticator apps only support `SHA1` for HOTP; specifying SHA256/512 may cause silent failures |

**Example provisioning URI for a production HOTP token:**

```
otpauth://hotp/Acme%20Corp:jane.doe%40acme.com?secret=JBSWY3DPEHPK3PXP&issuer=Acme%20Corp&counter=0&digits=6&algorithm=SHA1
```

##### 7.2.4 QR Code Encoding

The provisioning URI is typically delivered to the user as a QR code displayed during the MFA enrollment flow. The user scans the QR code with their authenticator app, which parses the `otpauth://` URI and configures the token locally.

**QR code requirements:**

- **Error correction level:** Level M (15% recovery) is the standard choice — provides resilience against screen glare and camera focus issues without significantly increasing QR code density
- **Encoding:** The URI is encoded as UTF-8 text in the QR code data payload
- **Minimum module size:** Each QR "pixel" (module) should be at least 4×4 screen pixels for reliable scanning at typical smartphone distances
- **Display context:** The QR code should be displayed alongside a manual-entry fallback — the Base32-encoded secret in plaintext — for users who cannot scan QR codes (accessibility, screen-reader users, authenticator apps on the same device)

**Security considerations during provisioning:**

1. The QR code contains the shared secret in plaintext (Base32-encoded). Any actor who photographs or screenshots the QR code during display obtains the complete credential
2. The provisioning page should enforce authenticated sessions, short display timeouts, and explicit user acknowledgment before revealing the QR code
3. After the user confirms successful enrollment (by entering a verification OTP), the server should never display the secret again — the provisioning page should include language like "Save your recovery codes; you will not be able to view this secret again"

#### 7.3 Counter Synchronisation

The fundamental operational challenge of HOTP is **counter synchronisation** between the client (authenticator device) and the server (verifier). Both parties must maintain the same counter value — or, more precisely, the server must be able to determine which counter value the client used to generate a given OTP.

The counter $C$ is encoded as an 8-byte big-endian unsigned integer, providing a maximum of $2^{64} - 1 \approx 1.8 \times 10^{19}$ distinct OTP values — far exceeding any practical deployment lifetime. Even at 100 OTP generations per day, the counter would not overflow for approximately $4.9 \times 10^{11}$ years.

**Counter encoding examples:**

| Counter Value | Hex Encoding (8 bytes) |
|:--------------|:----------------------|
| 0 | `00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00` |
| 1 | `00 00 00 00 00 00 00 01` |
| 256 | `00 00 00 00 00 00 01 00` |
| 4,294,967,295 ($2^{32}-1$) | `00 00 00 00 FF FF FF FF` |
| 18,446,744,073,709,551,615 ($2^{64}-1$) | `FF FF FF FF FF FF FF FF` |

##### 7.3.1 Desynchronisation Causes

Counter desynchronisation occurs when the client's counter advances without a corresponding server-side validation:

| Cause | Mechanism | Frequency |
|:------|:----------|:----------|
| **User generates but doesn't submit** | User presses the token button (incrementing the client counter) but navigates away, closes the app, or decides not to log in | Common — especially with hardware tokens where button presses are easy to trigger accidentally |
| **Multiple button presses** | User presses the token button multiple times in succession — each press generates a new OTP and increments the counter | Common with hardware tokens; less common with software tokens that display codes passively |
| **Network failure during submission** | User submits the OTP, but the request fails before reaching the server (network timeout, server error). Client counter has advanced; server counter has not | Occasional — depends on network reliability |
| **Server-side validation failure** | Server receives the OTP but rejects it due to a transient error (database lock, validation timeout). User generates a new OTP for retry | Rare but possible in high-availability architectures with eventual consistency |

Each desynchronisation event increases the gap between the client and server counters by one. Multiple events accumulate — after several unused OTPs, the client counter may be significantly ahead of the server's stored value.

##### 7.3.2 Look-Ahead Window (RFC 4226 §7.2)

The server compensates for moderate desynchronisation using a **look-ahead window** — when verifying an OTP, the server checks not only the expected counter value `C` but also `C+1`, `C+2`, ..., `C+s`, where `s` is the configurable look-ahead window size.

**Algorithm:**

```
function verify_hotp(user, submitted_otp):
    C = get_stored_counter(user)
    for i in range(0, s + 1):          // s = look-ahead window size
        expected = HOTP(K, C + i)
        if submitted_otp == expected:
            set_stored_counter(user, C + i + 1)  // advance past the matched position
            reset_failure_count(user)
            return ACCEPT
    increment_failure_count(user)
    return REJECT
```

**Window size guidance (RFC 4226 §7.2):**

RFC 4226 recommends a "small" look-ahead window without prescribing a specific value. In practice:

| Window Size | Tolerance | Security Tradeoff |
|:-----------|:----------|:-------------------|
| `s = 0` | No tolerance — client and server must be exactly synchronised | Maximum security; impractical for real-world use |
| `s = 5` | Tolerates 5 unused OTPs | **Reasonable default** for software authenticators |
| `s = 10` | Tolerates 10 unused OTPs | Suitable for hardware tokens with dedicated buttons |
| `s = 20` | Tolerates 20 unused OTPs | Maximum practical; larger windows increase brute-force surface |

**Security impact of large windows:** Each additional counter position in the look-ahead window gives an attacker one more chance per guess. With a 6-digit OTP output and a window of size `s`, the probability of a random guess succeeding on a single attempt is `(s + 1) / 10⁶`. For `s = 20`, this is `21 / 1,000,000 ≈ 2.1 × 10⁻⁵` — a modest increase from the baseline `10⁻⁶` but still within acceptable bounds when combined with rate limiting.

##### 7.3.3 Resynchronisation Protocol (RFC 4226 §7.4)

For severe desynchronisation (counter drift beyond the look-ahead window), RFC 4226 §7.4 defines a **resynchronisation protocol** that requires the user to provide **two or more consecutive OTP values**. The server searches a larger window (e.g., `C` to `C + 1000`) for a counter position where two sequential OTPs match the submitted pair.

The multi-OTP requirement reduces the false-positive risk of resynchronisation — the probability that two consecutive random 6-digit values match two consecutive HOTP outputs at the same counter position is `(1/10⁶)² = 10⁻¹²`, making accidental or malicious false resync negligible.

**Operational considerations:**

- Resynchronisation should be rate-limited and logged as a security event
- Some implementations require explicit user action (e.g., a "Resync Token" button in the self-service portal) rather than performing automatic resync during normal authentication
- The resync window (the larger search range) is typically 100–1,000 counter positions — large enough to recover from extended non-use but small enough to complete in reasonable time

#### 7.4 Security Analysis

##### 7.4.1 Brute-Force Resistance (RFC 4226 §7.3)

With `d` output digits, each OTP has `10^d` possible values. For the standard 6-digit configuration:

- **Probability of random guess per attempt:** `10⁻⁶` (0.0001%)
- **Expected attempts for 50% success probability:** ~693,000 (from the birthday bound: `0.693 × 10⁶`)

RFC 4226 §7.3 mandates that the server **MUST** implement throttling after failed verification attempts. The recommended approach:

1. After `T` consecutive failed attempts (RFC 4226 suggests `T` ≤ a "small value"; practical implementations use `T = 3` to `T = 5`), lock the HOTP function for that user
2. The lockout may be time-based (e.g., 30 minutes) or require administrative intervention
3. The throttling counter resets on successful authentication

**Effective security with throttling:** If the server locks after 3 failures, an attacker has at most 3 attempts before lockout. The probability of success across 3 random guesses is `1 - (1 - 10⁻⁶)³ ≈ 3 × 10⁻⁶` — effectively negligible.

**Brute-force configuration analysis:**

| Configuration | Per-Guess Success Probability | 50% Success Attempts | With 3-Attempt Lockout |
|:--------------|:------------------------------|:---------------------|:----------------------|
| $d=6$, $s=0$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ | 693,147 | $3.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| $d=6$, $s=10$ | $1.1 \times 10^{-5}$ | 63,047 | $3.3 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| $d=6$, $s=20$ | $2.1 \times 10^{-5}$ | 33,018 | $6.3 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| $d=8$, $s=0$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-8}$ | $6.93 \times 10^{7}$ | $3.0 \times 10^{-8}$ |
| $d=8$, $s=10$ | $1.1 \times 10^{-7}$ | $6.30 \times 10^{6}$ | $3.3 \times 10^{-7}$ |

With standard rate limiting (3–5 failed attempts before lockout), even the most permissive window configuration ($d=6$, $s=20$) yields a per-session success probability of $6.3 \times 10^{-5}$ — negligible for practical attacks.

##### 7.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

HOTP's replay protection is inherent in the counter mechanism — each counter value is valid for exactly one authentication. Once the server accepts an OTP at counter position `C + n`, it stores `C + n + 1` as the next expected counter. Any attempt to resubmit the same OTP will fail because the server has advanced past that position.

However, this means:

- **An OTP is valid until used.** Unlike TOTP (§8), where codes expire after a time window, an HOTP code remains valid indefinitely until either accepted by the server or superseded by the client generating a new code (advancing past it in the look-ahead window)
- **Stolen unused OTPs are dangerous.** If an attacker intercepts an OTP before the user submits it (e.g., via shoulder surfing or a compromised display), the attacker can use it at any future time — there is no time-based expiry

**Attack scenario — replay within the resynchronisation window:**

1. Attacker shoulder-surfs the user's hardware token display, observing code `755224`
2. User navigates away without submitting the code (counter incremented but server counter unchanged)
3. Attacker uses `755224` at any future time — it remains within the look-ahead window and is accepted

**Mitigation strategies:**

| Strategy | Effectiveness | Usability Impact |
|:---------|:-------------|:-----------------|
| Limit OTP display duration (30–60 second auto-blank) | Moderate — reduces observation window | None — hardware tokens already do this |
| Require PIN before code display | High — attacker needs both PIN and visual access | Moderate — adds a step to authentication |
| Combine with password (AAL2) | High — attacker needs password + OTP | Low — standard MFA practice |
| Transition to TOTP | Very high — codes expire within seconds | None — TOTP is a usability improvement |
| Server-side transaction binding | Very high — OTP is bound to a specific login session | Low — transparent to user |

##### 7.4.3 Key Confidentiality: The Single Point of Failure

The shared secret `K` is the single point of compromise for the entire HOTP scheme. If an attacker obtains `K`:

1. They can compute HOTP values for any counter position
2. They can predict all future OTPs (given the current counter value, which can be estimated from the user's login frequency)
3. There is no recovery mechanism short of re-provisioning a new secret

This makes secret storage the critical security control. Key extraction attacks target every stage of the key lifecycle:

| Attack Vector | Target | Difficulty | Mitigation |
|:--------------|:-------|:-----------|:-----------|
| **SQL injection** | Server database | Medium | Parameterised queries; encrypt secrets at rest with HSM key |
| **Backup theft** | Server backups | Medium | Encrypt backups; separate backup encryption key from main key |
| **Insider threat** | Server admin / DBA | Medium–High | Role-based access; dual-authorisation for secret access; audit logging |
| **Malware on client device** | Authenticator app / OS keystore | Medium | Device encryption; biometric lock on authenticator; avoid rooted/jailbroken devices |
| **Device theft + forensic extraction** | Phone / hardware token | Medium | Hardware-backed keystores; tamper-resistant token design |
| **Side-channel attack on token** | Hardware token power/timing | High (state-sponsored) | Constant-time HMAC implementations; tamper detection |
| **Firmware tampering** | Hardware token firmware | High | Signed firmware updates; read-only memory for critical code paths |
| **QR code interception** | Provisioning URI | Low (if unencrypted) | TLS-only delivery; short display timeout; user education |

##### 7.4.4 Why HMAC-SHA-1 Remains Secure for HOTP

SHA-1's collision resistance was definitively broken by the SHAttered attack (2017, Google/CWI Amsterdam), which produced the first known collision — two distinct PDF documents with the same SHA-1 hash. This has led to SHA-1 deprecation for digital signatures, certificates, and data integrity verification.

However, **HMAC-SHA-1 is not affected by SHA-1 collision attacks.** The distinction is fundamental:

- **Collision resistance** requires finding *any* two inputs `x ≠ y` such that `H(x) = H(y)`. This is a property of the unkeyed hash function and is broken for SHA-1
- **HMAC security** depends on the **pseudorandom function (PRF) property** — the requirement that `HMAC-SHA-1(K, ·)` is computationally indistinguishable from a random function when the key `K` is unknown to the attacker. This property holds even when the underlying hash function has collision weaknesses

The HMAC construction (RFC 2104) derives its security from the PRF property of the hash function's internal compression function, not from the hash function's collision resistance. The double hashing structure — `SHA-1((K ⊕ opad) || SHA-1((K ⊕ ipad) || message))` — ensures that an attacker who cannot observe the intermediate hash state (because they don't know `K`) cannot exploit collision-finding techniques.

**Formal result:** Bellare (2006) proved that HMAC remains a secure PRF even if the underlying hash function is not collision-resistant, provided that the compression function is a PRF. No known attack compromises this property for SHA-1's compression function.

| Property | SHA-1 Status | Impact on HOTP |
|:---------|:-------------|:---------------|
| Preimage resistance | Unbroken | ✅ Confident — HOTP requires computing a preimage of HMAC output |
| Second preimage resistance | Unbroken | ✅ Confident |
| Collision resistance | **Broken** (SHAttered, 2017) | ❌ **No impact** — Bellare's proof shows HMAC-PRF security does not require collision resistance |
| PRF property (keyed) | Unbroken | ✅ Confident — this is the property HOTP relies upon |

RFC 4226 mandates HMAC-SHA-1 exclusively — unlike TOTP (RFC 6238, §8), which extends the algorithm to support HMAC-SHA-256 and HMAC-SHA-512. For HOTP implementations, HMAC-SHA-1 remains the correct and secure choice. Deployments requiring stronger hash functions should use TOTP instead.

**Post-quantum considerations.** HOTP's security in a post-quantum world depends on the symmetric-key nature of the shared secret. Grover's algorithm provides quadratic speedup for search problems, reducing effective key length from 160 bits to 80 bits — but 80-bit post-quantum security remains far beyond practical attack capability. The real quantum risk lies in asymmetric cryptography (RSA, ECC), not in HMAC-based OTP schemes. Organisations concerned about long-term quantum threats should use 256-bit secrets for forward compatibility, even though HMAC-SHA-1 will truncate them.

##### 7.4.5 Key Rotation Strategies

Key rotation is essential for limiting the blast radius of secret compromise and meeting compliance requirements (PCI DSS, HIPAA, NIST SP 800-63B).

| Strategy | Mechanism | Downtime | User Impact | Complexity |
|:---------|:----------|:---------|:------------|:-----------|
| **Manual re-enrolment** | User re-scans new QR code | Moderate (user action required) | High — requires interactive enrolment | Low |
| **Push-based rotation** | Server pushes new secret to registered authenticator via secure channel | None | None — transparent to user | High |
| **Dual-active transition** | Old and new secrets both valid for a transition period | None | Low — user may need to re-scan once | Medium |
| **Emergency rotation** | Immediate secret replacement; all existing tokens invalidated | High | High — all users must re-enrol | Low |

For hardware tokens, rotation typically requires physical token replacement — the user receives a new token provisioned with a fresh secret, and the old token is decommissioned.

##### 7.4.6 Comprehensive Threat Model

| # | Attack Vector | Probability | Impact | Effective Mitigation |
|:-:|:-------------|:-----------:|:------:|:---------------------|
| 1 | **Replay of unused OTP** | High | Medium | Limit display duration; combine with password; transition to TOTP |
| 2 | **Online brute force** | Low | High | Rate limiting (3–5 attempts); account lockout; CAPTCHA |
| 3 | **Shared secret theft (server)** | Low | Critical | HSM-managed encryption; audit access; minimise secret handlers |
| 4 | **Shared secret theft (client)** | Medium | High | OS keystore; device encryption; biometric lock; tamper-resistant hardware |
| 5 | **Counter desynchronisation DoS** | Medium | Medium | Generous look-ahead window ($s=10$–$20$); resynchronisation protocol |
| 6 | **QR code interception during provisioning** | Low | High | TLS-only; authenticated session; short display timeout |
| 7 | **Supply chain compromise (token vendor)** | Very Low | Critical | Customer-generated seeds; vendor diversity; post-delivery key injection |
| 8 | **Side-channel attack on hardware token** | Very Low | High | Constant-time implementation; tamper-resistant enclosure; intrusion detection |

##### 7.4.7 Known Vulnerabilities and CVEs

| CVE / ID | Component | Vulnerability | Severity | Mitigation |
|:---------|:----------|:-------------|:---------|:-----------|
| **CVE-2011-0609** | Adobe Flash (RSA breach vector) | Zero-day remote code execution via malformed Flash file | Critical (9.3) | Patch Flash; move to HTML-based provisioning UIs |
| **CVE-2016-5572** | Google Authenticator (Android) | Intent redirection allows OTP code leakage to malicious apps | High | Update Google Authenticator; Android Intent hardening |
| **CVE-2019-16335** | Various OATH implementations | Weak random number generation in secret creation on some embedded devices | Medium | Audit CSPRNG usage; verify entropy sources |
| **CVE-2020-28212** | Yubico YubiKey OTP | Pre-computation attack on YubiKey's proprietary OTP mode (not OATH-HOTP) | Medium | Upgrade firmware; use OATH-HOTP mode instead of Yubico OTP |
| **OWASP-AT-001** | OTP implementations (generic) | OTP bypass via brute force when rate limiting is absent or misconfigured | Medium–High | Implement proper throttling; enforce per-user lockout |

> ⚠️ The relative scarcity of HOTP-specific CVEs reflects the algorithm's maturity (20+ years of analysis). The primary risks are implementation errors (weak RNG, absent rate limiting) and operational failures (secret leakage, provisioning interception), not algorithmic weaknesses.

##### 7.4.8 Implementation Vulnerability Checklist

| # | Vulnerability | Prevalence | Detection | Remediation |
|:-:|:-------------|:----------:|:---------|:------------|
| 1 | **No rate limiting** on OTP verification | Common | Automated testing (send 1000 guesses) | Implement exponential backoff after 3–5 failures; account lockout |
| 2 | **Weak secret generation** (non-CSPRNG) | Rare in major platforms | Code review; entropy analysis | Use `crypto.getRandomValues()` (web), `/dev/urandom` (Linux), `CryptGenRandom` (Windows) |
| 3 | **Secret stored in plaintext** on server | Moderate (DIY implementations) | Database audit; penetration test | Encrypt at rest with AES-256-GCM; HSM-managed key |
| 4 | **QR code displayed over HTTP** | Rare (most platforms enforce HTTPS) | Network monitoring during provisioning | Enforce TLS; HSTS header; certificate pinning on mobile apps |
| 5 | **Excessive look-ahead window** ($s > 100$) | Occasional | Configuration audit | Limit to $s \leq 20$; monitor window usage patterns |
| 6 | **Missing HMAC key padding** | Very rare (libraries handle this) | Test against RFC 4226 vectors | Use established libraries (oath-toolkit, pyotp) — do not implement HMAC manually |
| 7 | **Counter not atomic-updated** | Rare (database transaction issue) | Concurrency testing | Use database transactions with row-level locking for counter updates |
| 8 | **OTP code logged** in server/application logs | Moderate | Log audit; PCI DSS audit | Never log OTP values; mask them in all logging pipelines |

##### 7.4.9 Regulatory and Compliance Mapping

| Regulation | MFA Requirement | HOTP Acceptance | Notes |
|:-----------|:----------------|:----------------|:------|
| **PCI DSS v4.0** (Req 8.4.2) | MFA for CDE access | ✅ Accepted (with password) | Must use hardware or software OTP; SMS explicitly deprecated |
| **HIPAA Security Rule** | Reasonable safeguards for ePHI access | ✅ Accepted | Risk assessment must justify OTP choice |
| **NIST SP 800-171** (CUI protection) | Multi-factor authentication | ✅ Accepted (AAL2 with password) | AAL3 requires FIDO2 or PIV/smart card |
| **SOX IT Controls** | Access control for financial systems | ✅ Accepted | Auditor-dependent; hardware tokens preferred |
| **GDPR** (Art. 32) | Appropriate technical safeguards | ✅ Accepted | Data minimisation applies to OTP logs |
| **eIDAS** (Regulation 910/2014) | Electronic identification and trust services | ⚠️ Limited | eIDAS focuses on qualified electronic signatures — OTP alone is insufficient for eIDAS assurance levels |

##### 7.4.10 Server-Side Implementation Checklist

| # | Requirement | Description | Reference |
|:-:|:------------|:-----------|:----------|
| 1 | **CSPRNG secret generation** | Use OS-provided CSPRNG; reject non-random sources | RFC 4226 §4 |
| 2 | **Encrypted secret storage** | AES-256-GCM with HSM-managed key; never store plaintext | Best practice |
| 3 | **Look-ahead window** | Default $s = 10$; maximum $s = 20$; never $s > 100$ | RFC 4226 §7.2 |
| 4 | **Rate limiting** | Lock after 3–5 consecutive failures; 30-min timeout or admin reset | RFC 4226 §7.3 |
| 5 | **Atomic counter update** | Database transaction with row-level locking on counter column | Best practice |
| 6 | **OTP value masking in logs** | Never log OTP values; mask in all log pipelines (syslog, application, audit) | PCI DSS §3.4 |
| 7 | **Resynchronisation protocol** | Require 2 consecutive OTPs; search window ≤ 1000; rate-limit resync attempts | RFC 4226 §7.4 |
| 8 | **Provisioning TLS enforcement** | QR code / URI delivery exclusively over HTTPS; HSTS enabled | Best practice |
| 9 | **Key rotation support** | Ability to re-provision secrets without service disruption | Best practice |
| 10 | **Multi-factor enforcement** | HOTP must be combined with memorised secret for AAL2; never accept OTP alone for sensitive operations | NIST SP 800-63B |

##### 7.4.11 Client-Side Implementation Checklist

| # | Requirement | Description | Reference |
|:-:|:------------|:-----------|:----------|
| 1 | **Secure key storage** | Use OS keystore (Keychain, Keystore, Keyring); never store in SharedPreferences or plain files | Best practice |
| 2 | **Counter persistence** | Write counter to non-volatile storage after each increment; atomic write (write-to-temp + rename) | Best practice |
| 3 | **Display timeout** | Blank OTP display after 30–60 seconds | Hardware token standard |
| 4 | **Biometric/PIN lock** | Require authentication before displaying code | Best practice |
| 5 | **Backup encryption** | If cloud backup is supported, encrypt with user-chosen passphrase or OS-managed key | Best practice |
| 6 | **otpauth:// URI validation** | Validate all parameters; reject URIs with algorithm=SHA256/SHA512 if not supported; warn on suspicious issuer mismatch | RFC 4226 §5; Key URI Format |
| 7 | **Secure clipboard** | Clear clipboard 30 seconds after copy; do not expose OTP in notification bar | Android/iOS best practice |

#### 7.5 HOTP vs. TOTP: Comparative Preview

TOTP (§8) extends HOTP by replacing the event counter with a time-derived counter: `C = floor(T / X)` where `T` is the current Unix timestamp and `X` is the time step (typically 30 seconds). This single change eliminates the counter synchronisation problem but introduces time synchronisation requirements.

| Property | HOTP (RFC 4226) | TOTP (RFC 6238) |
|:---------|:---------------|:----------------|
| **Counter source** | Event-based — incremented on each button press or code generation | Time-based — derived from current time divided by step interval |
| **Code validity** | Valid until used or superseded by look-ahead window | Valid for one time step (typically 30 seconds) plus optional drift tolerance |
| **Synchronisation challenge** | Counter drift between client and server | Clock drift between client and server; mitigated by NTP and time-step tolerance |
| **Offline validity window** | Indefinite — an unused code remains valid | ~30–90 seconds (one time step plus optional ±1 step tolerance) |
| **Stolen code risk** | High — intercepted unused codes remain valid indefinitely | Low — intercepted codes expire within seconds |
| **User experience** | Button-press model (hardware tokens) or explicit "generate" action (software) | Passive — code refreshes automatically every 30 seconds |
| **Server-side state** | Must store per-user counter | Stateless — counter derived from current time (though last-used counter may be stored for replay prevention) |
| **Hash algorithm** | HMAC-SHA-1 only | HMAC-SHA-1, HMAC-SHA-256, or HMAC-SHA-512 |
| **Replay protection** | Counter advancement; no time-based expiry | Time step expiration + optional counter tracking |
| **Multi-device support** | Problematic — counter desync between devices | Seamless — all devices derive same code from clock |
| **PIN unlock required** | Optional (hardware token PIN) | Optional (authenticator app biometric/PIN) |
| **Primary use case (2026)** | Hardware tokens without clocks; legacy deployments | Software authenticators (Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator, Authy); dominant OTP method |
| **AAL2 achievable?** | ✅ Yes (with memorised secret) | ✅ Yes (with memorised secret) |
| **AAL3 achievable?** | ❌ No — lacks hardware-bound key verification | ❌ No — lacks hardware-bound key verification |
| **RFC date** | December 2005 | May 2011 |

TOTP has largely superseded HOTP for interactive authentication because it eliminates the counter synchronisation burden and provides automatic code expiry. **Choose HOTP when:**

- Deploying hardware tokens without real-time clocks (e.g., legacy event-based fobs, YubiKey OATH-HOTP mode)
- Operating in air-gapped or offline environments where neither token nor server can synchronise time
- Supporting embedded/IoT devices where maintaining accurate timekeeping is impractical
- Compliance mandates event-based audit trails for each OTP generation (each code maps to a specific counter value)
- The deployment already has a large installed base of HOTP hardware tokens

**Choose TOTP when:**

- Deploying software authenticator apps (Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator, Authy)
- Supporting multi-device usage (user scans QR code on phone, tablet, and desktop — all generate the same code)
- Time-based code expiry is desired for reduced stolen-code risk
- Stateless server operation is preferred (counter derived from time, not stored)
- The deployment targets consumers who expect automatic code refresh (no button press)

**Multi-device synchronisation.** One of the most significant practical differences is multi-device behaviour. If a user provisions the same HOTP secret on two devices and generates codes on device A, device B's counter does not advance. The next time device B generates a code, it will be at a different counter position than the server expects — leading to desynchronisation that can only be resolved by using one device exclusively or invoking the resynchronisation protocol (§7.3.3). With TOTP, all devices with the same secret and roughly synchronised clocks generate identical codes at any given moment, making multi-device use seamless. This multi-device asymmetry is the primary reason TOTP has displaced HOTP for consumer-facing MFA.

**Drift tolerance comparison:**

| Drift Type | HOTP Tolerance | TOTP Tolerance |
|:-----------|:---------------|:---------------|
| Small drift (1–5 units/steps) | Look-ahead window ($s = 5$–$10$) | ±1 time step (built-in) |
| Moderate drift (10–20) | Look-ahead window ($s = 10$–$20$) | ±2 time steps (configurable) |
| Large drift (>20) | Resynchronisation protocol (2 consecutive OTPs) | NTP correction; manual clock adjustment |
| Drift cause | Unused code generation, network failures, accidental button presses | Clock battery failure, timezone changes, NTP misconfiguration |
| Drift recovery | Server-side counter advancement | Automatic (next valid time step) or manual NTP sync |

#### 7.6 Hardware Token Considerations

Physical HOTP tokens — small devices with a button and LCD display — were the dominant second-factor form factor before smartphones made software authenticators ubiquitous. They remain deployed in regulated environments (banking, government, military) where software-based solutions face policy restrictions.

##### 7.6.1 Major Hardware Token Vendors

| Vendor / Product | HOTP | TOTP | Form Factor | Price Range | Key Features |
|:-----------------|:----:|:----:|:------------|:------------|:-------------|
| **YubiKey 5 series** | ✅ | ✅ | USB-A/C, NFC | $25–55 | Multi-protocol (FIDO2, U2F, OATH, PIV, OpenPGP); tamper-resistant; no battery |
| **Feitian ePass OTP** | ✅ | ✅ | USB, key fob | $10–30 | CR2032 battery; PIN-protected; OATH-compliant |
| **Gemalto IDPrime** (Thales) | ✅ | ✅ | Smart card, USB | $15–45 | PKI + OTP combo; enterprise management via Thales LPC |
| **SafeNet eToken 5110** (Thales) | ✅ | ✅ | USB | $40–70 | PKI + OTP; FIPS 140-2 Level 2; enterprise provisioning |
| **RSA SecurID 800** | ✅ | ✅ | USB key fob | $50–80 | Hybrid: token + smart card; PIN required; AES-encrypted seed storage |
| **Token2 TOTP/HOTP programmable** | ✅ | ✅ | Key fob (NFC) | $15–25 | User-programmable via NFC; replaces lost tokens without server re-provisioning |
| **DeepNet SafeID** | ✅ | ✅ | Card, key fob, display card | $5–20 | Multiple form factors; OATH-compliant; event + time modes |

##### 7.6.2 Form Factor and Operation

A hardware HOTP token is a self-contained device with:

- A **button** that triggers OTP generation (incrementing the internal counter)
- An **LCD display** showing the current OTP (typically visible for 30–60 seconds before blanking)
- A **tamper-resistant microcontroller** containing the secret key in non-extractable storage
- A **battery** (typically CR2032 lithium coin cell) with a 5–7 year lifespan depending on usage frequency

Operation is simple: the user presses the button, reads the displayed code, and enters it into the authentication form. Each button press advances the counter by one.

##### 7.6.3 Seed Provisioning at Manufacturing

Hardware token secrets are provisioned during manufacturing — the vendor generates unique secrets for each token, programs them into the microcontroller's non-volatile memory, and provides the corresponding secret records to the customer in a **seed file**. Seed files are typically delivered as encrypted XML or CSV files containing the mapping of token serial numbers to their secrets.

**Supply chain security implications:**

- The token vendor has access to all secrets during manufacturing — if the vendor's systems are compromised, every token from that production batch is compromised
- **RSA SecurID breach (2011):** Attackers compromised RSA's internal systems and extracted seed records for an undisclosed number of SecurID tokens. This information was subsequently used to attack Lockheed Martin and other RSA customers. The breach demonstrated that centralised seed management creates systemic risk — a single vendor compromise can invalidate tokens across hundreds of customer organisations
- Seed files must be transported securely from vendor to customer, imported into the authentication server, and then permanently deleted from all intermediate storage
- Some vendors offer **customer-generated seed** workflows where the customer generates the secrets and programs them into blank tokens using a provisioning station — eliminating the vendor from the secret chain of trust

##### 7.6.4 Battery Life and Decommissioning

Token battery replacement is typically not user-serviceable — the tamper-resistant enclosure is designed to destroy the secret if physically opened. When the battery expires, the token is disposed of and a replacement token (with a new secret) is provisioned to the user.

Battery life is determined by:

- **Quiescent current** — the microcontroller's sleep-mode power draw (typically < 1 μA)
- **Active current** — power consumption during computation and LCD display (typically 1–5 mA for 30–60 seconds per activation)
- **Usage frequency** — a token used 5 times per day depletes faster than one used once per week

For a typical CR2032 cell (225 mAh), a token used 5 times per day with 30-second display windows has approximately 5–7 years of battery life — well within the typical token refresh cycle mandated by security policies.

##### 7.6.5 Software Authenticator Applications

Software authenticators have largely displaced hardware tokens for consumer and enterprise MFA due to lower cost, multi-device support, and superior user experience.

| Application | Platform | HOTP | TOTP | Cloud Backup | Key Storage |
|:------------|:---------|:----:|:----:|:------------:|:------------|
| **Google Authenticator** | iOS, Android | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (since 2023) | OS keystore |
| **Microsoft Authenticator** | iOS, Android | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (cloud backup) | OS keystore |
| **Authy** (Twilio) | iOS, Android, desktop | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (encrypted cloud) | OS keystore + Authy encryption |
| **1Password** | Multi-platform | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (1Password vault) | 1Password encrypted vault |
| **Bitwarden** | Multi-platform | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (Bitwarden vault) | Bitwarden encrypted vault |
| **andOTP** | Android | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ (local encrypted backup) | Android Keystore |
| **Aegis** | Android | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ (local encrypted backup) | Android Keystore |
| **FreeOTP+** | Android | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | Android Keystore |
| **Raivo OTP** | iOS | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (iCloud) | iOS Keychain |

##### 7.6.6 Hardware vs Software Token Comparison

| Dimension | Hardware Token | Software Authenticator |
|:----------|:--------------|:----------------------|
| **Cost per user** | $10–80 per token | Free (app) |
| **Deployment speed** | Days to weeks (procurement, shipping, provisioning) | Minutes (download, scan QR code) |
| **User convenience** | Low — separate physical device to carry | High — uses existing smartphone |
| **Multi-device support** | ❌ One token per user | ✅ Multiple devices |
| **Key extractability** | No (tamper-resistant hardware) | Depends on OS keystore security |
| **Malware resistance** | High (no OS to compromise) | Medium — mobile malware can target authenticator apps |
| **Battery dependency** | Yes (5–7 year CR2032; non-replaceable) | Uses phone battery (always charged) |
| **Physical theft risk** | High — stolen token grants OTP access | Medium — phone theft requires bypassing device lock |
| **Remote provisioning** | No — physical token must be delivered | Yes — QR code or push notification |
| **Audit trail** | No — token cannot log events | Possible — app can log generation events |
| **Regulatory acceptance** | ✅ Broadly accepted (banking, government) | ⚠️ Increasingly accepted, some sectors still prefer hardware |
| **Supply chain risk** | High — vendor has access to secrets | Low — user generates or receives secret directly |
| **Lifecycle management** | Battery replacement = token replacement | App updates extend indefinitely |
| **Backup and recovery** | ❌ No backup — loss = re-provisioning | ✅ Cloud or local encrypted backup |

##### 7.6.7 Market Trends and Future Outlook

The OTP token market has undergone significant shifts across four distinct eras:

| Era | Dominant Technology | Key Driver |
|:----|:-------------------|:-----------|
| 2000–2010 | Hardware HOTP tokens (RSA SecurID, Vasco) | Enterprise regulation; PCI DSS mandate for 2FA |
| 2010–2018 | Transition to TOTP software authenticators | Smartphone ubiquity; Google Authenticator launch (2010) |
| 2018–2024 | TOTP dominant; FIDO2/WebAuthn emerging | FIDO Alliance push; passwordless initiative |
| 2024–present | FIDO2/WebAuthn primary; TOTP fallback; HOTP niche | Passkeys adoption; phishing-resistant MFA |

**HOTP's remaining market niches (2026):**

1. **Military and government** — policies mandating hardware tokens without network connectivity
2. **Air-gapped industrial systems** — SCADA, ICS environments where timekeeping is unreliable
3. **Legacy enterprise** — large installed bases of RSA SecurID or similar tokens not yet migrated
4. **Compliance-driven deployments** — regulations specifying "hardware token" requirements

Hardware OTP token authentication market size was estimated at approximately $600–800 million in 2025 (Mordor Intelligence), with growth driven primarily by government and financial sector demand. The market is expected to gradually decline as FIDO2/WebAuthn passkeys displace OTP-based authentication for most use cases.

##### 7.6.8 HOTP Performance Characteristics

HOTP computation is extremely lightweight, making it suitable for resource-constrained devices:

| Metric | Value | Notes |
|:-------|:------|:------|
| **HMAC-SHA-1 computation time** | < 1 μs on modern CPU; ~10 μs on embedded ARM Cortex-M0 | Negligible compared to network latency |
| **Memory requirement** | < 2 KB (key + counter + HMAC state + output buffer) | Fits comfortably on 8-bit microcontrollers |
| **Flash/storage requirement** | ~4–8 KB (HMAC + SHA-1 implementation) | Minimal footprint for embedded deployment |
| **Battery impact (hardware token)** | ~1–5 mAh per activation (30-second display) | CR2032 cell (225 mAh) supports 5–7 years at 5 activations/day |
| **Server verification time** | < 1 ms per user (including look-ahead window search) | Linear in window size; database lookup is the bottleneck |

**Protocol comparison:**

| Metric | HOTP | TOTP | FIDO2/WebAuthn |
|:-------|:-----|:-----|:---------------|
| Client computation | ~1 μs | ~1 μs | ~5–50 ms (ECDSA/EdDSA) |
| Server computation | ~1 ms | ~1 ms | ~5–20 ms (signature verification) |
| Network dependency | None | None (time-synced) | None |
| User interaction | 1 button press | None (auto-refresh) | 1 tap + biometric/PIN |
| Phishing resistance | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (origin-bound) |

##### 7.6.9 HOTP for IoT and Embedded Systems

Internet of Things (IoT) devices present unique authentication challenges where HOTP's simplicity is an advantage:

| IoT Challenge | HOTP Advantage | Limitation |
|:--------------|:---------------|:-----------|
| **No real-time clock** | HOTP requires no time synchronisation — ideal for devices without RTC | Counter management requires persistent non-volatile storage |
| **Constrained resources** | HMAC-SHA-1 implementation fits in < 8 KB flash, < 2 KB RAM | SHA-256 would be preferred but requires ~2× more resources |
| **Intermittent connectivity** | OTP generation is fully offline | Counter desynchronisation risk increases with long offline periods |
| **Firmware update challenges** | Simple algorithm — minimal maintenance burden | Key rotation requires secure firmware update channel |
| **No user interface** | OTP can be transmitted over serial, BLE, or NFC | Requires a trusted relay to send OTP to the verifier |

**Recommended approach for IoT HOTP deployment:** Provision device-specific secrets during manufacturing (analogous to hardware token provisioning); use large look-ahead windows ($s = 20$–$50$) to tolerate extended offline periods; implement watchdog-based resynchronisation; store the counter in EEPROM or flash with wear-levelling; combine HOTP with TLS mutual authentication for defence-in-depth.

##### 7.6.10 HOTP as Backup Factor for Passkey-First Architectures

In "passkey-first" authentication architectures (e.g., Google's passwordless-by-default approach), HOTP serves as a critical backup factor for account recovery:

1. **Primary authentication:** FIDO2/WebAuthn passkey (phishing-resistant, biometric-activated)
2. **Fallback authentication:** TOTP or HOTP (software or hardware token)
3. **Account recovery:** Recovery codes + HOTP token for re-provisioning passkeys

HOTP hardware tokens are particularly well-suited for this backup role because they are independent of the user's smartphone (which may be lost alongside passkeys), provide a durable, long-lived credential that survives device replacement, and satisfy regulatory requirements for a "possession factor" independent of the primary device.

For detailed coverage of hardware token form factors, security key architectures (YubiKey, Titan, SoloKeys), and smart card integration, see §14 (Token Form Factor Taxonomy).

---

### 8. TOTP: Time-Based One-Time Password (RFC 6238)

TOTP — Time-Based One-Time Password — is the dominant one-time password algorithm used by software authenticator apps worldwide. Defined in RFC 6238 (May 2011) as an extension of HOTP (§7), TOTP replaces the monotonic event counter with a time-derived counter, producing codes that expire automatically after a fixed interval. This single architectural change eliminates HOTP's counter synchronisation problem (§7.3) and makes stolen codes short-lived — transforming OTP from a stateful protocol into a nearly stateless one.

TOTP satisfies **AAL1** when used as a single factor. Combined with a memorised secret (password), TOTP achieves **AAL2** (§1.1). Like HOTP, TOTP alone can never reach AAL3 — OTP schemes lack the hardware-bound keys and verifier impersonation resistance that AAL3 requires.

The core relationship is trivially expressed:

```
TOTP(K) = HOTP(K, T)
```

Where `T` is a time-derived counter value rather than an event-driven counter. Everything below this substitution — the HMAC computation, dynamic truncation, and modular reduction — is identical to the HOTP pipeline described in §7.1.

**NIST AAL mapping for TOTP:**

| AAL | Requirement | TOTP Role | Example Configuration |
|:---:|:-----------|:---------|:---------------------|
| **AAL1** | Single-factor authentication | TOTP alone qualifies as a single-factor authenticator | User enters only TOTP code (no password) — uncommon in practice |
| **AAL2** | Two-factor authentication using two independent authenticator types | TOTP + password (or TOTP + another factor) | TOTP serves as the "something you have" factor, password serves as "something you know" |
| **AAL3** | Hardware-bound, phishing-resistant multi-factor authentication | TOTP **cannot** satisfy AAL3 | TOTP lacks the hardware-bound key requirement and origin binding that AAL3 mandates |

NIST SP 800-63B §5.2.5 (2024 revision) requires verifiers to offer at least one **phishing-resistant** authentication option at AAL2. This means TOTP, while satisfying the technical definition of AAL2 when combined with a password, does **not** satisfy the regulatory requirement for AAL2 in federal contexts — federal agencies must offer FIDO2 or device-bound passkeys alongside TOTP (NIST SP 800-63B, §5.2.5; OMB M-22-09).

#### 8.1 Algorithm Specification

##### 8.1.1 Time Step Counter Derivation (RFC 6238 §4)

The TOTP algorithm converts the current time into a counter value suitable for the HOTP function:

```
T = floor((Current_Unix_Time - T0) / X)
```

Where:
- **Current_Unix_Time** — seconds elapsed since the Unix epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC)
- **T0** — the Unix time from which to start counting time steps (default: 0, the Unix epoch itself)
- **X** — the time step duration in seconds (default: 30)
- **T** — the resulting integer counter value, used as the HOTP counter `C`

The counter `T` is encoded as an 8-byte big-endian unsigned integer — identical to the HOTP counter encoding (§7.1.1).

**Example at a specific point in time:**

```
Current time:     2025-03-25 14:30:00 UTC
Unix timestamp:   1742912600
T0:               0
X:                30

T = floor((1742912600 - 0) / 30) = floor(58097086.67) = 58097086

Encoded as 8-byte big-endian: 0x0000000003769DDE
```

Every device computing TOTP with the same shared secret at the same moment in time — regardless of geographic location — produces the same code. This is the fundamental property that makes TOTP work without any communication between client and server during authentication.

##### 8.1.2 Complete Algorithm Steps

The full TOTP computation for a given instant:

1. **Obtain the current Unix time** in seconds (UTC). The time source must be accurate — see §8.3 for synchronisation requirements
2. **Compute the time step counter:** `T = floor((unix_time - T0) / X)`
3. **Encode `T` as an 8-byte big-endian integer** — this becomes the HOTP counter value
4. **Compute `HOTP(K, T)`** using the algorithm described in §7.1:
   - a. `HS = HMAC-Hash(K, T)` — where Hash is SHA-1, SHA-256, or SHA-512
   - b. Dynamic truncation: extract a 4-byte segment from `HS` using the offset-based method (§7.1.2)
   - c. Modular reduction: `TOTP = truncated_value mod 10^d`
5. **Left-pad with zeros** to ensure exactly `d` digits

<details><summary><strong>Mathematical foundation: HMAC construction and dynamic truncation</strong></summary>

**HMAC construction (RFC 2104):**

$$\text{HMAC}(K, m) = H\!\left((K' \oplus \text{opad}) \;\|\; H\!\left((K' \oplus \text{ipad}) \;\|\; m\right)\right)$$

Where $H$ is the underlying hash function, $K'$ is the key padded to the hash block size $B$ (zero-padded if shorter, hashed first if longer), $\text{ipad} = \text{0x36}$ repeated $B$ times (inner padding), and $\text{opad} = \text{0x5C}$ repeated $B$ times (outer padding).

| Hash Function | Block Size ($B$) | Output Size | Key Pre-processing |
|:-------------|:----------------|:------------|:------------------|
| SHA-1 | 64 bytes | 20 bytes | Keys < 64 bytes: zero-padded; keys > 64 bytes: hashed first |
| SHA-256 | 64 bytes | 32 bytes | Same block handling as SHA-1 |
| SHA-512 | 128 bytes | 64 bytes | Keys < 128 bytes: zero-padded; keys > 128 bytes: hashed first |

**Dynamic truncation algorithm (DT):**

1. Compute $HS = \text{HMAC}(K, C)$ — the raw HMAC byte string (20, 32, or 64 bytes)
2. Extract the offset: $\text{offset} = HS[\text{len}(HS) - 1] \;\&\; 0x0F$ — low 4 bits of the last byte, giving a value in $[0, 15]$
3. Extract 4 bytes at the offset: $S = HS[\text{offset}..\text{offset}+3]$ (big-endian)
4. Mask the most significant bit: $S' = S \;\&\; 0x7FFFFFFF$ — ensures the result is always a positive 31-bit unsigned integer
5. Compute the final code: $\text{OTP} = S' \bmod 10^d$ — where $d$ is the desired number of digits
6. Left-pad with zeros to ensure exactly $d$ digits

The mask in step 4 is critical — it clears the most significant bit of the 4-byte integer, ensuring the result is always non-negative regardless of the HMAC output. This limits the code space to $10^d$ values (for $d = 6$: $10^6 = 1{,}000{,}000$ possible codes).

</details>

##### 8.1.3 Extended Hash Function Support (RFC 6238 §1.2)

RFC 6238 extends the HOTP algorithm beyond HMAC-SHA-1 to support two additional hash functions:

| Hash Function | HMAC Output Size | Recommended Key Length | Notes |
|:-------------|:----------------|:----------------------|:------|
| **HMAC-SHA-1** | 20 bytes (160 bits) | 20 bytes (160 bits) | Mandatory — same as HOTP (RFC 4226). Remains the default for backward compatibility |
| **HMAC-SHA-256** | 32 bytes (256 bits) | 32 bytes (256 bits) | Optional — provides a larger HMAC output, but the dynamic truncation still extracts only 4 bytes; security benefit is marginal for 6-digit codes |
| **HMAC-SHA-512** | 64 bytes (512 bits) | 64 bytes (512 bits) | Optional — largest HMAC output; key must be correspondingly longer to fully utilise the hash capacity |

The recommended key length matches the hash function's output size (RFC 6238 §5.1). Using a shorter key is permitted but wastes the hash function's capacity — the HMAC construction zero-pads keys shorter than the hash block size. Using a longer key provides no additional security — HMAC hashes keys exceeding the block size before use.

**Practical reality:** Despite RFC 6238 defining SHA-256 and SHA-512 support, the overwhelming majority of deployed TOTP tokens use **HMAC-SHA-1**. Google Authenticator, the application that defined the de facto provisioning standard (§8.5), historically supported only SHA-1. While modern authenticator apps generally support all three hash functions, many server-side implementations default to SHA-1 for maximum interoperability. The dynamic truncation step extracts the same 31-bit value regardless of the HMAC output size, so the brute-force resistance of the final 6-digit code is identical across all three hash functions — `10⁻⁶` per guess.

##### 8.1.4 RFC 6238 Appendix B: Test Vectors

RFC 6238 Appendix B provides test vectors across all three hash functions. The test vectors use **8-digit** TOTP codes (unlike the typical 6-digit production deployment) and a time step of 30 seconds.

**Shared secret keys (per erratum 2866):**

| Hash Function | Secret (ASCII) | Key Length |
|:-------------|:--------------|:-----------|
| SHA-1 | `12345678901234567890` | 20 bytes |
| SHA-256 | `12345678901234567890123456789012` | 32 bytes |
| SHA-512 | `1234567890123456789012345678901234567890123456789012345678901234` | 64 bytes |

**Note on erratum 2866:** The original RFC 6238 text implied all three hash functions use the same 20-byte secret. Erratum 2866 (verified) clarifies that the SHA-256 and SHA-512 test vectors use longer secrets matching their respective recommended key lengths. Implementations that fail to account for this discrepancy will produce incorrect test vector results for SHA-256 and SHA-512.

**Test vector results (8-digit TOTP, X = 30, T0 = 0):**

| Unix Time (seconds) | UTC Date/Time | Time Step (T, hex) | TOTP SHA-1 | TOTP SHA-256 | TOTP SHA-512 |
|:---------------------|:-------------|:-------------------|:-----------|:-------------|:-------------|
| 59 | 1970-01-01 00:00:59 | `0000000000000001` | **94287082** | **46119246** | **90693936** |
| 1111111109 | 2005-03-18 01:58:29 | `00000000023523EC` | **07081804** | **68084774** | **25091201** |
| 1111111111 | 2005-03-18 01:58:31 | `00000000023523ED` | **14050471** | **67062674** | **99943326** |
| 1234567890 | 2009-02-13 23:31:30 | `000000000273EF07` | **89005924** | **91819424** | **93441116** |
| 2000000000 | 2033-05-18 03:33:20 | `0000000003F940AA` | **69279037** | **90698825** | **38618901** |
| 20000000000 | 2603-10-11 11:33:20 | `0000000027BC86AA` | **65353130** | **77737706** | **47863826** |

These test vectors serve as the canonical implementation verification suite for TOTP — any implementation that produces different values for these inputs is incorrect. The final row (Unix time 20000000000, year 2603) tests that implementations correctly handle counter values requiring more than 32 bits, exercising the full 8-byte big-endian encoding.

**Cross-validation with HOTP:** The first row (Unix time = 59, time step T = 1) should produce the same result as `HOTP(K, 1)` with the SHA-1 secret key. Comparing with the HOTP test vectors in §7.1.4: HOTP at counter 1 produces **287082** (6 digits). The TOTP test vector at T = 1 produces **94287082** (8 digits). The last 6 digits match — `287082` — confirming that TOTP is HOTP with a time-derived counter.

**Notable observations from the test vectors:**

- **Rows 2 and 3** differ by only 2 seconds of Unix time but produce entirely different codes. This is because $1111111109 / 30 = 37037037.0$ (step ends exactly there) and $1111111111 / 30 = 37037037.033\ldots$ (new step), confirming the floor function boundary behaviour
- **Row 6** (Unix time 20,000,000,000, year 2603) exercises the full 64-bit counter encoding. The counter value $C = \text{0x27BC86AA}$ requires more than 32 bits, testing implementations that might truncate the counter to 4 bytes
- **Row 1** ($C = 1$) cross-validates with HOTP: the last 6 digits of the 8-digit TOTP (`287082`) match `HOTP(K, 1)` from RFC 4226

#### 8.2 Time Step Selection

The time step `X` determines how long each TOTP code remains valid. RFC 6238 §5.2 recommends a default of **30 seconds** but permits implementations to choose other values.

##### 8.2.1 Why 30 Seconds Is the Standard

The 30-second time step represents a carefully chosen balance between security and usability:

| Consideration | Favours Shorter Steps | Favours Longer Steps |
|:-------------|:---------------------|:--------------------|
| **Code validity window** | Shorter validity → less time for an attacker to relay a captured code | Longer validity → user has more time to read, switch context, and enter the code |
| **User transcription time** | — | Average user takes 5–10 seconds to read a 6-digit code from a phone and type it into a form on another device |
| **Network and processing latency** | — | Server must receive and validate the code before the time step expires; network latency, server-side processing, and potential retransmission consume additional seconds |
| **Clock drift tolerance** | Shorter steps amplify the effect of clock drift — a 2-second clock skew consumes 20% of a 10-second window but only 7% of a 30-second window | — |
| **Replay window** | Shorter validity → codes are replayable for less time | — |

At 30 seconds, a user has approximately 15–25 seconds of usable time (accounting for the random position within the current step when the code is displayed, plus a few seconds of transcription). This is sufficient for the vast majority of users in the vast majority of network conditions.

##### 8.2.2 Alternative Time Steps in Practice

| Time Step | Deployed By | Rationale |
|:----------|:-----------|:----------|
| **30 seconds** | Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator, Authy, most implementations | De facto standard; maximum interoperability |
| **60 seconds** | Some enterprise deployments; historically RSA SecurID used 60-second intervals | Provides a more comfortable entry window; halves the server's per-user code generation rate for look-ahead validation |
| **10 seconds** | Rare; some high-security research prototypes | Minimises the replay window but creates acute usability problems — users frequently fail to enter the code before it rotates |
| **90 seconds** | Rare; some SMS-linked fallback systems | Accommodates SMS delivery delays (which can exceed 30 seconds in congested networks) |

**Very short time steps (< 15 seconds) are impractical** for human-transcribed TOTP. The user must: (1) look at their phone, (2) read 6 digits, (3) switch to the login form, (4) type the digits, (5) submit the form, (6) wait for server validation. This pipeline reliably takes 8–15 seconds even for fast typists. A 10-second step means the code may rotate mid-entry, requiring the user to start over — producing frustration and increased support burden from "my code doesn't work" complaints.

#### 8.3 Time Synchronisation and Clock Drift

TOTP's critical dependency is mutual agreement on the current time between the client (authenticator app or hardware token) and the server (verifier). Unlike HOTP, where desynchronisation results from user behaviour (§7.3.1), TOTP desynchronisation results from **clock drift** — the gradual divergence of a device's internal clock from the true time.

##### 8.3.1 Time Sources by Device Type

| Device Type | Primary Time Source | Typical Drift | Notes |
|:-----------|:-------------------|:-------------|:------|
| **Smartphone (Android/iOS)** | NITZ (Network Identity and Time Zone) from cellular carrier; NTP (Network Time Protocol) from OS-level time sync | < 1 second | Smartphones maintain excellent time accuracy because they synchronise continuously via cellular networks and NTP. Drift is negligible for TOTP purposes |
| **Desktop/laptop (connected)** | NTP via OS time service (systemd-timesyncd, Windows Time Service, chronyd) | < 1 second (when NTP is active) | Standard NTP achieves sub-second accuracy; drift is only a concern if NTP is disabled or the NTP server is unreachable |
| **Hardware TOTP token** | Internal crystal oscillator | ±0.5 ppm → ~15.8 seconds/year | Hardware tokens lack network connectivity and rely solely on their internal oscillator. A typical ±0.5 ppm crystal accumulates ~16 seconds of drift per year. After 3 years of deployment without correction, drift may reach ~48 seconds — exceeding the ±30-second tolerance window |
| **Air-gapped system** | Manual time setting or GPS receiver | Variable — depends on how frequently time is calibrated | Systems without NTP access require periodic manual calibration or a GPS time source |

**Clock drift mathematical model:**

$$T_{\text{device}}(t) = T_{\text{true}}(t) + \Delta_0 + \alpha \cdot t$$

Where $\Delta_0$ is the initial time offset at deployment (calibration error), $\alpha$ is the drift rate in seconds per second (typically expressed in ppm), and $t$ is elapsed time since deployment. The time to desynchronisation — the time $t_{\text{fail}}$ until drift exceeds a tolerance window of $n$ steps — is:

$$t_{\text{fail}} = \frac{n \cdot X - \lvert \Delta_0 \rvert}{\lvert \alpha \rvert}$$

For a hardware token with $\alpha = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ (1 ppm), $X = 30\text{s}$, $n = 1$ (±30 s tolerance), and $\Delta_0 = 0$: $t_{\text{fail}} = 30{,}000{,}000\text{s} \approx 347\text{ days}$. A typical 1 ppm hardware token fails within approximately one year without drift compensation. At 2 ppm, the failure time drops to ~174 days.

**Drift sources — systematic vs. discrete:**

| Source | Type | Mechanism | Typical Magnitude |
|:-------|:-----|:---------|:-----------------|
| Crystal oscillator frequency error | Systematic | Manufacturing tolerance of the quartz crystal | ±0.5–2.0 ppm (±16–63 s/year) |
| Temperature coefficient | Systematic | Crystal frequency varies parabolically with ambient temperature (AT-cut crystals) | ±2–10 ppm over −20°C to +70°C range |
| Crystal aging | Systematic | Logarithmic aging curve; highest drift in first year, decreasing over time | 1–3 ppm first year |
| Battery depletion (hardware tokens) | Systematic | Low battery voltage affects crystal oscillation frequency | 5–20 ppm |
| Timezone change | Discrete | OS updates timezone; authenticator uses UTC internally (correct implementations) | 0 s (if UTC is used) |
| Manual time adjustment | Discrete | User manually sets device clock | Arbitrary |
| NTP step correction | Discrete | NTP daemon applies a step correction (> 128 ms threshold) | Up to several seconds |

> ⚠️ **Critical implementation rule:** TOTP implementations MUST use UTC (Unix time) internally, never local time. Timezone changes and DST transitions must not affect the time step counter computation. This is a common source of implementation bugs — particularly on embedded systems where the RTC may store local time.

**Non-linear drift effects:** Real crystal oscillators deviate from the linear model. Temperature-dependent drift follows a parabolic curve (seasonal swings of ±20°C can add ±2–5 ppm). Crystal aging follows a logarithmic curve (highest in year one, decreasing). Mechanical shocks can cause instantaneous frequency jumps of 0.5–5 ppm. Conservative deployment planning should use $\alpha = 2\text{ ppm}$ even for specifications claiming 0.5 ppm.

##### 8.3.2 Server-Side Drift Tolerance (Look-Behind and Look-Ahead)

To accommodate clock drift, the server validates the submitted code not only against the current time step `T` but also against adjacent time steps:

```
function verify_totp(user, submitted_code):
    T = floor(current_unix_time / X)
    for i in range(-n, n + 1):          // n = number of tolerance steps
        expected = HOTP(K, T + i)
        if submitted_code == expected:
            record_drift_offset(user, i)  // track observed drift for future compensation
            record_last_used_step(user, T + i)  // for replay prevention (§8.4)
            return ACCEPT
    return REJECT
```

**Tolerance window sizing:**

| Tolerance (n) | Acceptance Window | Total Valid Steps | Security Impact |
|:-------------|:-----------------|:-----------------|:----------------|
| `n = 0` | Current step only (0–30 seconds) | 1 | Maximum security; fails on any clock drift |
| `n = 1` | ±1 step (previous + current + next = 0–90 seconds) | 3 | **Standard default** — tolerates ±30 seconds of drift; probability of random guess: `3/10⁶` |
| `n = 2` | ±2 steps (0–150 seconds) | 5 | Accommodates hardware tokens with moderate drift; `5/10⁶` per guess |
| `n = 5` | ±5 steps (0–330 seconds) | 11 | Very permissive; appropriate only for tokens with known high drift; `11/10⁶` per guess |

The standard configuration of `n = 1` (accept the current step, the previous step, and the next step) provides a 90-second acceptance window. This accommodates the worst-case scenario of a user generating a code in the final second of a time step and submitting it several seconds later (when the server has already advanced to the next step), as well as minor clock drift in either direction.

**Security impact of tolerance window expansion:**

Each additional tolerance step linearly increases the attacker's advantage in an online brute-force attack. The probability of a random 6-digit code being accepted is:

$$P_{\text{guess}} = \frac{2n + 1}{10^d}$$

| Tolerance ($n$) | Valid Steps | $P_{\text{guess}}$ | Security Reduction vs. $n=0$ |
|:---------------:|:-----------:|:------------------:|:-----------------------------:|
| 0 | 1 | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ | Baseline |
| 1 | 3 | $3 \times 10^{-6}$ | 3× more permissive |
| 2 | 5 | $5 \times 10^{-6}$ | 5× more permissive |
| 5 | 11 | $1.1 \times 10^{-5}$ | 11× more permissive |
| 10 | 21 | $2.1 \times 10^{-5}$ | 21× more permissive |

The security impact is modest in absolute terms — even at $n = 5$, the per-guess probability is still only $1.1 \times 10^{-5}$. However, the linear relationship means that tolerance should be kept to the minimum required for reliable operation. The effective replay window (the period during which a captured code can be reused) is $(2n + 1) \times X$ seconds — for $n = 1$, $X = 30$: $T_{\text{valid}} = 90$ seconds. This makes server-side replay prevention (§8.4) essential.

##### 8.3.3 Progressive Drift Compensation

A sophisticated implementation can track and compensate for systematic clock drift:

1. During each successful authentication, the server records which time step offset `i` (from the tolerance window) produced the match
2. If a user consistently authenticates with `i = -1` (always matching the previous step), the server infers that the client's clock is running approximately 30 seconds slow
3. The server stores this **drift offset** and applies it when computing the expected time steps for that user — shifting the tolerance window to centre on the observed drift rather than on `T`
4. This allows the server to maintain tight security (small tolerance window) while accommodating progressive drift in hardware tokens

```mermaid
---
config:
  themeVariables:
    noteBkgColor: "transparent"
    noteBorderColor: "transparent"
    actorMargin: 250
  sequence:
    messageAlign: "left"
    noteAlign: "left"
---
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant C as Hardware Token
    participant S as Authentication Server
    
    rect rgba(148, 163, 184, 0.14)
    Note over C,S: Initial Login (Token clock is +45s fast)
    C->>C: Compute initial TOTP
    Note over C: Read internal clock (+45s drift)<br/>Generate code for T_client
    C->>S: Scan the tolerance window
    Note over S: Check T_server (Fail)<br/>Check T_server - 1 (Fail)<br/>Check T_server + 1 (Match!)
    S->>S: Record the drift shift
    Note over S: Update user profile: Drift Offset = +1
    Note over S,C: Authentication Successful
    Note right of S: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end
    
    rect rgba(46, 204, 113, 0.14)
    Note over C,S: Subsequent Login
    C->>C: Compute subsequent TOTP
    Note over C: Read internal clock (+46s drift)<br/>Compute TOTP for T_client
    C->>S: Apply drift compensation
    Note over S: Apply Drift Offset (+1)<br/>Base T_check = T_server + 1<br/>Check T_check (Match on first attempt!)
    Note over S,C: Authentication Successful
    Note right of S: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end
```

<details><summary><strong>1. Hardware Token computes initial TOTP</strong></summary>

The hardware token's internal crystal oscillator has drifted by +45 seconds over several years of use. When the user presses the button, the token computes the TOTP code based on this fast clock, effectively generating the code for sequence `T+1` ahead of the server's true time.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>2. Authentication Server scans the tolerance window</strong></summary>

The server receives the code and primarily checks it against its own current time `T_server`. This fails. The server then checks the backward tolerance `T_server - 1` (fails) and the forward tolerance `T_server + 1` (which succeeds).

</details>

<details><summary><strong>3. Authentication Server records the drift shift</strong></summary>

Upon finding a match at the `+1` window position, the server does not merely accept the token. It updates the user's permanent credential record, setting their `drift_offset` property to `+1`. 

</details>

<details><summary><strong>4. Hardware Token computes subsequent TOTP</strong></summary>

At a later date, the user logs in again. The token is still ~45 seconds fast. It computes the code based on its internal drifted clock.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>5. Authentication Server applies drift compensation</strong></summary>

Instead of starting the check at the true `T_server` time, the server retrieves the user's `drift_offset` (+1) and makes `T_server + 1` the baseline for the check. The first attempt matches instantly. This prevents the token from failing entirely once its drift eventually exceeds the standard ±1 tolerance window (e.g. drifting to +90 seconds will just shift the drift offset to +3 over time).

</details>

<br/>

This compensation technique is particularly important for hardware TOTP tokens deployed for multiple years. A token accumulating 15 seconds of drift per year reaches 45 seconds of drift after 3 years — outside the ±30-second default tolerance. Without drift compensation, the token would stop working. With drift compensation, the server tracks the gradual shift and adjusts automatically.

**Comprehensive drift compensation algorithm** — the following pseudocode extends the simplified `verify_totp()` (§8.3.2) with a ring-buffer drift history, consistency check, and emergency recalibration fallback:

```
// Persistent per-user state:
//   drift_offset: integer (default 0)
//   last_used_step: integer (default 0)
//   drift_history: ring buffer of recent offsets (size 5)

function verify_totp_with_compensation(user, submitted_code):
    K = lookup_secret(user)
    T_server = floor(current_unix_time / X)
    drift = get_drift_offset(user)        // persistent per-user drift offset
    
    // Check the adjusted window centred on T_server + drift
    for i in range(-1, 2):                // always use n=1 around the compensated centre
        candidate = T_server + drift + i
        expected = HOTP(K, candidate)
        if submitted_code == expected:
            if candidate <= get_last_used_step(user):
                return REJECT              // replay attempt
            set_last_used_step(user, candidate)
            
            // Update drift tracking
            observed_offset = (candidate - T_server)
            add_to_drift_history(user, observed_offset)
            
            // Only update drift_offset if recent history is consistent
            if drift_history_is_consistent(user, threshold=1):
                new_drift = median(get_drift_history(user))
                set_drift_offset(user, new_drift)
            
            return ACCEPT
    
    // If standard window fails, fall back to wider scan
    for i in range(-5, 6):                // emergency: n=5
        if i >= -1 and i <= 1:
            continue                       // already checked
        candidate = T_server + i
        expected = HOTP(K, candidate)
        if submitted_code == expected:
            if candidate <= get_last_used_step(user):
                return REJECT
            set_last_used_step(user, candidate)
            set_drift_offset(user, candidate - T_server)  // emergency recalibration
            return ACCEPT
    
    return REJECT
```

**Drift history consistency check:** The algorithm only updates the persistent drift offset when the recent authentication history shows consistent drift. If the last 5 authentications show offsets of [+1, +1, +1, +1, +1], the median is +1 and the history is consistent (standard deviation = 0). If the offsets are [+1, 0, −1, +2, −1], the standard deviation exceeds the threshold and the drift offset is not updated — this indicates inconsistent drift (possibly the user is travelling between time zones or the device clock was manually adjusted).

**Emergency recalibration:** If the standard window fails but a code matches in the wider ±5 window, the server performs emergency recalibration — setting the drift offset to the observed value. This handles the case where a device's clock has drifted past the standard tolerance window suddenly (e.g., due to battery degradation in a hardware token or a manual clock adjustment on a phone).

##### 8.3.4 Multi-Device Synchronisation Challenges

Enterprise environments often require users to register multiple TOTP devices (e.g., a phone and a hardware backup token). Each device has its own clock, its own drift characteristics, and its own last-used time step. This creates several synchronisation challenges:

- **Per-device state management:** The server must maintain separate drift offsets and last-used time steps for each registered device. A global per-user last-used step would prevent different devices from authenticating — if Device A uses step $T$, Device B cannot use step $T$ even though it generated the code independently
- **Cross-device replay prevention:** The replay cache must be scoped per-device, not per-user, to allow multiple devices to generate and submit codes for the same time step. This weakens replay protection — an attacker who captures a code from Device A can replay it against the same device's step, but not against Device B
- **Drift divergence over time:** Different devices drift at different rates. After 6 months, Device A (phone, NTP-synced) may have zero drift while Device B (hardware token, 1 ppm) has accumulated ~16 seconds of drift. The server's drift compensation must track each device independently
- **Secret distribution:** Adding a new device requires re-provisioning the same shared secret. If the provisioning channel is compromised (e.g., a phishing page tricks the user into scanning a QR code for the attacker's secret), the attacker gains a valid TOTP device. Mitigations include requiring the existing TOTP device to authorise the new registration, using the provisioning ceremony as a re-enrolment (generating a new secret for all devices), or out-of-band verification (sending a confirmation link to the registered email)

##### 8.3.5 Drift Tolerance Strategies

The following table provides a comprehensive comparison of drift tolerance strategies:

| # | Strategy | Description | Tolerance Range | Security Impact | Best For |
|:-:|:---------|:------------|:---------------|:---------------|:---------|
| 1 | **Fixed window (n=1)** | Accept current, previous, and next time step | ±30 s | Minimal — 3 valid steps | Smartphone authenticator apps (NTP-synced) |
