|:-----------------|:-----------|:-------------------|
| `purpose` | Permitted key operations (SIGN, ENCRYPT, DECRYPT, VERIFY) | Confirm the key is restricted to its intended use (e.g., signing only) |
| `algorithm` | Cryptographic algorithm (RSA, EC, AES, HMAC) | Verify the key uses the expected algorithm |
| `keySize` | Key size in bits | Confirm minimum key size requirements |
| `digest` | Permitted hash algorithms (SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512) | Verify acceptable digest algorithms |
| `origin` | Where the key was generated — `GENERATED` (inside hardware), `IMPORTED`, `UNKNOWN` | **Critical:** confirm `origin == GENERATED` to ensure the key was created inside the hardware boundary, not imported from software |
| `osVersion` | Android OS version at key generation time (format: MMmmpp — Major, minor, patch) | Enforce minimum OS version requirements |
| `osPatchLevel` | Android security patch level (format: YYYYMM) | Enforce minimum patch level — reject devices with outdated security patches |
| `attestationApplicationId` | The signing certificate hash and package name of the application that created the key | Verify the key was created by the expected application — prevents a malicious app from claiming keys generated by the legitimate app |
| `rootOfTrust` | Contains the verified boot state: `verifiedBootKey`, `deviceLocked`, `verifiedBootState` (Verified, SelfSigned, Unverified, Failed) | **Critical:** confirm `verifiedBootState == Verified` and `deviceLocked == true` — a device with an unlocked bootloader or modified boot image cannot be trusted for high-assurance attestation |

<details><summary><strong>Full ASN.1 Schema: KeyDescription and AuthorizationList</strong></summary>

```asn1
KeyDescription ::= SEQUENCE {
    attestationVersion          INTEGER,           -- currently 3 or 4
    attestationSecurityLevel    [0] ENUMERATED,    -- Software/TrustedEnvironment/StrongBox
    keymasterVersion            INTEGER,           -- Keymaster HAL version
    keymasterSecurityLevel      [1] ENUMERATED,
    attestationChallenge        OCTET STRING,      -- server-provided nonce
    uniqueId                    OCTET STRING,      -- deprecated, empty in v3+
    softwareEnforced            AuthorizationList,
    teeEnforced                 AuthorizationList,  -- or StrongBox-enforced
}

AuthorizationList ::= SEQUENCE {
    purpose                     [1] SEQUENCE OF INTEGER,
    algorithm                   [2] INTEGER,
    keySize                     [3] INTEGER,
    digest                      [4] SEQUENCE OF INTEGER,
    padding                     [5] SEQUENCE OF INTEGER,
    ecCurve                     [6] INTEGER,
    rsaPublicExponent           [7] INTEGER,
    rollbackResistance          [8] NULL OPTIONAL,
    activeDateTime              [10] INTEGER OPTIONAL,
    originationExpireDateTime   [11] INTEGER OPTIONAL,
    usageExpireDateTime         [12] INTEGER OPTIONAL,
    nonce                       [13] OCTET STRING OPTIONAL,
    applicationId               [600] OCTET STRING OPTIONAL,
    creationDateTime            [701] INTEGER OPTIONAL,
    origin                      [702] INTEGER OPTIONAL,
    rollbackResistant           [703] NULL OPTIONAL,
    rootOfTrust                 [704] RootOfTrust OPTIONAL,
    osVersion                   [705] INTEGER OPTIONAL,
    osPatchLevel                [706] INTEGER OPTIONAL,
    attestationApplicationId     [709] AttestationApplicationId OPTIONAL,
    attestationIdBrand          [710] OCTET STRING OPTIONAL,
    attestationIdDevice         [711] OCTET STRING OPTIONAL,
    attestationIdProduct        [712] OCTET STRING OPTIONAL,
    attestationIdSerial         [713] OCTET STRING OPTIONAL,
    attestationIdImei           [714] OCTET STRING OPTIONAL,
    attestationIdMeid           [715] OCTET STRING OPTIONAL,
    attestationIdManufacturer   [716] OCTET STRING OPTIONAL,
    attestationIdModel          [717] OCTET STRING OPTIONAL,
    vendorPatchLevel            [718] INTEGER OPTIONAL,
    bootPatchLevel              [719] INTEGER OPTIONAL,
}

RootOfTrust ::= SEQUENCE {
    verifiedBootKey             OCTET STRING,      -- hash of the verification key
    deviceLocked                BOOLEAN,           -- bootloader lock state
    verifiedBootState           ENUMERATED,        -- Verified/Unverified/Failed
    verifiedBootHash            OCTET STRING       -- hash of VBMeta
}
```

This complete schema includes all `AuthorizationList` tags (including the ID attestation fields `attestationIdBrand` through `attestationIdModel`, available only to device owner apps) and the `vendorPatchLevel`/`bootPatchLevel` fields introduced in Android 12. The `teeEnforced` (or `strongBoxEnforced`) variant of the `AuthorizationList` carries the hardware-guaranteed values — a prudent verifier should trust only these fields for security decisions (Android Key Attestation, Android Developers).

</details>

##### 13.1.2.1 AuthorizationList Verification Reference

Every tag in the `AuthorizationList` carries specific security semantics. The following table provides a complete reference for server-side verification (Android Key Attestation, Android Developers):

| Tag | ASN.1 Context | Security Significance | Verification Guidance |
|:----|:-------------|:---------------------|:----------------------|
| **purpose** | [1] | Defines allowed cryptographic operations (encrypt, decrypt, sign, verify, wrap, derive) | Reject if purpose does not match expected usage (e.g., signing key should not have encrypt purpose) |
| **algorithm** | [2] | Key algorithm (RSA, EC, AES, HMAC) | Verify algorithm matches key type in certificate |
| **keySize** | [3] | Key size in bits | Reject RSA < 2048, EC < 256 for attestation-grade keys |
| **digest** | [4] | Approved digest algorithms (SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512) | Ensure SHA-1 is not listed for security-sensitive operations |
| **padding** | [5] | Approved padding modes (RSA-PKCS1, RSA-OAEP, PSS) | Verify padding is appropriate for the algorithm |
| **ecCurve** | [6] | NID of the elliptic curve | Verify P-256 or P-384 for production use |
| **rsaPublicExponent** | [7] | RSA public exponent value | Verify $e = 65537$ (0x10001) is standard |
| **rollbackResistance** | [8] | Key is anti-rollback protected | Required for high-assurance use cases |
| **activeDateTime** | [10] | Epoch milliseconds: key becomes valid | Verify key is active |
| **originationExpireDateTime** | [11] | Expiration for key generation | Verify not expired |
| **usageExpireDateTime** | [12] | Expiration for key usage | Verify not expired |
| **nonce** | [13] | Application-provided nonce at key creation | Replay detection for key provisioning events |
| **applicationId** | [600] | SHA-256 of signed APK package info | Verify matches expected app package |
| **creationDateTime** | [701] | Key creation timestamp (epoch ms) | Audit logging, not a security gate |
| **origin** | [702] | `GENERATED` (0) vs `IMPORTED` (1) vs `SECURELY_IMPORTED` (2) | Ensure `origin == GENERATED` for attestation trust |
| **rollbackResistant** | [703] | Key material stored in anti-rollback storage | Duplicate of [8], check either |
| **rootOfTrust** | [704] | Boot chain integrity state | **Critical:** verify `deviceLocked == true` and `verifiedBootState == Verified` |
| **osVersion** | [705] | Major.Minor.Patch (e.g., 12000000 = 12.0.0) | Reject below minimum supported version |
| **osPatchLevel** | [706] | YYYY-MM (e.g., 202603 = March 2026) | Reject if more than 90 days behind current |
| **attestationApplicationId** | [709] | Structured app identity (package names, signatures) | Verify package names and signing certificate digests |
| **vendorPatchLevel** | [718] | Vendor firmware patch level (Android 12+) | Verify within acceptable window |
| **bootPatchLevel** | [719] | Bootloader patch level (Android 12+) | Verify within acceptable window |

Fields present in `teeEnforced` (or `StrongBox-enforced`) are guaranteed by hardware. Fields present in `softwareEnforced` are asserted by the Android OS and can be forged by a rooted device — a prudent verifier should trust only `teeEnforced` fields for security decisions. The ID attestation fields (`attestationIdBrand` through `attestationIdModel`, tags [710]–[717]) contain device-identifying information and are populated only when the calling app holds the device owner privilege; RPs should avoid persisting these values due to privacy implications.

##### 13.1.2.2 Server-Side Verification Algorithm

The following pseudo-code outlines the complete verification process for Android key attestation. RPs should implement this as a dedicated verification service (Android Key Attestation, Android Developers) rather than inlining validation in business logic:

```
function verifyAndroidKeyAttestation(certChain, challenge, expectedAppPackage):
    // Step 1: Verify certificate chain to Google root
    rootCert = loadTrustedRoot("google_hardware_attestation_root")
    leafCert = certChain[0]
    intermediateCerts = certChain[1..-1]
    verifyChain(leafCert, intermediateCerts, rootCert)

    // Step 2: Extract and parse KeyDescription extension
    ext = leafCert.getExtension("1.3.6.1.4.1.11129.2.1.17")
    kd = parseASN1_KeyDescription(ext.value)

    // Step 3: Verify challenge (nonce) — prevents replay
    if kd.attestationChallenge != challenge:
        return REJECT("challenge mismatch")

    // Step 4: Verify attestation version
    if kd.attestationVersion < 3:
        return REJECT("attestation version too old")

    // Step 5: Verify security level — require hardware backing
    if kd.attestationSecurityLevel < TRUSTED_ENVIRONMENT:
        return REJECT("attestation not hardware-backed")

    // Step 6: Verify root of trust (teeEnforced only)
    rot = kd.teeEnforced.rootOfTrust
    if rot == null:
        return REJECT("no rootOfTrust in teeEnforced")
    if rot.verifiedBootState != VERIFIED:
        return REJECT("verified boot not passed")
    if rot.deviceLocked != true:
        return REJECT("bootloader unlocked")

    // Step 7: Verify OS patch level (reject if >90 days old)
    minPatch = computeMinPatchLevel(90)
    if kd.teeEnforced.osPatchLevel < minPatch:
        return REJECT("OS patch level too old")

    // Step 8: Verify app identity
    appId = kd.teeEnforced.attestationApplicationId
    if expectedAppPackage not in appId.packageNames:
        return REJECT("app package not in attestation")

    // Step 9: Verify key origin — must be GENERATED, not IMPORTED
    if kd.teeEnforced.origin != GENERATED:
        return REJECT("key not generated in hardware")

    // Step 10: Check revocation via CRL
    intermediateCert = certChain[1]
    crl = fetchCRL(intermediateCert.getCRLDistributionPoint())
    if leafCert.serialNumber in crl.revokedSerials:
        return REJECT("attestation certificate revoked")

    return ACCEPT(kd)
```

Key design decisions in this algorithm: the challenge nonce (step 3) must be generated fresh per attestation request and bound to the user session; the `teeEnforced` list is checked exclusively (step 6) because `softwareEnforced` values are forgeable on rooted devices; and the CRL check (step 10) should be performed against Google's attestation CRL to catch certificates revoked after a TEE or SE vulnerability disclosure.

##### 13.1.3 Security Levels

The `attestationSecurityLevel` and `keyMintSecurityLevel` fields classify the hardware boundary protecting the attestation and key operations:

| Security Level | Hardware Boundary | Protection Guarantee |
|:--------------|:-----------------|:--------------------|
| **Software** (0) | None — attestation produced by Android OS software | ❌ No hardware protection. The attestation is only as trustworthy as the OS. A rooted device can forge software-level attestations |
| **TrustedEnvironment** (1) | ARM TrustZone TEE | ✅ Key operations occur inside the TEE's Secure World. Resistant to OS-level compromise — a rooted device cannot extract TEE-resident keys. Vulnerable to sophisticated TEE exploits (rare but documented — e.g., Qualcomm QSEE vulnerabilities) |
| **StrongBox** (2) | Dedicated Secure Element chip | ✅✅ Strongest protection. SE is physically isolated from the application processor. Resistant to both OS compromise and TEE exploits. Certified to CC EAL5+ in most implementations |

**RP enforcement strategy:** High-assurance RPs (banking, government identity) should require `attestationSecurityLevel >= TrustedEnvironment` and `origin == GENERATED` in the `hardwareEnforced` list. For maximum assurance, require `StrongBox` — though this excludes devices without a dedicated SE chip.

##### 13.1.4 Key Attestation vs. ID Attestation

Android supports two distinct attestation mechanisms:

- **Key attestation** (standard) — attests properties of a cryptographic key: where it was generated, its capabilities, the device's security state at generation time. Available to all applications. The attestation proves the key's security properties but does not identify the specific device

- **ID attestation** (restricted) — attests device-identifying information: serial number, IMEI, MEID, manufacturer, model. ID attestation requires `DEVICE_ID_ATTESTATION` capability and is restricted to **device owner apps** (enterprise MDM) or **zero-touch enrollment** scenarios. Consumer apps cannot request ID attestation

ID attestation fields appear in the `AuthorizationList` as `deviceBrand`, `deviceModel`, `deviceProduct`, `serialNumber`, `imei`, and `meid` — but only when the calling app has the device owner privilege. This restriction prevents consumer apps from using attestation as a device fingerprinting mechanism.

##### 13.1.5 Provisioned Attestation Key Revocation

When a TEE or SE vulnerability is discovered that could allow attestation key extraction, Google revokes the affected attestation keys by adding them to the public attestation certificate revocation status database. Google distributes revocation information through:

1. **Certificate Revocation List (CRL)** — published at a well-known URL; RPs should check the CRL during certificate chain validation
2. **Google Play Services updates** — compromised devices receive updates that disable the affected attestation keys and provision new ones (via Remote Key Provisioning)
3. **FIDO Metadata Service (MDS)** — for WebAuthn attestation (§10.5), Google publishes status reports for affected AAGUIDs

This revocation architecture ensures that a TEE vulnerability discovered in one chipset vendor's implementation (e.g., a Qualcomm QSEE exploit) results in the revocation of attestation certificates for affected devices without impacting devices from other manufacturers.

RPs have two complementary mechanisms for checking attestation certificate revocation:

| Mechanism | Scope | Authority | Update Frequency | Privacy |
|:----------|:------|:----------|:-----------------|:--------|
| **CRL** | Individual intermediate CAs | Google per-OEM | Daily or on-demand | Requires fetching CRL from Google servers |
| **MDS3** | All registered authenticators | FIDO Alliance | Continuous (cached blob) | Queried via FIDO Metadata Service API |

The FIDO Metadata Service (MDS3, FIDO Alliance) provides a structured JSON blob containing authenticator status entries (mapping an AAGUID or attestation certificate fingerprint to statuses such as `NOT_FABRICATED`, `USER_VERIFICATION_BYPASS`, `ATTESTATION_KEY_COMPROMISE`), biometric status entries, and vendor contacts. Verifiers should check **both** CRL and MDS3 status on every attestation verification (FIDO Metadata Service v3, FIDO Alliance).

#### 13.2 Apple App Attest and DeviceCheck

Apple provides two complementary mechanisms for servers to establish trust in client devices: **App Attest** (cryptographic proof of device and app authenticity) and **DeviceCheck** (persistent device-level state tracking). Together, they enable fraud prevention, API abuse protection, and device trust decisions — though they differ fundamentally in their security model and capabilities.

##### 13.2.1 App Attest (iOS 14+)

App Attest allows an app to generate a cryptographic key pair inside the Secure Enclave and have Apple certify that the key was generated on a genuine Apple device running an unmodified copy of the app. The attestation is bound to the specific app identity (`teamID.bundleID`), creating a cryptographic link between the device, the app binary, and the key.

**Key generation:**

```swift
let attestService = DCAppAttestService.shared
guard attestService.isSupported else { return }

attestService.generateKey { keyId, error in
    // keyId: a unique identifier for the P-256 key pair
    // generated inside the Secure Enclave
    // The private key never leaves the SE
}
```

The `generateKey()` method creates a P-256 (secp256r1) ECDSA key pair inside the Secure Enclave. The `keyId` returned is a reference handle — the private key material is non-exportable and never accessible to the application or the operating system.

**Attestation ceremony:**

```swift
// Server provides a one-time challenge (nonce)
let clientDataHash = Data(SHA256.hash(data: challengeFromServer))

attestService.attestKey(keyId, clientDataHash: clientDataHash) { attestationObject, error in
    // attestationObject: CBOR-encoded attestation (WebAuthn format)
    // Send to server for verification
}
```

The `attestKey(_:clientDataHash:)` method sends the key's public key and the client data hash to Apple's attestation service. Apple verifies the device's authenticity, generates an attestation object, and returns it to the app. The attestation object is CBOR-encoded following the WebAuthn attestation object format.

**Attestation object structure:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Root["`**Attestation Object**
    (CBOR map)`"]
    
    fmt["`**fmt**: apple-appattest`"]
    
    attStmt["`**attStmt**`"]
    x5c["`**x5c**: [attestation cert, intermediate CA cert]
    *Certificate chain rooted in Apple App Attest Root CA*`"]
    receipt["`**receipt**: opaque receipt for risk metric queries`"]
    
    authData["`**authData**`"]
    rpIdHash["`**rpIdHash**: SHA-256(teamID.bundleID)
    *binds to app identity*`"]
    flags["`**flags**: UP (user present), AT (attested credential data present)`"]
    counter["`**counter**: 0
    *initial value; increments with each assertion*`"]
    aaguid["`**aaguid**: appattest\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00
    *or appattestdevelop (development)*`"]
    credentialId["`**credentialId**: SHA-256(public key)`"]

    Root --> fmt
    Root --> attStmt
    Root --> authData
    
    attStmt --> x5c
    attStmt --> receipt
    
    authData --> rpIdHash
    authData --> flags
    authData --> counter
    authData --> aaguid
    authData --> credentialId
    
    classDef node text-align:left
    class Root,fmt,attStmt,x5c,receipt,authData,rpIdHash,flags,counter,aaguid,credentialId node
```

**Server-side verification steps:**

1. Decode the CBOR attestation object
2. Extract the certificate chain from `attStmt.x5c`
3. Verify the certificate chain roots in Apple's App Attestation Root CA
4. Compute `nonce = SHA-256(authData || clientDataHash)` and verify it matches the nonce in the attestation certificate's OID `1.2.840.113635.100.8.2` extension
5. Verify `rpIdHash == SHA-256(teamID + "." + bundleID)` — confirms the attestation is bound to the correct app
6. Verify `counter == 0` for initial attestation
7. Check `aaguid` to distinguish production from development environment
8. Store the `credentialId` (public key hash) and `counter` for subsequent assertion verification

**AAGUID and environment validation:** Apple uses different Authenticator Attestation GUIDs (AAGUIDs) for production and development environments. Production attestation objects use the standard `appattest` AAGUID, while development builds use `appattestdevelop`. During development, Apple requires an additional DeviceCheck token alongside the attestation — the token is sent to Apple's server to validate the development environment, preventing development attestation objects from being used to impersonate production attestation. Servers **must** validate the AAGUID and reject attestation objects with development AAGUIDs in production environments (App Attest, Apple Developer Documentation).

**Nonce binding (OID 1.2.840.113635.100.8.2):** The critical security binding in App Attest is the nonce extension in the leaf attestation certificate. The nonce prevents replay attacks (captured attestation objects cannot be reused against a different server nonce), cross-app attacks (an attestation obtained for App A cannot be submitted to App B), and pre-computed attestation attacks (the server must generate a fresh nonce for each attestation request). Best practices: use a **cryptographically random** nonce of at least 32 bytes, bind the nonce to the **user session** (include the session ID in the nonce or store a nonce-to-session mapping server-side), and set a **short TTL** (e.g., 5 minutes) for unused nonces (App Attest, Apple Developer Documentation).

##### 13.2.2 App Attest Assertions

After the initial attestation, the app uses the attested key to **sign assertions** — cryptographic proofs that specific requests originated from the attested device/app pair:

```swift
let clientDataHash = Data(SHA256.hash(data: requestPayload))

attestService.generateAssertion(keyId, clientDataHash: clientDataHash) { assertion, error in
    // assertion: CBOR-encoded assertion object
    // Include in API request to server
}
```

The assertion contains the `authenticatorData` (with an incremented counter) and a signature over the concatenation of `authenticatorData` and `clientDataHash`, signed by the Secure Enclave-resident private key. The server verifies the signature using the stored public key, checks that the counter has incremented monotonically (counter reuse indicates a cloned assertion), and validates the `rpIdHash`.

**Use cases:**

- **API abuse prevention** — only genuine app instances can call protected server endpoints; automated scripts and modified app binaries cannot produce valid assertions
- **In-app purchase validation** — the server verifies that purchase requests originate from a legitimate app, not a jailbroken device or modified binary
- **Promotional offer protection** — one-time offers (e.g., free trial, referral bonus) are bound to attested device/app pairs, preventing abuse through device cloning or app modification
- **Game integrity** — leaderboard submissions and competitive game actions are signed by attested keys, preventing score manipulation

**Assertion counter edge cases:** The monotonic counter provides lightweight replay detection but has two known limitations. First, **counter reset on app reinstall** — deleting and reinstalling the app resets the counter to 0. The server must handle this by allowing a counter reset only if the attestation is re-performed (i.e., the client repeats the initial attestation ceremony with a fresh nonce). Second, **32-bit counter overflow** — the counter wraps at $2^{32} - 1$. While this is not a practical concern for most applications (it would require billions of assertions), the server should handle the overflow edge case gracefully rather than rejecting the assertion outright.

##### 13.2.2.1 Server-Side App Attest Verification

The relying party server must perform the following checks when receiving an App Attest attestation (App Attest, Apple Developer Documentation):

```
function verifyAppleAppAttest(attestationObj, clientChallenge, expectedTeamId, expectedBundleId):
    // Step 1: Parse CBOR attestation object
    cborMap = CBORDecoder.decode(attestationObj)
    assert cborMap["fmt"] == "apple-appattest"

    // Step 2: Extract certificate chain
    x5c = cborMap["attStmt"]["x5c"]
    leafCert = parseX509(x5c[0])
    intermediateCert = parseX509(x5c[1])

    // Step 3: Verify certificate chain to Apple Root CA - G3
    appleRootCA = loadTrustedRoot("Apple_Root_CA_G3")
    verifyChain(leafCert, [intermediateCert], appleRootCA)

    // Step 4: Verify leaf certificate properties
    assert leafCert.OID("1.2.840.113635.100.6.11.1") == expectedTeamId
    assert leafCert.CN == expectedBundleId

    // Step 5: Verify nonce (challenge binding)
    nonceExt = leafCert.getExtension("1.2.840.113635.100.8.2")
    assert nonceExt.value == clientChallenge

    // Step 6: Verify attestation signature
    clientData = SHA256(clientChallenge)
    authenticatorData = cborMap["authData"]
    signedData = authenticatorData + clientData
    leafPubKey = leafCert.publicKey
    assert verifyECDSA(leafPubKey, signedData, cborMap["attStmt"]["receipt"])

    // Step 7: Verify AAGUID in authData extensions
    extMap = CBORDecoder.decode(cborMap["authData"][37..])
    aaguid = extMap["aaguid"]
    // Reject development AAGUID in production
    if aaguid == DEVELOPMENT_AAGUID: return REJECT("dev AAGUID")

    return ACCEPT(credentialId = extMap["credential"], aaguid = aaguid)
```

Key implementation notes: the `clientData` hash (step 6) is computed as `SHA-256(clientChallenge)`, not as the raw challenge bytes; the `receipt` field in `attStmt` serves as the attestation signature; and the team ID is verified against OID `1.2.840.113635.100.6.11.1` in the leaf certificate's Organizational Unit field, while the bundle ID is verified against the Common Name.

##### 13.2.3 DeviceCheck (iOS 11+)

DeviceCheck is a simpler, non-cryptographic mechanism that allows a server to associate **two bits of persistent state** with each device, per developer. Unlike App Attest, DeviceCheck does not provide cryptographic proof of device authenticity — it provides a device uniqueness signal.

**Client-side token generation:**

```swift
DCDevice.current.generateToken { token, error in
    // token: device-specific, short-lived token
    // Send to server for use with Apple's DeviceCheck API
}
```

**Server-side API interaction:**

The server sends the device token to Apple's DeviceCheck API (`https://api.devicecheck.apple.com/v1/`) along with its developer credentials. Apple maintains a mapping from device to two boolean bits per developer. The server can:

- **Query bits** — retrieve the current values of `bit0` and `bit1` for this device
- **Update bits** — set `bit0` and/or `bit1` to true or false

**Use cases:**

| Bit Configuration | Meaning (Example) |
|:-----------------|:-----------------|
| `bit0 = false, bit1 = false` | New device — never seen before |
| `bit0 = true, bit1 = false` | Device has redeemed a promotional offer |
| `bit0 = true, bit1 = true` | Device has been flagged for fraudulent activity |

DeviceCheck bits persist across app reinstallation and device reset (they are tied to the hardware, not the app installation). This makes DeviceCheck effective for preventing account fraud — a device flagged as fraudulent remains flagged even after a factory reset and fresh app install.

##### 13.2.4 App Attest vs. DeviceCheck Comparison

| Feature | App Attest | DeviceCheck |
|:--------|:-----------|:------------|
| **Key storage** | Secure Enclave (ECDSA P-256) | Apple-managed service |
| **Attestation format** | CBOR with X.509 certificate chain | JWT token |
| **Per-app key** | ✅ Yes — per-device, per-app | ❌ No — per-device, per-developer |
| **Replay detection** | ✅ Monotonic counter in assertions | ❌ No built-in counter |
| **Fraud tracking** | Via assertion counter | Two-bit persistent state |
| **Key attestation** | ✅ Proves key is in Secure Enclave | ❌ No key attestation |
| **App authenticity proof** | ✅ Certificate chain to Apple Root CA | ⚠️ Limited — DeviceCheck token only |
| **Minimum OS** | iOS 14.0 | iOS 11.0 |
| **Mac support** | macOS 12.0+ | macOS 10.14+ |
| **Offline capable** | ✅ Yes — assertion signing works offline | ❌ No — requires Apple server round-trip |
| **Development mode** | Supported with DeviceCheck token | Always available |
| **Revocation** | Via certificate chain validation | Via Apple server |
| **Anti-jailbreak** | ✅ Attestation fails on jailbroken devices (Apple's attestation service rejects them) | ❌ Jailbroken devices can generate DeviceCheck tokens |
| **Primary use** | API abuse prevention, request signing, app integrity verification | Device fraud tracking, one-time offer enforcement |

#### 13.3 TPM 2.0 Attestation

The Trusted Platform Module (TPM) 2.0 — introduced architecturally in §11.2.3 — provides two forms of attestation: **platform attestation** (proving the device's boot state and software integrity) and **key attestation** (proving that a specific cryptographic key was generated inside the TPM). These mechanisms are standardised by the Trusted Computing Group (TCG) in ISO/IEC 11889 and are the foundation of enterprise remote attestation, measured boot, and conditional access.

##### 13.3.1 Platform Attestation: Remote Attestation via TPM2_Quote

Platform attestation — also called **remote attestation** — allows a verifier to obtain cryptographic proof of the device's current software state. The TPM produces a signed statement of its Platform Configuration Register (PCR) values, which encode the measurements of every component loaded during the device's boot process (§11.2.3 for PCR definitions).

**Quote generation:**

```
TPM2_Quote(
    signHandle   = AK_handle,          // Attestation Key (AK) — signs the quote
    qualifyingData = server_nonce,      // Server-provided nonce — prevents replay
    PCRselect    = {PCR[0], PCR[1], PCR[2], PCR[4], PCR[7], PCR[11]}
)
```

The `TPM2_Quote` command instructs the TPM to:
1. Read the current values of the selected PCRs
2. Compute a SHA-256 digest over the concatenation of the selected PCR values
3. Construct a `TPMS_ATTEST` structure containing the PCR digest, the qualifying data (nonce), and metadata about the signing key
4. Sign the `TPMS_ATTEST` structure with the Attestation Key (AK)

**`TPMS_ATTEST` structure:**

```
TPMS_ATTEST {
    magic          = 0xFF544347,        // "TCG" magic value — identifies this as a TPM-generated attestation
    type           = TPM_ST_ATTEST_QUOTE,
    qualifiedSigner = AK_qualified_name, // Identifies the AK that signed this quote
    extraData      = server_nonce,       // The server-provided nonce — RP verifies this matches
    clockInfo      = {
        clock      = tpm_clock_value,    // TPM's internal clock (milliseconds since last TPM2_Clear)
        resetCount = n,                  // Number of TPM resets
        restartCount = m                 // Number of TPM restarts
    },
    firmwareVersion = tpm_fw_version,
    attested       = TPMS_QUOTE_INFO {
        pcrSelect  = selected_PCRs,      // Which PCRs were quoted
        pcrDigest  = SHA-256(PCR_values)  // Digest of the quoted PCR values
    }
}
```

The `pcrDigest` is computed as the hash of the concatenation of the selected PCR values:

$$
\text{pcrDigest} = H(\text{PCR}[s_0] \,\|\, \text{PCR}[s_1] \,\|\, \cdots \,\|\, \text{PCR}[s_n])
$$

where $s_0, s_1, \ldots, s_n$ are the selected PCR indices and $H$ is the hash algorithm specified in `pcrSelect.hash` (typically SHA-256). The `extraData` field is critical — it binds the quote to the verifier's nonce, preventing replay attacks. The `clockInfo` structure provides additional context: `clock` records the TPM's internal uptime (milliseconds since last `TPM2_Clear`), while `resetCount` and `restartCount` allow the verifier to detect suspicious TPM state changes that may indicate tampering.

**Server-side verification workflow:**

1. **Validate the AK certificate chain** — the AK's certificate must chain to the TPM manufacturer's root CA (Infineon, STMicroelectronics, NXP, Intel, AMD). The Endorsement Key (EK) certificate, provisioned by the manufacturer, vouches for the AK's association with a genuine TPM
2. **Verify the quote signature** — validate the `TPMS_ATTEST` signature using the AK's public key. A valid signature proves the quote was generated inside the TPM
3. **Check the nonce** — verify `extraData == server_nonce`. Nonce mismatch indicates replay
4. **Verify PCR values** — compare the `pcrDigest` against **expected ("golden") PCR measurements**. The verifier maintains a database of known-good PCR configurations for approved firmware versions, bootloader versions, and OS builds. If the PCR values match, the device's boot chain is unmodified
5. **Validate against the event log** — the device provides a **TCG Event Log** alongside the quote — a sequential record of every measurement extended into each PCR. The verifier replays the event log (re-computing `PCR_new = SHA-256(PCR_old || measurement)` for each entry) to confirm the event log produces the quoted PCR values. This replay ensures the event log is consistent with the PCRs and allows the verifier to identify exactly which software components were loaded

##### 13.3.1.1 TCG Event Log Format

The TPM event log records every measurement event that extends a PCR. Understanding its format is essential for reconstructing the expected PCR values and comparing them against the quoted values (TCG TPM 2.0 Specification, Part 2).

The log begins with a `TCG_EfiSpecIdEvent` structure:

```
TCG_EfiSpecIdEvent {
    signature          BYTE[16],        // "Spec ID Event03"
    platformClass      UINT32,          // 0 = Client, 1 = Server
    specVersionMinor   UINT8,
    specVersionMajor   UINT8,
    specErrata         UINT8,
    uintnSize          UINT8,           // 2 for 32-bit, 4 for 64-bit
    numberOfAlgorithms UINT32,
    digestSizes[]      {
        algorithmId     UINT16,         // TPM_ALG_SHA256 = 0x000B
        digestSize      UINT16,         // 32 for SHA-256
    }
}
```

Each subsequent event is a `TCG_PCR_EVENT2` structure:

```
TCG_PCR_EVENT2 {
    pcrIndex        UINT32,           // Which PCR this event extends
    eventType       UINT32,           // EV_ACTION, EV_S_CRTM_VERSION, EV_SEPARATOR, etc.
    digests[]       {
        algorithmId  UINT16,          // Hash algorithm (e.g., SHA-256 = 0x000B)
        digest       BYTE[n]          // Hash of the event data
    },
    eventSize       UINT32,           // Length of eventData
    eventData       BYTE[eventSize]   // Variable-length event content
}
```

To verify a TPM quote against the event log, the verifier must: (1) reconstruct the expected PCR values by replaying the event log — computing $\text{PCR}_i = H(\text{PCR}_i \,\|\, \text{digest})$ for each event targeting PCR $i$; (2) compute the composite digest over the selected PCRs using the formula from §13.3.1; (3) compare the recomputed digest against the `pcrDigest` in the quote; and (4) verify the AIK's signature over the `TPMS_ATTEST` structure.

##### 13.3.1.2 PCR Replay Attack Taxonomy

PCR replay attacks attempt to present a platform state that was valid at some point in the past but is no longer current (ISO/IEC 11889). The following taxonomy classifies the known attack vectors and their mitigations:

| Attack Type | Description | Mitigation |
|:------------|:------------|:------------|
| **Static replay** | Attacker captures a valid quote and replays it later | Nonce binding (`extraData`) prevents this — each quote request requires a fresh server nonce |
| **Boot-time replay** | Attacker boots a known-good OS, captures quote, then boots malicious OS and replays the captured quote | Nonce freshness + real-time quote request; the server must request quotes in real-time, not accept pre-captured ones |
| **PCR rollback** | Attacker resets PCRs to a known-good state (requires hardware attack on TPM) | PCR anti-rollback via `TPM_HierarchyChangeAuth` prevents unauthorised PCR reset; PCRs 0–7 are extended only during boot and cannot be reset without `TPM2_Clear` |
| **PCR flooding** | Attacker extends PCRs with benign events to dilute the measurement | Proper event log analysis — the verifier must replay the full event log, not just compare final PCR values |
| **Firmware downgrade** | Attacker flashes older firmware with known-good PCR values | Use PCRs 0–7 (immutable after boot) and verify firmware version in `clockInfo.firmwareVersion`; maintain a minimum version policy |
| **Partial quote** | Attacker provides quote over a subset of PCRs, omitting suspicious ones | Verifier must specify the **full** required PCR selection set — never accept an incomplete PCR selection |

Defence in depth strategies: request all security-relevant PCRs (typically PCRs 0–7 for firmware/UEFI and PCRs 8–15 for OS boot loader and OS components); validate against a reference database of known-good PCR values for approved OS/firmware combinations; check `clockInfo` (TPM clock, reset count, restart count) for consistency with the claimed boot sequence; and cross-correlate with network telemetry (the claimed firmware version should be consistent with observed TLS fingerprints and HTTP User-Agent strings).

##### 13.3.2 Key Attestation: TPM2_Certify

TPM key attestation proves that a specific key was generated inside the TPM and has particular properties (non-exportable, hardware-generated, bound to a specific parent key).

```
TPM2_Certify(
    objectHandle = target_key_handle,   // The key being attested
    signHandle   = AK_handle            // The AK that signs the certify statement
)
```

The TPM produces a `TPMS_ATTEST` structure with `type = TPM_ST_ATTEST_CERTIFY` containing:

- **`objectName`** — the cryptographic name of the attested key (a hash of its public area)
- **`qualifiedName`** — the full qualified name including the key's position in the TPM's key hierarchy
- **Key properties** — derived from the key's `TPMA_OBJECT` attributes:
  - `fixedTPM` — the key cannot be duplicated to another TPM
  - `fixedParent` — the key cannot be moved to a different parent key
  - `sensitiveDataOrigin` — the key's sensitive data (private key material) was generated by the TPM, not imported
  - `userWithAuth` — the key requires user authorisation (PIN, password, or HMAC session) for each use

**RP verification:** After validating the AK signature, the RP checks that the attested key's properties include `fixedTPM`, `fixedParent`, and `sensitiveDataOrigin` — confirming the key is truly hardware-bound and was generated inside the TPM (not imported from software).

##### 13.3.3 Endorsement Key (EK) Certificate Chain

Every TPM contains a unique **Endorsement Key (EK)** — an asymmetric key pair provisioned by the TPM manufacturer during fabrication. The EK certificate is signed by the manufacturer's CA, creating a certificate chain that establishes the TPM's authenticity:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EK["`**EK Certificate**
    **Subject**: TPM EK public key (unique per TPM)
    **Issuer**: TPM Manufacturer Intermediate CA
    **Signed by**: manufacturer intermediate key`"]
    style EK text-align:left

    INT["`**Manufacturer Intermediate CA**
    **Issuer**: Manufacturer Root CA
    **Signed by**: manufacturer root key`"]
    style INT text-align:left

    ROOT["`**Manufacturer Root CA**
    (e.g., Infineon, NXP, STMicro, Intel, AMD)
    Self-signed root certificate`"]
    style ROOT text-align:left

    EK -->|Signed By| INT
    INT -->|Signed By| ROOT
```

The EK is used exclusively for **encryption and key agreement** — it cannot sign data directly. To produce attestation signatures, the TPM derives **Attestation Keys (AKs)** from the EK. The AK certificate chain extends the EK chain by one level, with the AK certified as belonging to the same TPM as the EK.

**TPM manufacturer CAs:** Each major TPM chip vendor operates its own certificate hierarchy:

| Vendor | TPM Products | Root CA |
|:-------|:------------|:--------|
| Infineon Technologies | OPTIGA TPM (SLB 9670, SLB 9672) | Infineon OPTIGA RSA Root CA / ECC Root CA |
| STMicroelectronics | ST33 series TPM | STMicroelectronics TPM Root CA |
| NXP Semiconductors | EdgeLock SE050 (TPM-compatible) | NXP Semiconductors TPM Root CA |
| Intel | Platform Trust Technology (PTT) — firmware TPM integrated in Intel ME/CSME | Intel TPM Root CA |
| AMD | fTPM (firmware TPM in AMD Secure Processor / PSP) | AMD TPM Root CA |
| Microsoft | Pluton Security Processor (TPM-compatible, integrated in select AMD/Intel/Qualcomm CPUs) | Microsoft Pluton Root CA |

The EK is a globally unique, hardware-bound identifier — it never changes for the lifetime of the TPM. This creates inherent privacy risks:

| Concern | Description | Mitigation |
|:--------|:-------------|:------------|
| **Cross-service tracking** | Same EK presented to multiple services enables correlation | Use AIKs instead of EKs for attestation; AIKs are per-service |
| **Persistent identity** | EK never changes for the lifetime of the TPM | EK rotation is not possible; privacy depends on AIK isolation |
| **EK certificate leakage** | If EK cert is exposed, the device is permanently identifiable | Never transmit EK certificates in the clear; use `TPM2_ActivateCredential` |
| **Nation-state tracking** | TPM vendor CAs could theoretically track devices | No technical mitigation; policy/legal controls required |

The standard mitigation is the **Privacy CA** model: the TPM generates a new AIK for each service interaction, the AIK is certified by a Privacy CA (which verifies the EK but does not disclose it), and the verifier trusts the AIK certificate — which contains no EK information — rather than the EK itself (ISO/IEC 11889).

##### 13.3.4 Privacy-Preserving Attestation

Standard TPM attestation using the EK or a directly certified AK reveals a persistent device identity to the verifier — the same EK certificate appears in every attestation, enabling cross-service tracking. The TCG specification provides two mechanisms to mitigate this privacy concern:

**Direct Anonymous Attestation (DAA):**

DAA is a zero-knowledge cryptographic protocol that allows a TPM to prove it is a genuine TPM — i.e., it possesses a valid credential issued by a trusted authority — without revealing which specific TPM it is. The protocol uses group signatures based on elliptic curve cryptography (ECC-DAA in TPM 2.0):

1. **Issuer (Privacy CA)** — a trusted third party that verifies the TPM's EK certificate and issues a DAA credential. The credential certifies that the holder is a genuine TPM without embedding any identifying information
2. **Prover (TPM)** — generates a DAA signature on the attestation data using its DAA credential. The signature proves membership in the "genuine TPM" group without revealing the individual identity
3. **Verifier** — validates the DAA signature, confirming the attestation came from a genuine TPM. The verifier cannot determine which TPM produced the signature, nor link multiple attestations to the same device

DAA supports two signature modes:
- **Fully anonymous** — no linkability between signatures; the verifier cannot determine if two attestations came from the same TPM
- **Pseudonymous (basename)** — the verifier provides a basename; signatures from the same TPM with the same basename are linkable (enabling rogue TPM detection) but signatures with different basenames are unlinkable

<details><summary><strong>DAA Mathematical Construction and Protocol Phases</strong></summary>

Direct Anonymous Attestation uses group signatures based on a bilinear group setting:

$$
\mathbb{G}_1, \mathbb{G}_2, \mathbb{G}_T \text{ with pairing } e: \mathbb{G}_1 \times \mathbb{G}_2 \to \mathbb{G}_T
$$

**Setup phase (Issuer):** The Issuer generates the bilinear group and system parameters. The Issuer secret key is $(x, y) \in \mathbb{Z}_p^*$; the Issuer public key is $(X = x \cdot g_1, Y = y \cdot g_1, g_1, g_2)$.

**Join phase (one-time per TPM):**

1. TPM generates a secret $f \in_R \mathbb{Z}_p^*$ and computes $F = f \cdot g_1$
2. TPM sends a commitment to the Issuer using a zero-knowledge proof of knowledge of $f$
3. Issuer verifies the EK certificate and issues a DAA credential $\text{cred} = (A, e, v)$ where $A = (g_1 / (X^e \cdot Y^v))^{1/x}$, $e$ is a random prime, and $v$ is a random blinding value
4. TPM verifies the credential: $A^x \cdot X^e \cdot Y^v = g_1$
5. TPM stores $(f, A, e, v)$ as its DAA credential

**Sign phase (per attestation):** Given a verifier nonce $n_V$ and PCR values $\{\text{PCR}_i\}$, the TPM produces a non-interactive zero-knowledge proof using the Fiat-Shamir heuristic:

$$
\text{NIZK} \left\{ (f, A, e, v, \text{PCR}_i) : A^x \cdot X^e \cdot Y^v = g_1 \wedge F = f \cdot g_1 \right\}
$$

The DAA signature proves (in zero knowledge) that the signer holds a valid credential issued by the Issuer. The proof is computationally infeasible to forge without knowledge of the DAA secret key, yet reveals no information about which TPM created the signature (ISO/IEC 11889).

**Privacy properties:** | Property | Description |
|:---------|:------------|
| **Anonymity** | The verifier cannot determine which TPM signed the quote |
| **Unlinkability** | The verifier cannot link two signatures from the same TPM (unless basename is used) |
| **Traceability** | The Issuer (but not the Verifier) can trace signatures back to the TPM (for revocation) |
| **Non-frameability** | No party other than the legitimate TPM can create signatures attributed to that TPM |

</details>

**Attestation Identity Keys (AIKs):**

The simpler, more widely deployed approach: the TPM generates multiple Attestation Identity Keys, each certified by a **Privacy CA** (a trusted third party). The Privacy CA verifies the TPM's EK, creates a credential for the AIK, and wraps it so only the TPM's EK can decrypt it. The TPM uses different AIKs for different verifiers, preventing cross-service correlation. Unlike DAA, this approach relies on the Privacy CA not colluding with verifiers — the Privacy CA sees both the EK and the AIK mappings.

**The privacy challenge:** Attestation inherently creates a tension between **security assurance** and **user privacy**. A unique attestation certificate can be used to track a device across services and over time. Even with per-service AIKs, correlation is possible through side channels:

| Side Channel | Description | Mitigation |
|:-------------|:-------------|:------------|
| **IP address** | Same IP address across services reveals same device | Use different network paths (VPN, Tor) per service |
| **TLS fingerprint** | JA3/JA4 fingerprint identifies the OS/browser combination | Vary TLS configuration (caveat: may break compatibility) |
| **Timing patterns** | Similar request timing patterns across services | Add random delays (limited effectiveness) |
| **Device model / OS version** | Attestation includes OS version and patch level | These are low-entropy and cannot be varied |
| **Certificate chain** | Same intermediate CA narrows the device population | Cannot be mitigated without CA infrastructure changes |

The reality is that complete anonymisation of attestation is not achievable while maintaining meaningful security assurances. The practical approach is to minimise correlation to the degree that is operationally feasible — per-service AIKs with a trusted Privacy CA provide the best balance of privacy and deployability for most enterprise use cases (ISO/IEC 11889).

##### 13.3.4.1 AIK Certificate Lifecycle

Attestation Identity Keys follow a defined lifecycle from creation through revocation (ISO/IEC 11889):

| Phase | Description | TPM Command | Notes |
|:------|:-------------|:------------|:-------|
| **Creation** | Generate a new AIK key pair inside the TPM | `TPM2_Create` (restricted, decrypt) | AIK private key never leaves the TPM |
| **Loading** | Load the AIK into the TPM's transient object space | `TPM2_Load` | Required before the AIK can sign quotes |
| **Certification** | Prove to a Privacy CA that the AIK is TPM-resident | `TPM2_Certify` + `TPM2_ActivateCredential` | Privacy CA issues an AIK certificate binding the AIK to the TPM's EK (without revealing the EK) |
| **Usage** | Sign `TPM2_Quote` messages for platform attestation | `TPM2_Quote` | AIK signs the `TPMS_ATTEST` structure containing PCR values |
| **Rotation** | Generate a new AIK, deprecate the old one | `TPM2_Create` (new AIK) | Old AIK can be evicted from TPM memory; no credential transfer needed. Recommended every 90 days |
| **Revocation** | Invalidate an AIK if the TPM is compromised | Admin action + CA publication | Privacy CA publishes revocation; verifiers check CRL. Rotating the AIK does not require re-provisioning the EK |

##### 13.3.4.2 AIK Rotation Strategies

To limit cross-service correlation, AIKs should be rotated according to the following strategy comparison:

| Strategy | Rotation Frequency | Complexity | Privacy Level |
|:---------|:-------------------|:-----------|:-------------|
| **Per-session AIK** | Every authentication session | High (requires Privacy CA interaction each time) | Maximum — each session uses a different AIK |
| **Per-service AIK** | One AIK per relying party | Medium (one-time Privacy CA interaction per service) | High — services cannot correlate |
| **Per-period AIK** | Monthly or quarterly | Low (batch rotation) | Medium — correlation possible within rotation period |
| **Static AIK** | Never rotated | Minimal | Low — all services see the same AIK |

**Recommendation:** use **per-service AIKs** as the default. This provides strong privacy protection without the latency overhead of per-session rotation. Per-period rotation should be used as a minimum — a static AIK that never changes provides no privacy benefit against a colluding verifier and Privacy CA.

##### 13.3.5 Enterprise Platform Attestation

TPM-based platform attestation is a cornerstone of enterprise device health assessment. Major enterprise endpoint management platforms consume TPM attestation to make conditional access decisions:

**Microsoft Intune / Endpoint Manager:**

Microsoft Intune uses TPM-based **Device Health Attestation (DHA)** to verify the security state of Windows devices before granting access to corporate resources. The DHA flow:

1. The Windows device collects boot measurements from the TPM (PCR values, TCG Event Log)
2. The device sends the TPM quote and event log to the **Microsoft Device Health Attestation Service** (a cloud service)
3. The DHA service verifies the quote, replays the event log, and evaluates the device's health claims:
   - Secure Boot enabled and verified
   - BitLocker encryption active
   - Code Integrity (Device Guard / HVCI) enforced
   - Early Launch Anti-Malware (ELAM) driver loaded
   - No known bad measurements (rootkits, bootkits)
4. The DHA service returns a **Device Health Token** — a signed JWT containing the health status claims:

| JWT Claim | Description | Example Value |
|:----------|:-------------|:--------------|
| `iss` | Token issuer | `https://has.spsvc.microsoft.com/` |
| `aud` | Intended audience | `https://management.microsoft.com/` |
| `exp` | Expiration time | `1740000000` |
| `deviceid` | Azure AD device object ID | `a1b2c3d4-e5f6-...` |
| `hardwareid` | TPM-generated hardware hash | Base64-encoded SHA-256 |
| `healthstatus` | Overall health assessment | `0` (healthy) |
| `pcrecord` | PCR values that were verified | JSON array of `{index, value}` |
| `bootdiagnostics` | Boot integrity checks | JSON object with Secure Boot, ELAM status |
| `defendereval` | Windows Defender status | `{status: "on", definitions: "current"}` |
| `bitlockereval` | BitLocker encryption status | `{status: "on", pinSet: true}` |
| `securebooteval` | Secure Boot status | `{status: "on"}` |

5. Intune receives the Device Health Token and evaluates it against **compliance policies**
6. Microsoft Entra ID Conditional Access uses the compliance status to grant or deny access to protected resources

**VMware Workspace ONE / Google BeyondCorp:**

Similar patterns exist in other enterprise platforms — Workspace ONE evaluates device posture including TPM attestation status, and Google BeyondCorp Enterprise uses device signals (including Chrome Verified Access, which uses TPM-backed certificates) for access tier decisions.

#### 13.4 Device-Bound Keys and Hardware-Backed Credentials

Device-bound keys — keys where the hardware guarantees non-exportability — are the strongest form of credential binding available on consumer and enterprise devices. This section synthesises the hardware-backed key properties from §11.2 with the attestation mechanisms from §13.1–§13.3 and the WebAuthn attestation ceremonies from §10.4.

##### 13.4.1 Device-Bound Key Property Summary

A key is **device-bound** when the underlying hardware enforces that the private key material cannot leave the security boundary:

| Platform | Hardware | Property | Verification Mechanism |
|:---------|:---------|:---------|:----------------------|
| Apple iOS/macOS | Secure Enclave | Non-exportable by design — SE has no key export API | App Attest attestation (§13.2); WebAuthn `packed` attestation with Apple Anonymous Attestation |
| Android | StrongBox SE | `setIsStrongBoxBacked(true)` → key generated in dedicated SE chip | Android Key Attestation — `attestationSecurityLevel == StrongBox` (§13.1) |
| Android | TEE (TrustZone) | `setUserAuthenticationRequired(true)` → key operations in TEE | Android Key Attestation — `attestationSecurityLevel == TrustedEnvironment` (§13.1) |
| Windows | TPM 2.0 | `fixedTPM | fixedParent | sensitiveDataOrigin` → key generated inside and bound to this TPM | TPM key attestation via `TPM2_Certify` (§13.3.2) |
| FIDO2 security keys | On-key SE | Key generated inside the security key's secure element | WebAuthn attestation — `packed`, `tpm`, or `android-key` attestation format (§10.4) |

**The fundamental insight:** without attestation, the server must trust the client's claim that a key is hardware-backed. A compromised or emulated device could generate a software key and claim it was generated in hardware. Attestation provides the cryptographic proof that the claim is true — the attestation certificate chain, signed by the hardware manufacturer's root CA, vouches for the key's hardware provenance.

##### 13.4.2 Key Lifecycle for Device-Bound Credentials

Device-bound credentials follow a lifecycle that mirrors the device's own lifecycle — because the key cannot be exported, events affecting the device directly affect the credential:

**Phase 1 — Provisioning:**

1. Device generates a key pair inside the hardware security boundary (SE, TEE, TPM, StrongBox)
2. Key attestation is performed — the hardware produces an attestation statement (certificate chain, signed quote, or CBOR attestation object)
3. Server verifies the attestation: validates the certificate chain, checks the nonce, inspects key properties (security level, origin, device state)
4. Server stores the public key and attestation metadata (security level, device model, OS version, patch level) alongside the user's account record
5. Key is bound to authentication policy — biometric requirement, PIN requirement, access scope

**Phase 2 — Usage:**

- Every authentication or transaction signing operation invokes the hardware module — the private key is used inside the security boundary
- The server may verify additional signals per-operation: assertion counter (App Attest, WebAuthn), DPoP proof (§29.3), or transaction-specific signatures
- If biometric binding is configured (`kSecAccessControlBiometryCurrentSet` on iOS, `AUTH_BIOMETRIC_STRONG` on Android), each key use requires a live biometric verification

**Phase 3 — Rotation:**

Key rotation for device-bound credentials requires generating a **new** key pair (the old key cannot be transferred):

1. Device generates a new key pair with fresh attestation
2. Server verifies the new attestation
3. User authenticates with the old credential to prove identity continuity
4. Server replaces the old public key with the new one
5. Old credential is revoked server-side (removed from the user's registered credentials)

Rotation triggers include: hardware security advisory (TEE vulnerability requiring new attestation keys), compliance policy change (upgrading from TEE to StrongBox), biometric re-enrollment (§12.6 — keys with `BiometryCurrentSet` are automatically invalidated), and periodic rotation policies (some enterprise policies require key rotation every 90–365 days).

**Phase 4 — Decommissioning:**

- **User-initiated:** user removes the credential from their account (e.g., deletes a passkey, deregisters a security key)
- **Device wipe:** factory reset destroys all hardware-resident keys. RPs that require attestation will detect the decommissioning only when the user fails to authenticate — there is no push notification mechanism for key destruction
- **Enterprise MDM remote wipe:** MDM platforms (Intune, Jamf, Workspace ONE) can issue a remote wipe command that destroys all device-resident keys. The MDM platform can proactively mark the associated credentials as revoked in the IdP
- **Device loss/theft:** user reports device loss through the IdP's self-service portal or IT helpdesk. The RP revokes all credentials associated with the lost device. This is where **credential inventory** is critical — the RP must maintain a mapping between devices and credentials to enable surgical revocation without affecting the user's other devices

#### 13.5 Device Trust Signals: Integrity Verdicts and Play Integrity

Attestation (§13.1–§13.3) answers the question "was this key generated in genuine hardware?" Device trust signals answer a broader question: **"is this device trustworthy right now?"** — encompassing software integrity, jailbreak/root status, app binary authenticity, and compliance with enterprise security policies. Identity providers consume these signals to make **adaptive authentication decisions** (§20).

##### 13.5.1 Google Play Integrity API

The Google Play Integrity API (successor to the deprecated SafetyNet Attestation API, which was fully shut down in January 2025) provides a multi-dimensional integrity verdict for Android devices. The API evaluates the device, the app, and the Google Play licensing status in a single response.

**Verdict structure:**

```json
{
  "requestDetails": {
    "requestPackageName": "com.example.app",
    "timestampMillis": "1711843200000",
    "nonce": "server-provided-base64-nonce"
  },
  "appIntegrity": {
    "appRecognitionVerdict": "PLAY_RECOGNIZED",
    "packageName": "com.example.app",
    "certificateSha256Digest": ["base64-encoded-cert-hash"],
    "versionCode": 42
  },
  "deviceIntegrity": {
    "deviceRecognitionVerdict": [
      "MEETS_DEVICE_INTEGRITY",
      "MEETS_STRONG_INTEGRITY"
    ]
  },
  "accountDetails": {
    "appLicensingVerdict": "LICENSED"
  }
}
```

**Device integrity verdict levels:**

| Verdict | Meaning | Technical Basis (Android 13+) |
|:--------|:--------|:------------------------------|
| **`MEETS_BASIC_INTEGRITY`** | Device passes basic integrity checks — may have an unlocked bootloader, may not be Google-certified, but is not an emulator or actively compromised by API hooks | Attestation root of trust is provided by Google. Does not require locked bootloader |
| **`MEETS_DEVICE_INTEGRITY`** | Device is a genuine, Google-certified Android device with a locked bootloader running a certified manufacturer image | ✅ Hardware-backed proof (Android Platform Key Attestation) that bootloader is locked and OS image is certified |
| **`MEETS_STRONG_INTEGRITY`** | Device meets `MEETS_DEVICE_INTEGRITY` requirements **and** has received a security update within the last year | ✅ Hardware-backed proof + recent patch level verification. As of May 2025, the recency requirement is enforced for Android 13+ devices |
| *(empty verdict)* | Device shows signs of compromise: API hooking, rooted with framework modifications, running on an emulator, or system integrity check failed | ❌ Device cannot be trusted for sensitive operations |

**App integrity verdict:**

| Verdict | Meaning |
|:--------|:--------|
| `PLAY_RECOGNIZED` | App binary matches the version published on Google Play — no repackaging or tampering detected |
| `UNRECOGNIZED_VERSION` | App is installed but the version does not match Google Play — possible sideload or modified APK |
| `UNEVALUATED` | App integrity was not evaluated (app not distributed via Google Play) |

**App licensing verdict:**

| Verdict | Meaning |
|:--------|:--------|
| `LICENSED` | User has a valid license — the app was installed from Google Play for this Google account |
| `UNLICENSED` | User does not have a valid license — sideloaded, pirated, or account mismatch |
| `UNEVALUATED` | Licensing status could not be determined |

##### 13.5.2 Request Types: Standard vs. Classic

The Play Integrity API supports two request modes, offering a tradeoff between latency, freshness, and attack resistance:

| Dimension | Standard Request | Classic Request |
|:----------|:----------------|:----------------|
| **Latency** | Low — averaging hundreds of milliseconds | High — averaging several seconds |
| **Mechanism** | Uses on-device caching and pre-computed tokens; delegates some anti-attack protections to Google Play | Initiates a fresh, full integrity evaluation on each request |
| **Nonce binding** | ✅ Server-provided nonce is bound to the cached verdict | ✅ Server-provided nonce is bound to a fresh verdict |
| **Recommended use** | On-demand checks for routine user actions and API calls — suitable for most apps | Infrequent checks for highly sensitive or high-value actions where fresh evaluation is essential |
| **Resource impact** | Minimal — low battery and data consumption | Higher battery and data consumption due to full evaluation |

**May 2025 security upgrade:** Google enhanced Play Integrity verdicts for Android 13+ devices to leverage **hardware-backed security signals** (Android Platform Key Attestation) rather than software-only evaluation. This transition improved bypass resistance, reduced device signal collection by ~90%, and improved verdict latency by up to 80%. All existing integrations automatically transitioned to the improved verdicts. As of May 2025, `MEETS_STRONG_INTEGRITY` now requires a dedicated **StrongBox** security chip (not just a TEE) and Android 13+, meaning devices without StrongBox hardware can only achieve `MEETS_DEVICE_INTEGRITY`. The upgrade also introduced detection of Xposed/Frida framework hooks on the Play Integrity client itself, server-side analysis of device behavioural patterns, and machine-learning models that detect emulators based on sensor data anomalies (Google Play Integrity, Android Developers).

**Anti-emulator detection layers:** Play Integrity employs multiple detection layers to identify emulated environments:

1. **Hardware fingerprinting** — Play Services queries hardware sensors (accelerometer, gyroscope, magnetometer) and compares the data against known emulator patterns. Real devices produce sensor noise with specific statistical properties that emulators fail to replicate
2. **Timing analysis** — the API measures execution timing of specific operations. Emulators have characteristic timing profiles due to virtualisation overhead
3. **Play Services integrity** — the Play Services APK itself is verified for tampering. Frida or Xposed hooks on the Play Integrity client are detected via self-integrity checks
4. **Server-side analysis** — Google's servers maintain device reputation profiles. A device exhibiting anomalous behaviour (e.g., rapid integrity requests from multiple apps) is flagged
5. **Certificate transparency** — the attestation certificate chain is cross-referenced against Google's database of known-valid certificates

**Nonce binding best practices:** The Play Integrity API requires a server-provided nonce (minimum 16 bytes, recommended 32 bytes). Best practice: bind the nonce to the specific request context using `nonce = SHA-256(session_id || action || timestamp)` — this prevents a valid integrity token from being reused for a different action.

##### 13.5.3 Apple Device Trust: Composite Approach

Apple does not expose a single "device integrity API" equivalent to Google's Play Integrity. Instead, Apple's device trust model is distributed across multiple mechanisms:

| Mechanism | What It Provides | Equivalent Play Integrity Dimension |
|:----------|:----------------|:-----------------------------------|
| **App Attest** (§13.2.1) | Cryptographic proof of genuine device + unmodified app binary | `deviceRecognitionVerdict` + `appRecognitionVerdict` |
| **DeviceCheck** (§13.2.3) | Persistent device-level fraud tracking (2 bits per developer) | No direct equivalent — Play Integrity has no persistent state API |
| **Managed Device Attestation** (iOS 16+) | MDM-enrolled devices can attest to their managed status using ACME device attestation | Enterprise compliance attestation |
| **Jailbreak detection** (passive) | App Attest attestation fails on jailbroken devices; no explicit "jailbreak detected" API | Empty `deviceRecognitionVerdict` on rooted Android |

Apple's approach is more privacy-preserving — there is no server-side API that returns a device integrity "score." Instead, the attestation is binary (valid or invalid) and bound to the specific app, preventing cross-app device fingerprinting. The tradeoff is less granularity — an RP cannot distinguish between "device is genuine but has an outdated OS" and "device is genuine and fully patched" using App Attest alone.

##### 13.5.4 Enterprise Device Management Signals

Beyond platform-level integrity APIs, enterprise identity providers aggregate device trust signals from Mobile Device Management (MDM) platforms to make conditional access decisions:

| Signal Category | Sources | Examples |
|:---------------|:--------|:--------|
| **MDM enrollment status** | Intune, Jamf, Workspace ONE, Kandji | Device is enrolled in the organisation's MDM → eligible for corporate resource access |
| **Compliance policies** | MDM compliance engine | Encryption enabled, minimum OS version, screen lock configured, no jailbreak/root detected |
| **Device health attestation** | TPM-based DHA (§13.3.5), Chrome Verified Access | Boot integrity verified, Secure Boot enabled, BitLocker active |
| **Patch recency** | MDM inventory data | Last OS update within N days; last security patch within N days |
| **App inventory** | MDM managed app catalogue | Required apps installed; prohibited apps absent |
| **Network posture** | VPN/ZTNA agent, managed DNS | Device connected via corporate VPN or managed network; DNS filtering active |

**Device compliance signal detail.** Each compliance signal is evaluated by the MDM agent on the device and reported to the policy engine. The following table maps signals to their enforcement mechanism (NIST SP 800-63B, §5.2.4):

| Compliance Signal | Source | Typical Requirements |
|:-----------------|:------|:-------------------|
| Device encryption | Intune / Jamf / Workspace ONE | Full-disk encryption enabled |
| OS version | MDM agent | Minimum OS version (e.g., Windows 10 22H2+) |
| Patch level | MDM agent | No critical unpatched vulnerabilities |
| Jailbreak/root detection | MDM agent | Device not jailbroken or rooted |
| Device health attestation | Windows Health Attestation | Secure boot, BitLocker, ELAM enabled |
| Managed device | Entra ID join or hybrid join | Device registered in Entra ID |

Device compliance signals are evaluated as conditions in Conditional Access policies. The policy can require a compliant device for access to sensitive applications while allowing non-compliant devices (with limited functionality) for low-risk resources. When a device loses compliance status mid-session, Continuous Access Evaluation (§31) can revoke or downgrade the session in near-real-time rather than waiting for token expiry.

##### 13.5.5 Trust Signal Aggregation in Identity Providers

Identity providers (IdPs) consume device trust signals from multiple sources and combine them into an aggregate trust decision. This aggregation is where attestation (§13.1–§13.3) meets adaptive authentication (§20):

**Microsoft Entra ID Conditional Access:**

Entra ID Conditional Access evaluates device signals as **grant controls** in access policies:
- **Require device to be marked as compliant** — checks Intune compliance status (which itself consumes TPM DHA, patch level, encryption status)
- **Require Microsoft Entra hybrid joined device** — requires the device to have both on-premises AD and Entra ID registration
- **Require approved client app** — restricts access to pre-approved applications
- Device filters can evaluate custom attributes: `device.model`, `device.operatingSystem`, `device.trustType`

**Okta Device Trust:**

Okta evaluates device posture through its Okta Verify agent (installed on the device) and integrations with MDM platforms. Device trust signals include certificate-based device identity, jailbreak/root detection, disk encryption status, and screen lock presence.

**Google BeyondCorp Enterprise:**

Google's zero-trust access platform uses Chrome Verified Access (TPM-backed device certificates) and endpoint verification (device inventory) to evaluate device trustworthiness. Access tiers (full access, limited access, no access) are assigned based on device posture scores.

#### 13.6 Attestation Revocation and Compromise Response

##### 13.6.1 Revocation Mechanisms by Ecosystem

Each attestation ecosystem handles revocation differently. The following table summarises the mechanisms, scope, distribution, and latency across the major ecosystems:

| Ecosystem | Revocation Mechanism | Scope | Distribution | Latency |
|:----------|:---------------------|:------|:-------------|:--------|
| **Android Key Attestation** | CRL per intermediate CA + MDS3 status | Per-certificate or per-OEM | CRL download from Google; MDS3 blob | Hours |
| **Apple App Attest** | Apple server-side revocation + certificate expiry | Per-app credential | Apple checks at validation time | Near-real-time |
| **TPM 2.0 (AIK)** | Privacy CA CRL + EK cert revocation | Per-AIK or per-EK | CA-specific CRL distribution | Depends on CA operator |
| **Play Integrity** | Google server-side revocation | Per-device reputation | Internal Google system | Real-time |
| **FIDO2/WebAuthn** | MDS3 authenticator status | Per-AAGUID or per-fingerprint | FIDO Alliance MDS3 blob | Hours to days |
| **Intune DHA** | Microsoft-managed revocation | Per-device compliance state | Azure AD device object | Real-time |

##### 13.6.2 Certificate Transparency for Attestation

Certificate Transparency (CT) logs provide a public, append-only record of issued certificates. Applying CT to attestation certificates offers detection of unauthorised issuance and auditability. However, attestation-specific challenges exist:

- **Privacy** — attestation certificates may contain device identifiers (IMEI, serial number). Publishing these to public CT logs is a privacy violation. Solution: use **Confidential CT** or CT log redaction
- **Volume** — Android devices alone generate millions of attestation certificates per day. Standard CT logs are not designed for this volume
- **Short-lived certificates** — attestation certificates issued via RKP may have validity periods of hours, making CT log monitoring impractical

##### 13.6.3 Compromise Response Procedures

When an attestation key or device is compromised, RPs should follow a structured response:

1. **Detection** — identify the compromise source (key extraction, CA compromise, firmware bypass). Sources include vulnerability disclosure, anomaly detection in attestation patterns, and vendor advisories
2. **Scope assessment** — determine the blast radius (see §13.7.4). Classify whether a single device, a device batch, or a platform-wide compromise has occurred
3. **Revocation request** — submit a revocation request to the appropriate CA (Google, Apple, TPM vendor, FIDO Alliance)
4. **Policy update** — update verifier logic to reject attestation from the compromised device or device batch. Add the affected serial numbers or AAGUIDs to a blocklist
5. **Credential rotation** — if the compromised key is used for device-bound credentials (WebAuthn passkeys), initiate credential recovery flows for affected users
6. **Post-incident review** — analyse the root cause and update attestation policies to prevent recurrence

##### 13.6.4 Blast Radius Analysis

The blast radius of an attestation compromise depends on the type and scope:

| Compromise Scenario | Blast Radius | Recovery Effort |
|:--------------------|:-------------|:----------------|
| **Single device key extraction** (Tier 3 attack on one TPM/SEP) | One device | Revoke that specific AIK or attestation key; issue new credentials |
| **OEM intermediate CA compromise** | All devices from that OEM | Revoke intermediate CA certificate; reissue all affected device attestation keys |
| **TPM vendor root CA compromise** | All devices with that vendor's TPM | Replace root CA in all verifiers; mass re-provisioning |
| **Google RKP service compromise** | All Android 12+ devices during attack window | Revoke affected attestation certificates; patch RKP service |
| **Privacy CA compromise** | All AIKs certified by that CA | Replace Privacy CA; re-issue all AIK certificates |
| **FIDO MDS3 compromise** | All FIDO authenticators | MDS3 is read-only; compromise would require CA-level revocation |

#### 13.7 Cross-Platform Attestation Comparison

| Dimension | Android Key Attestation | Apple App Attest | TPM 2.0 Attestation |
|:----------|:----------------------|:----------------|:--------------------|
| **What is attested** | Cryptographic key properties, device security state, app identity | Key pair on genuine device, app identity (teamID.bundleID) | Platform boot state (PCR measurements), key properties |
| **Attestation format** | X.509 certificate chain with custom ASN.1 extension | CBOR attestation object (WebAuthn format) | Signed `TPMS_ATTEST` structure |
| **Root of trust** | Google Hardware Attestation Root CA | Apple App Attestation Root CA | TPM manufacturer CA (Infineon, STMicro, NXP, Intel, AMD) |
| **Hardware boundary** | TEE (TrustZone) or StrongBox (SE) | Secure Enclave | TPM (discrete or firmware) |
| **Nonce binding** | ✅ `attestationChallenge` in extension | ✅ `clientDataHash` in attestation ceremony | ✅ `qualifyingData` in `TPMS_ATTEST` |
| **Key properties in attestation** | ✅ Full `AuthorizationList`: algorithm, key size, origin, security level, OS version, patch level, app identity | ⚠️ Limited: app identity (rpIdHash), counter, environment (prod/dev) | ✅ Key attributes: `fixedTPM`, `fixedParent`, `sensitiveDataOrigin` |
| **Device identity** | ❌ Not in standard attestation (ID attestation restricted to enterprise) | ❌ No device identity — privacy-preserving by design | ⚠️ EK certificate reveals TPM identity; DAA provides anonymity |
| **Boot integrity** | ✅ `rootOfTrust` in `AuthorizationList`: verified boot state, locked bootloader | ❌ Not exposed — Apple handles boot integrity internally | ✅ PCR values encode full measured boot chain |
| **Revocation model** | ✅ Google CRL + Remote Key Provisioning (short-lived certificates) | ⚠️ Certificate chain validation; no public revocation list | ✅ Manufacturer CRL; enterprise can revoke individual TPM-backed credentials |
| **Privacy model** | Pseudonymous — attestation certificates do not contain device-identifying information (except ID attestation) | Anonymous — no persistent device identifier; fresh key per attestation | ⚠️ EK is a persistent identifier; DAA provides zero-knowledge anonymity; AIKs provide pseudonymity |
| **WebAuthn integration** | ✅ `android-key` attestation format in WebAuthn registration | ✅ `apple` attestation format (Apple Anonymous Attestation) | ✅ `tpm` attestation format |
| **Primary deployment** | Mobile devices (phones, tablets) | Apple ecosystem (iPhone, iPad, Mac, Apple Watch) | PCs, laptops, servers, enterprise endpoints |

#### 13.8 Verification Anti-Patterns and Architecture Recommendations

##### 13.8.1 Common Implementation Mistakes

The following table catalogues frequently observed mistakes in attestation verification implementations (NIST SP 800-147B):

| Anti-Pattern | Description | Impact | Correct Approach |
|:-------------|:-------------|:--------|:-----------------|
| **Trust all certificates** | Accepting any certificate that chains to a trusted root without checking the KeyDescription/App Attest claims | Attacker can use any device's valid attestation to impersonate | Always parse and validate the attestation-specific extension or claims |
| **Skip nonce verification** | Accepting attestation with any nonce or no nonce | Attestation can be replayed | Always verify the nonce matches the server-generated value |
| **Trust `softwareEnforced` fields** | Using Android `softwareEnforced` AuthorizationList fields for security decisions | Rooted device can forge these values | Trust only `teeEnforced` or StrongBox-enforced fields |
| **Accept expired certificates** | Not checking certificate expiry dates | Compromised or rotated keys may still be accepted | Check expiry on every verification |
| **Ignore CRL/MDS status** | Not checking revocation status | Compromised devices remain trusted | Check CRL and MDS3 on every verification |
| **Accept any AAGUID** | Not validating the AAGUID in production | Development attestation may pass production checks | Maintain an AAGUID allowlist for production |
| **Store attestation identifiers** | Persisting device serial, IMEI, or EK in database | Privacy violation, GDPR/CCPA risk | Hash and salt identifiers; avoid long-term storage |
| **Use attestation as sole auth factor** | Relying solely on device attestation without user authentication | Stolen device grants access | Combine attestation with user authentication (MFA) |
| **Accept old attestation versions** | Allowing `attestationVersion < 3` on Android | Missing security fields, potentially forgeable | Require minimum attestation version 3 (or 4) |
| **Ignore counter value** | Not checking App Attest monotonic counter | Replay attacks possible | Store and verify monotonic counter on each assertion |

##### 13.8.2 Production Verification Architecture

A production attestation verification service should follow these architectural recommendations:

1. **Separate attestation verification from business logic** — implement attestation verification as a dedicated microservice with a well-defined API. This allows independent updates to trust root stores and verification logic without deploying business logic changes
2. **Cache trust roots** — load root and intermediate certificates into memory; refresh from the source periodically (daily for root CAs, hourly for CRLs). Pin root certificates and monitor for rotation announcements
3. **Log all verifications** — record attestation results (accept/reject, reason, timestamp) for audit purposes. **Do not** log attestation payloads or device identifiers — this creates a tracking database
4. **Implement rate limiting** — prevent attestation brute-forcing by rate-limiting verification requests per client IP and per user session. An attacker should not be able to enumerate valid attestations by replaying captured certificates
5. **Support graceful degradation** — if the attestation service is unavailable, fall back to a reduced-assurance mode rather than failing closed (unless policy requires fail-closed). This prevents a single point of failure from locking out all users

#### 13.9 Emerging Trends: Post-Quantum and Unified Frameworks

##### 13.9.1 Post-Quantum Attestation

With the advancement of quantum computing, attestation mechanisms face two distinct threats: **store-and-break** (an adversary records attestation signatures today and forges new ones after a quantum computer can break the underlying algorithm) and **retroactive key extraction** (if a quantum computer can solve ECDLP, the adversary can derive private attestation keys from public certificates).

The migration path follows a hybrid approach:

$$
