\text{Current: } \text{AttestationKey}_{\text{ECDSA-P256}} \;\to\; \text{Hybrid: } \text{AttestationKey}_{\text{ECDSA-P256}} \,\|\, \text{AttestationKey}_{\text{ML-DSA-65}} \;\to\; \text{Future: } \text{AttestationKey}_{\text{ML-DSA-65}}
$$

Challenges specific to post-quantum attestation (NIST SP 800-208):

- **Certificate size** — ML-DSA-65 public keys are approximately 1,952 bytes, compared to 64 bytes for P-256. Attestation certificates will grow significantly
- **Signature size** — ML-DSA-65 signatures are approximately 3,309 bytes, impacting network bandwidth and storage
- **TPM firmware updates** — TPM chips have limited flash storage; post-quantum algorithms may not fit in existing TPM firmware without major hardware redesign
- **Performance** — Post-quantum signature verification is 10–100× slower than ECDSA, which may impact latency-sensitive attestation flows

##### 13.9.2 Unified Attestation Frameworks

Several industry efforts aim to provide a unified attestation API across platforms:

| Framework | Scope | Status | Key Features |
|:----------|:------|:--------|:-------------|
| **FDO** (FIDO Device Onboard) | IoT device provisioning and attestation | FIDO Alliance specification | TO protocol, device-unique credentials |
| **DICE** (Device Identifier Composition Engine) | Hardware-layer device identity | TCG specification | Layered attestation, compositor certificates |
| **RAV** (Relying Party Attestation Verification) | Cross-platform attestation verification | IETF draft (`draft-ietf-rats-ar4si`) | Evidence model, appraisal policy |
| **CIM** (Concise Identity Module) | Firmware identity | TCG / Linux Foundation | CBOR-based firmware identity claims |
| **PARSEC** (Platform AbstRaction for SECurity) | Secure service API abstraction | Linux Foundation project | Common API for TEE, TPM, KeyStore |

The DICE architecture is particularly promising because it provides a hardware-rooted identity chain that composes through each firmware and OS layer:

$$
\text{UDS} \to \text{CDI}_0 \to \text{CDI}_1 \to \cdots \to \text{CDI}_n \to \text{Application Identity}
$$

where UDS is the Unique Device Secret (burned into ROM) and each CDI (Compound Device Identity) is derived by measuring the next layer of firmware or software (TCG DICE Specification).

##### 13.9.3 Delegated Attestation/Attestation-as-a-Service

Cloud providers are beginning to offer attestation verification as a managed service, abstracting away the complexity of maintaining root certificate stores, verifying certificate chains, and replaying event logs:

- **Azure Attestation** — Microsoft's cloud service for verifying TPM-based platform attestation, SEV-SNP attestation for AMD confidential computing, and SGX attestation for Intel enclaves
- **AWS Nitro Attestation** — verifies the integrity of AWS Nitro Enclaves and AWS Lambda confidential compute
- **Google Confidential Space** — verifies the integrity of GKE workloads running in confidential VMs

These services introduce a dependency on the cloud provider's attestation infrastructure, creating a new trust anchor. The tradeoff is reduced operational complexity (no root certificate management, no CRL distribution) in exchange for a single point of trust that may not be acceptable for all regulatory environments. Organisations in regulated sectors should evaluate whether delegating attestation verification to a cloud provider meets their compliance requirements before adoption.

##### 13.9.4 Emerging Authentication Modalities

Several proximity-based and tap-to-authenticate technologies may further expand device authentication capabilities in the 2027–2030 timeframe, with implications for how attestation and device trust are established:

| Emerging Technology | How It Works | Maturity | Security Implications |
|:-------------------|:------------|:---------|:---------------------|
| **NFC tap-to-authenticate** | User taps NFC-enabled device (phone, ring, card) on NFC reader to authenticate; FIDO2 credential is activated by proximity | Mature technology; emerging use cases (YubiKey NFC, smart rings) | Fast, hygienic, accessible; does not require device unlock if the credential is configured for presence-only (AAL1); requires proximity — harder to exploit remotely |
| **Passwordless proximity-based authentication** | Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) proximity detection triggers automatic authentication when the user's phone is near the workstation | Early commercial (Proximity by Yubico, Windows Dynamic Lock) | Seamless UX — no explicit action required; limited by BLE range and accuracy; cannot distinguish between two users in close proximity |

NFC tap-to-authenticate is particularly relevant to device attestation because the NFC channel can carry attestation certificates from the token to the reader in a single tap — the relying party receives both the authentication response and the device attestation in one protocol exchange. BLE-based proximity authentication, by contrast, typically requires a separate channel for attestation verification since the BLE link itself does not carry attestation payloads.

#### 13.10 OTP Token Device Considerations

While FIDO2/WebAuthn and platform authenticators are the forward-looking standard for device-bound authentication (§10, §13), a significant installed base of hardware and software OTP tokens remains in production — particularly in financial services, where EMV CAP/DPA readers and OCRA-based transaction signing tokens are mandated by banking regulations (PSD2 Strong Customer Authentication, §4.4). This section covers device lifecycle considerations for OTP token deployments that are relevant to the device attestation chapter's scope of hardware-bound credentials.

##### 13.10.1 Migration Paths from Legacy OTP Algorithms

Organisations currently using HOTP or TOTP that require transaction signing capabilities have several migration options (RFC 6287, §6):

| From | To | Migration Complexity | Key Impact |
|:-----|:---|:---------------------|:-----------|
| TOTP | OCRA (challenge-response) | High — new token provisioning, UX changes | Same key can be reused with different DataInput |
| HOTP | OCRA (challenge-response + counter) | Medium — counter state is compatible | Key reuse possible; add PIN parameter |
| TOTP | WebAuthn/FIDO2 | High — new protocol, new authenticator types | Different key material entirely |
| CAP/DPA reader | Mobile app (software OCRA) | Medium — same protocol logic, different form factor | Key must be provisioned to secure element |

The shared key $K$ used in HOTP or TOTP can typically be reused in an OCRA deployment without re-provisioning, since all three algorithms use the same HMAC-based cryptographic pipeline with the same key format. The difference is purely in how the DataInput is constructed before the HMAC computation. However, migrating to WebAuthn/FIDO2 requires entirely new key material and a new authenticator form factor — there is no key reuse path from OTP algorithms to asymmetric WebAuthn credentials.

##### 13.10.2 Client-Side Implementation: Hardware vs. Software Tokens

**Hardware tokens** implement OTP algorithms in firmware with form-factor constraints that directly affect security properties and user experience:

| Constraint | Impact | Typical Implementation |
|:-----------|:-------|:-----------------------|
| **Limited display** (6–10 digit LCD) | Constrains output digit count and challenge display | Numeric challenges preferred; 6–8 digit output |
| **Keypad input** | User must manually type challenge digits | Numeric challenges (QN) for keypad efficiency |
| **EMV secure element** | Key storage is tamper-resistant | Shared key $K$ stored in chip card, not in reader |
| **No network connectivity** | Challenge must be manually entered or optically scanned | Colour-code scanning (Rabobank) or USB (ABN AMRO) |
| **Battery-powered** | Computation must be energy-efficient | HMAC-SHA-1 preferred over SHA-256 for battery life |

**Software tokens** (mobile apps) have more flexibility but introduce different trust considerations:

| Capability | Implementation |
|:-----------|:---------------|
| **Push notification** | Server pushes challenge to app; no manual challenge entry needed |
| **Biometric unlock** | Face/fingerprint to unlock the OTP computation (replaces PIN entry) |
| **Secure element** | Key stored in phone's Secure Enclave / StrongBox / TEE |
| **QR code scanning** | Challenge encoded as QR code; camera scan replaces manual entry |
| **Transaction display** | Full transaction details rendered in native UI (WYSIWYS via software) |
| **Multiple suites** | App supports multiple OCRASuite configurations for different services |

The key tradeoff: hardware tokens offer superior key protection (EMV secure element, no network attack surface) but limited UX (manual challenge entry, small display). Software tokens offer rich UX (push notifications, transaction display, biometric unlock) but the key protection depends on the mobile device's security posture — which is itself subject to the attestation and device trust considerations discussed throughout this chapter (§13.1–§13.5).

##### 13.10.3 Key Management for OTP Tokens

Key management for OTP tokens follows the same principles as attestation key lifecycle management (§13.4) but with OTP-specific operational considerations:

| Aspect | Recommendation |
|:-------|:---------------|
| **Key generation** | Use CSPRNG; minimum 160 bits (SHA-1), 256 bits (SHA-256), 512 bits (SHA-512) |
| **Key provisioning** | Secure channel (TLS, manual entry of 20+ character base32 key, or QR code) |
| **Key storage (server)** | Encrypt at rest with HSM-managed key wrapping; access logging |
| **Key storage (client)** | EMV secure element (hardware) or platform TEE/Secure Enclave (software) |
| **Key rotation** | Define rotation period (e.g., 2–3 years); support dual-key transition with overlap period |
| **Key revocation** | Server-side key deactivation; immediate session termination for compromised keys |
| **Key export** | Never export private keys from secure elements; use key derivation for multi-device support |

Key rotation is the most operationally challenging aspect: unlike WebAuthn credentials which can be re-registered at any time, OTP token key rotation requires physical replacement of hardware tokens or secure re-provisioning of software tokens. The dual-key overlap period (both old and new keys accepted simultaneously) is essential to avoid locking out users during the transition window.

#### 13.11 FIDO2/WebAuthn Biometric Authentication Protocol

The following sequence diagram illustrates the end-to-end flow of a biometric authentication protocol using FIDO2/WebAuthn architecture. This is the current best practice for biometric authentication in production systems and serves as a concrete example of how the device attestation mechanisms described throughout this chapter (TEE/SE key generation, attestation certificates, origin-bound signatures) are composed into a complete authentication protocol:

```mermaid
---
config:
  sequence:
    messageAlign: left
    noteAlign: left
    actorMargin: 250
  themeVariables:
    noteBkgColor: "transparent"
    noteBorderColor: "transparent"
---
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant User
    participant App as Client Application
    participant Browser as Browser / OS
    participant Auth as FIDO2 Server
    participant RP as Relying Party Server

    Note over User, RP: Phase 1 — Registration (Enrollment)

    User->>App: Initiate biometric enrollment
    App->>Browser: navigator.credentials.create()
    Browser->>User: System biometric prompt (Touch ID / Face ID)
    User->>Browser: Present biometric (fingerprint / face)
    Browser->>Browser: Sensor capture + liveness check
    Browser->>Browser: TEE/SE generates key pair + attestation
    Browser->>App: Return credential (public key + attestation)
    App->>RP: Send registration response
    RP->>Auth: Store public key + credential ID + user mapping
    Auth-->>RP: Registration confirmed
    RP-->>App: Registration complete

    Note over User, RP: Phase 2 — Authentication

    User->>App: Initiate login
    App->>RP: Request authentication challenge
    RP->>Auth: Generate challenge + allowed credentials list
    Auth-->>RP: challenge, timeout, rpId, allowCredentials[]
    RP->>App: Return WebAuthn options
    App->>Browser: navigator.credentials.get(options)
    Browser->>User: System biometric prompt
    User->>Browser: Present biometric
    Browser->>Browser: Sensor capture + liveness check
    Browser->>Browser: TEE/SE signs challenge with private key
    Browser->>App: Return assertion (authenticatorData + clientDataJSON + signature)
    App->>RP: Send authentication response
    RP->>Auth: Verify signature, challenge, origin, user presence
    Auth-->>RP: Verification result (success/fail)
    RP-->>App: Authentication result
    App-->>User: Grant / deny access
    Note right of RP: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
```

<details><summary><strong>1. User initiates biometric enrollment</strong></summary>

The user opens the application (or web client) and selects the option to register a biometric authenticator. The application evaluates the client environment to ensure the `window.PublicKeyCredential` API is available.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>2. Client Application calls navigator.credentials.create()</strong></summary>

The application invokes the WebAuthn API to start the registration ceremony. It passes a `PublicKeyCredentialCreationOptions` payload containing the relying party identity (`rpId`), user information (`id`, `name`), cryptographic preferences (e.g., `ES256`, `RS256`), and a newly generated high-entropy random challenge from the server to prevent replay attacks.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>3. Browser / OS displays system biometric prompt</strong></summary>

The operating system intercepts the WebAuthn request and renders a native, un-spoofable biometric modal (like Touch ID, Face ID, or Windows Hello). Because this prompt is rendered entirely outside the browser DOM, it cannot be simulated or phished by malicious JavaScript.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>4. User presents biometric factor</strong></summary>

The user physically interacts with the local device sensor (fingerprint scanner, facial recognition camera array). This satisfies the "User Verification" (UV) and "User Presence" (UP) requirements of the WebAuthn specification.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>5. Browser / OS performs sensor capture and liveness check</strong></summary>

The underlying OS biometric subsystem captures the analog biometric data. It performs hardware-level liveness checking to reject spoofs, masks, or synthetic inputs. The biological data itself immediately vanishes or remains locked inside the sensor's memory—it is never transmitted to the browser or OS at large.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>6. Browser / OS TEE generates key pair and attestation</strong></summary>

Upon successful biometric verification, the OS instructs the hardware Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) or Secure Element (SE) to generate a new asymmetric key pair bound to the specified origin. It formats an attestation statement cryptographically proving the key's hardware-bound nature.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>7. Browser / OS returns credential to Client Application</strong></summary>

The OS returns control to the browser, which resolves the `navigator.credentials.create()` promise. The payload includes the new public key, the raw credential ID, and the signed attestation object. The private key remains permanently entombed in the hardware.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>8. Client Application sends registration response to Relying Party Server</strong></summary>

The front-end application formats the WebAuthn response into a JSON payload, typically base64url-encoding the binary array buffers, and POSTs the registration artifact to the Relying Party (RP) backend server.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>9. Relying Party Server instructs FIDO2 Server to store credentials</strong></summary>

The RP passes the registration payload to its dedicated FIDO2 infrastructure. The FIDO2 server validates the attestation chain, confirms the origin matches the `rpId`, verifies the challenge signature, and persists the public key paired with the unique credential ID against the user's account record.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>10. FIDO2 Server confirms registration to Relying Party Server</strong></summary>

The FIDO2 server responds to the core backend with a success status, formally promoting the credential to an active state. From this point forward, the user has a globally unique public key associated with their identity for this specific origin.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>11. Relying Party Server signals registration complete to Client Application</strong></summary>

The RP transmits an HTTP 200/201 Success back to the front-end application. The user interface updates to reflect that the biometric authenticator has been successfully enrolled.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>12. User initiates login</strong></summary>

At a later time, the user returns to the application and initiates a login flow, typically by clicking a "Sign in with biometrics" button or entering their username.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>13. Client Application requests authentication challenge from Relying Party Server</strong></summary>

The application sends an initialization request to the backend indicating the intent to perform a WebAuthn assertion (authentication) ceremony.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>14. Relying Party Server generates challenge and allows credentials</strong></summary>

The backend requests assertion parameters from the FIDO2 server. The FIDO2 server generates a massive, cryptographically secure random challenge (minimum 32 bytes) and retrieves the `allowCredentials` array—the list of specific credential IDs that are permitted to answer the challenge for this user.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>15. FIDO2 Server returns challenge parameters to Relying Party Server</strong></summary>

The FIDO2 server hands back the challenge, the timeout constraints, the origin `rpId`, and the array of allowed credentials.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>16. Relying Party Server returns WebAuthn options to Client Application</strong></summary>

The RP backend encapsulates the FIDO2 parameters into a JSON response and serves it to the front-end application.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>17. Client Application calls navigator.credentials.get()</strong></summary>

The front-end converts the base64url-encoded challenge and credential IDs back into binary array buffers and invokes `navigator.credentials.get()` with the `PublicKeyCredentialRequestOptions` object to trigger the authentication ceremony.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>18. Browser / OS displays system biometric prompt</strong></summary>

The operating system once again intercepts the WebAuthn API call, verifying the origin, and displays the native system biometric dialog asking the user to authorize the login to the specific RP.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>19. User presents biometric factor</strong></summary>

The user interacts with the sensor, proving their physical presence (UP) and cryptographically verifying their identity (UV) against the local hardware enclave.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>20. Browser / OS performs sensor capture and liveness check</strong></summary>

The sensor hardware ensures the input is live and matches the previously enrolled biometric template stored completely isolated from the OS.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>21. Browser / OS TEE signs challenge with private key</strong></summary>

Once the biometric match is confirmed, the TEE unlocks the specific private key associated with the requested credential ID. It computes a cryptographic signature over the client data hash (which contains the challenge and origin) and the authenticator data (which contains flags like UV/UP and the signature counter). 

</details>
<details><summary><strong>22. Browser / OS returns assertion to Client Application</strong></summary>

The `navigator.credentials.get()` promise resolves, yielding an assertion payload. This contains the `authenticatorData`, the `clientDataJSON`, and the mathematical signature proving possession of the private key.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>23. Client Application sends authentication response to Relying Party Server</strong></summary>

The client serializes the assertion buffers and POSTs them to the backend API.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>24. Relying Party Server instructs FIDO2 Server to verify assertion</strong></summary>

The RP hands the assertion to the FIDO2 component. The FIDO2 server recalculates the client data hash, verifies the origin, checks for replay attacks by ensuring the challenge exactly matches the issued challenge, validates the signature counter to detect cloned authenticators, and finally validates the cryptographic signature using the stored public key.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>25. FIDO2 Server returns verification result</strong></summary>

If all checks pass and the mathematical signature is valid, the FIDO2 server returns a definitive success status to the RP backend.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>26. Relying Party Server returns authentication result to Client Application</strong></summary>

The RP establishes a secure session (e.g., issues a JWT or sets a secure, HTTP-only session cookie) and returns an HTTP 200 Success to the client.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>27. Client Application grants access to User</strong></summary>

The front-end routes the user into the authenticated area of the application. The entire end-to-end flow is completed without a shared secret ever being transmitted or stored on the server.

</details>

<br/>

---

### 14. Token Form Factor Taxonomy

This chapter classifies the physical and logical form factors used for authentication tokens — from software authenticator apps through hardware security keys to smart cards. It serves as the comprehensive reference for choosing the right token type for a given deployment scenario. The preceding chapters establish the cryptographic underpinnings — HOTP/TOTP shared-secret algorithms (§7–§8), WebAuthn asymmetric key ceremonies (§10), hardware key storage architectures (§11), and attestation mechanisms (§13). This chapter maps those capabilities onto the **concrete devices** that users carry, plug in, or tap.

The central tension in token selection is the tradeoff between **security ceiling** and **deployment friction**. Software authenticators are free and instantly deployable but inherit the security posture of the host device. Hardware security keys provide cryptographic isolation but require physical distribution and carry per-unit cost. Smart cards offer full PKI capabilities but demand reader infrastructure. No single form factor dominates — the optimal choice depends on the threat model, regulatory requirements, user population, and organisational logistics.

$$\text{Assurance} \propto \frac{\text{Hardware Binding Strength} \times \text{Protocol Sophistication}}{\text{User Friction} \times \text{Deployment Complexity}}$$

Software TOTP authenticators provide low assurance (AAL1) with minimal friction. Push-based authenticators occupy the middle ground (AAL2 when combined with a device-bound key, though often only AAL1 in practice). FIDO2 hardware security keys provide the highest practical assurance (AAL3) with moderate friction. Smart cards with biometric verification can also achieve AAL3 but introduce significant deployment complexity.

#### 14.1 Software Authenticators

Software authenticators are applications running on a user's smartphone or computer that generate or approve authentication challenges. They are the most widely deployed second-factor mechanism due to zero marginal cost and instant provisioning — any user with a smartphone can enroll within seconds by scanning a QR code.

##### 14.1.1 TOTP-Based Authenticators

TOTP-based authenticators implement RFC 6238 (§8) — generating time-based one-time passwords from a shared secret provisioned via the `otpauth://` URI (§7.2.2, §8.5). The authenticator stores the shared secret in the device's software keychain and computes a new 6- or 8-digit code every 30 seconds.

**Major implementations:**

| Authenticator | Vendor | Cloud Backup | Multi-Device Sync | Platforms |
|:-------------|:-------|:-------------|:------------------|:----------|
| **Google Authenticator** | Google | ✅ Google Account sync (since April 2023) | ✅ Via Google Account | iOS, Android |
| **Microsoft Authenticator** | Microsoft | ✅ iCloud (iOS) / Microsoft Account (Android) | ✅ Via cloud backup | iOS, Android |
| **Authy** | Twilio | ✅ Encrypted cloud backup | ✅ Multi-device enabled by default | iOS, Android, Desktop |
| **1Password** | 1Password | ✅ Via 1Password vault sync | ✅ All linked devices | iOS, Android, macOS, Windows, Linux, Browser |
| **Bitwarden Authenticator** | Bitwarden | ✅ Via Bitwarden vault sync (premium) | ✅ All linked devices | iOS, Android, Desktop, Browser |

**TOTP feature compliance across major authenticator apps:**

| Feature | Google Authenticator | Microsoft Authenticator | Authy (Twilio) | 1Password | Bitwarden | Aegis | FreeOTP+ |
|:--------|:--------------------|:------------------------|:---------------|:----------|:----------|:------|:---------|
| **HOTP (RFC 4226)** | Yes | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **SHA-256 / SHA-512** | No (SHA-1 only) | No (SHA-1 only) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Custom time step** | No (30s fixed) | No (30s fixed) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Custom digits (6/8)** | 6 only | 6 only | 6/8 | 6/8 | 6/8 | 6/8 | 6/8 |
| **Manual secret entry** | Yes | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Encrypted export** | No | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Open source** | No | No | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |

Google Authenticator and Microsoft Authenticator only support HMAC-SHA1 for TOTP computation. While RFC 6238 permits SHA-256 and SHA-512, most Relying Parties default to SHA-1 because they must interoperate with these dominant apps — effectively locking the TOTP ecosystem into SHA-1 for the foreseeable future.

**Interoperability:** TOTP tokens are fundamentally interchangeable — any RFC 6238-compliant authenticator can generate valid codes for any service that provisions a standard `otpauth://totp/` URI. A secret enrolled in Google Authenticator produces identical codes if the same secret is imported into Authy, 1Password, or any other compliant app. This interoperability is a direct consequence of the algorithm's simplicity: the only inputs are the shared secret, the current time, and the hash function (SHA-1/SHA-256/SHA-512). No vendor-specific protocol negotiation is involved.

**Fundamental security limitations:** TOTP has several inherent weaknesses that cannot be mitigated by implementation improvements. The shared secret is transmitted to the user during enrollment (typically via QR code) — any compromise of this secret allows OTP generation indefinitely until rotated. Critically, TOTP codes are not tied to the Relying Party — a phishing site can collect a valid TOTP code and replay it to the legitimate site within the 30-second window. Automated phishing tools (e.g., EvilGinx, Modlishka) proxy authentication requests in real-time, relaying TOTP codes within the validity window. This is the primary reason NIST SP 800-63B classifies TOTP as AAL1-restricted. With 6-digit codes, the OTP space is $10^6 = 1{,}000{,}000$ values — without robust rate limiting, an attacker who obtains the time window has a 0.1% success probability per attempt.

**Cloud backup implications:** Early TOTP authenticators (Google Authenticator pre-2023) stored secrets only on the local device — device loss meant permanent loss of all enrolled TOTP tokens, requiring re-enrollment with every service. Modern authenticators universally offer cloud backup, which improves recovery but introduces a new trust dependency: the cloud account (Google Account, iCloud, Microsoft Account) becomes the custodian of all TOTP secrets. Compromise of the cloud account → compromise of all backed-up TOTP secrets.

**Cloud backup architecture comparison:**

| Backup Service | Encryption Model | Key Derivation | Recovery Mechanism | Risk Profile |
|:---------------|:-----------------|:---------------|:-------------------|:-------------|
| **Google Authenticator** | Google Account OAuth token | Google-managed | Google Account recovery (email/SMS) | High — cloud account compromise exposes all TOTP secrets |
| **Microsoft Authenticator** | Azure cloud backup | Microsoft-managed | Microsoft Account recovery | High — tied to Microsoft identity lifecycle |
| **Authy** | Twilio cloud backup | App-specific key + device check | SMS verification + Authy desktop | Medium — SMS recovery is a known weakness |
| **1Password** | AES-256-GCM with SRP | PBKDF2-SHA256 (100K iterations) | Account recovery kit / biometric | Low — zero-knowledge architecture |
| **Bitwarden** | AES-256-CBC with Argon2id | Argon2id (user master password) | Master password reset (org-dependent) | Low — zero-knowledge; open-source audited |
| **Aegis encrypted export** | AES-256-GCM | PBKDF2 (user passphrase) | N/A (file-based, user-managed) | Lowest — no cloud dependency |

The critical distinction is whether the backup provider has access to the plaintext secrets. **Knowledge-of-secret providers** (Google, Microsoft, Authy) store encrypted TOTP secrets but control the encryption key — a compromise of the provider's infrastructure or the user's cloud account exposes all secrets. **Zero-knowledge providers** (1Password, Bitwarden) encrypt secrets with a key derived from the user's master password — the provider cannot decrypt the vault, even under legal compulsion.

##### 14.1.2 Push-Based Authenticators

Push-based authenticators replace the manual code-entry workflow with an approval-based flow: the identity provider sends a push notification to the user's enrolled device, the user reviews the authentication request and taps "Approve" (or enters a matching number), and the approval signal is transmitted back to the IdP.

**Protocol flow:**

```mermaid
---
config:
  themeVariables:
    noteBkgColor: "transparent"
    noteBorderColor: "transparent"
  sequence:
    messageAlign: left
    noteAlign: left
    actorMargin: 250
---
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as User (PC)
    participant IDP as IdP (Server)
    participant P as User's Phone<br/>(Authenticator)

    U->>IDP: Login attempt
    IDP->>P: Push notification
    Note right of P: Display: "Sign in to Acme Corp?"<br/>Number match: "Is the number 42?"
    P-->>IDP: Approve (signed)
    IDP-->>U: Auth success
    Note right of P: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
```

<details><summary><strong>1. User attempts login</strong></summary>

The user initiates a login attempt on their PC, providing their primary credentials (e.g., username and password) to the Identity Provider (IdP).

</details>
<details><summary><strong>2. IdP issues push notification</strong></summary>

Upon verifying the primary credentials, the IdP triggers an out-of-band push notification over its secure channel (e.g., APNs or FCM) to the user's enrolled mobile device.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>3. User approves request</strong></summary>

The authenticator app displays the request context. With number matching enforced, the user verifies the displayed number on the PC and enters it into the phone. The authenticator then signs the challenge with its device-bound private key.

</details>
<details><summary><strong>4. IdP grants authentication</strong></summary>

The IdP verifies the authenticator's cryptographic response. Once validated, the IdP issues the final authentication tokens and grants the session to the user's PC.

</details>

<br/>

**Major implementations:**

- **Microsoft Authenticator** — push approval for Entra ID (Azure AD) authentication. Supports **number matching** (§6.3.2): the login screen displays a two-digit number; the user must enter that number in the phone app to approve. Number matching was made the default for all Entra ID push notifications in May 2023 to mitigate MFA prompt bombing
- **Duo Mobile** (Cisco) — push approval for Duo-protected applications. Supports Duo Push with **Verified Duo Push** (number matching equivalent). The Duo push protocol is proprietary — Duo tokens work only with Duo-integrated services
- **Okta Verify** — push approval for Okta-protected applications. Supports number challenge and biometric verification before approval

**Push protocol technical comparison:**

| Dimension | Microsoft Authenticator (Entra ID) | Duo Mobile | Okta Verify |
|:----------|:-----------------------------------|:-----------|:------------|
| **Transport** | Azure Notification Hubs (APNs/FCM) | Duo Push Service (APNs/FCM) | Okta Push Service (APNs/FCM) |
| **Device registration** | Azure AD device object + certificate | Duo device record + activation | Okta device profile + key pair |
| **Cryptographic binding** | Device certificate (issued at enrollment) | RSA-2048 key pair (generated on device) | RSA-2048 or ECDSA key pair |
| **Number matching** | Optional (configurable per policy) | Standard (since 2022) | Optional (configurable) |
| **Location awareness** | Approximate (IP geolocation) | Yes (GPS + IP geolocation) | Yes (IP geolocation) |
| **Offline support** | Limited (cached OTP) | No | No |
| **Vendor lock-in** | High (requires Entra ID) | Medium (Duo Gateway required) | High (requires Okta platform) |

**Vendor lock-in:** Push-based authenticators are inherently vendor-locked — unlike TOTP where any compliant app works with any service, push tokens are bound to their vendor's notification infrastructure. A Duo push enrollment works only with Duo's service; Microsoft Authenticator push works only with Entra ID; Okta Verify push works only with Okta. The push notification channel, approval protocol, and cryptographic binding are all vendor-proprietary. This lock-in is a fundamental architectural difference from TOTP — organisations that adopt push authentication commit to a specific vendor's ecosystem.

**MFA prompt bombing mitigation:** Before number matching became standard, push-based authentication was vulnerable to **MFA fatigue attacks** (prompt bombing): an attacker who has compromised a user's password repeatedly triggers push notifications until the user, frustrated by the interruptions, taps "Approve" without scrutinising the request. Number matching eliminates this attack by requiring the user to actively read and enter a number displayed only on the legitimate login screen — an attacker triggering push notifications from their own session cannot predict the number the user sees.

Additional defence mechanisms beyond number matching:

1. **Location awareness** — displays the approximate geographic location of the login attempt, enabling users to deny requests from implausible locations
2. **Application context** — displays the application name the login attempt is targeting, useful when an attacker targets a specific application (e.g., VPN portal)
3. **Push notification rate limiting** — limits the number of push notifications per hour (e.g., 10/hour, 20/day), forcing attackers to slow down and increasing detection probability
4. **Push notification delay** — adds a mandatory delay (e.g., 30 seconds) before the Approve button becomes active, preventing reflexive approval
5. **Automatic block after N denials** — if a user denies M consecutive push notifications from the same session, automatically blocks the session and alerts the security team

Number matching alone reduces MFA fatigue success rate by an estimated 95%+. The additional mechanisms above provide defence-in-depth for high-security environments.

##### 14.1.3 Software Passkey Providers

Software passkey providers store FIDO2/WebAuthn credentials (§10.7) in software-backed vaults rather than in hardware security modules. The private key is generated in software, encrypted, and synchronised across the user's devices via the provider's encrypted sync fabric.

**Major implementations:**

- **iCloud Keychain** (Apple) — the default passkey provider on iOS, iPadOS, and macOS. Passkeys are synced across all Apple devices signed into the same Apple ID via iCloud Keychain's end-to-end encrypted sync. Keys are protected by the device's Secure Enclave (§11.2.1) at rest on each device, but the sync mechanism means the key exists on multiple devices — making it a **synced passkey** (§10.12)
- **Google Password Manager** — the default passkey provider on Android and Chrome. Passkeys sync across all Android devices and Chrome browsers signed into the same Google Account. Protected by the device's TEE/StrongBox at rest
- **1Password** — third-party passkey provider supporting iOS, Android, macOS, Windows, Linux, and browser extensions. Passkeys sync via 1Password's encrypted vault infrastructure. Available as a credential provider on iOS 17+ and Android 14+
- **Bitwarden** — open-source password manager with passkey support. Syncs via Bitwarden's encrypted vault. Available as a credential provider on iOS 17+ and Android 14+
- **Dashlane** — third-party passkey provider with cross-platform sync

**Passkey sync architecture comparison:**

| Provider | Sync Mechanism | E2EE Model | Cross-Platform | Recovery Model |
|:---------|:---------------|:-----------|:---------------|:---------------|
| **Apple iCloud Keychain** | iCloud Keychain | iCloud E2EE (account password + device key) | Apple devices only | Apple ID recovery |
| **Google Password Manager** | Google account sync | Google-managed encryption | Android, Chrome (all platforms) | Google account recovery |
| **1Password** | Vault sync | AES-256-GCM, user master password (zero-knowledge) | All platforms | Recovery kit / family organizer |
| **Bitwarden** | Vault sync | AES-256-CBC, Argon2id master key (zero-knowledge) | All platforms | Master password reset (org-dependent) |
| **Dashlane** | Vault sync | AES-256, user master password | All platforms | Account recovery via email |
| **NordPass** | Vault sync | XChaCha20, user master password (zero-knowledge) | All platforms | Account recovery via email |

**E2EE implementation details:** The encryption strength of each provider's sync mechanism is a critical security differentiator. Apple iCloud Keychain uses a combination of the user's iCloud account password and a device-specific key (Secure Enclave on iOS, T2 chip on Macs) — at least 2 Apple devices are required to enable iCloud Keychain, and Apple does not have access to plaintext passkeys. Google Password Manager's encryption keys are managed by Google — not a true zero-knowledge architecture, though Google states they do not access plaintext credentials. 1Password implements a true zero-knowledge model: the master password is never transmitted to 1Password's servers, the encryption key is derived using PBKDF2-SHA256 with 100,000 iterations combined with a 128-bit Secret Key, and even a full server compromise cannot expose vault contents:

$$K_{\text{vault}} = \text{PBKDF2}(\text{master\_password}, \text{salt}, 100{,}000) \oplus \text{Secret\_Key}$$

**Interoperability:** Unlike push-based authenticators, software passkey providers are interoperable at the protocol level — they all implement the same WebAuthn standard. A passkey created by 1Password is cryptographically indistinguishable from one created by iCloud Keychain or Google Password Manager. The relying party sees a standard WebAuthn assertion regardless of the provider. However, the choice of provider determines the sync fabric, recovery model, and device coverage — these are operational differences, not protocol differences.

**Security properties:** Software authenticators — whether TOTP, push, or passkey — are classified as "something you have," but the possession proof is only as strong as the device protection. The authenticator secret (TOTP shared key, push registration key, or passkey private key) resides in software on a general-purpose device. Device compromise — through malware, OS vulnerability, or physical access with known passcode — exposes the authenticator secret. This is the fundamental security ceiling of software authenticators: they cannot provide AAL3 assurance (§1.1) because the key is not bound to a non-exportable hardware boundary.

**Device-bound vs synced passkeys:**

| Dimension | Device-Bound Passkeys | Synced Passkeys |
|:----------|:---------------------|:----------------|
| **Storage location** | TPM, Secure Enclave, or security key | Cloud-encrypted vault |
| **Cross-device availability** | Single device only | All devices with vault access |
| **Max AAL** | AAL3 (hardware-bound key) | AAL2 (key material is synced) |
| **Phishing resistance** | Full (origin-bound) | Full (origin-bound) |
| **Loss scenario** | Credential loss if device is lost/destroyed | No loss (recoverable from vault) |
| **Recovery** | Re-enrollment required at each RP | Seamless (new device gets all passkeys) |
| **Enterprise control** | Full (MDM can manage device keys) | Limited (depends on vault provider) |
| **NIST SP 800-63B classification** | Hardware-protected authenticator (multi-factor) | Single-factor device-bound authenticator |

The key tradeoff: device-bound passkeys provide higher assurance (AAL3) but introduce a credential recovery problem — if the device is lost, the user must re-enroll at every Relying Party. Synced passkeys provide lower assurance (AAL2) but eliminate the recovery problem entirely.

**Passkey recovery models:**

| Recovery Model | Mechanism | Security Tradeoff | User Experience | Supported By |
|:---------------|:----------|:------------------|:----------------|:-------------|
| **Device-bound (no recovery)** | Re-enroll at each RP | Highest security | Worst UX (hours of re-enrollment) | All FIDO2 authenticators |
| **Cloud sync recovery** | New device downloads vault | Depends on cloud security (AAL2) | Best UX (seamless) | iCloud, Google, 1Password, Bitwarden |
| **Recovery code** | User stores backup code during enrollment | Code is single point of failure | Medium (code management burden) | Some enterprise deployments |
| **Enterprise admin recovery** | IT admin re-issues device-bound credential | Admin has high privilege | Medium (requires IT ticket) | Enterprise MDM + passkey |
| **Multi-device backup** | Keys registered on multiple devices simultaneously | Key exists on multiple devices | Good (redundancy without cloud) | FIDO2 allows multiple authenticators |

#### 14.2 Hardware Security Keys

Hardware security keys are dedicated physical devices that perform cryptographic authentication operations inside a tamper-resistant secure element. The private key is generated inside and never leaves the hardware boundary — even the device's own firmware cannot extract it. This non-exportable key property is the defining characteristic that separates hardware keys from software authenticators and enables AAL3 compliance.

##### 14.2.1 YubiKey (Yubico)

The YubiKey is the dominant hardware security key in enterprise and consumer deployments. Its distinguishing feature is **multi-protocol support** — a single YubiKey device supports six distinct authentication protocols, enabling it to serve as a universal authenticator across modern and legacy systems.

**Protocol support across the YubiKey 5 Series:**

| Protocol | Standard | Function | Credential Capacity |
|:---------|:---------|:---------|:-------------------|
| **FIDO2 / WebAuthn** | W3C WebAuthn, FIDO Alliance CTAP2 (§10) | Phishing-resistant passwordless and second-factor authentication. Primary use case for modern deployments | Up to 100 discoverable credentials (passkeys) with firmware 5.7+; unlimited non-discoverable (server-side) credentials |
| **FIDO U2F** | FIDO Alliance U2F 1.2 | Legacy second-factor authentication — precursor to FIDO2. Still widely supported | Unlimited (server-side credentials) |
| **PIV (Smart Card)** | FIPS 201, NIST SP 800-73 (§14.3) | Certificate-based authentication, digital signatures, email encryption. Windows smart card logon, macOS smart card pairing | 24 certificate slots (4 mandatory PIV + 20 retired key management slots) |
| **OATH HOTP/TOTP** | RFC 4226 (§7), RFC 6238 (§8) | Hardware-stored OTP credentials — TOTP codes generated on the YubiKey rather than in a phone app | Up to 64 OATH credentials (firmware 5.7+) |
| **OpenPGP** | OpenPGP Card 3.4 | PGP key storage for email encryption, code signing, SSH authentication. Private keys generated in or imported to the YubiKey's secure element | 3 key slots (signing, encryption, authentication) + attestation |
| **YubiOTP** | Yubico proprietary | 128-bit AES-encrypted OTP string. The YubiKey generates a 44-character Modhex-encoded OTP containing an encrypted payload with a session counter, timestamp, and usage counter. Validated by Yubico's YubiCloud or a self-hosted validation server | 2 OTP configuration slots |

**Credential capacity per protocol:**

| Protocol | Max Credentials | Storage Mechanism | Notes |
|:---------|:---------------:|:------------------|:------|
| **FIDO2 (discoverable)** | 25+ resident keys | Secure Element NVM | Each credential ~200 bytes; actual count depends on RP ID length |
| **FIDO2 (server-side)** | Unlimited | Server stores credential ID + public key | Key generates credential on-demand from RP ID + user handle |
| **U2F** | Unlimited | Server stores key handle + public key | Same on-demand generation model as FIDO2 non-discoverable |
| **PIV** | 4 slots (9A, 9C, 9D, 9E) + 20 retired | Smart card NVM | Yubico adds 20 retired key management slots beyond the 4 mandatory PIV slots |
| **OATH (TOTP/HOTP)** | Up to 64 | Secure Element NVM | Firmware 5.7+; configurable via ykman CLI |
| **OpenPGP** | 3 key slots (sign, encrypt, auth) | Smart card NVM | RSA up to 4096-bit, ECC (ed25519, cv25519, p256, p384) |
| **YubiOTP** | 2 configuration slots | Static configuration | AES-128 encrypted OTP; deprecated in favour of FIDO2 |

**YubiKey 5 Series model variants:**

All YubiKey 5 Series models support all six protocols listed above. The differences between models are purely physical — form factor and connectivity:

| Model | Connector(s) | Form Factor | Target Use Case |
|:------|:------------|:-----------|:----------------|
| **YubiKey 5 NFC** | USB-A + NFC | Keychain fob | Cross-platform: USB-A desktops + NFC mobile devices |
| **YubiKey 5C NFC** | USB-C + NFC | Keychain fob | Modern laptops (USB-C) + NFC mobile. Most versatile model |
| **YubiKey 5Ci** | USB-C + Lightning | Keychain fob | Apple ecosystem: Lightning iPhones/iPads + USB-C Macs |
| **YubiKey 5 Nano** | USB-A | Ultra-compact | Semi-permanent insertion in desktop USB-A port |
| **YubiKey 5C Nano** | USB-C | Ultra-compact | Semi-permanent insertion in laptop USB-C port |

**Specialised variants:**

- **YubiKey Bio — FIDO Edition** — includes an integrated fingerprint sensor for on-device biometric user verification. Fingerprint templates are stored in the YubiKey's secure element and never leave the device. Supports FIDO2/WebAuthn and U2F only (no PIV, OATH, OpenPGP, or YubiOTP). The biometric verification occurs entirely on the key — the host device's biometric system is not involved. Available in USB-A and USB-C
- **YubiKey 5 FIPS Series** — FIPS 140-2 Level 1 and Level 2 validated. Same protocol support as the standard 5 Series but with FIPS-specific configurations: unique per-key attestation certificates, enforced power-up self-tests, and restricted algorithm sets (FIPS-approved algorithms only). Required for US federal deployments under FISMA
- **YubiKey 5 Enhanced PIN Series** — pre-configured with strengthened PIN policies: minimum 6-character PIN length, automatic PIN complexity enforcement, and `alwaysUV` (always user verify) enabled by default. Targets organisations with stringent compliance and audit requirements

**Physical durability:** All YubiKey models are IP68 rated (water-resistant and dustproof), crush-resistant (withstands > 500N crushing force), and contain no batteries — powered entirely by USB bus power or NFC electromagnetic field. No moving parts. Expected operational lifespan exceeds 10 years under normal use.

**Enterprise deployment — Yubico Enterprise Subscription:**

Yubico offers the **YubiEnterprise Subscription** (YES) for organisations deploying YubiKeys at scale. YES provides:

- **YubiEnterprise Delivery** — direct shipment of YubiKeys to end users (home addresses, office locations), managed via a web portal. Eliminates IT department physical distribution logistics
- **Key lifecycle management** — pre-registration of FIDO2 credentials during shipment, enabling users to authenticate with their new key immediately upon receipt without an in-person IT visit
- **Replacement key flow** — self-service key replacement: user reports lost/broken key → new key shipped → old key's FIDO2 credentials revoked
- **Fleet management dashboard** — inventory tracking, deployment status, and usage analytics across the key population
- **Policy enforcement** — enforce PIN complexity, user presence requirements, and restrict allowed protocols per key serial number
- **Remote revocation** — revoke a YubiKey's FIDO2 credentials across all integrated Relying Parties (requires pre-registered YES subscription)
- **Compliance reporting** — dashboards showing enrollment rates, key usage patterns, and authentication success/failure metrics
- **Pricing** — subscription-based, approximately $2–5 per key per year depending on feature tier and volume

**MDM integration — PIV certificate auto-enrollment:** Microsoft Intune can automate PIV certificate enrollment for YubiKey devices through a five-step workflow: (1) the user connects their YubiKey to their Windows/macOS device, and Intune detects the PIV card via the native smart card stack; (2) Intune generates a CSR (Certificate Signing Request) on behalf of the YubiKey's PIV slot 9A — the private key never leaves the YubiKey; (3) the CSR is submitted to the enterprise PKI (typically Microsoft AD CS or a third-party CA), which issues a certificate; (4) Intune loads the issued certificate into the YubiKey's PIV slot 9A via the Windows Smart Card resource manager or Yubico PIV tool; (5) the certificate's Subject Alternative Name (SAN) is mapped to the user's Active Directory object, enabling SSO to domain-joined resources. This automation reduces enrollment time from ~30 minutes (manual) to ~5 minutes per user.

##### 14.2.2 Google Titan Security Key

The Google Titan Security Key is a **purpose-built FIDO2 security key** — deliberately limited to a single protocol (FIDO2/WebAuthn + legacy U2F) with no support for PIV, OATH, OpenPGP, or proprietary OTP schemes. This single-purpose design reflects Google's philosophy that a security key should do one thing and do it well, minimising the attack surface.

**Specifications:**

| Feature | Detail |
|:--------|:-------|
| **Protocols** | FIDO2/WebAuthn, FIDO U2F |
| **Passkey storage** | Up to 250 discoverable credentials (passkeys) — significantly more than the YubiKey 5's 100 |
| **Connectivity** | USB-A/NFC variant; USB-C/NFC variant |
| **Secure element** | Google-designed firmware on a hardware secure element. Google verifies hardware integrity at manufacturing |
| **NFC** | ✅ Supported on both variants — enables mobile authentication via tap |
| **PIV / Smart Card** | ❌ Not supported |
| **OATH HOTP/TOTP** | ❌ Not supported |
| **OpenPGP** | ❌ Not supported |
| **On-device management** | ❌ No built-in passkey browsing or deletion tool — management via browser settings |
| **Price** | ~$30 (USB-A/NFC), ~$35 (USB-C/NFC) |

**Google Advanced Protection Program (APP):** The Titan Key is a core component of Google's APP — the highest-security setting for Google Accounts, designed for journalists, political campaigns, and other high-risk users. APP requires FIDO2 security keys for all authentication and disables all non-FIDO authentication methods (SMS, TOTP, phone prompts), making the account immune to phishing, SIM swapping, and prompt bombing attacks.

##### 14.2.3 SoloKeys (Solo V2)

SoloKeys Solo V2 is an **open-source hardware security key** — both firmware and hardware designs are publicly available for community auditing, enabling independent verification of the key's security properties.

**Specifications:**

| Feature | Detail |
|:--------|:-------|
| **Protocols** | FIDO2/WebAuthn, FIDO U2F |
| **Firmware** | Written in Rust — memory-safe language that eliminates entire classes of firmware vulnerabilities (buffer overflows, use-after-free). Open-source on GitHub |
| **Hardware design** | Open-source — PCB schematics and component specifications publicly available |
| **FIDO certification** | First open-source security key to achieve FIDO2 certification |
| **Connectivity** | USB-A and USB-C variants; NFC on some models |
| **Target audience** | Privacy-conscious users, security researchers, and organisations that require verifiable firmware — enabling supply-chain transparency that closed-source keys cannot provide |

**Open-source value proposition:** The core differentiator of SoloKeys is **verifiability**. With closed-source security keys (YubiKey, Titan), users trust that the firmware behaves as documented — but cannot independently verify this claim. SoloKeys' open-source model enables:

- Independent security audits by third parties
- Community review of cryptographic implementations
- Firmware reproducibility — users can build the firmware from source and compare it to the shipped binary
- Supply-chain transparency — the full hardware bill of materials is public

##### 14.2.4 Feitian BioPass FIDO2

Feitian's BioPass series are **FIDO2 security keys with integrated capacitive fingerprint sensors** — enabling on-device biometric user verification without relying on the host device's biometric system.

**Key models:**

| Model | Connector | Fingerprint Capacity | Credential Capacity | Certification |
|:------|:----------|:--------------------|:--------------------|:-------------|
| **K26** | USB-A/USB-C | Up to 32 templates | Up to 128 credentials | FIPS 140-2 Level 2 |
| **K45** | USB-A | Up to 50 templates | Up to 128 credentials | FIPS 140-2 Level 2 |
| **K49 Pro** | USB-C | Up to 50 templates | Up to 128 credentials | — |
| **K50 Pro** | USB-A | Up to 50 templates | Up to 128 credentials | — |

Fingerprint data is stored within the key's embedded security chip and never transmitted to the host device. The on-device fingerprint verification satisfies the WebAuthn user verification (UV) requirement — the authenticator sets the `UV` flag in the authenticator data without involving the host OS's biometric subsystem. This architecture is valuable in shared-device environments (kiosks, shared workstations) where the host device's biometric enrollment cannot be trusted — the security key carries its own biometric identity.

##### 14.2.5 Hardware Security Key Vendor Comparison

The hardware security key market has expanded beyond Yubico, with multiple vendors offering FIDO2-compatible devices at various price points and feature sets:

| Dimension | YubiKey 5 NFC | Google Titan | SoloKeys V2 | Feitian BioPass | Entersekt | Nitrokey 3A |
|:----------|:--------------|:-------------|:------------|:----------------|:----------|:------------|
| **FIDO2/WebAuthn** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **NFC support** | Yes | Yes | No | Yes | No | No |
| **USB-A / USB-C** | Model variants | USB-A/USB-C variants | Both | Model variants | USB-C | Model variants |
| **Fingerprint sensor** | Bio model only | No | No | Yes (K26, K45) | No | No |
| **PIV / smart card** | Yes | No | No | No | No | Yes (OpenPGP) |
| **OATH (TOTP)** | Yes | No | No | No | No | No |
| **OpenPGP** | Yes | No | No | No | No | Yes |
| **Open source firmware** | No | No | Yes (Rust) | No | No | Yes (C/Rust) |
| **FIDO Alliance certified** | L1 + L2 | L1 + L2 | L1 + L2 | L1 + L2 | L1 | L1 + L2 |
| **FIPS 140-3** | FIPS model | No | No | No | No | No |
| **Unit price (USD)** | $45–55 | $30–35 | $25–30 | $25–40 | $30–45 | $30–40 |
| **Resident key capacity** | 25+ | ~250 | 25 | 25 | 50 | 25+ |
| **Enterprise management** | YubiEnterprise | No | No | Feitian Management | Entersekt Console | No |
| **Secure element** | NXP A700x | NXP (undisclosed) | NXP A40AR | Infineon SLE 78 | NXP | NXP A40AR |

**FIDO Alliance certification levels:** The FIDO Alliance operates a three-tier certification program:

- **Level 1 (L1) — Functional:** Verifies correct implementation of the FIDO2/WebAuthn specification (registration, authentication, attestation, credential management). All commercially available FIDO2 keys must achieve L1
- **Level 2 (L2) — Security:** Verifies required security measures including secure boot chain, protected key storage, updatable firmware, resistance to basic physical attacks, attestation certificate chain validation, and PIN/UV protocol implementation
- **Level 3 (L3) — Formal:** Requires formal security evaluation equivalent to Common Criteria EAL4+ or higher. As of early 2026, no consumer hardware key has achieved L3. The YubiKey 5 FIPS series meets FIPS 140-3 Level 1 (overall) with Level 3 physical security, providing equivalent assurance in some regulatory contexts

$$\text{FIDO L3} \approx \text{Common Criteria EAL4+} \approx \text{FIPS 140-3 Level 3 (physical)}$$

##### 14.2.6 Hardware Token OTP Devices (RSA SecurID)

Hardware OTP tokens are **standalone, dedicated devices** that generate one-time passwords without any connectivity to the user's other devices. The RSA SecurID token is the most widely deployed hardware OTP token in enterprise environments — a category that predates both FIDO2 and smartphone-based authenticators.

**RSA SecurID architecture:**

Each RSA SecurID hardware token contains a unique **128-bit symmetric seed** (shared secret) that was programmed into the token's tamper-resistant chip at manufacturing. The token and the RSA Authentication Manager server both know this seed. The token computes a new OTP every 60 seconds using RSA's proprietary time-based algorithm (functionally similar to TOTP but predating RFC 6238 by decades):

```
TokenCode = RSA_Algorithm(seed, current_time)
Passcode  = PIN + TokenCode
```

The user's passcode is the concatenation of their memorised PIN and the currently displayed token code — providing two-factor authentication (something you know + something you have) in a single entry.

**RSA SecurID hardware token models:**

| Model | Form Factor | Features |
|:------|:-----------|:---------|
| **SID700** | Key fob | Compact OTP display. Standard enterprise deployment model |
| **SID800** | Key fob with USB | USB connector + smart card chip. Supports automatic token code entry via USB and digital certificate storage |
| **SID900** | Key fob | Transaction signing capabilities for financial applications — displays transaction details for user confirmation before generating a signing code |

**Operational characteristics:**

- **No connectivity** — the token operates entirely offline. No USB data transfer (SID700), no Bluetooth, no NFC (for authentication). The token requires no batteries from the user — it contains an integral lifetime battery (typically 4–7 years depending on display usage)
- **Proprietary ecosystem** — RSA SecurID tokens work exclusively with RSA Authentication Manager. The token seed records are delivered to the customer as encrypted seed files that must be imported into the Authentication Manager server. There is no standard interchange format — this is a fully closed, vendor-proprietary system
- **Declining deployment** — hardware OTP tokens are being replaced by smartphone-based authenticators (TOTP apps, push notifications) and FIDO2 security keys in most new deployments. The per-unit cost ($50–$100 per token), seed file management complexity, physical distribution logistics, and inability to support phishing-resistant authentication make hardware OTP tokens a legacy technology. However, they remain widely deployed in regulated environments (banking, government, critical infrastructure) where long-established compliance frameworks reference RSA SecurID specifically

**HOTP token protocol integration.** Hardware OTP tokens (whether RSA SecurID or OATH-compliant HOTP/TOTP devices) integrate with enterprise authentication infrastructure through standardised protocols:

| Protocol | Integration Method | Server Requirement | Notes |
|:---------|:-------------------|:-------------------|:------|
| **RADIUS** (RFC 2865) | RADIUS server validates OTP via shared secret + counter | FreeRADIUS with OATH module; commercial RADIUS servers | Most common for VPN, Wi-Fi, network access |
| **SAML** | SAML IdP delegates OTP validation to RADIUS or OATH server | Shibboleth, Keycloak, Azure AD with MFA adapter | OTP presented as second factor after SSO |
| **OIDC** | OIDC provider prompts for OTP after primary authentication | Keycloak, Auth0, Okta with HOTP MFA module | Standard OIDC + ACR values for step-up |
| **LDAP/PAM** | PAM module validates OTP against OATH server | OATH-PAM, privacyIDEA | Linux/Unix system-level authentication |

Note that WebAuthn/FIDO2 is fundamentally incompatible with OTP integration — it uses public-key cryptography rather than shared secrets, and operates at a different protocol layer. HOTP and FIDO2/WebAuthn cannot share credentials; a user enrolling both has two independent second-factor credentials (see below).

##### 14.2.7 HOTP and FIDO2/WebAuthn Coexistence Strategies

As organisations adopt FIDO2/WebAuthn passkeys for phishing-resistant authentication, HOTP tokens remain in service alongside them. A coexistence strategy is needed:

| Strategy | Description | Recommended For |
|:---------|:-----------|:----------------|
| **Tiered MFA** | WebAuthn for high-risk users/systems; HOTP for low-risk or legacy systems | Enterprises with mixed risk profiles |
| **Fallback chain** | WebAuthn as primary; HOTP as fallback when security key unavailable | Consumer services maximising accessibility |
| **Parallel acceptance** | Accept either WebAuthn or HOTP for all users; incentivise WebAuthn migration | Organisations in early WebAuthn adoption phase |
| **Protocol-layered** | HOTP used for VPN/network access (RADIUS); WebAuthn for web applications (OIDC/SAML) | Enterprises with separate network and application authentication |

HOTP and FIDO2/WebAuthn operate at different protocol layers and cannot share credentials. A user enrolling both a HOTP token and a FIDO2 security key has two independent second-factor credentials registered with the authentication server. The server's authentication logic must support both verification paths and enforce appropriate policies (e.g., "accept any registered MFA method" vs. "require FIDO2 for admin accounts").

#### 14.3 Smart Cards and PIV (NIST SP 800-73)

Smart cards are credit card-sized devices containing an embedded secure element (§11.2.4) that performs cryptographic operations — key generation, digital signing, decryption, and certificate storage. Unlike security keys (which are optimised for FIDO2 web authentication), smart cards provide **full PKI (Public Key Infrastructure)** capabilities: they store X.509 certificates, support certificate-based authentication (§6.4), and enable qualified electronic signatures.

**Physical and communication interfaces:** Smart cards communicate with readers through two primary interface standards:

*Contact interface (ISO 7816):* Credit-card dimensions (85.60 × 53.98 × 0.76 mm, CR80 standard) with an 8-contact electrical interface (VCC, RST, CLK, GND, I/O, and reserved contacts). Communication uses a half-duplex serial protocol (T=0 byte-level or T=1 block-level) at a default clock speed of 3.57 MHz, with extended speeds up to 1.2 Mbps on modern cards. The fundamental communication unit is the APDU (Application Protocol Data Unit) — Command APDUs from reader to card, Response APDUs from card to reader — with extended length supporting responses up to 65,535 bytes.

*Contactless interface (ISO 14443):* Operating at 13.56 MHz with two modulation types — Type A uses ASK 100% modulation with Miller/Manchester coding (used by MIFARE, PIV contactless, CAC), while Type B uses ASK 10% modulation with NRZ-L/BPSK coding (used by some national ID implementations). Both support data rates from 106 kbps up to 848 kbps with a typical operating range of 0–10 cm. Most modern PIV and CAC cards support both contact and contactless interfaces. The contactless interface enables tap-to-login scenarios but introduces additional attack vectors (relay attacks, eavesdropping).

##### 14.3.1 PIV: Personal Identity Verification (FIPS 201/NIST SP 800-73)

PIV is the US federal government standard for employee and contractor identity credentials. Defined by FIPS 201 (Personal Identity Verification of Federal Employees and Contractors) and NIST SP 800-73 (Interfaces for Personal Identity Verification), PIV specifies the data model, cryptographic operations, and interoperability requirements for identity cards used in federal systems.

**PIV card architecture:**

A PIV card is a contact smart card (ISO 7816) containing:

- **Four mandatory certificate slots:**

| Slot | Certificate | Purpose | PIN Required |
|:-----|:-----------|:--------|:-------------|
| **9A** | PIV Authentication | Authenticates the cardholder to systems and applications. Used for Windows smart card logon, VPN authentication, and web application access | ✅ PIN required for private key operations |
| **9C** | Digital Signature | Creates digital signatures on documents and emails. Non-repudiation — the cardholder explicitly intended to sign | ✅ PIN required for every signature operation (no caching) |
| **9D** | Key Management | Key establishment (key agreement/key transport) for encrypting emails and files. Enables decryption of encrypted content | ✅ PIN required |
| **9E** | Card Authentication | Authenticates the PIV card itself (not the cardholder) for physical access control. Used by door readers and turnstiles | ❌ PIN not required — contactless access |

- **CHUID (Card Holder Unique Identifier)** — a data object containing the cardholder's FASC-N (Federal Agency Smart Credential Number), the card's UUID, and an expiration date. The CHUID is digitally signed by the card issuer and can be read without PIN verification — enabling quick visual and electronic verification of card validity
- **Biometric data** — fingerprint templates and/or facial image stored on the card for biometric matching during high-assurance authentication
- **PIN protection** — the PIV Authentication certificate's private key requires PIN entry before use. The PIN is verified by the card's on-chip operating system — never transmitted to the host. After a configurable number of failed PIN attempts (typically 6–10), the card locks and must be reset by the issuing authority

**Complete PIV data model:**

| Data Element | PIV OID | Access | Required | Description |
|:-------------|:--------|:-------|:--------:|:------------|
| **Card Holder Unique Identifier (CHUID)** | 0x5FC101 | Read (no PIN) | Yes | FASC-N, GUID, expiry, issuer signature |
| **Card Holder Fingerprints** | 0x5FC103 | Read (opt. PIN) | Conditional | Minutiae data for biometric verification |
| **Printed Information** | 0x5FC102 | Read (no PIN) | Yes | Name, org, FASC-N, expiry |
| **Card Holder Facial Image** | 0x5FC105 | Read (opt. PIN) | Conditional | JPEG photo for on-card biometric comparison |
| **Security Object** | 0x5FC106 | Read (no PIN) | Yes | Signed CHUID, certifies card authenticity |
| **Discovery Object** | 0x7E | Read (no PIN) | Yes | Card capabilities (PIN policy, supported algorithms) |
| **Key History Object** | 0x5FC10C | Read (no PIN) | Conditional | Maps retired certificates to key references |
| **Retired Certificates (R1–R20)** | 0x5FC10D–0x5FC120 | Varies | No | Up to 20 archived certificates |
| **Biometric Information Template** | 0x5FC10E | Match on-card | Conditional | On-card biometric comparison data |

##### 14.3.2 PIV-I: PIV-Interoperable

PIV-Interoperable (PIV-I) extends the PIV credential model to **non-federal organisations** — state/local governments, first responders, contractors operating at federal facilities, and critical infrastructure operators. PIV-I cards follow the same SP 800-73 interface specification and data model as federal PIV cards, enabling cross-organisation interoperability at federal facilities. The PKI trust chain for PIV-I cards is rooted in the Federal Bridge Certification Authority (FBCA) — enabling federal relying parties to validate PIV-I certificates through the same trust infrastructure used for federal PIV cards.

##### 14.3.3 CAC: Common Access Card

The Common Access Card (CAC) is the US Department of Defense's implementation of PIV. The CAC is a multi-function credential combining:

- **PKI smart card** — contains the same certificate slots as PIV (authentication, signature, key management, card authentication) for logical access to DoD networks, systems, and applications
- **Photo identification** — printed photo, name, rank/grade, and affiliation on the card face
- **Physical access control** — contactless chip (ISO 14443) or barcode for building entry and secure area access
- **Geneva Conventions identification** — for uniformed military personnel, the CAC serves as the Geneva Conventions identification card under international humanitarian law

Over 3.5 million active CAC cards are issued to DoD military personnel, civilian employees, and eligible contractors.

**CAC vs PIV comparison:**

| Dimension | PIV (FIPS 201) | CAC (DoD) |
|:----------|:---------------|:----------|
| **Scope** | Federal civilian employees/contractors | DoD military, civilian, contractor personnel |
| **Certificate slots** | 4 (9A, 9C, 9D, 9E) | 3 (PIV: 9A, 9D; DoD: 0x10, 0x15) |
| **PKI hierarchy** | Federal PKI (FBCA/Cross-cert) | DoD PKI + External CA Authority |
| **Personnel identifier** | GUID (16 bytes) | EDIPI (10 digits) |
| **Middleware** | PIV middleware (Windows native) | ActivClient (third-party) or OS native |
| **Biometric** | On-card matching (optional) | Off-card matching (DoD AFIS) |
| **Card life** | 3–6 years | 3 years (renewable) |
| **Issuance volume** | ~5M cards | ~3.5M+ active cards |
| **Contactless** | ISO 14443 (optional) | ISO 14443 (mandatory on newer cards) |

##### 14.3.4 eIDAS Qualified Electronic Signature Creation Devices (QSCD)

Under the EU's eIDAS Regulation (Regulation (EU) No 910/2014), smart cards certified under Common Criteria (§11.3.2) can serve as **Qualified Signature Creation Devices (QSCDs)** — the hardware required to create **qualified electronic signatures (QES)** that carry the same legal effect as handwritten signatures across all EU member states.

**QSCD requirements:**

- The signature creation data (private key) must be generated and stored within the QSCD — it must never exist outside the device's security boundary
- The QSCD must be certified under a recognised protection profile (typically BSI-CC-PP-0084 — Secure Signature Creation Device) at EAL4+ or higher
- The QSCD must ensure that the signature creation data can be used only by the legitimate signatory (via PIN, biometric, or equivalent activation mechanism)

Common QSCD implementations include smart cards from vendors such as Gemalto (now Thales), Infineon (SECORA cards), and NXP (JCOP-based cards), certified under the relevant BSI or ANSSI protection profiles.

**QSCD certification paths by form factor:**

| Form Factor | QSCD Eligible | Certification Path | Typical Use |
|:------------|:--------------:|:-------------------|:------------|
| **PIV Smart Card (FIPS 201)** | Conditional | FIPS 140-3 + SOG-IS CC evaluation | eIDAS-compliant signatures if QTSP-operated |
| **YubiKey 5 FIPS** | Conditional | FIPS 140-3 Level 1 (physical Level 3) | eIDAS signatures in qualified trust service context |
| **Software passkey** | No | Not applicable | Not a QSCD — key material is not in certified hardware |
| **Custom smart card with CC evaluation** | Yes | SOG-IS CC EAL4+ augmented | Qualified electronic signatures |
| **Cloud HSM-backed signing** | Conditional | Common Criteria or FIPS 140-3 for the HSM | Remote QSCD if qualified trust service provides it |

##### 14.3.5 Smart Card Operating Systems

Smart card operating systems provide the runtime environment for cryptographic applications (applets) running on the card's secure element:

| OS | Description | Market Position |
|:---|:-----------|:---------------|
| **JavaCard** | Stripped-down JVM providing sandboxed applet execution (§11.2.4). Dominant runtime — supports applet isolation, firewall, atomic transactions, and comprehensive cryptographic API | ~70%+ of global smart card SE deployments |
| **MULTOS** | Multi-application OS with formal security verification. Applications run in isolated virtual machines. Used in high-assurance government and banking deployments | Niche — primarily UK government and select banking |
| **NXP JCOP** | NXP's implementation of JavaCard OS for their secure element chips. Widely used in government ID cards, PIV cards, and payment cards | Major — NXP SEs are the most widely deployed SE hardware |
| **Infineon SECORA** | Infineon's JavaCard-based secure element platform. Optimised for IoT, payment, and government identity applications | Growing — strong in European government ID |

**JavaCard technical deep dive:**

*Applet lifecycle:* Every JavaCard application follows a strict five-step lifecycle managed by the card's operating system: (1) **Installation** — the applet's CAP file is loaded onto the card via the Card Manager; (2) **Registration** — the applet registers one or more Application Identifiers (AIDs) with the JavaCard Runtime Environment (JCRE), and incoming APDUs are dispatched based on AID; (3) **Selection** — when the card receives a SELECT APDU matching the applet's AID, the JCRE invokes `select()`, and if it returns `true`, the applet becomes active; (4) **Processing** — the active applet processes incoming APDUs via its `process()` method, dispatching to handlers based on the INS byte; (5) **Deselection** — when a different AID is selected, `deselect()` is called and the applet releases resources.

*JavaCard firewall:* The firewall enforces isolation between applets on multi-application cards (e.g., PIV + PIV-I + OpenPGP on a single YubiKey). Each applet has its own execution context — applets cannot directly access objects belonging to other applets. The only sanctioned inter-applet communication mechanism is the **Shareable Interface Object (SIO)**, which allows controlled data exchange. The JCRE's bytecode verifier ensures that applet code cannot bypass firewall restrictions at the bytecode level, preventing pointer manipulation or direct memory access.

*Transaction mechanism:* The JavaCard Transaction API ensures atomic updates to persistent data (EEPROM/Flash). Without transactions, a power loss or card removal during a write could leave the card in an inconsistent state. All writes within a transaction are committed together, or none are — providing atomicity and durability. Once committed, writes survive power loss. Transactions have a limited buffer size (typically the entire EEPROM for a single-applet card); exceeding it throws `SystemException.NO_RESOURCE`.

**JavaCard cryptographic API (selected algorithms):**

| Algorithm | Key Generation | Signature | Encryption | Key Agreement |
|:----------|:-------------:|:---------:|:----------:|:-------------:|
| **RSA** | 1024–4096 bit | PKCS#1 v1.5, PSS | PKCS#1 v1.5, OAEP | No |
| **ECDSA** | P-192 to P-521 | SHA-256/384/512 | No | ECDH |
| **EdDSA** | Ed25519, Ed448 | EdDSA (pure) | No | X25519 |
| **AES** | 128, 192, 256 | N/A | CBC, ECB, GCM, CCM | N/A |
| **SHA** | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A |

##### 14.3.6 GlobalPlatform Card Management

GlobalPlatform is the industry standard for managing applications on smart cards — defining secure messaging, applet lifecycle management, and card lifecycle states.

**SCP03 (Secure Channel Protocol 03)** is the mandatory secure channel protocol for GlobalPlatform card management, providing mutual authentication between the card and the card management system (CMS), plus encryption and integrity protection for all management commands. The SCP03 key set consists of three keys:

- **ENC key** — AES-128 or AES-256, used for APDU command/response encryption
- **MAC key** — AES-128 or AES-256, used for APDU command/response integrity (CMAC)
- **DEK key** — AES-128 or AES-256, used for encrypting sensitive data (e.g., new keys being loaded)

Key diversification ensures each card has a unique key set derived from a master key:

$$K_{\text{card}} = \text{KDF}(K_{\text{master}}, \text{card\_serial\_number})$$

where KDF is typically AES-CMAC-based key derivation as specified in NIST SP 800-108.

**Card lifecycle states:** GlobalPlatform defines five states that control permitted operations:

| State | Allowed Operations | Notes |
|:------|:-------------------|:------|
| **OP_READY** | Initialize card, load Issuer Security Domain | Factory state |
| **INITIALIZED** | Secure channel establishment, load first applet | After personalization |
| **SECURED** | Load/install/delete applets, manage keys | Production state |
| **CARD_LOCKED** | Unblock only (with issuer keys) | Locked due to failed auth |
| **TERMINATED** | No operations allowed | End-of-life |

##### 14.3.7 Smart Card vs. Security Key

The boundary between smart cards and security keys has blurred — particularly because the YubiKey supports both paradigms on a single device. The fundamental distinction remains:

| Dimension | Smart Card (PIV/CAC) | Security Key (FIDO2) |
|:----------|:--------------------|:--------------------|
| **Primary protocol** | X.509 certificate-based authentication (§6.4) — PKI model with certificate chains, CRLs, and OCSP | WebAuthn/CTAP2 (§10) — origin-bound public key authentication without certificates |
| **Credential model** | Full PKI — certificates issued by a CA, with validity periods, revocation, and renewal lifecycle | Self-contained key pairs — each RP gets a unique public key; no CA infrastructure required |
| **Signature capability** | ✅ Digital signatures with legal effect (qualified electronic signatures under eIDAS) | ❌ WebAuthn assertions are ephemeral authentication proofs, not durable digital signatures |
| **Infrastructure requirement** | Card reader (contact ISO 7816) or NFC reader (contactless ISO 14443). PKI infrastructure: CA, certificate lifecycle management | USB port or NFC. No PKI infrastructure — each service manages its own public key store |
| **Physical form factor** | Credit card sized (ID-1 form factor, 85.6 × 53.98 mm) | Key fob or nano form factor |
| **Enterprise management** | Card management system (CMS) for issuance, certificate lifecycle, PIN management | FIDO2 key registration via IdP admin console or self-service portal |
| **AAL ceiling** | **AAL3** — hardware-bound, non-exportable keys with certificate-based authentication | **AAL3** — hardware-bound, non-exportable keys with verifier impersonation resistance |

The YubiKey bridges both paradigms — its PIV applet (§14.2.1) provides smart card functionality (certificate slots, PIN-protected private keys, Windows smart card logon) alongside FIDO2 web authentication.
