Organisations migrating from PIV-based authentication to FIDO2 can use the same physical device throughout the transition.

#### 14.4 Security Properties Comparison Matrix

The following matrix provides a comprehensive comparison across all authentication token form factors, enabling architecture-level selection decisions based on security requirements, deployment constraints, and user experience targets.

**NIST SP 800-63B authenticator requirements by AAL:**

| Requirement | AAL1 Authenticators | AAL2 Authenticators | AAL3 Authenticators |
|:------------|:--------------------|:--------------------|:--------------------|
| **Restricted authenticator** | Memorized secret OR look-up secret | Memorized secret OR single-factor OTP OR single-factor device | Hardware-backed multi-factor device |
| **Proof of possession** | Not required for memorized secrets | Required for all non-memorized secrets | Required; must be hardware-bound |
| **Verifier impersonation resistance** | Not required | Required (phishing-resistant preferred) | Required (phishing-resistant mandatory) |
| **Replay resistance** | Not required for memorized secrets | Required | Required |
| **Multi-factor authentication** | Single factor sufficient | Two factors from different categories | Hardware authenticator + user verification (PIN/biometric) |
| **Token binding** | Not required | Recommended | Required |
| **Approved form factors** | Password, TOTP, push notification | TOTP + hardware, passkey, push with device binding | FIDO2 hardware key, PIV smart card, device-bound passkey |

For AAL3, the authenticator must be a "hardware authenticator" that is "restricted to a single device" and implements "cryptographic authentication" with a key that "cannot be extracted from the authenticator" — eliminating synced passkeys and push notifications from AAL3 consideration.

| Dimension | Software TOTP | Software Push | Software Passkey | Hardware Security Key (YubiKey) | Hardware FIDO-Only Key (Titan) | Smart Card (PIV) | Hardware OTP Token (RSA SecurID) |
|:----------|:-------------|:-------------|:----------------|:-------------------------------|:------------------------------|:----------------|:-------------------------------|
| **Protocols supported** | TOTP (RFC 6238) | Proprietary push | WebAuthn/CTAP2 | FIDO2, U2F, PIV, OATH, OpenPGP, YubiOTP | FIDO2, U2F | PIV (FIPS 201), PKCS#11, X.509 | Proprietary RSA algorithm |
| **Key storage** | Software keychain (device OS) | Software keychain + push registration key | Software vault (encrypted, synced) | Hardware secure element — non-exportable | Hardware secure element — non-exportable | Hardware secure element (ISO 7816 chip) — non-exportable | Hardware tamper-resistant chip — non-exportable |
| **Phishing resistance** | ❌ No — OTP can be phished via AiTM proxies (§16.5) | ⚠️ Partial — number matching mitigates but push can still be socially engineered | ✅ Yes — origin binding (§10.3) prevents credential use on phishing domains | ✅ Yes — origin binding | ✅ Yes — origin binding | ✅ Yes — certificate-based authentication is bound to the RP's certificate trust chain | ❌ No — OTP can be phished via real-time relay |
| **AAL ceiling (NIST SP 800-63B)** | AAL1 (single factor) / AAL2 (with password) | AAL2 | AAL2 (synced passkey) | **AAL3** (device-bound key + UV + verifier impersonation resistance) | **AAL3** | **AAL3** | AAL1 (single factor) / AAL2 (with PIN) |
| **User verification** | None (code displayed passively) | App unlock (biometric/PIN) + approval tap. Number matching adds active verification | Device biometric (Face ID, fingerprint) or device PIN | Touch (user presence), PIN, or biometric (Bio edition) | Touch (user presence) or PIN | PIN (verified on-card) | None (code displayed passively); PIN entered alongside code |
| **Provisioning model** | QR code scan → instant enrollment | App install → push registration with IdP | Browser/OS prompt → credential creation | Physical distribution (mail, in-person) → FIDO2 registration | Physical distribution → FIDO2 registration | Card issuance via Card Management System → certificate provisioning | Seed file import to Authentication Manager → physical token distribution |
| **Device management** | User self-service (app install, cloud backup) | MDM-managed or user self-service | OS/credential provider-managed | Enterprise: YubiEnterprise Subscription. Consumer: self-managed | Self-managed via Google Account security settings | Enterprise Card Management System (CMS) | RSA Authentication Manager — centralised server |
| **Recovery on device loss** | Cloud backup restore to new device | Re-enrollment with IdP admin or self-service recovery | Synced passkey: cloud account recovery. Device-bound: re-registration required | Re-registration required. Recommend enrolling 2+ keys per user | Re-registration required | Card reissuance by issuing authority — PKI certificate revocation + new card | New token provisioned by admin — old token seed deactivated |
| **Cost per unit** | Free (app download) | Free (app download) | Free (built into OS/password manager) | $45–$75 per key (varies by model) | $30–$35 per key | $15–$50 per card + reader infrastructure ($20–$100 per reader) | $50–$100 per token + server licensing |
| **Typical deployment** | Consumer MFA, small business | Enterprise MFA (Entra ID, Duo, Okta) | Consumer passwordless, enterprise passwordless | Enterprise phishing-resistant MFA, privileged access, regulated industries | Consumer high-security (Google APP), cost-conscious FIDO2 | Government (PIV/CAC), eIDAS QES, regulated enterprise | Legacy enterprise MFA, banking, critical infrastructure |

**Recommended use cases by form factor:**

| Form Factor | Recommended For | Not Recommended For |
|:-----------|:---------------|:-------------------|
| **Software TOTP** | Legacy service compatibility, user-managed personal accounts, services that don't support FIDO2 | High-assurance access, phishing-targeted users, AAL3 requirements |
| **Software Push** | Enterprise MFA at scale (quick deployment, good UX), step-up authentication triggers | Environments without mobile device management, offline authentication |
| **Software Passkey** | Consumer passwordless adoption, enterprise workforce with managed devices | Privileged access requiring AAL3, air-gapped environments |
| **Hardware Security Key (multi-protocol)** | Enterprise phishing-resistant MFA, privileged access workstations, compliance-driven deployments (FIPS, PCI DSS), organisations migrating from PIV to FIDO2 | Cost-sensitive deployments at massive scale, deployments requiring no physical logistics |
| **Hardware FIDO-Only Key** | Cost-conscious FIDO2 deployments, consumer high-security accounts, backup/secondary key | Environments requiring PIV/smart card logon, legacy systems requiring OATH/OTP |
| **Smart Card (PIV/CAC)** | Government deployments (FIPS 201 mandate), eIDAS qualified electronic signatures, environments requiring certificate-based authentication and digital signatures | Consumer deployments, environments without card reader infrastructure |
| **Hardware OTP Token** | Legacy compliance (regulatory frameworks referencing RSA SecurID), air-gapped environments with no USB/NFC capability, users unable to use smartphones | New deployments (FIDO2 preferred), cost-sensitive environments, deployments requiring phishing resistance |

**Regulatory compliance mapping:**

*PCI DSS v4.0 (Requirement 8.4.2)* requires MFA for all access to the cardholder data environment. The standard does not prescribe specific form factors but requires that authentication factors be from different categories:

| Form Factor | PCI DSS 8.4.2 Compliant? | Notes |
|:------------|:--------------------------|:------|
| Password + TOTP | Yes | TOTP secret stored in app qualifies as possession factor |
| Password + Push | Yes | Device = "something you have" |
| Password + Hardware Key | Yes | Strongest possession factor |
| Password + Biometric only | Conditional | Requires a separate possession factor |
| Smart Card (PIN + card) | Yes | PIN = "something you know", card = "something you have" |
| FIDO2 (single-factor) | No | Only one factor; requires additional factor |
| FIDO2 Bio (biometric + key) | Conditional | Two factors if biometric is enforced |
| Passkey (device-bound) + password | Yes | Device possession + knowledge |

*HIPAA MFA guidance:* The HIPAA Security Rule (45 CFR 164.312(d)) requires "person or entity authentication" but does not mandate MFA specifically. However, 2024 HITECH Act amendments and OCR guidance strongly recommend MFA. Multiple HIPAA breach settlement agreements (e.g., Columbia University, $4.75M in 2022; YesFit, $75K in 2023) cite the absence of MFA as an aggravating factor. Recommended minimum: password + push notification (AAL2) for workforce access to ePHI; FIDO2 hardware key (AAL3) for system administrators.

*SOX control mapping:* Authentication controls map to several SOX-relevant COSO control objectives:

| SOX Control Objective | Recommended Form Factor | Evidence Required |
|:----------------------|:-----------------------|:------------------|
| Access control over financial systems | Hardware key or PIV smart card | MFA enrollment logs, authentication audit logs |
| Segregation of duties | PIV smart card (role-based certificates) | Certificate mapping to AD groups, login logs |
| Change management (deployment pipelines) | Hardware key + CI/CD integration | Deployment logs with authentication events |
| Audit trail integrity | PIV digital signature slot (9C) | Signed audit logs with certificate chain |
| Privileged access management | Hardware key (AAL3) | Admin login logs, key assignment records |

**Cost per user by deployment scale:**

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{hardware}} + C_{\text{personalization}} + C_{\text{distribution}} + C_{\text{enrollment}} + C_{\text{support}} + C_{\text{replacement}}$$

| Cost Component | 100 Users | 1,000 Users | 10,000 Users | 100,000 Users |
|:---------------|:---------:|:-----------:|:------------:|:-------------:|
| **Hardware (YubiKey 5 NFC)** | $50 | $45 | $40 | $35 |
| **Personalization** | $15 | $10 | $5 | $2 |
| **Distribution (shipping)** | $12 | $8 | $5 | $3 |
| **Enrollment (IT time)** | $25 | $20 | $15 | $10 |
| **Annual support** | $5 | $4 | $3 | $2 |
| **Annual replacement (5% loss)** | $2.50 | $2.25 | $2.00 | $1.75 |
| **Total year 1** | $109.50 | $89.25 | $70.00 | $53.75 |

Economies of scale reduce per-user cost by approximately 51% from 100 to 100,000 users. The largest savings come from bulk hardware pricing (30% reduction) and reduced per-user enrollment time as processes mature.

**Credential lifecycle management:** Effective token deployment requires defined processes for each phase: *Issuance* (Day 0) — verify identity, personalize the token, associate with directory, distribute, and enroll for each application. *Renewal* (typically every 3–5 years for PIV, 3 years for CAC) — generate new CSR on existing token, issue new certificate, load into PIV slot, retire old certificate. *Revocation* — revoke certificate(s) on CA CRL/OCSP, disable directory account, trigger remote revocation (if using YubiEnterprise), recover physical token if possible. *Recovery* — issue replacement token with fresh credentials, re-enroll for all applications.

**BYOD vs corporate-issued token strategy:**

| Dimension | Corporate-Issued Hardware Key | Corporate-Issued Smart Card | BYOD Software Passkey | BYOD Push Authenticator |
|:----------|:-----------------------------|:---------------------------|:---------------------|:------------------------|
| **Hardware cost** | $35–55/user | $15–25/user + reader | $0 | $0 |
| **AAL achievable** | AAL3 | AAL3 | AAL2 (synced) / AAL3 (device-bound) | AAL1–AAL2 |
| **Phishing resistance** | Full | Full | Full | Partial |
| **Offboarding** | Collect key, revoke certs | Collect card, revoke certs | Remote credential revocation | Remote deactivation |
| **Regulatory compliance** | Best (FIPS, Common Criteria) | Best (FIPS 201, PIV) | Good for most industries | Limited for high-assurance |

Most organisations benefit from a hybrid approach: hardware security keys for high-security users (IT admins, executives, privileged accounts); BYOD passkeys or push authenticator for the general workforce (AAL2); hardware keys or smart cards for compliance-critical roles (finance, legal, HIPAA-covered); and temporary push-based MFA or software TOTP for contractors.

**Five-year TCO projection (10,000 users):**

| Cost Category | Software TOTP | Push MFA (Duo) | Synced Passkeys | Hardware Key (YubiKey 5) | PIV Smart Card |
|:--------------|:------------:|:--------------:|:---------------:|:-------------------------:|:--------------:|
| **Year 1 cost** | $50,000 | $460,000 | $290,000 | $600,000 | $550,000 |
| **Annual license/renewal** | $0 | $360,000/yr | $240,000/yr | $0 | $0 |
| **Annual support** | $30,000/yr | $50,000/yr | $30,000/yr | $60,000/yr | $80,000/yr |
| **5-year undiscounted TCO** | $200,000 | $2,410,000 | $1,490,000 | $970,000 | $1,210,000 |
| **Per-user per-year** | $3.54 | $41.50 | $25.66 | $17.28 | $21.00 |

Hardware security keys offer the best security-to-cost ratio ($17.28/user/year for AAL3 security) — the one-time hardware cost is amortized over the key's 5+ year lifespan. Push MFA has the highest TCO ($41.50/user/year) due to per-user licensing. At a 20% annual loss rate (common in mobile workforces), replacement costs increase per-user TCO by only $0.80/year — hardware keys remain cost-competitive even with high loss rates.

**Form factor selection decision tree:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["`**Start: Select Token Form Factor**
    What is the required assurance level?`"]
    B["`**AAL1 required**
    Software TOTP`"]
    C["`**AAL2 required**
    Push authenticator or synced passkey`"]
    D["`**AAL3 required**
    Hardware security key or smart card`"]
    E["`**Phishing resistance required?**`"]
    F["`**Regulatory mandate?**
    (FIPS 201, PCI DSS, HIPAA)`"]
    G["`**Budget > 50 USD/user?**`"]
    H["`**User population technical?**`"]

    (MS Authenticator, Duo)`"]

    (iCloud Keychain, 1Password)`"]

    (platform authenticator)`"]

    A --> C

    G -- Yes --> O

    style B text-align:left
```

### 15. Custom Wallet SDKs in Banking Applications

Custom wallet SDKs represent the banking industry's approach to owning the entire authentication credential lifecycle within a mobile application — from key generation through transaction signing to credential revocation. Unlike platform-native authenticators (WebAuthn passkeys managed by the OS — §10) or standalone hardware tokens (§14), a wallet SDK is a software library embedded directly in the bank's mobile app that generates, stores, protects, and uses cryptographic credentials under the bank's full control. The end user interacts with the bank's branded UI; the SDK vendor operates invisibly beneath the surface.

The architectural motivation is autonomy: banks require deterministic control over credential provisioning, activation, suspension, and revocation — capabilities that platform-native credential stores do not expose. When a customer reports a stolen device, the bank needs to revoke the device credential within seconds; when a regulatory audit requires credential state history, the bank needs server-side logs of every lifecycle transition. Platform keychain and passkey infrastructure provide excellent security (§11.2) but limited operational visibility. Wallet SDKs fill this gap by implementing a parallel credential management layer that delegates hardware key storage to the platform's secure hardware (Secure Enclave, StrongBox, TEE — §11.2) while retaining full lifecycle orchestration in the SDK's server-side component.

This chapter covers the architecture, key protection model, credential lifecycle, and platform integration patterns of embedded wallet SDKs as deployed in European and global banking applications. The primary use case is **PSD2 Strong Customer Authentication (SCA)** — the combination of possession (device-bound key) + knowledge (PIN) or inherence (biometric) that wallet SDKs implement as a unified, in-app authentication ceremony.

**Regulatory context.** Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) is defined in Article 4(30) of PSD2 (Directive (EU) 2015/2366) as:

> an authentication based on the use of two or more elements categorised as **knowledge** (something only the user knows), **possession** (something only the user possesses), and **inherence** (something only the user is) that are **independent**, in the sense that the breach of one does not compromise the reliability of the others, and is designed in such a way as to protect the confidentiality of the authentication data.

Article 10 of the PSD2 Regulatory Technical Standards (RTS — Commission Delegated Regulation 2018/389) requires at least two elements combined in each SCA procedure. Crucially, the RTS mandates **independence** (Article 6):

$$\text{Compromise}(F_i) \not\Rightarrow \text{Compromise}(F_j), \quad \forall i \neq j$$

This excludes combinations where a single compromise breaks both factors: password + password-derived OTP (same knowledge factor), SMS OTP sent to the device used for the transaction (possession factor shared with communication channel), and device fingerprint + browser cookie (both possession factors on the same device). This independence requirement is the foundational reason why platform-native passkeys, despite excellent phishing resistance, are insufficient for banking SCA on their own.

Beyond the two-factor requirement, SCA mandates **dynamic linking** (RTS Article 8) — the authentication code must be specific to the amount and payee of the transaction:

$$\text{AuthCode} = f(\text{Amount}, \text{Payee}, \text{SessionContext})$$

This prevents a man-in-the-middle from capturing a valid SCA code and replaying it against a modified transaction with a different amount or payee. The user must see and confirm the exact transaction parameters before generating the code.

**Dynamic linking implementation patterns.** The RTS (Article 8) mandates three requirements: (1) the PSP must generate an authentication code specific to the transaction amount and payee, (2) the user must be informed of the exact amount and payee before confirming, and (3) each transaction must receive a unique code with no reuse. Five patterns satisfy these requirements to varying degrees:

| Pattern | Mechanism | Dynamic Linking? | User Experience |
|:--------|:----------|:-----------------|:----------------|
| **Push notification with transaction details** | Server pushes amount + payee to bank's mobile app; user confirms in-app | ✅ (user sees and confirms exact details) | Good — native UI, but requires mobile app installation |
| **Card reader / TAN generator** | User inserts card into reader, confirms transaction on reader display | ✅ (dedicated hardware display) | Poor — requires separate hardware device |
| **SMS OTP with enriched payload** | SMS contains last digits of amount and payee; user compares against transaction | ⚠️ (limited by SMS character limit; not EBA-preferred) | Moderate — convenient but SMS is insecure (interceptable) |
| **Soft token with transaction confirmation** | Bank app generates OTP after user confirms amount + payee in-app | ✅ (user confirms in trusted environment) | Good — leverages smartphone as trusted device |
| **PSD2 SCA API (SCA delegated to TPP)** | Third-party provider (AISP/PISP) delegates SCA to ASPSP's SCA interface | ✅ (ASPSP handles dynamic linking) | Variable — depends on ASPSP's UX |

The EBA Opinion on SCA (EBA/Op/2018/09) explicitly states that SMS OTP alone does not inherently satisfy dynamic linking — the OTP must be linked to the specific transaction parameters. Many implementations that rely on SMS OTP without in-band transaction details are non-compliant. Banks that rely solely on SMS OTP for SCA have been pressured by national regulators to migrate to app-based or hardware-based solutions.

Key PSD2 RTS articles relevant to wallet SDKs include: Article 4 (SCA exemptions for low-value payments and trusted beneficiaries), Article 7 (exemptions based on risk analysis), Article 8 (dynamic linking of SCA to specific transaction amount and payee), and Articles 11–13 (authentication code generation, per-transaction SCA, and risk-based exemption conditions). National regulators add further requirements: BaFin (Germany) expects MFA for all high-value transactions regardless of amount; DNB (Netherlands) requires evidence of device attestation integrity; ACPR (France) and Banca d'Italia each publish interpretive guidance that may impose stricter requirements than the EU baseline.

#### 15.1 Embedded Wallet Architecture

##### 15.1.1 Definition and Scope

A wallet SDK in the banking context is a software library (distributed as an XCFramework for iOS, an AAR for Android) that a bank integrates into its mobile application. The SDK manages:

- **Cryptographic key pairs** — asymmetric keys (ECDSA P-256 or RSA-2048) generated in hardware-backed storage, used for authentication and transaction signing
- **Credential lifecycle** — provisioning, activation, suspension, revocation, and key rotation orchestrated between the SDK and the bank's backend Credential Management Server (CMS)
- **User verification** — PIN entry, biometric authentication, and device attestation as layered gates before the signing key is unlocked
- **Runtime protection** — anti-tampering, anti-debugging, root/jailbreak detection, and code obfuscation to protect the SDK from reverse engineering and runtime manipulation

The SDK does **not** manage identity tokens (OAuth access/refresh tokens), session cookies, or federation protocol flows — those remain in the bank's application logic. The wallet SDK's responsibility is strictly the **cryptographic credential** — the signing key that proves device possession and authorises transactions.

**Internal component model.** A production-grade wallet SDK is not monolithic but composed of interdependent modules:

- **Crypto Engine** — foundational module for all cryptographic operations: asymmetric key pair generation (ECDSA P-256, Ed25519), digital signature creation and verification, symmetric encryption for credential storage, and cryptographic hash computation. Abstracts platform-specific APIs (Apple Security.framework, Android Conscrypt/Bouncy Castle) behind a unified interface
- **Key Store Adapter** — hardware-backed abstraction over the platform secure element. Wraps Secure Enclave via `SecKeyCreateRandomKey` on iOS; wraps Android Keystore via `KeyGenParameterSpec.Builder().setIsStrongBoxBacked(true)` on Android. Handles fallback to software-based TEE with appropriate security warnings surfaced to the backend
- **Attestation Module** — generates cryptographic proof of device and application integrity, producing attestation certificates that chain to the device manufacturer's root CA (Apple DeviceCheck, Google Play Integrity). Payload includes device model, OS version, application package signature, and a server-challenge nonce
- **RASP Module** — continuously monitors the runtime environment for signs of tampering, debugging, rooting, or hooking (§15.2.4). Communicates findings to the backend for risk-based decisions; can trigger self-termination on critical integrity violations
- **UI Layer** — provides pre-built authentication UI components that enforce bank-defined UX policies: PIN entry screens with rate limiting, biometric consent dialogs, and transaction confirmation displays. Deliberately opinionated to prevent developers from accidentally weakening the authentication UX
- **Network Layer** — handles secure communication with the bank's backend and CMS. Implements certificate pinning, mutual TLS where required, request signing, response validation, and offline queueing of authentication events

**Three-party architecture.** The wallet SDK operates within a three-party model:

```
                    +-------------------+
                    |   Bank Backend    |
                    |  (Authorisation   |
                    |   Server, AML,    |
                    |   Fraud Engine)    |
                    +--------+----------+
                             |
              +--------------+--------------+
              |                             |
     +--------v----------+       +---------v--------+
     | Credential Mgmt   |       |   Wallet SDK     |
     | Server (CMS)      |       |  (Mobile App)    |
     | Key provisioning  |       |  - Crypto Engine |
     | Attestation check |       |  - Key Store     |
     | Revocation mgmt   |       |  - RASP Module   |
     | Policy engine     |       |  - UI Layer      |
     +-------------------+       |  - Network Layer |
                                 +------------------+
```

The CMS is the authoritative source of truth for device-credential mappings and enforces the bank's device policies (maximum devices per user, activation grace periods, device type restrictions). The bank backend consumes attestation data and authentication tokens from the CMS for authorisation decisions; the fraud engine correlates RASP device health signals with transaction patterns for real-time risk scoring.

##### 15.1.2 Comparison with Platform-Native Approaches

| Dimension | Platform-Native (WebAuthn/Passkeys — §10) | Embedded Wallet SDK |
|:----------|:------------------------------------------|:-------------------|
| **Key storage** | Platform keychain / credential manager | SDK delegates to same hardware (SE/StrongBox/TEE — §11.2) but wraps access in its own API |
| **Credential lifecycle** | Managed by OS + cloud sync fabric (iCloud Keychain, Google Password Manager) | Managed by bank's CMS — full PROVISIONED → ACTIVATED → SUSPENDED → REVOKED state machine |
| **Remote revocation** | Limited — RP can refuse assertion but cannot delete the credential from the device | Full — bank's backend instructs SDK to zeroize the local key and transition to REVOKED |
| **Multi-device policy** | Platform sync determines which devices hold the credential | Bank's CMS maintains explicit device registry — each device has an independent key pair |
| **Key rotation** | Not supported — passkey credential ID is permanent; key pair is immutable | Supported — SDK generates new key pair, signs rotation request with old key, CMS updates binding |
| **User verification** | Platform-managed biometric prompt (§12.6) | SDK-managed PIN + biometric + device attestation — layered and configurable |
| **Attestation** | WebAuthn attestation at registration (§10.2) | Continuous device attestation at enrollment, at each authentication, and periodically |
| **Regulatory visibility** | Limited metadata — RP receives authenticator data flags | Full audit trail — every credential state transition logged server-side with timestamps |
| **Vendor lock-in** | Interoperable (W3C WebAuthn standard) | Vendor-specific — migration between SDK vendors requires re-enrollment |

The fundamental tradeoff: platform-native passkeys are **interoperable and low-maintenance** but offer limited lifecycle control; wallet SDKs are **proprietary and complex** but provide the operational granularity that banking regulators and risk teams demand.

##### 15.1.3 Vendor Landscape

The embedded wallet SDK market is dominated by a small number of specialised security vendors, most of whom serve the financial services sector exclusively:

| Vendor | Product | Protocol Support | FIPS Level | Attestation Method | Key Differentiator |
|:-------|:--------|:-----------------|:-----------|:------------------|:-------------------|
| **Signicat** (acquired Encap Security, 2021) | MobileID / Encap SCA | OATH, FIDO2, mTLS | Level 3 | DeviceCheck + Play Integrity | Proportional security model adapting to transaction risk; on-premise option; strong Nordic/Benelux presence |
| **OneSpan** | Mobile Security Suite | OATH, FIDO2, PKI | Level 3 | DeviceCheck + SafetyNet | Patented Cronto visual cryptogram for dynamic linking (§9.3); OTP + transaction signing + FIDO2 + shielding in single SDK |
| **Entersekt** | Transakt SDK | Proprietary, PKI | Level 2 | DeviceCheck + Play Integrity | Out-of-band encrypted channel; digital certificate-based device identity; strong African banking presence |
| **Thales** (acquired Gemalto) | Mobile Protector SDK | OATH, FIDO2, PKI | Level 3 | DeviceCheck + SafetyNet | Deep integration with Thales Luna HSMs; HSM-based key provisioning; randomised secure PIN pad |
| **Futurex** | Authentication SDK | PKI, FIDO2 | Level 3 | Custom HSM attestation | HSM-centric architecture (VirtuCrypt); FIPS 140-3 focus; primarily North American financial institutions |
| **Promon** | SHIELD | N/A (RASP only) | N/A | Device integrity check | Specialised RASP vendor; often paired with another vendor's authentication SDK for the runtime protection layer |
| **HID Global** | ActivID Authentication | OATH, PKI, FIDO2 | Level 3 | DeviceCheck + SafetyNet | Government and banking crossover; strong physical access card integration; eIDAS QTSP certification |
| **V-Key** | V-OS Mobile Security | Proprietary, PKI | Level 2 | Proprietary attestation | Virtual Secure Element (software-based SE replacement); APAC focus; works without hardware SE |
| **Azoft** (formerly IDEMIA) | mobileSDK | PKI, OATH | Level 3 | DeviceCheck + SafetyNet | EU/Africa/Middle East presence; IDEMIA identity heritage; multi-modal biometric support |

These SDKs are **white-labelled** — the end user sees the bank's logo, colour scheme, and branded authentication screens. The SDK vendor's identity is invisible to the customer. The bank purchases a per-device or per-user licence and integrates the SDK into its build pipeline.

##### 15.1.4 PSD2 Compliance and Certification

PSD2 compliance is a table-stakes requirement for banking wallet SDKs operating in the European Economic Area. The following table compares each vendor's certifications (PSD2, Article 8):

| Dimension | Signicat | OneSpan | Entersekt | Thales | Futurex | Promon | HID | V-Key | Azoft |
|:----------|:---------|:--------|:----------|:--------|:---------|:-------|:----|:------|:-------|
| EBA RTS SCA compliant | Yes (audited) | Yes (audited) | Yes (audited) | Yes (audited) | Yes (audited) | Yes (audited) | Yes (audited) | Yes (audited) | Yes (audited) |
| Dynamic linking (Art. 8) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| National regulator approval | BaFin, DNB, FSA | BaFin, ACPR, AMF | Multiple African regulators | ACPR, BaFin, Consob | OCC, BaFin | BaFin, ACPR | MAS, BSP | AMF, CBJ |
| FIDO2 certification | L2 | L2 | L1+ | L2 | L2 | N/A | L2 | L1 | L1 |
| eIDAS QTSP | Yes | Yes | No | Yes | No | No | Yes | No | No |
| Annual pen test | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| SOC 2 Type II | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | Yes |
| ISO 27001 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |

##### 15.1.5 Vendor Pricing and Commercial Model

Pricing structures vary significantly across vendors and impact total cost of ownership (TCO):

| Vendor | Pricing Model | Typical Annual Cost Range | Volume Discounts | Notes |
|:-------|:-------------|:--------------------------|:-----------------|:------|
| Signicat | Per-user + platform fee | 50K–500K EUR | Yes (10K+ users) | Strong Nordic volume |
| OneSpan | Per-seat + platform licence | 80K–1M EUR | Yes (enterprise) | Pilot tier (100 users) |
| Entersekt | Per-transaction + platform fee | 40K–300K EUR | Yes (1M+ transactions) | Transaction-based model |
| Thales | Per-device + HSM licence | 100K–2M EUR | Yes (volume) | HSM licensing premium |
| Futurex | Per-HSM + per-device licence | 150K–3M EUR | Limited | HSM-centric pricing |
| Promon | Per-app licence (RASP only) | 20K–200K EUR | Yes (multi-app) | Community edition available |
| HID | Per-seat + platform fee | 60K–800K EUR | Yes (enterprise) | Government+banking bundle |
| V-Key | Per-device + platform fee | 30K–250K EUR | Yes (APAC focus) | Virtual SE differentiator |
| Azoft | Per-user + integration fee | 40K–400K EUR | Yes (10K+ users) | Integration services included |

⚠️ Pricing ranges are indicative and based on publicly available information, RFP responses, and analyst reports (Gartner, Forrester, Aite-Novarica). Actual pricing is negotiated case-by-case and depends on deployment scope, geographic requirements, and integration complexity.

#### 15.2 Key Protection Strategies

Wallet SDKs implement a **layered protection model** that combines hardware, software, and behavioural defences. Each layer independently contributes to the security posture; the combination satisfies PSD2 SCA requirements for multi-factor authentication.

##### 15.2.1 Layer 1: Device Binding (Possession Factor)

During enrollment, the SDK generates a device-specific **asymmetric key pair** in the platform's hardware-backed storage:

- **iOS:** `SecKeyCreateRandomKey` with `kSecAttrTokenIDSecureEnclave` — the private key is generated inside the Secure Enclave and is non-exportable (§11.2.1)
- **Android:** `KeyPairGenerator` with `KeyGenParameterSpec.Builder.setIsStrongBoxBacked(true)` — the private key resides in StrongBox (if available) or TEE (§11.2.2)

The private key **never leaves the hardware security boundary**. The SDK extracts the public key and transmits it to the bank's CMS during the enrollment ceremony. The CMS binds the public key to the user's account, establishing a cryptographic link between the physical device and the user identity.

This key pair is the **possession factor** — proof that the authentication request originates from the specific enrolled hardware device. The device-bound key cannot be cloned, exported, or replicated on another device, even if the attacker has access to the full application binary and all software state.

The choice of signature algorithm affects key size, security level, signature size, and platform performance. The following table compares the algorithms used or considered by wallet SDK vendors:

| Algorithm | Key Size (bits) | Security Level (bits) | Signature Size (bytes) | Performance |
|:----------|:---------------|:---------------------|:----------------------|:------------|
| ECDSA P-256 | 256 | 128 | 64 | Fast — native hardware acceleration on all modern devices |
| ECDSA P-384 | 384 | 192 | 96 | ~1.5× slower than P-256; some devices lack hardware acceleration |
| Ed25519 | 256 | 128 | 64 | Very fast — no RFC 6979 deterministic nonce overhead |
| RSA-2048 | 2048 | 112 | 256 | Slow — large key operations, not SE-native on all platforms |
| RSA-3072 | 3072 | 128 | 384 | Very slow — rarely used in mobile wallet SDKs |
| CRYSTALS-Dilithium (PQC) | 2048–4096 | 128–256 | 2420–4627 | Slow — post-quantum; hybrid classical+PQC support emerging |

ECDSA P-256 is the dominant choice across all vendors, offering 128-bit security with compact 64-byte signatures and native hardware acceleration on both iOS (Secure Enclave) and Android (StrongBox/TEE). Ed25519 is gaining traction in newer SDK releases due to its simpler implementation and faster verification. Post-quantum algorithms (CRYSTALS-Dilithium) are not yet production-ready for mobile SDKs but should be on the vendor evaluation radar for long-term deployments.

##### 15.2.2 Layer 2: PIN Activation (Knowledge Factor)

The user sets a numeric PIN during enrollment. The SDK uses this PIN to derive a **key encryption key (KEK)** via PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-256 (§11.4.1) with a device-specific salt and a minimum of 210,000 iterations (NIST SP 800-63B Rev 4 guidance):

```
KEK = PBKDF2(HMAC-SHA-256, PIN, device_salt, iterations=210000, dkLen=32)
```

The salt prevents pre-computed dictionary attacks (rainbow tables) by ensuring that identical PINs produce different KEKs across different devices. The iteration count increases the cost of brute-forcing the KEK from a captured encrypted token. The brute-force time for a single GPU can be estimated as:

$$T_{attack} = 10^6 \times c \times t_{hash}$$

Where $10^6$ is the number of possible 6-digit PINs, $c$ is the PBKDF2 iteration count, and $t_{hash}$ is the time per HMAC-SHA-256 computation (~100 ns on a modern GPU). With $c = 210{,}000$:

$$T_{attack} \approx 10^6 \times 2.1 \times 10^5 \times 10^{-7}\text{s} \approx 21\text{s}$$

This ~21-second brute-force time per GPU explains why PBKDF2 iteration counts must be regularly increased (NIST SP 800-132 recommends a minimum of 600,000 iterations for 2026). With a 100-GPU cluster, the time drops to ~0.2 seconds — which is why **rate limiting** (typically 5 attempts before lockout with exponential backoff) is the primary defence against brute-force, not the iteration count alone.

The KEK does **not** directly protect the hardware-backed signing key (which is protected by the SE/StrongBox's own access control). Instead, the KEK protects an **intermediate activation token** — a symmetric key that the SDK's server-side component issues during enrollment and that the SDK stores in encrypted form on-device. To perform a signing operation, the SDK must:

1. Derive the KEK from the user's PIN
2. Decrypt the activation token
3. Present the activation token to the hardware key's access control policy (via `LAContext` or `BiometricPrompt` CryptoObject)
4. Execute the signing operation inside the hardware security boundary

This two-key architecture — hardware-bound signing key + PIN-derived activation token — ensures that neither the PIN alone nor physical device possession alone is sufficient to sign. Both are required simultaneously, satisfying the PSD2 SCA two-factor requirement.

##### 15.2.3 Layer 3: Biometric Activation (Inherence Factor)

Optionally (and commonly in modern deployments), the SDK binds the signing key to biometric authentication instead of or in addition to PIN:

- **iOS:** The signing key is created with `kSecAccessControlBiometryCurrentSet` (§12.7.1) — the Secure Enclave releases the key only after successful Face ID or Touch ID authentication
- **Android:** The signing key is created with `setUserAuthenticationRequired(true)` and `AUTH_BIOMETRIC_STRONG` (§12.7.1) — the `BiometricPrompt` must succeed before `CryptoObject` operations are permitted

When biometric activation is enabled, the signing flow becomes:

1. SDK triggers the platform biometric prompt (system-managed UI — §12.6)
2. Biometric match succeeds inside the SE/TEE
3. Hardware security boundary releases the signing key for a single cryptographic operation
4. SDK signs the transaction data and transmits the signature to the bank's backend

Biometric activation replaces PIN entry for the vast majority of day-to-day transactions, providing a **frictionless yet cryptographically strong** authentication ceremony. The PIN remains available as a fallback (biometric lockout, accessibility needs) and is required for high-risk operations (beneficiary changes, international transfers) in some bank configurations.

**Biometric security metrics.** Unlike PIN entropy (which is precisely quantifiable), biometric security is characterised by two probabilistic metrics:

- **False Match Rate (FMR):** the probability that the system incorrectly accepts an unauthorised user's biometric sample. For banking-grade fingerprint sensors, $\text{FMR} \approx 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$
- **False Non-Match Rate (FNMR):** the probability that the system rejects a legitimate user — also called the False Rejection Rate (FRR). Typical values: $\text{FNMR} \approx 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$

These two rates are inversely related: lowering the FMR threshold increases the FNMR. The crossover point (FMR = FNMR) is the **Equal Error Rate (EER)**, the standard benchmark for comparing biometric systems. The effective security entropy can be expressed as:

$$H_{bio} = -\log_2(\text{FAR})$$

For a banking-grade fingerprint sensor with FAR $= 10^{-5}$: $H_{bio} \approx 16.6$ bits. This is lower than the ~20-bit entropy of a 6-digit PIN or the 128-bit security of ECDSA P-256. However, biometrics are not primarily valued for entropy — they provide the regulatory **inherence factor** and dramatically improve user experience by eliminating PIN entry friction for routine authentication (ISO/IEC 30107-3).

##### 15.2.4 Layer 4: Device Attestation and Runtime Protection

Beyond the three authentication factors, wallet SDKs implement continuous device integrity verification:

**Device attestation (§13):**
- **iOS:** App Attest (§13.2) — the SDK generates a DCAppAttestService attestation at enrollment and assertion at each authentication, proving to the bank's backend that the SDK is running in a genuine, unmodified app on genuine Apple hardware
- **Android:** Play Integrity API (§13.5) — provides an integrity verdict (MEETS_DEVICE_INTEGRITY, MEETS_BASIC_INTEGRITY, MEETS_STRONG_INTEGRITY) that the bank's backend evaluates before accepting the signed transaction

**Runtime Application Self-Protection (RASP):**

| Defence | Mechanism | Attack Mitigated |
|:--------|:---------|:-----------------|
| **Root/jailbreak detection** | Checks for Cydia/Sileo (iOS), su binary, Magisk, or SELinux permissive mode (Android) | Rooted devices expose the hardware key store to privileged access; some SDKs refuse to operate on rooted devices |
| **Anti-debugging** | Detects attached debuggers (ptrace, Frida, lldb) and terminates if a debugger is present | Dynamic analysis and runtime key extraction |
| **Code obfuscation** | ProGuard/R8 (Android), LLVM-based obfuscation (iOS) — renames classes, methods, and variables; inserts opaque predicates | Static reverse engineering of SDK logic |
| **Integrity verification** | SDK computes a hash of its own binary at startup and compares against a known-good value | Binary patching — modifying the SDK to bypass security checks |
| **Anti-hooking** | Detects method swizzling (iOS) and Xposed/Frida hooks (Android) | Runtime interception of SDK function calls |
| **Screen overlay detection** | Detects transparent overlays drawn on top of the SDK's UI (Android only — `TYPE_APPLICATION_OVERLAY`) | Clickjacking and screen overlay attacks that trick users into approving transactions |

These defences operate **continuously** — not just at login, but throughout the application session. If any integrity check fails, the SDK can: refuse to sign, alert the bank's fraud engine, or force a credential re-enrollment.

##### RASP Techniques Catalogue

The following subsections catalogue the major RASP techniques used in production banking wallet SDKs, organised by threat category.

##### Anti-Debugging

Production wallet SDKs employ multiple redundant anti-debugging checks:

**iOS:**
- `ptrace(PT_DENY_ATTACH, 0, NULL, 0)` — prevents debugger attachment at the POSIX syscall level
- `sysctl` querying the `P_TRACED` flag on the current process info structure — a non-zero value indicates a debugger is attached
- Objective-C runtime introspection for debugging-related classes (e.g., `LLDBDebugger`, `FLEXManager`) that should not exist in production builds

**Android:**
- Reading `/proc/self/status` and checking the `TracerPid` field — a non-zero value indicates a debugging process is attached
- `android.os.Debug.isDebuggerConnected()` — the standard Android API (easily bypassed via hooking, used as a baseline check)
- JDWP (Java Debug Wire Protocol) port scanning — checking whether port 8700 is open on the local loopback, indicating ADB debug forwarding

##### Jailbreak and Root Detection

**iOS jailbreak detection signatures:**

| Check Method | Indicator | Bypass Difficulty |
|:------------|:----------|:------------------|
| File system checks | `/Applications/Cydia.app`, `/Library/MobileSubstrate`, `/bin/bash`, `/etc/apt` | Medium |
| URL scheme checks | `cydia://` URL scheme registration | Low |
| DYLD environment | `DYLD_INSERT_LIBRARIES` environment variable set | Medium |
| Sandbox violation | Writing outside the app sandbox container | Low |
| Symbolic link checks | `/private/var/stash`, `/var/mobile/Library/SBSettings/Themes` | Medium |
| System API anomalies | `fork()` returning 0 (unexpected child process), suspicious `posix_spawn` calls | High |
| Task port check | `mach_task_self()` returning unexpected port rights | High |

**Android root detection signatures:**

| Check Method | Indicator | Bypass Difficulty |
|:------------|:----------|:------------------|
| su binary | Presence of `/system/bin/su`, `/system/xbin/su`, `/sbin/su` | Low (Magisk hide) |
| Root management apps | Magisk Manager, SuperSU, KingRoot packages installed | Medium |
| SELinux status | `getenforce()` returning `Permissive` | Medium |
| System properties | `ro.debuggable == 1`, `ro.secure == 0` | Medium |
| Mount flags | `/system` mounted as read-write | Medium |
| Bootloader anomalies | `/proc/sys/kernel/random/boot_id` indicating custom recovery | High |
| Play Integrity | Failing Google's device attestation API | High |
| Native library injection | `/proc/self/maps` containing unexpected libraries from `/data/local` | High |

##### Anti-Hooking Techniques

Instrumentation frameworks (Frida, Xposed, Cydia Substrate) allow attackers to intercept and modify function calls at runtime:

- **Method swizzling detection (iOS):** The SDK periodically compares the IMP (implementation pointer) of critical methods against known-good values stored during initialisation. A changed IMP indicates the method has been swizzled
- **Frida detection (cross-platform):** Scanning `/proc/self/maps` (Android) or `dyld_image_add_callback` (iOS) for libraries containing "frida" in the path; checking for Frida's default communication port (27042) via TCP connection to `127.0.0.1:27042`; enumerating threads for Frida's "gmain" thread
- **Xposed detection (Android):** Checking call stacks for `de.robv.android.xposed` package frames; inspecting `declaringClass` for replacement by Xposed modules; reading `/proc/self/maps` for `XposedBridge.jar`

##### Code Obfuscation Techniques

Static code obfuscation raises the cost of reverse engineering. While not a runtime protection per se, it is bundled with RASP solutions:

| Technique | Description | Impact on Reverse Engineering |
|:----------|:-----------|:------------------------------|
| Control flow flattening | Breaks structured control flow into a state machine with a single switch inside a loop | High — decompilers produce nearly unreadable output |
| String encryption | Encrypts string literals at compile time; decrypts at runtime only when needed | Medium — prevents keyword-based analysis |
| Opaque predicates | Inserts always-true or always-false conditions that cannot be statically resolved | High — forces manual branch analysis |
| Class and method renaming | Replaces meaningful identifiers with short, meaningless names | Low — easily overcome with context analysis |
| Dummy code injection | Inserts code paths that are never executed but confuse static analysis | Medium — increases analysis time |
| Arithmetic obfuscation | Replaces simple arithmetic with equivalent complex expressions | Low — symbolic execution resolves easily |
| Reflection-based dispatch | Replaces direct method calls with reflective invocation | High — breaks static call graph analysis |
| Native layer migration | Moves critical logic from Java/Kotlin/Swift to C/C++ compiled libraries | High — requires native reverse engineering skills |

##### Integrity Verification and Certificate Pinning

**APK/IPA integrity verification:** The SDK reads the application's signing certificate from the `PackageManager` (Android) or the embedded code signature (iOS) at runtime and verifies it matches an expected fingerprint stored securely. Any discrepancy indicates a repackaged or modified binary.

**Certificate pinning** restricts the set of acceptable TLS certificates for communication with the CMS and bank backend, preventing MITM attacks via compromised or fraudulent CA certificates:

1. **Public key pinning:** The SDK stores a SHA-256 hash of the server's public key and verifies that the presented certificate matches. More resilient to certificate reissuance
2. **Certificate chain pinning:** The SDK stores a set of acceptable root or intermediate CA certificates and validates the chain. Simpler but requires SDK updates when certificates rotate

**Self-update integrity:** When the SDK downloads updates or configuration from the CMS, it verifies the update package's digital signature before applying it, using the CMS's public key hardcoded into the SDK binary.

##### Screen Overlay Detection

On Android, overlay attacks (tapjacking / clickjacking) place a transparent window over the banking UI to trick users into approving transactions. Detection methods include:

1. **`FLAG_SECURE`** on all windows containing sensitive data — prevents screenshots and screen recording
2. **Overlay permission check** — querying `Settings.canDrawOverlays()` to detect apps with overlay permission
3. **Accessibility service enumeration** — listing all active accessibility services via `AccessibilityManager.getEnabledAccessibilityServiceList()` and flagging non-standard services
4. **Touch event validation** — comparing `MotionEvent.getRawX()` / `getRawY()` coordinates against the application's window bounds to detect touches originating from overlay windows

##### 15.2.5 Entropy Budget Analysis

The effective security of the multi-layer protection model can be quantified by analysing the **entropy budget** — the uncertainty each layer contributes for an attacker. The composite entropy determines the brute-force resistance of the overall system (NIST SP 800-63B).

**Layer 1 entropy (device binding):** ECDSA P-256 provides 128 bits of security — the best known classical attack (Pollard's rho) requires $\approx 2^{128}$ elliptic curve operations. The effective entropy is lower because the attacker needs physical access to the specific device. With $P_{device} \approx 10^{-6}$ (one in a million), the effective entropy is:

$$H_{1,eff} = 128 + \log_2\left(\frac{1}{10^{-6}}\right) \approx 148 \text{ bits}$$

**Layer 2 entropy (PIN):** A 6-digit numeric PIN provides $H_2 = \log_2(10^6) \approx 19.93$ bits. With a maximum of 5 consecutive failed attempts before lockout (common banking SDK default), the effective entropy against online attacks is $\approx 83.2$ bits (combinatorial advantage — the attacker does not know which 5 PINs were tried). With rate limiting of 1 attempt per 30 seconds, the probability of success is at most $5 \times 10^{-6}$.

**Layer 3 entropy (biometrics):** Biometric systems operate with a False Match Rate (FMR) of $\approx 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ for banking-grade fingerprint sensors, giving $H_3 = -\log_2(\text{FMR}) \approx 16$ to $20$ bits. Biometrics primarily provide regulatory inherence factor compliance rather than significant entropy contribution.

**Layer 4 (RASP):** Does not contribute direct entropy but increases the attacker's cost function. We model this as an additional work factor $W_4 \in [40, 2000]$ person-hours of reverse engineering effort, filtering out all but the most resourceful adversaries.

**Composite security:** The probability of a successful attack combines the per-layer probabilities:

$$P_{success} = P_{device} \times P_{PIN} \times P_{RASP\_bypass}$$

For a practical scenario with a population of 1 million users:

$$P_{success} \approx 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-2} = 5 \times 10^{-14}$$

This per-authentication probability translates to an expected time between successful compromises of approximately 634 years at one authentication attempt per second across the entire population — well within acceptable banking security thresholds.

##### 15.2.6 PSD2 RTS-to-SDK Mapping

Wallet SDKs must satisfy multiple articles of the PSD2 RTS (Commission Delegated Regulation 2018/389) to be considered SCA-compliant. The following table maps each RTS article to the corresponding SDK capability:

| RTS Article | Requirement | SDK Implementation |
|:-----------|:-----------|:-------------------|
| Article 5 — SCA elements | Combine at least two of: knowledge, possession, inherence | Layer 2 (PIN = knowledge), Layer 1 (device = possession), Layer 3 (biometric = inherence) |
| Article 6 — Independence | Compromise of one element must not affect others | Separate secure storage: PIN KEK in Keychain/Keystore, biometric policy in SE, device key in SE — hardware isolation |
| Article 7 — Dynamic linking | SCA linked to specific transaction amount and payee | Transaction signing module hashes amount + payee + timestamp; user confirms displayed data before signing |
| Article 8 — Exemptions | SCA exemptions under specific conditions | SDK supports fallback to SCA for non-exempt transactions; configuration-driven exemption rules |
| Article 10 — SCA communication | Auth codes must not be sent over the initiating channel | SDK uses push notification to trigger out-of-band authentication on device |
| Article 11 — Authentication code | Codes generated by the ASPSP's authentication system | CMS generates server-side challenge; SDK signs it with the device key |
| Article 12 — Per-transaction SCA | Each transaction individually authenticated | SDK requires explicit user confirmation (PIN/bio) per transaction signing ceremony |
| Article 13 — Exemption conditions | Risk-based analysis for low-value transactions | SDK sends device attestation + RASP signals to backend risk engine for scoring |

##### 15.2.7 PSD2 SCA Exemption Categories

PSD2 Article 18 and the RTS (Articles 10–18) define exemptions from SCA. These are **exemptions**, not waivers — the PSP may apply them but is not required to, and the customer's bank (ASPSP) must assess risk before accepting an exemption:

| Exemption | Condition | Risk Threshold | Recurrence |
|:----------|:-----------|:---------------|:-----------|
| **Low-value** | Transaction ≤ €30; cumulative ≤ €100; max 5 consecutive exempted transactions | Low | Recurring up to cumulative limit; then SCA required |
| **Low-risk** | PSP's transaction risk analysis (TRA) indicates low fraud risk; PSP monitors fraud rates and adjusts thresholds | Trajectory-based: PSP-specific fraud rates below €1 in 10,000 transactions | Continuous — PSP must recalculate fraud rates every 24 hours |
| **Trusted beneficiary** | Payment to a previously-verified beneficiary; user explicitly marked beneficiary as "trusted" | Medium — lower than new-beneficiary payments | Until user removes trusted status |
| **Corporate payments** | Payment initiated by a legal person using secure corporate processes | Low — but requires ASPSP approval | Recurring for approved corporate payers |
| **Intra-group transfers** | Transfers between accounts held at the same PSP by the same payer | Very low | Recurring |

**Transaction Risk Analysis (TRA)** is the mechanism for the low-risk exemption. The PSP computes a fraud probability:

$$P(\text{fraud} | \text{transaction context}) = f(\text{amount}, \text{location}, \text{device fingerprint}, \text{behavioral patterns}, \text{time}, \text{beneficiary history})$$

If $P(\text{fraud}) < \tau$ (the PSP's fraud threshold), the low-risk exemption applies. The PSP must adjust $\tau$ dynamically based on actual fraud rates: if the observed fraud rate exceeds the reference rate defined in the RTS, the PSP must **lower** $\tau$ (making the exemption harder to apply); if the observed fraud rate is below the reference rate, the PSP **may** raise $\tau$ (but is not required to). Wallet SDKs contribute to TRA by providing real-time device attestation and RASP integrity signals that feed into the PSP's fraud scoring engine.

##### 15.2.8 PSD3 and the Evolving Regulatory Landscape

The Payment Services Package (PSD3 + PSR — Payment Services Regulation) adopted in April 2024 introduces changes relevant to wallet SDK-based SCA:

| PSD2 | PSD3 / PSR |
|:-----|:-----------|
| SCA applies to "electronic payments" | SCA scope broadened to include certain crypto-asset transfers and instant credit transfers |
| Exemptions at ASPSP discretion | Mandatory SCA for instant credit payments above €30 (no exemption) |
| No explicit provisions for embedded finance | SCA delegation rules for embedded finance and open banking |
| Fraud liability unclear for TSPs | Explicit liability framework for Technical Service Providers and card scheme operators |
| No mention of device binding | Strong encouragement for device binding / session binding to prevent code interception |

Key implications for wallet SDK deployments: **device binding** becomes a de facto requirement — PSD3 strongly encourages PSPs to bind the SCA credential to a specific device, preventing attackers from intercepting OTPs and using them from different devices. **SCA for instant payments** — the €30 exemption threshold for instant credit transfers (SCT Inst) means most consumer instant payments now require SCA, significantly increasing the volume of SCA events processed by wallet SDKs. **Embedded finance** — PSD3 clarifies that when an embedded finance provider delegates SCA to a bank, the bank's SCA interface must meet the same dynamic linking requirements as if the customer were interacting directly with the bank.

#### 15.3 Credential Lifecycle and Operational Procedures

Wallet SDK credentials progress through a defined lifecycle — from initial device enrollment through active use, rotation, multi-device synchronisation, and eventual decommissioning. The following sections detail each phase of this lifecycle, covering the ceremonies, state transitions, failure modes, and regulatory audit requirements that production deployments must address.

##### 15.3.1 Enrollment Ceremony

The enrollment flow establishes the cryptographic binding between a user account and a specific physical device. The following sequence diagram illustrates the standard enrollment ceremony:

```mermaid
---
config:
  sequence:
    messageAlign: left
    noteAlign: left
    actorMargin: 250
  themeVariables:
    noteBkgColor: "transparent"
    noteBorderColor: "transparent"
---
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber

    participant User
    participant App as Banking App<br/>(Wallet SDK)
    participant SE as Secure Enclave /<br/>StrongBox
    participant CMS as Bank CMS<br/>(Backend)

    rect rgba(148, 163, 184, 0.14)
        Note over User, CMS: Phase 1 — User Identity Verification
        User->>App: Launch banking app
        App->>CMS: Authenticate via existing channel<br/>(online banking credentials / in-branch / video-ID)
        CMS-->>App: Identity verified — issue enrollment session token
        Note right of CMS: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(52, 152, 219, 0.14)
        Note over User, CMS: Phase 2 — Key Generation and Device Attestation
        App->>SE: Generate ECDSA P-256 key pair<br/>(non-exportable, hardware-backed)
        SE-->>App: Public key
        App->>App: Perform device attestation<br/>(App Attest / Play Integrity)
        App->>CMS: Submit {public_key, attestation_proof,<br/>device_fingerprint, enrollment_session_token}
        Note right of CMS: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(46, 204, 113, 0.14)
        Note over User, CMS: Phase 3 — Backend Verification and Binding
        CMS->>CMS: Verify attestation proof<br/>(validate certificate chain /<br/>check integrity verdict)
        CMS->>CMS: Bind public key to user account<br/>(store in device credential registry)
        CMS-->>App: Activation code (6–8 digit numeric,<br/>delivered via SMS / email / in-branch)
        Note right of CMS: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end

    rect rgba(241, 196, 15, 0.14)
        Note over User, CMS: Phase 4 — User Activation
        User->>App: Enter activation code
        App->>CMS: Submit activation code + signature<br/>(signed with device private key)
        CMS->>CMS: Verify activation code +<br/>verify signature against stored public key
        CMS-->>App: Credential state → ACTIVATED
        App->>User: Setup PIN and/or biometric enrollment
        Note right of CMS: ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀
    end
```

<details><summary><strong>1. User launches banking app</strong></summary>

The user opens the banking application containing the embedded wallet SDK. At this point, no cryptographic credential exists on the device — the SDK is in an unenrolled state. The enrollment flow is typically triggered by the bank's onboarding process, either during first-time app setup or when the bank migrates from a legacy authentication method (SMS OTP, hardware token) to the wallet SDK.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>2. Banking App authenticates via existing channel to Bank CMS</strong></summary>

Before the SDK can generate a credential, the user's identity must be established through an independent channel. The bank's CMS accepts identity verification from:

- **Online banking credentials** — the user enters their existing username/password, which the CMS validates against the identity store. This is the most common remote enrollment path
- **In-branch verification** — a bank employee verifies the user's identity in person (government-issued ID, face-to-face) and initiates the enrollment from the branch workstation
- **Video identification** — a real-time video call with an identity verification agent who checks the user's ID document and performs liveness verification. Common in neobanks and fully digital banks

The CMS issues a time-limited enrollment session token upon successful identity verification.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>3. Bank CMS issues enrollment session token</strong></summary>

The enrollment session token is a short-lived, single-use bearer token that authorises the SDK to submit a key binding request. The token expires after 5–15 minutes (bank-configurable) and is invalidated upon use. This prevents replay attacks where an attacker intercepts an enrollment session and attempts to bind their own device.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>4. Wallet SDK generates ECDSA P-256 key pair in Secure Enclave / StrongBox</strong></summary>

The SDK invokes the platform's hardware key generation API. The critical property is that the private key is generated **inside** the hardware security boundary and never exists in application memory:

- **iOS:** `SecKeyCreateRandomKey` with `kSecAttrKeyTypeECSECPrimeRandom`, `kSecAttrKeySizeInBits: 256`, and `kSecAttrTokenIDSecureEnclave`
- **Android:** `KeyPairGenerator.getInstance("EC", "AndroidKeyStore")` with `.setAlgorithmParameterSpec(ECGenParameterSpec("secp256r1"))` and `.setIsStrongBoxBacked(true)`

The result is a hardware-backed ECDSA P-256 key pair. The private key handle allows signing operations but never exposes the raw key material. The public key is extractable as a standard `SubjectPublicKeyInfo` structure.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>5. Secure Enclave / StrongBox returns public key</strong></summary>

The hardware security module returns the public key in standard DER-encoded format. The private key remains inside the SE/StrongBox — the SDK holds only an opaque handle (iOS: `SecKey` reference; Android: `KeyStore.PrivateKeyEntry`).

</details>

<details><summary><strong>6. Wallet SDK performs device attestation</strong></summary>

Before transmitting the public key to the bank's backend, the SDK generates a device attestation proof:

- **iOS:** `DCAppAttestService.attestKey(_:clientDataHash:)` — produces an attestation object containing the public key, a certificate chain rooted in Apple's App Attest CA, and a receipt proving the app's integrity (§13.2)
- **Android:** The SDK requests a Play Integrity token with a nonce derived from the enrollment session token, producing a signed verdict from Google's attestation server (§13.5)

The attestation proof cryptographically binds the public key to a genuine, unmodified application running on genuine hardware.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>7. Wallet SDK submits enrollment payload to Bank CMS</strong></summary>

The SDK transmits a JSON enrollment request to the bank's CMS:

```json
{
  "enrollment_session_token": "ey...enrollment-jwt...",
  "public_key": "MFkwEwYHKoZIzj0CAQYIKoZIzj0DAQcDQgAE...",
  "attestation_proof": "o2NmbXRv...base64-attestation...",
  "device_fingerprint": {
    "platform": "iOS",
    "os_version": "18.3",
    "device_model": "iPhone16,2",
    "sdk_version": "4.2.1"
  }
}
```

The enrollment payload is transmitted over TLS 1.3. Some SDK vendors additionally establish a proprietary encrypted channel on top of TLS (defence in depth against TLS MITM — §16.5).

</details>

<details><summary><strong>8. Bank CMS verifies attestation proof</strong></summary>

The CMS validates the attestation proof by verifying the certificate chain (iOS — rooted in Apple's App Attest CA) or by sending the integrity token to Google's verification server (Android — Play Integrity API). The CMS checks:

- Certificate chain validity and root CA trust
- The public key in the attestation matches the submitted public key
- The app bundle identifier / package name matches the bank's registered app
- The integrity verdict meets the bank's minimum requirements (e.g., `MEETS_DEVICE_INTEGRITY` for Android)

If attestation verification fails (rooted device, modified app binary, emulator), the CMS rejects the enrollment.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>9. Bank CMS binds public key to user account</strong></summary>

Upon successful attestation verification, the CMS creates a credential record in the device credential registry:

```json
{
  "user_id": "USR-2026-00042187",
  "credential_id": "CRED-A7F3-9B12",
  "device_id": "DEV-iPhone16-2-XK9M",
  "public_key": "MFkwEwYHKoZIzj0CAQYIKoZIzj0DAQcDQgAE...",
  "state": "PROVISIONED",
  "created_at": "2026-03-25T14:32:17Z",
  "attestation_valid_until": "2026-04-24T14:32:17Z"
}
```

The credential is in `PROVISIONED` state — the key pair exists and is bound, but the user has not yet confirmed possession via the activation code.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>10. Bank CMS sends activation code</strong></summary>

The CMS generates a 6–8 digit activation code and delivers it to the user through an independent channel (SMS, email, or in-branch handover). The activation code serves as **out-of-band confirmation** that the enrollment was initiated by the legitimate user, not by an attacker who compromised the user's online banking credentials. The code expires after 10–30 minutes and allows a limited number of entry attempts (typically 3).

</details>

<details><summary><strong>11. User enters activation code</strong></summary>

The user enters the activation code in the banking app. The SDK displays a secure input field (randomised PIN pad for activation code entry, matching the same anti-keylogging protections used for PIN entry — §11.1).

</details>

<details><summary><strong>12. Wallet SDK submits activation code with signature</strong></summary>

The SDK submits the activation code **signed with the device private key**:

```json
{
  "credential_id": "CRED-A7F3-9B12",
  "activation_code": "847291",
  "signature": "MEUCIQD...base64-ecdsa-signature...",
  "signed_data": "CRED-A7F3-9B12|847291|2026-03-25T14:35:42Z"
}
```

The signature proves that the activation code submission originates from the device holding the private key corresponding to the public key registered in step 9 — not from an attacker who intercepted the activation code.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>13. Bank CMS verifies activation code and signature</strong></summary>

The CMS validates:
1. The activation code matches the issued code and has not expired
2. The ECDSA signature over `signed_data` verifies against the stored public key
3. The credential is in `PROVISIONED` state (not already activated, suspended, or revoked)

If all checks pass, the CMS transitions the credential state to `ACTIVATED`.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>14. Bank CMS confirms credential activation</strong></summary>

The CMS returns the activated credential status to the SDK. The credential is now fully operational — the device can authenticate the user and sign transactions.

</details>

<details><summary><strong>15. User completes PIN and biometric setup</strong></summary>

The SDK prompts the user to set up their authentication factors:
- **PIN:** The user chooses a 4–6 digit PIN. The SDK derives the KEK via PBKDF2 (§15.2.2) and stores the encrypted activation token locally
- **Biometric:** The SDK binds the signing key to the platform's biometric authentication (§15.2.3) — the user confirms a Face ID or fingerprint prompt to complete the biometric enrollment

After this step, the enrollment is complete. Subsequent authentications require only PIN or biometric — the full enrollment ceremony is not repeated unless the credential is revoked or the user switches devices.

</details>

##### 15.3.2 Credential State Machine

The credential lifecycle follows a deterministic state machine with server-authoritative state transitions:

```mermaid
---
config:
---
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PROVISIONED: Key pair generated,<br/>public key bound to user
    PROVISIONED --> ACTIVATED: User enters activation code<br/>(out-of-band confirmation)
    ACTIVATED --> SUSPENDED: Bank suspends<br/>(fraud alert, user request,<br/>compliance hold)
    SUSPENDED --> ACTIVATED: Bank reactivates<br/>(investigation resolved)
    SUSPENDED --> REVOKED: Bank revokes<br/>(confirmed compromise)
    ACTIVATED --> REVOKED: Bank revokes<br/>(device lost/stolen,<br/>user departs)
    REVOKED --> [*]: Local key zeroized,<br/>credential permanently invalidated
    PROVISIONED --> REVOKED: Activation timeout<br/>(code expired, max attempts)
```

**State definitions:**

| State | Description | Permitted Operations |
|:------|:-----------|:--------------------|
| **PROVISIONED** | Key pair generated and bound; awaiting activation code confirmation | None — the credential cannot sign transactions |
| **ACTIVATED** | Fully operational — the credential is authorised for authentication and signing | Authentication, transaction signing, key rotation |
| **SUSPENDED** | Temporarily disabled — the credential exists but cannot be used | None — the SDK refuses signing requests; the CMS returns `credential_suspended` |
| **REVOKED** | Permanently invalidated — the local private key has been zeroized | None — re-enrollment required. The CMS archives the credential record for audit purposes |

**Server-authoritative model:** The CMS is the authoritative source of credential state. The SDK polls the CMS periodically (every 5–60 seconds, configurable) or receives state change notifications via push (APNs / FCM). When the SDK detects a state transition to SUSPENDED or REVOKED, it takes immediate local action:

- **SUSPENDED:** The SDK disables signing operations and displays a message directing the user to contact the bank
- **REVOKED:** The SDK invokes the platform API to delete the private key from the SE/StrongBox (`SecKeyDelete` on iOS, `KeyStore.deleteEntry()` on Android), zeroizes the stored activation token, and clears all local credential state. This is a **destructive, irreversible** operation — the credential cannot be recovered

**Formal state transition table.** Each state transition has associated guard conditions (prerequisites) and actions (side effects). This table provides a machine-readable specification complementing the Mermaid state diagram above:

| Current State | Event | Guard Conditions | Actions | Next State |
|:-------------|:------|:-----------------|:--------|:-----------|
| NONE | Enrollment initiated | User authenticated via eKYC, device passes attestation | Generate key pair, store public key on CMS | PROVISIONED |
| PROVISIONED | PIN set | CMS challenge verified | Derive KEK from PIN, encrypt key reference | PROVISIONED (PIN set) |
| PROVISIONED | Biometric enrolled | PIN already set, biometric hardware available | Store biometric access policy | ACTIVATED |
| PROVISIONED | Activation timeout | No activation within grace period (typically 24h) | Delete key pair, notify user | NONE (expired) |
| ACTIVATED | Authentication requested | Device attestation passes, RASP checks pass | Sign challenge, increment auth counter | ACTIVATED |
| ACTIVATED | Transaction signing | Device attestation passes, user confirms details | Sign transaction hash with amount+payee binding | ACTIVATED |
| ACTIVATED | PIN change requested | User verified via biometric or current PIN | Derive new KEK, re-encrypt key reference | ACTIVATED |
| ACTIVATED | Suspend requested | User-initiated or admin-initiated, MFA verified | Disable signing capability, keep key material | SUSPENDED |
| SUSPENDED | Resume requested | User re-verified via eKYC or alternative factor | Re-enable signing capability | ACTIVATED |
| SUSPENDED | Suspend timeout | No resume within 90 days | Initiate revocation workflow | REVOKED |
| ACTIVATED | Revoke requested | User-initiated or admin-initiated (fraud detection) | Destroy local key material, notify CMS, notify other devices | REVOKED |
| ANY | Attestation failure | RASP or device attestation returns fail | Block authentication, escalate to fraud engine | (no state change, auth blocked) |
| REVOKED | Re-enrollment | User re-authenticated via eKYC, new device attestation | Generate new key pair, full enrollment ceremony | PROVISIONED |

##### 15.3.3 Race Condition Analysis

Concurrent state transitions can lead to race conditions that undermine security or usability. Key scenarios and their mitigations:

**Scenario 1 — Concurrent revocation and authentication.** A user (or fraud team) revokes a credential while an authentication is in progress. The authentication should be rejected even if the signing operation completes locally, because the server-side revocation takes precedence.

→ **Mitigation:** The CMS uses optimistic concurrency control with version numbers. Each state change increments a version counter. The authentication validation endpoint checks the version counter at the time of signature verification — if the credential version has changed (due to revocation), the authentication is rejected regardless of signature validity.

**Scenario 2 — Concurrent activation on two devices.** A user initiates activation on device A and device B simultaneously. Both devices may believe they have a valid credential.

→ **Mitigation:** The CMS enforces a "first-to-activate-wins" policy. The activation endpoint is idempotent, but the second activation attempt fails with a "credential already activated" error. The bank backend additionally enforces a maximum device limit per user.

**Scenario 3 — Key rotation during transaction signing.** A key rotation is initiated while a transaction signing request is in flight. The old key signs the transaction, but the CMS has already registered the new key.

→ **Mitigation:** During the key rotation window (typically 30 minutes), both old and new keys are accepted by the CMS. The old key is marked as "rotating" (not "revoked") and can still sign transactions. After the rotation window expires, the old key is automatically revoked.

##### 15.3.4 Multi-Device Handling

Users commonly have the banking app installed on multiple devices (phone + tablet, personal phone + work phone). Wallet SDKs handle this by maintaining **independent credentials per device** in the bank's CMS:

- Each device generates its own key pair during its own enrollment ceremony
- The CMS maintains a device credential registry per user: `user → [credential₁(device₁), credential₂(device₂), ...]`
- Revoking one device's credential does not affect other devices — the bank can selectively revoke a compromised device while leaving the user's other devices operational
- Some banks impose a maximum device limit (e.g., 3 active devices per user) as a risk control — enrolling device₄ requires revoking one of the existing three

##### 15.3.5 Key Rotation

Periodic key rotation replaces the device's signing key pair with a fresh key pair. The rotation ceremony uses the **old key to authorise the new key**, creating a cryptographic chain of custody:

1. SDK generates a new ECDSA P-256 key pair in hardware-backed storage
2. SDK constructs a key rotation request containing the new public key
3. SDK signs the rotation request with the **old** private key — proving that the request originates from the currently enrolled device
4. CMS verifies the signature with the stored (old) public key
5. CMS updates the credential record: replaces the old public key with the new public key, logs the rotation event
6. CMS confirms the rotation to the SDK
7. SDK deletes the old private key from hardware-backed storage

The rotation ceremony ensures that even if an old private key is somehow compromised (e.g., through a future hardware vulnerability in the SE/StrongBox), the window of exposure is limited to the rotation period. Typical rotation intervals are 90–180 days, though some high-security deployments rotate as frequently as every 30 days.

##### 15.3.6 Disaster Recovery

If a user loses their device, the credential stored in the Secure Enclave / StrongBox is permanently lost — there is no mechanism to extract or transfer the private key. Recovery strategies include:

1. **Server-side re-enrollment:** The user completes a full eKYC verification on a new device. The CMS issues a new credential bound to the new device. All previous sessions on other devices remain valid
2. **Recovery codes:** Some SDK implementations generate one-time recovery codes (8-digit alphanumeric strings) during enrollment, stored or printed by the user. These codes can activate a credential on a replacement device without completing full eKYC
3. **Multi-device independence:** Each device receives an independent credential — losing one device does not affect credentials on other devices. The user can continue banking on remaining devices while enrolling a replacement
4. **Temporary session extension:** When device loss is reported, the CMS can extend the session lifetime on remaining devices, giving the user time to complete re-enrollment without service interruption

##### 15.3.7 Regulatory Audit Trail

PSD2 requires payment service providers to maintain comprehensive audit trails of authentication events. Wallet SDKs generate structured event logs for each significant lifecycle event:

| Event Type | Data Captured | Retention Period |
|:-----------|:-------------|:-----------------|
| Credential provisioning | User ID, device model, OS version, attestation result, timestamp | 5 years |
| Credential activation | User ID, device ID, activation method (PIN/biometric), timestamp | 5 years |
| Authentication (login) | User ID, device ID, auth method used, RASP status, attestation nonce, timestamp | 5 years |
| Transaction signing | Transaction ID, amount, payee, signed hash, auth method, timestamp | 7 years |
| Credential revocation | User ID, device ID, revocation reason (user-initiated / admin / suspicious), timestamp | 5 years |
| Failed authentication | User ID, device ID, failure reason (wrong PIN / biometric fail / RASP alert), attempt count | 5 years |
| Key rotation | Old credential ID, new credential ID, rotation reason, timestamp | 5 years |
| Device attestation check | Device ID, attestation result (pass/fail), risk signals, timestamp | 2 years |

#### 15.4 SDK Integration Patterns for Mobile Platforms

##### 15.4.1 iOS Integration

| Aspect | Details |
|:-------|:--------|
| **Distribution format** | XCFramework — universal binary supporting arm64 device + x86_64 simulator in a single distributable. Delivered via private CocoaPods repo, Swift Package Manager, or direct binary inclusion |
| **Key storage** | Secure Enclave via `Security.framework` — `SecKeyCreateRandomKey` with `kSecAttrTokenIDSecureEnclave` (§11.2.1) |
