#### Domain Investigation Paths

The following table summarises concrete investigation paths for each domain, with specific methodologies and expected outputs:

| Domain | Investigation Path | Methodology | Expected Output | Priority |
|:-------|:-------------------|:------------|:----------------|:---------|
| **Auth and Credentials** | Passkey adoption tracker | Quarterly data collection from FIDO Alliance, platform vendors, and relying parties; statistical analysis of adoption trends | Adoption dashboard; quarterly trend report | Medium |
| **Auth and Credentials** | EUDI Wallet UX evaluation | User testing with pilot wallet implementations; heuristic evaluation against social login baseline | UX evaluation report; design recommendations | High |
| **Architecture and Standards** | CAEP interop test suite | Develop conformance test vectors; test against 3+ implementations; publish results | Open-source test suite; interop report | High |
| **Architecture and Standards** | DBSC browser implementation tracker | Monitor browser vendor status pages; test Chrome DBSC with reference implementation | Browser support matrix; implementation guide | Medium |
| **Architecture and Standards** | Federated logout reliability study | Deploy CAEP-based logout in controlled environment; inject failures; measure reliability metrics | Reliability benchmarks; failure mode catalogue | Medium |
| **AI and Non-Human Identity** | AI agent permission model taxonomy | Literature review + expert interviews; classify existing approaches; identify gaps | Taxonomy paper; gap analysis | High |
| **AI and Non-Human Identity** | NHI growth empirical survey | Survey 100+ enterprises; collect NHI counts, management practices, incident data | NHI maturity benchmark report | High |
| **AI and Non-Human Identity** | AI agent session lifetime experiment | Deploy agents with varying session policies; measure task completion, security events, user satisfaction | Session policy recommendation | Medium |
| **Privacy and Regulation** | CIAM/WIAM market analysis | Vendor product analysis; customer case studies; pricing model comparison | Market analysis report | Low |
| **Privacy and Regulation** | Behavioral biometrics legal analysis | GDPR/CCPA case law review; DPA consultation; legal opinion commissioning | Legal risk assessment | Medium |
| **Cryptographic Evolution** | PQC authentication benchmark | Implement hybrid PQC for JWT, SAML, TLS, WebAuthn; measure latency, throughput, size | Benchmark report; migration cost model | High |
| **Cryptographic Evolution** | BBS+ vs SD-JWT comparative study | Implement both formats; test interop, performance, and security properties | Comparative evaluation report | Medium |

#### Research Agenda Summary

Based on the analysis above, five concrete research tracks are recommended, ordered by priority:

**Track 1 — AI Agent Identity Governance (OQ3, OQ4, OQ6):** Highest priority due to the fastest-moving and most uncertain variable in the authentication landscape. Programme includes empirical survey of 50+ enterprises on AI agent authentication practices, comprehensive threat model for AI agent authentication, prototype of the risk-tiered scoping framework, and IETF draft contribution for an AI-agent-specific OAuth RAR profile. Estimated effort: 18–24 months.

**Track 2 — Post-Quantum Authentication Migration (OQ8):** PQC migration affects every component of the authentication stack. Programme includes performance benchmarking of hybrid PQC mode across JWT signing, SAML assertion generation, TLS handshake, and WebAuthn ceremony completion; step-by-step migration playbook; and PQC-resistant WebAuthn/passkey feasibility assessment. Estimated effort: 12–18 months.

**Track 3 — CAEP Interoperability and Federated Logout (OQ5, OQ9):** Systematic interop testing between at least 3 CAEP implementations; CAEP-based federated logout evaluation with 5+ relying parties; reference architecture patterns for single-vendor, multi-vendor, and hybrid cloud environments. Estimated effort: 12–18 months.

**Track 4 — Privacy-Preserving Credential Formats (OQ2, OQ11):** Rigorous side-by-side evaluation of SD-JWT and BBS+ implementations; post-quantum readiness assessment of both approaches; EUDI Wallet pilot deployment field study. Estimated effort: 12–18 months.

**Track 5 — Behavioral Biometrics Privacy Framework (OQ12):** Legal analysis of behavioral biometric data classification under GDPR, CCPA/CPRA; tiered privacy framework specifying minimum requirements for on-device processing, data minimisation, consent management, and audit logging; production pilot evaluation with a behavioral biometrics vendor. Estimated effort: 12–15 months.

#### Monitoring Framework

To track progress on resolving the open questions, a monitoring framework with the following components is recommended:

| Signal Type | Description | Collection Method | Frequency |
|:------------|:-------------|:-----------------|:----------|
| **Standards signals** | RFC publications, draft progression, working group activity | IETF datatracker, W3C specs, OIDF publications | Monthly |
| **Market signals** | Vendor product announcements, pricing changes, partnership announcements | Vendor blogs, press releases, conference talks | Monthly |
| **Adoption signals** | Deployment metrics, customer case studies, usage statistics | Industry surveys, vendor reports, public case studies | Quarterly |
| **Regulatory signals** | Regulatory guidance, enforcement actions, legislative proposals | Official journals, regulatory agency publications | Monthly |
| **Research signals** | Academic publications, security conference presentations, vulnerability disclosures | Google Scholar, CVE databases, conference proceedings | Quarterly |
| **Community signals** | Open-source project activity, developer forum discussions, social media sentiment | GitHub, Stack Overflow, Reddit, Twitter/X | Monthly |

**Reporting cadence:** Monthly signal collection and preliminary analysis; quarterly structured assessment of each open question with updated probability estimates; annually comprehensive review with updated research agenda and adjusted track priorities.

**Escalation criteria:** A signal should trigger a deep-dive reassessment when: (1) a major vendor makes a significant product announcement affecting the question's assumptions; (2) a regulatory body publishes binding guidance that constrains or enables a resolution path; (3) a security incident demonstrates a previously theoretical risk; (4) a standards body publishes a final specification that materially changes the landscape; (5) market adoption data deviates significantly (>20%) from projected trends.

#### Research Readiness Indicators

For each open question, the current state of research readiness — how close the research community is to conducting the recommended investigations:

| Open Question | Data Availability | Tool Maturity | Community Engagement | Overall Readiness |
|:--------------|:-----------------|:-------------|:--------------------|:------------------|
| **OQ1** (Passkeys) | High | High | High | ✅ Ready |
| **OQ2** (BBS+) | Medium | Medium | Medium | 🔶 Approaching ready |
| **OQ3** (AI Agent Scoping) | Low | Low | Low | 🔴 Early research |
| **OQ4** (NHI Ratio) | Medium | Medium | Medium | 🔶 Approaching ready |
| **OQ5** (CAEP Interop) | Medium | Medium | Medium | ✅ Ready |
| **OQ6** (Agent Sessions) | Low | Low | Low | 🔴 Early research |
| **OQ7** (DBSC) | Medium | Medium | Medium | ✅ Ready |
| **OQ8** (Post-Quantum) | High | High | High | ✅ Ready |
| **OQ9** (Federated Logout) | Low | Medium | Medium | 🔶 Approaching ready |
| **OQ10** (CIAM/WIAM) | High | N/A | High | ✅ Ready |
| **OQ11** (EUDI Wallet) | Medium | Medium | High | 🔶 Approaching ready |
| **OQ12** (Behavioral Biometrics) | Medium | Medium | Medium | 🔶 Approaching ready |

Questions rated ✅ Ready can be investigated immediately with existing data and tools. Questions rated 🔶 Approaching ready require 6–12 months of preparation (tooling development, data collection setup). Questions rated 🔴 Early research require foundational work (defining metrics, developing tools, building community).

**Synthesis and forward outlook.** The twelve open questions identified in this chapter represent the authentic frontier of authentication and session management research — not gaps in the research but the leading edge of a rapidly evolving discipline. The questions cluster into five domains (Authentication and Credentials, Architecture and Standards, AI and Non-Human Identity, Privacy and Regulation, and Cryptographic Evolution), each with distinct characteristics that determine the appropriate research approach. AI agent identity governance and post-quantum migration are the most urgent tracks, while active investigation is maintained across all five domains. None of these questions will be resolved by a single event, publication, or deployment — they will be settled incrementally through the accumulated weight of standards progress, market adoption, regulatory guidance, and security incident evidence. The value of this research agenda lies not in predicting the answers but in ensuring that the right questions are being asked, the right signals are being monitored, and the right investigations are being conducted at the right time.

---

#### Findings to Recommendations to Open Questions Traceability

| Finding | Core Insight | Recommendation(s) | Open Question(s) |
|:--------|:-------------|:-------------------|:-----------------|
| **F1** (Auth bifurcation) | Consumer synced vs enterprise hardware-bound passkeys | R1 (Passkeys), R15 (Bootstrap) | OQ1 (Universal adoption) |
| **F2** (CIAM/WIAM divergence) | Architecturally disjoint, no convergence | R7 (Segregate pipelines) | OQ10 (CIAM/WIAM boundary) |
| **F3** (Legacy MFA insufficient) | AiTM, fatigue, SIM swap bypass traditional MFA | R1 (Passkeys) | OQ1 (Passkey adoption) |
| **F4** (Zero-secret M2M) | Static secrets → ephemeral credentials | R2 (Eliminate M2M secrets), R5 (NHI governance) | — |
| **F5** (NHI governance gap) | 100:1 ratio, no lifecycle management | R5 (NHI governance) | OQ4 (NHI ratio sustainability) |
| **F6** (AI agent identity) | Novel hybrid identity class | R10 (AI agent scopes) | OQ3 (Agent scoping), OQ6 (Agent sessions) |
| **F7** (Session > Auth complexity) | Session management is the real breach vector | R6 (BFF), R12 (Short JWTs) | OQ9 (Federated logout) |
| **F8** (Bearer → sender-constrained) | DPoP, mTLS, DBSC ending bearer tokens | R4 (DPoP), R6 (BFF) | OQ7 (DBSC ubiquity) |
| **F9** (Federated logout unsolved) | Front-channel broken, back-channel inconsistent | R8 (Deprecate front-channel) | OQ9 (Federated logout), OQ5 (CAEP interop) |
| **F10** (CAE future) | Continuous evaluation replaces static tokens | R3 (CAE), R12 (Short JWTs) | OQ5 (CAEP interop) |
| **F11** (Cross-device dominant) | Mobile as universal authenticator | R1 (Passkeys), R15 (Bootstrap) | OQ1 (Passkeys) |
| **F12** (Kerberos persists) | Entrenched in enterprise, no migration path | R13 (Retain Kerberos, migrate cloud) | — |
| **F13** (RBA for CIAM) | Friction only when risk signals deviate | R9 (Risk-based auth) | — |
| **F14** (Privacy crypto ready, not deployed) | BBS+, SD-JWT, ZKPs mathematically sound | R14 (Privacy-preserving proofs) | OQ2 (BBS+ in eIDAS), OQ8 (Post-quantum) |
| **F15** (Device posture prerequisite) | Hardware attestation for Zero Trust | R11 (Endpoint posture) | — |
| **F16** (Phishing + device binding converge) | FIDO2 + DPoP = unified security model | R1 (Passkeys), R4 (DPoP) | OQ1 (Passkeys), OQ7 (DBSC) |
| **F17** (ZKPs resolve identity vs privacy) | Identity-by-proof, not identity-by-assertion | R14 (Privacy-preserving proofs) | OQ2 (BBS+), OQ8 (Post-quantum) |
| **F18** (Front→back channel migration) | Browser sandbox unreliable for identity | R6 (BFF), R8 (Logout) | OQ9 (Federated logout) |
| **F19** (BFF/Token Handler for SPAs) | Move tokens out of browser sandbox | R6 (BFF), R4 (DPoP in BFF) | — |
| **F20** (Step-up auth chimera) | ACR/ACR subjective interpretation blocks universal step-up | R3 (CAE), R9 (RBA) | OQ5 (CAEP interop) |
| **F21** (Biometrics authenticator-bound) | Biometric data never leaves device | R1 (Passkeys) | — |
| **F22** (Passwordless bootstrap unsolved) | Recovery/bootstrap is weakest link | R15 (Bootstrap) | OQ1 (Passkeys) |
| **F23** (FIDO2 platform fragmentation) | Apple/Google/Microsoft implement differently | R1 (Passkeys), R15 (Bootstrap) | OQ1 (Passkeys), OQ11 (EUDI Wallet) |
| **F24** (Compliance trails crypto by decade) | Regulatory definitions written for SMS OTP | R14 (Privacy-preserving proofs) | OQ8 (Post-quantum), OQ12 (Biometrics surveillance) |
| **F25** (IAM + network convergence) | Identity signals replace network location | R3 (CAE), R11 (Endpoint posture) | OQ5 (CAEP interop) |

---

## References

This document uses **narrative-style inline references** throughout. Standards, RFCs, regulations, and specifications are cited in context at the point of discussion — e.g., `(RFC 9449)`, `(NIST SP 800-63B, §7.1)`, `(ISO 18013-5 §10.1.1.5)` — rather than collected in a numbered bibliography. Each chapter's prose contains the full citation context for every source it relies on.
